topics.title: Activities of Industrial Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Activities of Industrial Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Industrial Engineering (IE)** is the branch of engineering that deals with the **design, improvement, and installation of integrated systems** of **men, materials, machinery, and information**. It uses specialized knowledge and skills from the physical, mathematical, engineering, and management sciences to optimize complex processes, systems, or organizations.

The core philosophy of Industrial Engineering is the continuous pursuit of **productivity improvement** through the elimination of **waste (muda), irregularity (mura), and overburden (muri)** — a principle popularized by the Toyota Production System.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Phrasing):**
> *"Industrial Engineering is concerned with the design, improvement, installation, and management of integrated systems of personnel, materials, equipment, and processes to provide specified products or services most economically, while ensuring worker health, safety, and morale."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Analogy

Imagine a **hospital** that needs to treat patients efficiently. An **Industrial Engineer** is like the **hospital administrator**:
- They don't perform the surgery themselves (that's the surgeon/specialist).
- They don't build the operating theatre (that's the architect).
- They design the **patient flow** so that beds, doctors, nurses, and equipment are available **exactly when needed**.
- They measure **waiting time**, **OT utilization**, **cost per surgery**, and **patient satisfaction**.
- They continuously find ways to reduce delays, eliminate redundant tests, and improve the patient experience.

In a factory, the Industrial Engineer plays the same role: ensuring that **raw materials, machines, workers, and information** flow together smoothly to produce a quality product at minimum cost.

## 1.3 Classification of Industrial Engineering Activities

The activities of Industrial Engineering are broadly classified into **two categories**:

| **Sl. No.** | **Category** | **Primary Focus** |
|-------------|--------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Traditional (Classical) IE Activities** | Improving the *micro-level* efficiency of direct work processes |
| 2 | **Modern IE Activities** | Improving the *macro-level* efficiency of the entire enterprise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:** Traditional IE focuses on the *shop floor* (method study, time study, motion study), while Modern IE extends to the *entire organization* (OR, simulation, MIS, supply chain).

### 1.3.1 Traditional / Classical IE Activities

1. **Method Study** — Systematic recording, examination, and critical analysis of existing and proposed ways of doing work.
2. **Motion Study** — Analysis of the human motions used in a task to eliminate unnecessary movements (Frank & Lillian Gilbreth, USA).
3. **Time Study** — Determining standard time to perform a task using a stopwatch (Frederick W. Taylor, USA).
4. **Work Sampling** — Statistical technique to determine the proportion of time spent by workers on various activities.
5. **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** — Methods like MTM, MOST for setting standards without stopwatch.
6. **Wage Incentives / Financial Incentives** — Plans like Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux, Taylor to reward efficiency.
7. **Job Evaluation & Merit Rating** — Determining the relative worth of jobs and rating worker performance.
8. **Fatigue Study** — Analysis of physiological and psychological factors causing worker fatigue.
9. **Stool Design / Ergonomics (Micro)** — Designing workstations for human comfort.

### 1.3.2 Modern IE Activities

1. **Operations Research (OR)** — Application of mathematical models for decision-making (Linear Programming, Queuing, Simulation, etc.).
2. **Quality Engineering** — Statistical Quality Control (SQC), Total Quality Management (TQM), Six Sigma, ISO 9000.
3. **Production Planning & Control (PPC)** — Aggregate planning, master scheduling, MRP, JIT.
4. **Inventory & Materials Management** — EOQ, ABC analysis, JIT inventory.
5. **Plant Layout & Facility Design** — Process, Product, and Cellular layouts.
6. **Engineering Economics / Cost Analysis** — Break-even, time value of money, replacement analysis.
7. **Management Information Systems (MIS)** — Data-driven decision support.
8. **Ergonomics & Human Factors Engineering (Macro)** — Workplace design, occupational safety.
9. **Simulation** — Digital modelling of systems to test "what-if" scenarios.
10. **Supply Chain & Logistics** — Vendor management, distribution network design.
11. **Project Management** — CPM, PERT techniques.
12. **Value Engineering / Value Analysis** — Function-cost analysis to deliver required function at minimum cost.

## 1.4 Why Industrial Engineering is Multi-Disciplinary

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams — "Q-SPECTRUM":**
> **Q**uality • **S**ystems • **P**roduction • **E**conomics • **C**ost • **T**echnology • **R**esearch • **U**tilization • **M**anagement

An Industrial Engineer draws tools from **mechanical, electrical, civil, and computer engineering** as well as **management, statistics, and psychology** to solve the productivity equation:

$$\boxed{\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{\text{Output (Goods / Services)}}{\text{Input (Resources Consumed)}}}$$

The single goal of all IE activities is to **increase this ratio** — produce **more** output using **less** input, or produce the **same** output using **fewer** resources.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Analytical Framework for Each Major IE Activity

### 2.1.1 Method Study (Work Design)

Method Study follows a strict **8-step procedure** (British Standard BS 3138:1992) — a frequent KTU exam question.

| **Step** | **Name** | **Purpose** |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Select** the work to be studied | Define scope and boundaries |
| 2 | **Record** the present method | Use process charts, flow diagrams, man-machine charts |
| 3 | **Examine** the recorded facts | Use the **5W1H + 4 Es principle** |
| 4 | **Develop** the most practical/economic method | Use brainstorming, creativity techniques |
| 5 | **Define** the new method | Write a clear standard operating procedure |
| 6 | **Install** the new method | Get worker/supervisor buy-in and training |
| 7 | **Maintain** the new method | Periodic audits, audits by supervisors |
| 8 | **Develop** a higher standard | Continuous improvement (Kaizen) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5W1H + 4 Es Principle (Exam Favourite):**
> - **5W1H:** **W**hat is done? **W**hy is it done? **W**here is it done? **W**hen is it done? **W**ho does it? **H**ow is it done?
> - **4 Es:** **E**liminate (remove unnecessary work) → **E**liminate (or combine) → **E**conomize → **E**ngineer simplicity

### 2.1.2 Time Study & Standard Time Determination

Time study determines the **Standard Time (ST)** for a qualified worker operating at a **normal pace** under standard conditions.

The sequence is:
1. **Observe** and record actual time for each element (using stopwatch).
2. **Rate** the worker's performance using **Performance Rating (R)** factor.
3. Compute **Normal Time (NT)**:

$$NT = \text{Observed Time (OT)} \times \text{Performance Rating (R)}$$

4. Add **Allowances (A)** for personal, fatigue, and contingency needs:

$$ST = NT \times \left(1 + \text{Allowance Fraction}\right)$$

5. **Result:** The **Standard Time** per piece / per cycle.

### 2.1.3 Work Sampling — A Statistical Alternative

When stopwatch study is impractical (long, non-repetitive, or office work), **Work Sampling** is used. It is based on the theory of **binomial distribution**, which is approximated by the **normal distribution** for large samples.

The proportion of time a worker spends on a given activity is estimated from **random instantaneous observations**. The required sample size is:

$$N = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$$

where:
- **N** = number of random observations required
- **Z** = standard normal variate for desired confidence (e.g., 1.96 for 95%)
- **p** = estimated proportion of occurrence of the activity
- **E** = permissible absolute error (precision limit)

### 2.1.4 Operations Research (OR) in IE

OR provides **mathematical optimization** tools. The general OR approach involves:

1. **Formulate** the problem (decision variables, objective, constraints).
2. **Construct** a mathematical model.
3. **Solve** the model using analytical or iterative methods.
4. **Validate** the solution.
5. **Implement** the recommended decision.

Common OR techniques in IE:
- **Linear Programming (LP)** — resource allocation, product mix.
- **Transportation & Assignment Models** — distribution of goods/jobs.
- **Queuing Theory** — waiting line analysis (M/M/1, M/M/c models).
- **Game Theory** — competitive decision making.
- **Dynamic Programming** — multi-stage decisions.
- **Simulation** — when analytical models are infeasible.

### 2.1.5 Quality Engineering

Quality is no longer just "inspection" — it is built into the process. Key frameworks:

| **Framework** | **Core Idea** | **Origin** |
|---------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Inspection** | 100% check after production | Ancient |
| **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** | Control charts (X̄, R, p, c) | Shewhart, Deming, USA |
| **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | Customer focus, continuous improvement, employee involvement | Deming, Juran, Ishikawa |
| **Six Sigma** | 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO) | Motorola, 1986 |
| **ISO 9000 / QMS** | Documented quality management system | ISO, 1987 |
| **Lean Manufacturing** | Eliminate waste (Muda) | Toyota |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Bookmark this table — it covers 80% of numerical questions in KTU ESE.**

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Used In** |
|-------|-------------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Productivity | $P = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | units / units | All IE |
| 2 | Productivity Index | $PI = \dfrac{\text{Standard Hours Produced}}{\text{Hours Actually Worked}} \times 100$ | % | Wage incentives |
| 3 | Effectiveness Index | $EI = \dfrac{\text{Standard Hours Earned}}{\text{Standard Hours Allowed}}$ | % | Workforce |
| 4 | Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\text{Actual Working Time}}{\text{Total Available Time}}$ | % | Machine / Labour |
| 5 | Performance Rating | $R = \dfrac{\text{Normal Time}}{\text{Observed Time}}$ | ratio | Time study |
| 6 | Normal Time | $NT = OT \times R$ | min | Time study |
| 7 | Standard Time | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ | min | Time study |
| 8 | Standard Hours Earned | $SHE = \text{Units Produced} \times \text{ST per unit}$ | hrs | Incentives |
| 9 | Work Sampling Sample Size | $N = \dfrac{Z^2 p (1-p)}{E^2}$ | obs | Work sampling |
| 10 | Job Satisfaction Index (Example) | $JSI = \dfrac{\text{Satisfied Responses}}{\text{Total Responses}} \times 100$ | % | IE surveys |
| 11 | Cycle Time | $CT = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand}}$ | min/unit | Line balancing |
| 12 | Line Efficiency (Assembly) | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum ST_i}{n \times CT} \times 100$ | % | Assembly lines |
| 13 | Balance Delay | $BD = 100 - \eta$ | % | Assembly lines |
| 14 | EOQ (Economic Order Qty) | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | units | Inventory |
| 15 | Reorder Level | $ROL = \text{Demand} \times \text{Lead Time}$ | units | Inventory |

where:
- **OT** = Observed Time
- **R** = Performance Rating factor
- **A** = Allowance factor (decimal form, e.g., 0.15 for 15%)
- **D** = Annual demand, **S** = Ordering cost, **H** = Holding cost per unit per year

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of IE Activities

| **IE Activity** | **Industrial Application** |
|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Method Study | Redesigning a bank loan approval process |
| Time Study | Setting fair piece-rate wages in a garment factory |
| Work Sampling | Determining machine utilization in a CNC shop |
| Linear Programming | Optimal product mix in a petrochemical refinery |
| Queuing Theory | Designing the number of billing counters in a supermarket |
| EOQ | Reducing inventory holding in a pharmaceutical warehouse |
| Value Engineering | Cutting the cost of a smartphone housing without losing function |
| Ergonomics | Designing an air-hostess seat to reduce back pain on long flights |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners reward practical industrial examples.** In a 14-mark answer, weave in 1 or 2 industry applications for extra marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Standard Time from Time Study Observations

A KTU board favourite is to compute Standard Time from a small set of stopwatch readings and a performance rating. We now derive the formula formally.

### 3.1.1 Given
- Observed times for *n* cycles: $t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots, t_n$ (in minutes per cycle)
- Performance rating of the worker: $R$ (e.g., 110% means above average)
- Total allowance fraction: $A$ (e.g., 0.15 for 15% PF&D)

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Average Observed Time (Mean Cycle Time)

We take the arithmetic mean of the recorded times:

$$\bar{T} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$$

**Why?** Because under central limit theorem, the sample mean is the **best linear unbiased estimator (BLUE)** of the true mean cycle time.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Adjust to Normal Time

The **observed average** reflects the worker's *actual* pace. To convert this to a *standard normal pace* (the pace of a trained, qualified worker), we multiply by the performance rating factor.

If the worker was observed at 110% (i.e., faster than normal), observed time is *less* than what a normal worker would take. Hence we *add* the missing 10% by multiplying by 1.10:

$$NT = \bar{T} \times R$$

If $R = 1.10$ (110%), then $NT = 1.10 \times \bar{T}$ — the normal worker is theoretically **slower**, so the standard time for them is higher than what we observed. This is the **rating logic**.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Add Allowances

A worker cannot work at 100% efficiency for 8 hours straight. We add personal time, fatigue time, and contingency time as a **percentage** of normal time.

$$ST = NT + (Allowance) = NT + NT \cdot A$$

Factoring:

$$ST = NT \cdot (1 + A)$$

**Final compact formula:**

$$\boxed{ST = \left(\dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i\right) \cdot R \cdot (1+A)}$$

### 3.1.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard Style)

> **Problem:** A stopwatch time study of 10 cycles of an operation gave the following observed times (in min): 1.20, 1.25, 1.18, 1.22, 1.30, 1.20, 1.19, 1.24, 1.21, 1.23. The worker was rated at 105%. Allowances are 12%. Compute the Standard Time.

**Step 1 — Mean observed time:**

$$\bar{T} = \dfrac{1.20+1.25+1.18+1.22+1.30+1.20+1.19+1.24+1.21+1.23}{10}$$

$$\bar{T} = \dfrac{12.22}{10} = 1.222 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Normal Time:**

$$NT = 1.222 \times 1.05 = 1.2831 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Standard Time:**

$$ST = 1.2831 \times (1 + 0.12) = 1.2831 \times 1.12 = 1.4371 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{ST \approx 1.44 \text{ min per piece}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Work Sampling Sample Size

### 3.2.1 Governing Distribution

Each random observation is a **Bernoulli trial**: it either falls on the activity (success) or not (failure). For *N* independent observations, the count of successes follows the **Binomial distribution** with mean $Np$ and variance $Np(1-p)$.

For large $N$, the binomial distribution is approximated by the **normal distribution**:

$$\text{Mean} = Np, \quad \text{Standard Deviation} = \sigma = \sqrt{Np(1-p)}$$

### 3.2.2 Confidence Interval Logic

The estimate of the true proportion $p$ is $\hat{p} = X/N$. The standard error of $\hat{p}$ is:

$$SE(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

For a two-sided confidence interval at confidence level $1-\alpha$:

$$P\!\left(\; \vert \hat{p} - p \vert \leq E \;\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

This implies:

$$E = Z \cdot SE = Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

### 3.2.3 Solve for N

Squaring both sides:

$$E^2 = Z^2 \cdot \dfrac{p(1-p)}{N}$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- Higher confidence → larger $Z$ → more observations.
- Tighter precision (small $E$) → more observations.
- Maximum uncertainty at $p=0.5$ → $p(1-p) = 0.25$ → maximum $N$.

### 3.2.4 Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A supervisor wants to know the proportion of time workers spend in productive activity. Prior studies suggest $p \approx 0.6$. The IE wants 95% confidence with ±0.04 precision. Find the number of random observations.

**Solution:**
- $Z = 1.96$ (95% confidence)
- $p = 0.6$, so $(1-p) = 0.4$
- $E = 0.04$

$$N = \dfrac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.6 \times 0.4}{(0.04)^2} = \dfrac{3.8416 \times 0.24}{0.0016} = \dfrac{0.9220}{0.0016} = 576.25$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 577 \text{ observations (rounded up)}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Apply Each IE Activity

| **Problem Type** | **Best IE Tool** | **Reason** |
|------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Repetitive manual work, short cycle | Time Study (Stopwatch) | High accuracy, low cost |
| Long, irregular, office work | Work Sampling | Less obtrusive, statistical validity |
| Standard data required without timing | PMTS (MTM, MOST) | Pre-defined times, no stopwatch |
| Allocation of limited resources | Linear Programming | Optimal solution, well-understood |
| Waiting line / queue design | Queuing Theory | Models arrival and service rates |
| Complex stochastic system | Simulation | When closed-form math is too hard |
| Cost reduction without function loss | Value Engineering | Function analysis, creativity |
| Workplace discomfort | Ergonomics (Micro) | Anthropometric and biomechanical data |
| Supplier selection / Vendor rating | Linear Programming (Assignment) | Multi-criteria optimisation |
| Project scheduling | CPM / PERT | Network-based, handles uncertainty |
| Inventory control | EOQ / ABC Analysis | Minimise total cost |
| Quality defect reduction | SQC, Six Sigma, FMEA | Statistical and structured problem solving |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Map of Industrial Engineering Activities

The diagram below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing how all major IE activities are interconnected around the central goal of **productivity improvement**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Central["IE CENTRAL GOAL\nPRODUCTIVITY IMPROVEMENT\nP = Output / Input"]

    subgraph TRAD["TRADITIONAL IE ACTIVITIES"]
        direction TB
        MS["METHOD STUDY\n8-step procedure"]
        TS["TIME STUDY\nStopwatch + Rating"]
        WS["WORK SAMPLING\nStatistical"]
        PMTS["PMTS / MTM / MOST\nPredetermined Times"]
        MOT["MOTION STUDY\nTherbligs"]
        ERG["MICRO ERGONOMICS\nWorkstation Design"]
    end

    subgraph MOD["MODERN IE ACTIVITIES"]
        direction TB
        OR["OPERATIONS RESEARCH\nLP, Queuing, Sim"]
        QLY["QUALITY ENGINEERING\nSQC, TQM, Six Sigma"]
        PPC["PRODUCTION PLANNING\nMRP, JIT, Scheduling"]
        INV["INVENTORY MANAGEMENT\nEOQ, ABC, JIT"]
        LAY["PLANT LAYOUT\nProcess / Product"]
        EE["ENGINEERING ECONOMICS\nBreak-even, Time Value"]
        MIS["MANAGEMENT INFO SYSTEMS\nDecision Support"]
        VE["VALUE ENGINEERING\nFunction-Cost Analysis"]
        SAFE["SAFETY ENGINEERING\nAccident Prevention"]
    end

    INPUT["INPUTS\nMen / Material / Machine / Money / Information"] --> Central
    Central --> TRAD
    Central --> MOD
    TRAD --> OUTPUT["OUTPUTS\nHigher Quality, Lower Cost,\nFaster Delivery, Safer Work"]
    MOD --> OUTPUT
    OUTPUT --> FEEDBACK["FEEDBACK LOOP\nContinuous Improvement KAIZEN"]
    FEEDBACK --> Central
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Method Study (8-Step Procedure)

The block diagram below illustrates the **8-step method study procedure** in proper sequence, with the iterative **Kaizen** feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1\nSELECT work\nto be studied"] --> S2["STEP 2\nRECORD present\nmethod via charts"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3\nEXAMINE facts\nusing 5W1H and 4 Es"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4\nDEVELOP new\nimproved method"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5\nDEFINE new\nmethod as SOP"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6\nINSTALL new\nmethod in shop floor"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7\nMAINTAIN new\nmethod via audits"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP 8\nDEVELOP higher\nstandard KAIZEN"]
    S8 -.-> S1
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Standard Time Derivation

The diagram below shows the **flow of data and logic** from raw stopwatch observations to the final Standard Time value.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["RAW OBSERVATIONS\nt1, t2, t3, ... tn"] --> B["ARITHMETIC MEAN\nMean OT"]
    B --> C["MULTIPLY BY R\nPerformance Rating"]
    C --> D["NORMAL TIME\nNT"]
    D --> E["ADD ALLOWANCE\nNT x 1 + A"]
    E --> F["STANDARD TIME\nST per cycle"]
    A --> G["JOB ELEMENT\nSelection"]
    G --> C
    H["ALLOWANCE STUDY\nPersonal / Fatigue\nContingency"] --> E
```

## 4.4 Cross-Reference Matrix: IE Activities vs. Functional Areas

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IE["INDUSTRIAL ENGINEER"]

    subgraph FA["FUNCTIONAL AREAS OF ORGANIZATION"]
        direction TB
        PROD["PRODUCTION"]
        QC["QUALITY"]
        HR["HUMAN RESOURCES"]
        FIN["FINANCE"]
        PUR["PURCHASING"]
        STR["STORES / INVENTORY"]
        MKT["MARKETING"]
        MAIN["MAINTENANCE"]
    end

    IE -->|Method Study, Time Study| PROD
    IE -->|SQC, TQM, Six Sigma| QC
    IE -->|Job Eval, Merit Rating, Ergonomics| HR
    IE -->|Engineering Economics, Costing| FIN
    IE -->|Vendor Selection, EOQ| PUR
    IE -->|ABC Analysis, Inventory Models| STR
    IE -->|Forecasting, Simulation| MKT
    IE -->|Reliability, TPM| MAIN
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"Explain the role of an Industrial Engineer in a manufacturing industry"*, refer to the matrix above — it is a perfect answer framework.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q:** Define Industrial Engineering. List any **four** traditional activities of Industrial Engineering.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Industrial Engineering is the branch of engineering concerned with the **design, improvement, and installation of integrated systems** of men, materials, equipment, and information. It draws upon specialized knowledge in the mathematical, physical, and social sciences to **optimize productivity, quality, and cost** in industrial and service organizations.

**Four Traditional IE Activities:**
1. **Method Study** — Systematic analysis of work methods to develop improved procedures.
2. **Time Study** — Determination of standard time using stopwatch and performance rating.
3. **Motion Study** — Detailed analysis of human motions to eliminate waste.
4. **Work Sampling** — Statistical technique to determine the proportion of time spent on activities.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Wage Incentive Plans, Job Evaluation, Merit Rating, Predetermined Motion Time Systems.)*

**Mark Distribution:** Definition = 2 marks; List (4 items × 0.25 each = 1 mark) → 1 mark for any 4 activities.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q:** Differentiate between **Traditional IE** and **Modern IE** activities. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Traditional IE** | **Modern IE** |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| Focus | Shop floor, direct work | Entire organization, systems |
| Era | 1900s – 1950s | 1960s onwards |
| Tools | Stopwatch, charts, therbligs | Mathematical models, computers, simulation |
| Approach | Trial and error, observational | Analytical, model-based |
| Example 1 | Method Study | Operations Research |
| Example 2 | Time Study | Six Sigma / TQM |

**Mark Distribution:** Comparison table = 2 marks; Examples = 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **8-step procedure of Method Study** as per BS 3138. Discuss the **5W1H and 4 Es technique** used in the critical examination phase.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A stopwatch time study of an assembly operation yielded the observed times (in minutes) for 8 cycles: 2.10, 2.05, 2.20, 2.15, 2.12, 2.08, 2.18, 2.10. The performance rating of the worker was 110%. Personal, fatigue, and contingency allowances total 18%. Compute the **Standard Time per piece** and the **Standard Hours Earned per 8-hour shift** if the worker produces **250 units** in a shift.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks — 8-Step Method Study**

| **Step** | **Name** | **Activity Description** | **Marks** |
|----------|----------|---------------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Select | Identify work to be studied (high cost, bottleneck, repetitive, problem area) | 0.5 |
| 2 | Record | Use process charts, flow process charts, man-machine charts, multiple activity charts | 0.5 |
| 3 | Examine | Critical examination using 5W1H + 4 Es principles | 1.0 |
| 4 | Develop | Brainstorm, create alternatives, use motion economy principles | 0.5 |
| 5 | Define | Document the proposed method as a standard operating procedure | 0.5 |
| 6 | Install | Get approval, train workers, apply the new method | 0.5 |
| 7 | Maintain | Supervise, audit, take corrective action | 0.5 |
| 8 | Develop higher standard | Continuous improvement, Kaizen, ongoing study | 0.5 |

**5W1H Technique (2 marks):**

Each question challenges a different aspect of the task:

| **Question** | **Meaning** | **Example** |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|
| **What** | What is done? | "Welding the joint" |
| **Why** | Why is it done? | "To join the plates" |
| **Where** | Where is it done? | "On the floor" |
| **When** | When is it done? | "After fabrication" |
| **Who** | Who does it? | "Helper" |
| **How** | How is it done? | "Using arc welding" |

**4 Es Technique (2 marks):**

A logical sequence to challenge the necessity of each step:
1. **Eliminate** — Can the operation be eliminated entirely?
2. **Eliminate or Combine** — Can it be combined with another step?
3. **Economize** — Can the order or method be changed to make it cheaper?
4. **Engineer Simplicity** — Can the remaining operation be simplified?

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Standard Time Calculation**

**Step 1 — Mean Observed Time:**

$$\bar{T} = \dfrac{2.10+2.05+2.20+2.15+2.12+2.08+2.18+2.10}{8}$$

$$\bar{T} = \dfrac{16.98}{8} = 2.1225 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Normal Time:**

$$NT = \bar{T} \times R = 2.1225 \times 1.10 = 2.33475 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Standard Time:**

$$ST = NT \times (1+A) = 2.33475 \times 1.18 = 2.7550 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{ST \approx 2.76 \text{ min per piece}}$$

**Step 4 — Standard Hours Earned (SHE):**

$$SHE = \text{Units Produced} \times \text{ST per unit} = 250 \times 2.76 = 690 \text{ min}$$

Converting to hours:

$$SHE = \dfrac{690}{60} = 11.50 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{SHE = 11.5 \text{ hours per 8-hour shift}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students forget to convert the allowance from percentage (18%) to decimal (0.18). One full mark lost.
> - Students often write "ST = 2.33 min" — that is the **Normal Time**, not Standard Time. The allowance step is mandatory.
> - SHE must be in **hours** (not minutes) if the shift is in hours. Always show unit conversion explicitly.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **Work Sampling**? State the **statistical basis** of the technique. Derive the formula for determining the **number of observations required** for a given precision and confidence level.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An Industrial Engineer wants to estimate the proportion of time machines are idle in a workshop. Pilot study showed idle proportion = 0.30. How many random observations are required for:
(i) 95% confidence, ±3% precision?
(ii) 99% confidence, ±3% precision?

Comment on the results.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks — Work Sampling Theory**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Work sampling is a statistical technique used to determine the **proportion of time spent** by workers, machines, or any other resources on various activities. It is based on a large number of **random, instantaneous observations** taken over a period of time, rather than continuous measurement.

**Statistical Basis (2 marks):**
Each random observation is a **Bernoulli trial** (success = activity occurred; failure = activity did not occur). For $N$ independent trials, the count $X$ of successes follows the **Binomial distribution** $X \sim B(N, p)$ with:

$$\text{Mean } \mu = Np, \quad \text{Variance } \sigma^2 = Np(1-p)$$

For large $N$, by the **Central Limit Theorem**, the binomial distribution approaches the **normal distribution**. The sample proportion $\hat{p} = X/N$ has:

$$\text{Mean} = p, \quad \text{Standard Error } = \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

**Derivation (3 marks):** *(Same as Section 3.2.3 — refer to derivation.)*

$$N = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Sample Size Calculation**

**Given:** $p = 0.30$, so $(1-p) = 0.70$, and $p(1-p) = 0.21$.

**Case (i) — 95% confidence, $E = 0.03$:**
- $Z = 1.96$

$$N_1 = \dfrac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.21}{(0.03)^2} = \dfrac{3.8416 \times 0.21}{0.0009} = \dfrac{0.8067}{0.0009}$$

$$\boxed{N_1 \approx 897 \text{ observations}}$$

**Case (ii) — 99% confidence, $E = 0.03$:**
- $Z = 2.576$

$$N_2 = \dfrac{(2.576)^2 \times 0.21}{(0.03)^2} = \dfrac{6.6358 \times 0.21}{0.0009} = \dfrac{1.3935}{0.0009}$$

$$\boxed{N_2 \approx 1548 \text{ observations}}$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
Increasing confidence from 95% to 99% (keeping precision the same) requires about **73% more observations** (from 897 to 1548). This illustrates the **non-linear trade-off** between confidence level, precision, and cost of study. The IE must balance statistical rigour against practical resource constraints.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students use $Z = 2.33$ for 90% confidence or $Z = 2.58$ for 99% — verify your **Z-table** value carefully.
> - Always **round UP** the sample size, never round down. A fractional observation is meaningless.
> - Do not forget to show the **comment / interpretation** in part (b) — it carries at least 1 mark in 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Activities of Industrial Engineering"**

### Core Definitions to Memorize
- ✅ **Industrial Engineering** — design, improvement, and installation of integrated systems of *men, material, money, machine, information*.
- ✅ **Method Study** — systematic 8-step procedure (BS 3138) to improve work methods.
- ✅ **Time Study** — stopwatch-based determination of standard time.
- ✅ **Work Sampling** — statistical technique using random instantaneous observations.
- ✅ **Standard Time** — time required by a qualified worker at normal pace with allowances.
- ✅ **PMTS** — Predetermined Motion Time Systems (MTM, MOST).
- ✅ **Productivity** — ratio of output to input.

### Critical Formulas to Master
- ✅ $ST = \bar{T} \times R \times (1+A)$
- ✅ $N = \dfrac{Z^2 p(1-p)}{E^2}$
- ✅ $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$
- ✅ $ROL = D \times L$
- ✅ $SHE = \text{Units} \times ST$

### 8-Step Method Study Sequence
- ✅ **S**elect → **R**ecord → **E**xamine → **D**evelop → **D**efine → **I**nstall → **M**aintain → **D**evelop higher standard (Mnemonic: **"SR-EDD-IMD"**).

### 5W1H & 4 Es
- ✅ **5W1H** = What, Why, Where, When, Who, How
- ✅ **4 Es** = Eliminate → Eliminate/Combine → Economize → Engineer simplicity

### Key Distinctions (Often Asked)
- ✅ Traditional IE = Shop floor, observation-based (Time Study, Method Study).
- ✅ Modern IE = Organisation-wide, model-based (OR, Simulation, TQM, Six Sigma).
- ✅ Time Study vs Work Sampling — Time Study is *continuous and precise*, Work Sampling is *discrete and statistical*.
- ✅ Normal Time vs Standard Time — Standard Time = Normal Time + Allowances.

### Common Examiner Traps
- ⚠️ Forgetting to add allowances (computing Normal Time and calling it Standard Time).
- ⚠️ Confusing percentage (18%) with decimal (0.18) in allowance.
- ⚠️ Rounding down instead of up the work sampling sample size.
- ⚠️ Wrong Z-value for confidence level.
- ⚠️ Listing IE activities without classifying them as Traditional or Modern.

### Real-World Industrial Examples to Quote
- 🏭 Toyota Production System (Lean, JIT, Kaizen).
- 🏭 Ford Assembly Line (Time Study by Taylor).
- 🏭 Hospital queue management using Queuing Theory.
- 🏭 Amazon warehouse (Method Study, Layout Design).
- 🏭 Indian Railways scheduling (OR, Simulation).
- 🏭 ISRO project management (CPM, PERT).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques of Industrial Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Techniques of Industrial Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Industrial Engineering (IE)?

**Industrial Engineering (IE)** is the branch of engineering concerned with the design, improvement, and installation of integrated systems of people, materials, equipment, and energy. It draws upon specialized knowledge and skill in the mathematical, physical, and social sciences, together with the principles and methods of engineering analysis and design, to specify, predict, and evaluate the results to be obtained from such systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> Industrial Engineering is defined as **"human-centered productivity engineering"** — it is the discipline of optimizing complex processes, systems, or organizations by developing, improving, and implementing integrated systems of people, money, knowledge, information, equipment, energy, and materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The American Institute of Industrial Engineers (AIIE, now IISE) defines IE as: *"The art and science of installing, improving, and operating integrated systems of men, materials, and equipment to produce specified results with minimum expenditure of time and resources."*

---

## 1.2 What are "Techniques" of Industrial Engineering?

The **Techniques of Industrial Engineering** refer to the systematic, scientific toolset that an Industrial Engineer applies to analyze, design, improve, and control work systems. These are not random tools — they form a structured methodology called the **IE Toolbox**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Mechanic's Toolkit 🛠️

Think of a **car mechanic fixing a car engine**. A mechanic doesn't use just one tool — he has a toolbox containing screwdrivers, wrenches, diagnostic machines, gauges, etc. Each tool does a specific job, and the mechanic picks the right one based on the problem.

Similarly, an **Industrial Engineer** has a "toolbox" of techniques:
- When the **process is slow** → use **Method Study**
- When the **output is inconsistent** → use **Time Study** or **Work Sampling**
- When the **layout is messy** → use **Flow Process Charts** and **String Diagrams**
- When the **worker is uncomfortable** → apply **Ergonomics**
- When **wastes creep in** → apply the **7 Tools of Quality** (Ishikawa, Pareto, etc.)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember IE Techniques (4 Pillars):**
> 1. **M** — Method Study
> 2. **W** — Work Measurement (Time Study, Work Sampling, PMTS)
> 3. **E** — Ergonomics & Human Factors
> 4. **P** — Productivity Engineering (including Value Engineering & Standardization)

---

## 1.3 Broad Classification of IE Techniques

The IE techniques covered in **Module 1 of OEIET722 (KTU 2024 Scheme)** can be classified into the following categories:

| # | Category | Core Techniques |
|---|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Method Engineering** | Method Study, Motion Study, Process Charts, Flow Diagrams |
| 2 | **Work Measurement** | Time Study, Work Sampling, Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS) |
| 3 | **Ergonomics** | Workstation Design, Anthropometry, Workload Analysis |
| 4 | **Productivity & Standardization** | Productivity Measurement, Standardization, Value Engineering |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **Standard Time** = Normal Time + Allowances (in minutes per cycle)
> - **Productivity** = Output ÷ Input
> - **Performance Rating Factor** typically ranges from **80% to 120%**

---

## 1.4 The Strategic Position of IE Techniques

```
PRODUCTIVITY EQUATION:
                Output (Quality + Quantity)
Productivity = ─────────────────────────────
                   Input (Resources Used)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Every IE technique — whether it's a flowchart, a stopwatch study, or an ergonomic redesign — fundamentally aims to either **increase the Output** or **decrease the Input** in the productivity equation above. This is the single most important "why" behind the entire module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal IE Methodology — The 4-Stage Approach

Industrial Engineering follows a **scientific problem-solving approach** consisting of four sequential stages. Every technique you will study in this module fits into one of these stages.

### Stage 1: **DEFINE** the Problem
- Identify the work to be studied (symptom: complaints, delays, waste, accidents).
- State the objective clearly (e.g., "Reduce cycle time of assembly line A by 15%").

### Stage 2: **RECORD** the Facts (Data Collection)
- This is where the IE **techniques** are deployed:
  - Process Charts
  - Flow Diagrams
  - Time Study Sheets
  - Activity Sampling Logs
  - Questionnaires
- Use the **5 W's + 1 H** framework: **What, Where, When, Who, Why, How**.

### Stage 3: **EXAMINE & ANALYZE** the Data
- Apply the **ECRS Principle** (Eliminate, Combine, Rearrange, Simplify).
- Use **Critical Examination** to challenge every operation.
- Develop alternative methods and select the best.

### Stage 4: **INSTALL & MAINTAIN** the New Method
- Implement the chosen method.
- Train the workforce.
- Monitor results and refine continuously.

---

## 2.2 The Five Major Techniques — Deep Dive

### A. Method Study

**Method Study** is the systematic recording and critical examination of existing and proposed ways of doing work, with the aim of developing easier, more effective, and more economical methods.

**The 6-Step Method Study Procedure (British Standards Institution — BSI):**
1. **Select** the work to be studied
2. **Record** all relevant facts (using charts/diagrams)
3. **Examine** the recorded facts critically
4. **Develop** the most practical, economic, and effective method
5. **Install** the chosen method
6. **Maintain** the new method through regular follow-up

---

### B. Motion Study

**Motion Study** is the study of the body movements used in performing a task, with the aim of eliminating unnecessary movements, easing the necessary ones, and identifying the most economical sequence of movements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference — Method Study vs Motion Study (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Method Study** = Macro level (entire process, materials, flow)
> - **Motion Study** = Micro level (hand/limb movements of one operator)

---

### C. Time Study

**Time Study** is a work measurement technique for recording the times and rates of working for the elements of a specified job, carried out under specified conditions, and used to determine the **Standard Time** per cycle.

**The 5-Step Time Study Procedure:**
1. Define the job and operator
2. Break the job into **elements**
3. Time each element with a stopwatch (using **Continuous Timing** or **Snap-back Timing**)
4. **Rate** the operator's performance against a normal pace (100% rating)
5. Compute **Normal Time** and add **Allowances** to get **Standard Time**

---

### D. Work Sampling (Activity Sampling)

**Work Sampling** is a statistical technique used to determine the proportion of time spent by workers, machines, or facilities on various activities in a working environment. Instead of continuous observation, **random instantaneous observations** are made and statistically extrapolated.

---

### E. Ergonomics (Human Factors Engineering)

**Ergonomics** is the scientific study of the relationship between man and his working environment. It deals with designing workplaces, equipment, and systems to fit human physical, psychological, and physiological capabilities.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This formula sheet is critical for the KTU Board Examination (Dec 2024 & onwards).** Memorize all entries below.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Standard Constant |
|---|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Normal Time | $T_n = T_o \times R$ | $T_n$: Normal Time (min), $T_o$: Observed Time (min), $R$: Rating Factor | $R = 1.00$ for "Normal" worker |
| 2 | Standard Time | $T_s = T_n \times \left(1 + A\right)$ | $T_s$: Standard Time (min), $A$: Allowance (decimal) | Typical $A = 0.10$ to $0.20$ |
| 3 | Allowance Conversion | $A_{total} = 1 - \dfrac{T_n}{T_s}$ | All in fraction | Sum of Relaxation + Contingency + Process + Special |
| 4 | Work Sampling Estimate | $P = \dfrac{n}{N}$ | $P$: Proportion, $n$: observations of activity, $N$: total observations | Statistical confidence 95% → Z = 1.96 |
| 5 | Sample Size (WS) | $N = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$ | $Z$: confidence coefficient, $p$: estimated proportion, $E$: allowable error | $Z_{95\%} = 1.96$, $Z_{99\%} = 2.576$ |
| 6 | Productivity (Partial) | $P_{partial} = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{One specific input}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | — |
| 7 | Productivity (Total) | $P_{total} = \dfrac{\text{Total Output}}{\text{Total Input}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | — |
| 8 | Performance Rating | $R = \dfrac{\text{Actual Speed}}{\text{Normal Speed}}$ | Percentage | **Normal Speed = 1.00 (100%)** |
| 9 | Cycle Time | $T_c = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{No. of Units Required}}$ | Minutes per unit | — |
| 10 | Standard Output | $S_{out} = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \times 60$ | Units per hour | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not use vertical pipe `|` inside tables** — the KTU LaTeX system rejects broken tables. Always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in your answer sheets.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Techniques

| IE Technique | Industry Application |
|--------------|----------------------|
| Method Study | Toyota Production System (elimination of *muda* / waste) |
| Time Study | Garment industry for piece-rate wage fixation |
| Work Sampling | Hospital nursing workload analysis |
| Motion Study | Gaming keyboards / surgical instrument design |
| Ergonomics | Cockpit design (fighter jets, Airbus A380) |
| Value Engineering | Apple iPhone cost reduction across generations |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Time Formula

This is the most frequently asked derivation in the KTU Board Examination. We will derive it step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Define the Observed Time
The IE engineer, with a stopwatch, measures the actual time taken by the operator for one cycle of the job.

$$T_o \quad \text{= Observed Time (in minutes)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Performance Rating
The IE engineer judges whether the worker is faster, slower, or at the standard pace. The Rating Factor $R$ normalizes the observed time.

$$R = \dfrac{\text{Actual Performance Speed of Worker}}{\text{Normal Performance Speed}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the worker is moving at the standard pace, $R = 1.00$. If the worker is moving at 120% pace, $R = 1.20$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Normal Time
Normal Time is the time a **trained, qualified worker** would take working at a **normal pace**.

$$T_n = T_o \times R$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion logic**: $T_o$ is what we *measured*; $T_n$ is what a *normal worker* would take. Multiplying by $R$ "neutralizes" the worker's deviation from normal pace.

### Step 4 — Add Allowances
A worker cannot operate 100% of the time — they need breaks, personal time, rest, and contingency allowance. Let:

$$A = \text{Total Allowance Fraction}$$

For example, if Rest Allowance = 12%, Personal Allowance = 5%, Contingency Allowance = 3%, then:

$$A = 0.12 + 0.05 + 0.03 = 0.20$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Standard Time
Standard Time includes Normal Time **plus** the time for all allowed breaks.

$$T_s = T_n + (\text{Normal Time} \times A)$$

Factor out $T_n$:

$$T_s = T_n \times (1 + A)$$

### Step 6 — Final Consolidated Equation

$$\boxed{\,T_s = T_o \times R \times (1 + A)\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Memory Trick:** "**TORA**" = $T_o \times R \times (1+A)$. This is the holy formula of work measurement.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Time Study Calculation

**Question (Model KTU Problem):**
A time study of a CNC operator yielded the following data for 5 cycles:

| Cycle | Observed Time (min) | Rating |
|-------|---------------------|--------|
| 1 | 1.20 | 110% |
| 2 | 1.10 | 105% |
| 3 | 1.15 | 100% |
| 4 | 1.25 | 95% |
| 5 | 1.30 | 90% |

Total allowance = **20%**. Calculate the **Standard Time** per cycle.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step A — Average Observed Time:**

$$\bar{T_o} = \dfrac{1.20 + 1.10 + 1.15 + 1.25 + 1.30}{5}$$

$$\bar{T_o} = \dfrac{6.00}{5} = 1.20 \text{ min}$$

**Step B — Average Rating Factor:**

$$\bar{R} = \dfrac{110 + 105 + 100 + 95 + 90}{5}$$

$$\bar{R} = \dfrac{500}{5} = 100\% = 1.00$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU boards often accept either the average of averages or the average of products $(T_o \times R)$. The conservative approach is to use the **average of products** shown below.

**Step C — Average of (Time × Rating) method (more accurate, board-preferred):**

Cycle 1: $1.20 \times 1.10 = 1.320$
Cycle 2: $1.10 \times 1.05 = 1.155$
Cycle 3: $1.15 \times 1.00 = 1.150$
Cycle 4: $1.25 \times 0.95 = 1.1875$
Cycle 5: $1.30 \times 0.90 = 1.170$

$$\sum (T_o \times R) = 1.320 + 1.155 + 1.150 + 1.1875 + 1.170 = 5.9825$$

$$T_n = \dfrac{5.9825}{5} = 1.1965 \text{ min}$$

**Step D — Add Allowance (A = 0.20):**

$$T_s = T_n \times (1 + A) = 1.1965 \times (1 + 0.20)$$

$$T_s = 1.1965 \times 1.20 = 1.4358 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_s \approx 1.44 \text{ min per cycle}\,}$$

**Step E — Standard Output per Hour:**

$$S_{out} = \dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{T_s} = \dfrac{60}{1.44} \approx 41.67 \text{ units/hour}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Work Sampling Sample Size

Work sampling is **statistical**, not deterministic. The number of random observations $N$ is derived from the binomial distribution approximation.

### Step 1 — Define Allowable Error
If $p$ is the true proportion of time spent on an activity, and $\hat{p}$ is the observed proportion, the **error** is:

$$E = \vert \hat{p} - p \vert$$

### Step 2 — Set Confidence Level
At 95% confidence level, the Z-value = **1.96** (this is a fixed standard normal distribution constant).
At 99% confidence level, Z = **2.576**.

### Step 3 — Standard Error of Proportion

$$S_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

### Step 4 — Margin of Error

$$E = Z \cdot S_p = Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for N
Square both sides:

$$E^2 = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{N}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\,N = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Worked Example
Estimate the proportion of time a machine is idle. Prior data suggests $p = 0.30$. We want 95% confidence with $E = 0.05$.

$$N = \dfrac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.30 \times 0.70}{(0.05)^2}$$

$$N = \dfrac{3.8416 \times 0.21}{0.0025}$$

$$N = \dfrac{0.8067}{0.0025} = 322.7$$

$$\boxed{\,N \approx 323 \text{ observations needed}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conservative Rule:** If no prior data exists, use $p = 0.50$ (which maximizes $p(1-p) = 0.25$). This gives the largest required sample size and is the safest KTU board answer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis (Board-Favorite)

> [!TIP]
> **The 3-Way Comparison** is a high-weightage KTU question (usually 7 marks).

| Parameter | Method Study | Time Study | Work Sampling |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Purpose** | Find better method | Set standard time | Determine activity proportions |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Duration** | Days to weeks | Hours to days | Days to weeks |
| **Observer Fatigue** | Medium | High | Low |
| **Accuracy** | Qualitative (qualitative ranking) | High (deterministic) | Statistical (probabilistic) |
| **Statistical Basis** | None | Time series analysis | Binomial distribution |
| **Result Type** | Improved method | Standard Time (in min) | % of time on each activity |
| **Best for** | Repetitive, long-cycle jobs | Short, repetitive jobs | Long, non-repetitive jobs (e.g., doctors, machines) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The IE Methodology Flowchart

The following **Mermaid flowchart** depicts the universal 4-stage IE methodology covered in Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Stage 1: DEFINE Problem]) --> nodeB[Stage 2: RECORD Facts]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Stage 3: EXAMINE Critically]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Stage 4: INSTALL and MAINTAIN]
    nodeD --> nodeE([Improved Work System])

    nodeB --> nodeF[Method Study Charts]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Time Study Sheets]
    nodeB --> nodeH[Work Sampling Logs]
    nodeB --> nodeI[Activity Sampling Forms]

    nodeC --> nodeJ[ECRS Principle]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Eliminate]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Combine]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Rearrange]
    nodeJ --> nodeN[Simplify]

    classDef stageClass fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef techniqueClass fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef ecrsClass fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff

    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE stageClass
    class nodeF,nodeG,nodeH,nodeI techniqueClass
    class nodeJ,nodeK,nodeL,nodeM,nodeN ecrsClass
```

---

## 4.2 Process Chart Symbols (ANSI / ASME Standard)

The five universally accepted symbols for Method Study process charts are:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeOp[Operation]:::op
    nodeTr[Transport]:::tr
    nodeIn[Inspection]:::in
    nodeDl[Delay]:::dl
    nodeSt[Storage]:::st

    nodeOp --> nodeTr --> nodeIn --> nodeDl --> nodeSt

    classDef op fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tr fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef in fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dl fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef st fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
```

| Symbol | Name | Meaning | When Used |
|--------|------|---------|-----------|
| **O** (Big Circle) | Operation | A task that produces/changes the item | Drilling, cutting, assembling |
| **→** (Arrow) | Transport | Movement of material/worker between locations | Material moved from store to machine |
| **□** (Square) | Inspection | Checking quality or quantity of item | Quality check, dimension check |
| **D** (Big D-shape) | Delay | Waiting time; item is held up | Material waiting for next operation |
| **▽** (Triangle) | Storage | Item kept in a protected location | Finished goods in warehouse |

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Time Study Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1: Define Job and Worker] --> stepB[Step 2: Break Job into Elements]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Conduct Stopwatch Study]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Apply Performance Rating]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Compute Normal Time]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Add Allowances]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Compute Standard Time]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8: Standardize Output / Wages]

    stepC --> typ1[Continuous Timing]
    stepC --> typ2[Snap-back Timing]
    stepC --> typ3[Repetitive Timing]

    stepD --> rat1[Speed Rating 100 percent]
    stepD --> rat2[Westinghouse Rating System]
    stepD --> rat3[Synthetic Rating]

    classDef mainClass fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef variantClass fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff

    class stepA,stepB,stepC,stepD,stepE,stepF,stepG,stepH mainClass
    class typ1,typ2,typ3,rat1,rat2,rat3 variantClass
```

---

## 4.4 Ergonomics — Workstation Design Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hWorker[Human Operator] --> hPhysical[Physical Factors]
    hWorker --> hCognitive[Cognitive Factors]
    hWorker --> hOrganizational[Organizational Factors]

    hPhysical --> p1[Anthropometry - Body Dimensions]
    hPhysical --> p2[Workstation Height and Reach]
    hPhysical --> p3[Lighting and Noise]
    hPhysical --> p4[Posture and Repetitiveness]

    hCognitive --> c1[Information Processing]
    hCognitive --> c2[Decision Making Load]
    hCognitive --> c3[Memory and Attention]

    hOrganizational --> o1[Shift Patterns]
    hOrganizational --> o2[Work-Rest Cycles]
    hOrganizational --> o3[Training and Skill Level]

    p1 --> outcome[OPTIMAL WORK SYSTEM]
    p2 --> outcome
    p3 --> outcome
    p4 --> outcome
    c1 --> outcome
    c2 --> outcome
    c3 --> outcome
    o1 --> outcome
    o2 --> outcome
    o3 --> outcome

    classDef humanClass fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```
