**Key Inference**: This block diagram captures the **contingency logic** — organization context drives the choice of structure, which in turn drives the appropriate industrial example.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Line organization and Functional organization. List any two merits of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Line Organization | Functional Organization |
|---|---|---|
| **Authority** | Flows in a single direct vertical chain from top to bottom. | Authority is divided among functional specialists (Production, Finance, Marketing, etc.). |
| **Unity of Command** | Strictly maintained — one boss per subordinate. | Violated — a worker may receive orders from multiple functional heads. |
| **Origin** | Fayol's classical theory. | F.W. Taylor's Scientific Management. |
| **Merit 1** | Clear accountability and fast decision-making. | High specialization and expert handling. |
| **Merit 2** | Simple to establish, low cost, strong discipline. | Economical use of specialized resources. |

> **Marking Note**: A 2-column table with at least 2 distinct differences + 1 merit of each = **3 marks** awarded.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q2. What is Span of Control? State the factors that determine the span of control in an organization.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark)**: **Span of Control** is the number of subordinates that a manager can effectively supervise, direct, and control. A narrow span (few subordinates) leads to a **tall structure**; a wide span (many subordinates) leads to a **flat structure**.

**Factors (2 Marks)**:
1. **Capacity and competency of the superior** — a trained manager can supervise more subordinates.
2. **Capacity of the subordinates** — trained, experienced workers need less supervision.
3. **Nature of work** — simple, repetitive tasks allow wider span; complex tasks require narrow span.
4. **Degree of decentralization** — wider span demands greater delegation.
5. **Availability of staff assistance** — staff officers can reduce the supervisor's load.
6. **Communication and control technology** — telephones, ERP, and digital dashboards enable wider spans.
7. **Geographic dispersion** — widely scattered units force narrow spans.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination — Dec 2024, Module 1, Q1]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the Line and Staff organization structure in detail. Discuss its merits and demerits with a suitable diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark)**:
The Line and Staff organization is a hybrid structure that combines the **direct vertical chain of command** of line organization with the **specialized advisory support** of functional staff. It was developed to overcome the demerits of pure line and pure functional structures, retaining the advantages of both.

**Line Authority vs. Staff Authority (2 Marks)**:

- **Line Managers** possess both **command authority** (to give orders) and **responsibility** for execution. Examples: Production Manager, Sales Manager, Finance Manager.
- **Staff Managers** possess only **advisory authority**. They research, analyze, and recommend, but **cannot command** line personnel. Examples: HR Officer, Legal Advisor, R&D Head, Management Consultant.

The line authority flows vertically from the **Managing Director → Departmental Managers → Supervisors → Workers**. The staff officers are attached to the MD or to specific line managers and provide expert counsel through a **dashed advisory line** (clearly depicted in Diagram 3 of Section 4).

**Merits (2 Marks)**:

1. **Unity of Command preserved** — workers receive orders from only one line boss, eliminating the chaos of multiple commanders.
2. **Expert advice available** — line managers can leverage specialists for better decision-making in HR, legal, and technical matters.
3. **Reduction of top executive burden** — staff officers handle research, leaving the MD free for strategic work.
4. **Discipline and specialization together** — combines the discipline of line structure with the expertise of functional specialists.
5. **Flexibility** — staff officers can be added or removed as per organizational needs without disturbing the line hierarchy.

**Demerits (2 Marks)**:

1. **Conflict between line and staff** — line managers may resent staff interference and ignore their advice; staff may feel powerless and frustrated.
2. **High cost of operation** — dual hierarchy (line + staff) means double salary overheads.
3. **Lack of authority for staff** — staff officers have no power to enforce their recommendations, limiting their effectiveness.
4. **Confusion in roles** — unclear boundaries between line and staff functions can cause overlap and turf wars.
5. **Slow decision-making** — staff advice may be solicited from multiple sources, delaying action.

**Diagram (already in Section 4.3) — 1 mark** for a correctly drawn hierarchical chart showing line (solid) and staff (dashed) lines.

---

**(b) Compare Matrix organization with Line and Staff organization. Under what circumstances is a Matrix structure preferred? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark)**:
While the **Line and Staff structure** retains a single chain of command with auxiliary advisory staff, the **Matrix structure** is a grid-like dual-authority system where employees report simultaneously to **two bosses** — a functional manager and a project manager. The comparison is essential for selecting the right structure under varying industrial conditions.

**Comparative Analysis (3 Marks)**:

| Basis | Line and Staff Organization | Matrix Organization |
|---|---|---|
| **Authority Pattern** | Single line authority + staff advisory authority | Dual authority (functional + project) |
| **Unity of Command** | Maintained (one boss + one or more advisors) | Violated (two bosses) |
| **Specialization** | Functional (HR, Legal, R&D as staff) | Functional + Project specialization |
| **Flexibility** | Moderate — staff can be added/removed | High — employees can be redeployed across projects |
| **Cost** | High | Very High (dual managers + conflict-resolution overhead) |
| **Project Focus** | Low — projects handled within line departments | Very High — dedicated project managers drive delivery |
| **Employee Reporting** | Single boss (functional/line) | Dual reporting (functional + project) |
| **Conflict Type** | Line-vs-staff resentment | Functional-vs-project power struggle |

**Circumstances Favoring Matrix Structure (3 Marks)**:

1. **Multiple complex projects running simultaneously** — each requiring cross-functional expertise (e.g., aerospace, IT, R&D).
2. **Dynamic and uncertain environment** — frequent changes in customer demand, technology, or regulations require rapid re-allocation of resources.
3. **High need for innovation and creativity** — bringing together specialists from different functions generates novel solutions.
4. **Scarce specialist resources** — when experts (e.g., AI engineers, structural designers) must be shared across many projects.
5. **Strong project management culture** — the organization is mature enough to handle role conflict and dual reporting.
6. **Customer-centric and time-bound deliverables** — e.g., Boeing, ISRO, TCS, L&T, McKinsey.
7. **Decentralized decision-making** — matrix enables decision-making at the project level rather than the top.

> **Marking Note**: A comparative table with at least 5 distinct parameters = 3 marks. Clear enumeration of at least 5 circumstances = 3 marks. Valid introduction and contextual examples = 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Explain the Functional organization structure as proposed by F.W. Taylor. What are its salient features? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Historical Background (1 Mark)**:
The Functional organization was proposed by **Frederick Winslow Taylor** (the Father of Scientific Management) in his 1911 monograph *The Principles of Scientific Management*. Taylor argued that functional specialization at the supervisory level would dramatically improve industrial productivity, replacing the single foreman concept with **8 specialists** — 4 in the **planning office** and 4 in the **shop floor**.

**Concept of Functional Foremanship (2 Marks)**:

- **4 Planning Office Foremen**:
  1. **Route Clerk** — lays down the sequence of work and route for each job.
  2. **Instruction Card Man** — prepares detailed written instructions for workers.
  3. **Time and Cost Clerk** — records time taken and cost incurred for each operation.
  4. **Discipline and Grievance Man** — handles worker discipline, complaints, and rewards.

- **4 Shop Floor Foremen**:
  1. **Speed Boss** — ensures machines run at optimum speed.
  2. **Repair Boss** — maintains machinery in working condition.
  3. **Inspector** — checks the quality of output.
  4. **Gang Boss** — supervises a group of workers during production.

Each foreman commands the workers only in his specialized area, leading to **multiple-boss supervision**.

**Salient Features (2 Marks)**:
1. **Functional specialization** at every supervisory level.
2. **Division of work** by expert functions, not by products.
3. **Violation of Unity of Command** — workers receive orders from multiple specialists.
4. **Scientific selection and training** of workers.
5. **Coordination between mental and manual work** (planning office vs. shop floor).
6. **Application of scientific methods** to every task.

**Merits and Demerits (1 Mark each)**:
- *Merit*: Expert supervision, mass-production efficiency, standardized methods.
- *Demerit*: Unity of command violated, coordination problems, worker confusion, increased cost of multiple specialists.

**Conclusion (1 Mark)**:
While Taylor's Functional Foremanship was revolutionary for its time, modern organizations have modified it into a more practical **Functional Departmentation** at the executive level (Production Manager, Finance Manager, etc.) rather than at the supervisory level.

---

**(b) A medium-sized engineering firm with 500 employees and 3 active projects is facing coordination problems. Recommend a suitable organization structure and justify your choice. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Problem Analysis (2 Marks)**:
The firm's challenges are:
- 500 employees (medium-sized).
- **3 active projects** running simultaneously.
- Coordination problems (likely due to inter-departmental silos, unclear accountability, and project-level delays).

**Recommended Structure: MATRIX ORGANIZATION (1 Mark)**.

**Justification (4 Marks)**:

1. **Dual coordination solves the stated problem**: A matrix structure establishes a **project manager for each of the 3 projects** alongside the existing **functional managers** (Design, Production, Quality, Finance). The project manager coordinates cross-functional activities, eliminating the silo problem.

2. **Specialist sharing maximizes efficiency**: The 500-employee firm likely cannot afford 3 separate design or production teams. In a matrix, the same design engineers and production staff are **shared** across all 3 projects, optimizing resource utilization.

3. **Faster response and accountability**: With a dedicated project manager, each project has a **single point of accountability** for delivery, deadlines, and customer communication. The functional manager retains responsibility for technical standards and employee welfare.

4. **Maintains functional expertise**: Unlike a pure project organization (where employees leave their functions), the matrix **preserves functional depth** — the design team remains a design team, just temporarily augmented with project reporting.

5. **Conflict-resolution mechanisms**: The firm should establish a **Matrix Steering Committee** comprising the MD, all functional heads, and all project managers. This committee meets fortnightly to resolve priority conflicts (e.g., when an engineer is over-allocated to multiple projects).

**Implementation Roadmap (Optional — Bonus Marks)**:
- **Phase 1 (Month 1–2)**: Identify the 3 project managers and finalize the matrix charter.
- **Phase 2 (Month 3–4)**: Train employees in dual-reporting and conflict-resolution.
- **Phase 3 (Month 5+)**: Roll out, monitor role conflict, and refine the matrix balance.

> **Conclusion**: The matrix structure is the most suitable choice for this scenario because it directly addresses the **coordination problem** while preserving **specialist expertise and efficient resource use**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the originators**: Students often credit **Fayol** for the Functional structure. Correction: **Fayol** proposed **Line organization**; **F.W. Taylor** proposed **Functional organization**.
>
> 2. **Drawing wrong diagrams**: The matrix diagram must clearly show **both vertical (functional) and horizontal (project) authority lines**. Drawing only one line = -2 marks.
>
> 3. **Omitting the "Unity of Command" point**: This is the single most important analytical point in any organization structure question. Always state whether the structure **maintains** or **violates** unity of command.
>
> 4. **Writing "Line and Staff" as "Line and Functional"**: These are **not** the same. Staff is **advisory**; functional is **commanding**. Confusing them = up to -3 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to give real-world examples**: KTU examiners reward **industrial examples** (TATA, ISRO, Infosys, McKinsey) with bonus marks in the merit/suitability section.
>
> 6. **Not writing a conclusion**: A 14-mark answer without a concluding paragraph is considered **incomplete**. Always end with a summary sentence.
>
> 7. **Ignoring the "Suitability" sub-question**: When asked "which structure is suitable for X type of organization?", students often explain the structure but **fail to justify suitability** to the specific context. Always link back to the firm's **size, technology, and environment**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Line Organization** = oldest, simplest, **unity of command maintained**, suits small firms and army.
- ✅ **Functional Organization** = **Taylor's brainchild**, unity of command **violated**, suits mass production.
- ✅ **Line and Staff Organization** = hybrid, line commands + staff advises, unity of command **preserved**, suits medium-large firms.
- ✅ **Matrix Organization** = dual reporting (functional + project), unity of command **violated**, suits R&D, aerospace, IT.
- ✅ **Span of Control** = typically **6 to 8** at top levels; **20–30** at supervisory levels.
- ✅ **Fayol's principle** that is consistently tested = **Unity of Command** (subordinates should receive orders from only one boss).
- ✅ **Taylor's 8 Functional Foremen** = 4 in planning office + 4 in shop floor.
- ✅ **Authority Types**: **Line authority** = command power; **Staff authority** = advisory power; **Functional authority** = limited command within a function.
- ✅ **Suitability Triad**: Stability + Simplicity → **Line** | Specialization + Scale → **Functional** | Expert advice + Discipline → **Line and Staff** | Multiple projects + Innovation → **Matrix**.
- ✅ **Real-world anchors**: TATA (Line and Staff), ISRO/Boeing (Matrix), Army (Line), Old factories (Functional).
- ✅ **Common exam phrases to expect**: *"Compare Line and Line and Staff organization"*, *"Explain Taylor's Functional Foremanship"*, *"Discuss the demerits of Matrix structure"*, *"Which structure is suitable for an IT firm with multiple projects?"*.
- ✅ **Diagrams are mandatory** for any 7-mark sub-part — always draw a clean hierarchy with solid lines for command and dashed lines for advisory relationships.
- ✅ **Always end with a conclusion** linking the structure to a real industrial example for examiner satisfaction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Companies and Their Formation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Companies and Their Formation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition – Types of Companies

A **Company**, as defined under Section 2(20) of the Companies Act, 2013, is *an association of persons registered under the Companies Act or any earlier Companies Act as a company*. In the broader business sense, a company is a voluntary association of individuals formed to carry on a business for profit, having a distinct legal identity separate from its members.

In **Industrial Engineering & Management (IE&M)**, the study of company types is foundational because the legal structure of a business dictates:
- Its **liability exposure** (personal vs. limited)
- Its **capital-raising capacity**
- Its **managerial flexibility**
- Its **taxation pattern** and **regulatory compliance burden**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly differentiate between the **ownership pattern**, **liability status**, and **registration requirement** of each business form, as these are frequently asked as 3-mark short-answer questions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview – "The Restaurant Analogy"

Imagine you want to open a small food stall:

- **Sole Proprietorship** → You alone run the stall, you eat the profit, you also bear all losses (even from your personal savings). The stall has *no separate life* from you.
- **Partnership Firm** → You and 2-3 friends pool money, share profits, and *jointly* stand behind any debt. If one friend runs away, the others must pay his share.
- **Joint Stock Company (Private)** → You and a few close friends start a private restaurant chain — no outsiders can buy in, but your personal houses are safe from restaurant debt.
- **Joint Stock Company (Public)** → You launch an IPO — anyone in the public can buy a "share coupon" and become a part-owner; the company has a life of its own, totally independent of you.
- **One Person Company (OPC)** → A new innovation — a "single-person corporate shield." You are the only owner, but the company exists as a separate legal person, protecting your personal assets.
- **Limited Liability Partnership (LLP)** → A hybrid — partners share management, but no one partner is personally liable for another's negligence.
- **Co-operative Society** → A group of milk farmers pooling resources; democratic voting (one member, one vote), and the goal is mutual welfare, not corporate profit.

### 1.3 Broad Classification of Business Organizations

| S.No | Type | Ownership | Liability | Governing Law |
|------|------|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | Sole Proprietorship | Single Individual | **Unlimited** | No Specific Act |
| 2 | Partnership Firm | 2 – 50 Partners | **Unlimited (Joint & Several)** | Indian Partnership Act, 1932 |
| 3 | Joint Stock Company (Private) | 2 – 200 Members | **Limited** | Companies Act, 2013 |
| 4 | Joint Stock Company (Public) | Minimum 7 Members | **Limited** | Companies Act, 2013 |
| 5 | One Person Company (OPC) | 1 Member + 1 Nominee | **Limited** | Companies Act, 2013 |
| 6 | Limited Liability Partnership | 2 – Unlimited Partners | **Limited** | LLP Act, 2008 |
| 7 | Co-operative Society | Minimum 10 Members | **Limited** | Co-operative Societies Act, 1912 / State Acts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:** "**S**he **P**ours **C**offee **O**n **L**ean **C**rusts" → Sole, Partnership, Company (Private/Public), OPC, LLP, Co-operative.

### 1.4 Definition – Formation of a Company

The **Formation of a Company** refers to the legally prescribed sequence of stages through which an idea is converted into a registered corporate entity. For a public company, four sequential stages are mandatory under the Companies Act, 2013:

1. **Promotion Stage**
2. **Incorporation / Registration Stage**
3. **Capital Subscription Stage**
4. **Commencement of Business Stage**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Private Company** and a **One Person Company (OPC)** are exempt from Stages 3 and 4 — they can commence business immediately upon receiving the **Certificate of Incorporation**. This is a high-frequency KTU exam distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of Each Company Type

#### A. Sole Proprietorship
- **Definition:** A business owned, managed, and controlled by a single individual who receives all profits and bears all risks.
- **Legal Status:** Not a separate legal entity — the owner and business are *one and the same* in the eyes of law.
- **Liability:** **Unlimited** — creditors can recover dues even from the proprietor's personal property.
- **Capital:** Limited to the personal savings and borrowings of the proprietor.
- **KTU Exam Point:** "Easiest to form, hardest to scale" — remember this trade-off.

#### B. Partnership Firm
- **Definition:** An association of two or more persons (maximum 50) who agree to share profits of a business carried on by all or any of them acting for all (Section 4, Indian Partnership Act, 1932).
- **Liability:** **Joint and Several** — each partner is individually and collectively liable for the firm's debts.
- **Mutual Agency:** Every partner is both an agent and a principal of the other partners.
- **KTU Exam Point:** The death, insolvency, or lunacy of a partner *dissolves* the firm (subject to contract).

#### C. Joint Stock Company – Private
- **Members:** Minimum 2, Maximum 200.
- **Restriction on Transfer:** Shares cannot be transferred to the public.
- **Public Invitation:** Prohibited — cannot invite the public to subscribe to shares or debentures.
- **Name:** Must end with **"Private Limited"**.

#### D. Joint Stock Company – Public
- **Members:** Minimum 7, No maximum limit.
- **Transfer of Shares:** Freely transferable.
- **Public Invitation:** Allowed via a **Prospectus**.
- **Name:** Must end with **"Limited"**.

#### E. One Person Company (OPC) – Introduced via Companies Act, 2013
- **Members:** Only 1 (with a mandatory **nominee** who inherits the company in case of the sole member's death/incapacity).
- **Concept:** A hybrid between Sole Proprietorship and a Private Company — single ownership + limited liability.
- **Restriction:** OPC cannot be incorporated as a Section 8 company, cannot convert voluntarily into a public company directly, and has restrictions on investment in another company's shares.

#### F. Limited Liability Partnership (LLP)
- **Concept:** Combines the **flexibility of a partnership** with the **limited liability of a company**.
- **Liability:** Each partner's liability is limited to the extent of his agreed contribution. No partner is liable for the *unauthorized* acts of another partner.
- **Separate Legal Entity:** Yes, the LLP is a legal person distinct from its partners.

#### G. Co-operative Society
- **Principle:** *"Service, not Profit."*
- **Voting:** One member, one vote (irrespective of shareholding).
- **KTU Exam Point:** The Co-operative form is best suited for **agricultural credit, dairy, housing, and consumer welfare** activities.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet – Comparative Matrix

| Parameter | Sole Proprietorship | Partnership | Private Company | Public Company | OPC | LLP |
|-----------|--------------------|-------------|-----------------|----------------|-----|-----|
| **Min Members** | 1 | 2 | 2 | 7 | 1 | 2 |
| **Max Members** | 1 | 50 | 200 | Unlimited | 1 | Unlimited |
| **Liability** | Unlimited | Unlimited | Limited | Limited | Limited | Limited |
| **Legal Entity** | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Perpetual Succession** | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Public Invitation** | NA | NA | Prohibited | Allowed | Prohibited | Prohibited |
| **Formation Act** | Common Law | Partnership Act 1932 | Companies Act 2013 | Companies Act 2013 | Companies Act 2013 | LLP Act 2008 |
| **Auditor Mandatory** | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (if turnover/ capital exceeds limits) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Keyword Mastery for KTU Valuation:**
> - **Perpetual Succession** = "Members may come, members may go, but the company goes on forever."
> - **Common Seal** = The legal signature of a company (now optional under 2013 Act, but still asked in exams).
> - **Limited Liability** = A shareholder can lose only what he invested, *not* his personal assets.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Industrial Utility

In the **IE&M context**, the choice of company type directly impacts:
- **Project Scale:** A small machining workshop may start as a *Sole Proprietorship*; an automobile assembly plant requires a *Public Limited Company* for mass capital.
- **Risk Allocation in Joint Ventures:** Multi-national engineering collaborations prefer **LLP** structure to share technology and limit exposure.
- **Tender Eligibility:** Most government and PSU industrial tenders in Kerala mandate a **registered Private/Public Limited Company** as the bidding entity, often with a minimum 3-year operational track record.
- **Startup Ecosystem in Kerala:** A majority of KSUM (Kerala Startup Mission)-registered ventures initially opt for the **OPC** structure to enjoy limited liability while operating solo.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough – Formation of a Joint Stock Company

The formation of a **Public Limited Company** is a four-stage legal journey. Each stage has mandatory documents, statutory fees, and consequences for non-compliance. Below is the exhaustive KTU-board-style breakdown.

---

### Stage 1: Promotion Stage

**Who is a Promoter?**
A promoter is any person who undertakes the preliminary work of bringing a company into existence. The promoter is *not* a trustee of the company, but is liable for **mis-statements in the prospectus** and must disclose any secret profits.

**Step-by-step Logic of the Promotion Stage:**

1. **Idea Generation** → The promoter conceives a business opportunity (e.g., setting up a CNC machining unit in Kochi).
2. **Feasibility Analysis** → Technical feasibility, financial viability, and market study are undertaken.
3. **Assembling of Resources** → Identifying Directors, Bankers, Auditors, Legal Advisors, and Underwriters.
4. **Preparation of Documents** → Drafting the **Memorandum of Association (MOA)**, **Articles of Association (AOA)**, and **Prospectus** (for public issue).
5. **Fixing Name of the Company** → Application to the Registrar of Companies (ROC) via **RUN (Reserve Unique Name)** web service, ensuring the name does not resemble an existing company or contain restricted words.

**Key Documents in Promotion Stage:**

| Document | Purpose | KTU Significance |
|----------|---------|------------------|
| **Memorandum of Association (MOA)** | Defines the constitution and outer limits of the company's powers. Contains 6 clauses: Name, Registered Office, Objects, Liability, Capital, Association. | "The **Charter** of the Company" |
| **Articles of Association (AOA)** | Internal rules for day-to-day management of the company. | "The **Rules and Regulations** of the Company" |
| **Prospectus** | An invitation to the public to subscribe to shares/debentures. Must disclose all material facts. | Required only for **Public** companies inviting capital from the public |
| **Statement in Lieu of Prospectus** | Filed by private companies and public companies not inviting public subscription. | Alternative to prospectus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultra Vires Doctrine:** Any act done by the company *beyond* the objects clause of the MOA is **ultra vires** (beyond the powers) and is **void ab initio** — even if every shareholder consents. This is a very high-weightage KTU question.

**Duties of a Promoter (KTU 3-Mark Favorite):**
1. **Duty not to make secret profits** — must disclose any commission/benefit received.
2. **Duty to disclose all material facts** in the prospectus.
3. **Duty of care and diligence** towards the company.
4. **Duty not to enter into contracts on behalf of a non-existent company** — contracts made before incorporation are *void*, unless adopted by the company under a specific ratification.

---

### Stage 2: Incorporation / Registration Stage

**Statutory Steps:**

1. **Filing of Documents with ROC:**
   - Memorandum of Association (MOA) – signed by at least 7 subscribers for a public company, 2 for a private company, and 1 for an OPC.
   - Articles of Association (AOA).
   - **INC-32 (SPICe+ Form)** – Simplified Proforma for Incorporating Company Electronically.
   - **DIR-12** – Particulars of Directors.
   - Proof of Registered Office.
   - Declaration of Compliance (**INC-8** or **INC-14** for public).
   - **Identity Proof & Address Proof** of all subscribers and Directors (DSC + DIN).
   - Payment of prescribed **ROC fees** and **Stamp Duty**.

2. **Scrutiny by ROC** → The Registrar examines whether all legal requirements are complied with. If the name is identical to an existing company or if the MOA/AOA are defective, the application is **rejected**.

3. **Issue of Certificate of Incorporation** →
   - For a **Private Company / OPC** → *The company comes into existence the moment the Certificate of Incorporation is issued.* It can immediately commence business.
   - For a **Public Company** → It *comes into existence* upon incorporation, but *cannot commence business* or exercise borrowing powers until Stage 4 is complete.

> [!NOTE]
> **Legal Effect of Certificate of Incorporation:** It is **conclusive evidence** that all the requirements of the Companies Act have been complied with (Section 7(2)). The company becomes a **separate legal person** with **perpetual succession** and a **common seal**.

---

### Stage 3: Capital Subscription Stage (Public Company Only)

This stage is **mandatory for public companies** that propose to invite capital from the public.

**Step-by-Step Capital Subscription Process:**

1. **Issue of Prospectus** → A copy of the prospectus must be filed with the ROC **on or before the date of its publication** (Section 26).
2. **Minimum Subscription** → The company must raise a *minimum subscription* as specified in the prospectus within **120 days** of the issue. If not raised, the company must refund the entire application money within **130 days** (Section 39).
3. **Application to Stock Exchange** → If shares are to be listed, the prospectus is filed with the stock exchange(s).
4. **Appointment of Underwriters** → Persons who agree to take up unsubscribed shares.
5. **Allotment of Shares** → The Board of Directors allots shares after the minimum subscription is received.

**Key Conditions:**
- **Minimum application money** = at least **5% of the nominal value of each share**.
- **Subscription list** must remain open for a **minimum of 3 days** and a **maximum of 10 days**.
- The **Prospectus must contain** all the disclosures specified under Section 26 and Schedule I of the Companies Act, 2013.

---

### Stage 4: Commencement of Business Stage

**Mandatory only for Public Companies** (and for Private Companies/OPCs that have issued a prospectus above ₹50 lakhs in some contexts).

**Step-by-Step Legal Requirements:**

1. **File a Declaration** with the ROC (Form **INC-20A**) within **180 days** of incorporation confirming that:
   - Minimum subscription has been received.
   - Every Director has paid the application and allotment money on shares taken by him.
   - No money is liable to be refunded to applicants.
2. **Pay Stamp Duty** on the share capital.
3. **Verification by ROC** of the above declaration.
4. **Issue of Certificate of Commencement of Business** by the ROC.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Observation):** Many students wrongly assume that a Private Limited Company must also obtain a Certificate of Commencement. **This is incorrect.** A Private Company and an OPC are *exempted* from this requirement under Section 11(2) — they can begin business immediately upon receiving the Certificate of Incorporation.

---

### 3.1 Analytical Summary – The "Formation Pyramid"

The following summary table consolidates the stage-wise requirements and their applicability:

| Stage | Public Company | Private Company | OPC |
|-------|----------------|-----------------|-----|
| **1. Promotion** | Required | Required | Required |
| **2. Incorporation** | Required (Certificate of Incorporation) | Required (Certificate of Incorporation) | Required (Certificate of Incorporation) |
| **3. Capital Subscription** | Required (Prospectus + Minimum Subscription) | Not Required (if not inviting public) | Not Required |
| **4. Commencement** | Required (Certificate of Commencement) | **Exempted** | **Exempted** |

---

### 3.2 Doctrine Highlights (KTU Theory 14-Mark Favorites)

**1. Doctrine of Separate Legal Entity (Salomon v. Salomon & Co., 1897)**
- A company is a person distinct from its members.
- The company can own property, sue, and be sued in its own name.
- The shareholders are not the agents of the company.

**2. Doctrine of Limited Liability**
- A shareholder's liability is limited to the *unpaid amount on shares* or the *amount guaranteed*.
- Personal assets of shareholders are completely protected.

**3. Doctrine of Perpetual Succession**
- The company continues to exist irrespective of changes in membership.
- Death, insolvency, or transfer of shares by a member does not affect the company's existence.

**4. Doctrine of Indoor Management (Turquand's Rule)**
- Outsiders dealing with a company are entitled to assume that all internal procedures have been complied with.
- *Exception:* Knowledge of irregularity by the outsider voids the protection.

**5. Doctrine of Constructive Notice**
- Every person dealing with the company is deemed to have notice of the contents of MOA, AOA, and the Companies Act.
- *Linkage:* Indoor Management is the **counterpart** of Constructive Notice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flow Diagram – Stages of Formation of a Public Company

```mermaid
    G --> E
    F -- Yes --> H[File Incorporation Documents - SPICe+]
    H --> I[ROC Scrutiny]
    I --> J{All Documents in Order?}
    J -- No --> K[Resubmit Corrected Documents]
    K --> I
    J -- Yes --> L[Certificate of Incorporation Issued]
    L --> M[Company comes into Legal Existence]
    M --> N[Issue of Prospectus to Public]
    N --> O[Receive Minimum Subscription within 120 days]
    O --> P{Minimum Subscription Raised?}

    P -- Yes --> R[Allotment of Shares to Public]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (Model Answers Included)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Distinguish between a Private Company and a Public Company.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Private Company | Public Company |
|-------|-----------------|----------------|
| Minimum Members | 2 | 7 |
| Maximum Members | 200 | No Limit |
| Public Invitation | Prohibited | Allowed |
| Transfer of Shares | Restricted | Freely Transferable |
| Name | Must end with "Private Limited" | Must end with "Limited" |
| Certificate of Commencement | Not Required | Required |
| Number of Directors | Minimum 2 | Minimum 3 |

*For awarding full 3 marks, students must list **at least 4 distinctions** with crisp phrasing.*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> **What is a One Person Company (OPC)? Mention any two of its features.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A One Person Company (OPC) is a private company having only **one member** (Section 2(62), Companies Act, 2013). It was introduced to encourage individual entrepreneurs to enjoy the benefits of limited liability while operating solo.

**Features:**
1. **Single Ownership:** Only one shareholder-cum-director is permitted; a **nominee** must be appointed to take over in case of the sole member's death or incapacity.
2. **Limited Liability:** The liability of the single member is limited to the unpaid amount on his shares; personal assets are protected.
3. **No Public Invitation:** OPC cannot invite the public to subscribe to its shares or debentures.
4. **No Perpetual Conversion:** OPC cannot be converted into a Section 8 company or a company for charitable purposes.

*(Any two features to be explained for 3 marks.)*

---

### PART B: 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice – KTU Pattern)

---

#### **Question A: 14 Marks**

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> **(a)** Explain the different types of companies that can be registered under the Companies Act, 2013. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Describe in detail the stages involved in the formation of a public limited company. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Types of Companies (7 Marks):**

The Companies Act, 2013, classifies companies based on three criteria — *ownership pattern*, *liability*, and *access to public capital*.

1. **On the Basis of Membership / Capital Access:**
   - **Private Company:** A company having a minimum paid-up share capital (or such higher capital as may be prescribed) and a minimum of 2 members with a maximum of 200. Restricts the right to transfer shares and prohibits public invitation.
   - **Public Company:** A company that is *not* a private company. Minimum 7 members, no upper limit. Can freely invite public subscription through a prospectus.

2. **On the Basis of Liability:**
   - **Company Limited by Shares:** Liability of members is limited to the unpaid amount on shares.
   - **Company Limited by Guarantee:** Members guarantee a fixed amount in case of winding up (e.g., Section 8 companies).
   - **Unlimited Company:** Liability extends to the entire personal assets of members.

3. **Special Categories (Practical Relevance):**
   - **One Person Company (OPC):** Single-member private company.
   - **Holding and Subsidiary Company:** Where one company controls another.
   - **Associate Company:** Significant influence without control.
   - **Section 8 Company:** Non-profit promotion of commerce, art, science, religion, charity.
   - **Producer Company:** Aggregation of primary producers (farmers, artisans).
   - **Foreign Company:** Incorporated outside India but having a place of business in India.

4. **On the Basis of Domicile:**
   - **Indian Company:** Incorporated in India.
   - **Foreign Company:** Incorporated outside India.

*(For full marks, at least three classification bases with 1-2 examples each are expected.)*

**(b) Stages of Formation of a Public Limited Company (7 Marks):**

The formation of a public limited company is a four-stage legal process under the Companies Act, 2013.

**Stage 1 – Promotion:**
The promoter conceives the business idea, conducts a feasibility study, identifies directors, bankers, auditors, and prepares the foundational documents — the **Memorandum of Association (MOA)** and **Articles of Association (AOA)**. The promoter's duties include disclosure of secret profits and due diligence in prospectus drafting.

**Stage 2 – Incorporation:**
The signed MOA, AOA, and other documents (SPICe+ form, DIR-12, INC-8, identity proofs) are filed with the Registrar of Companies (ROC) along with the prescribed fees and stamp duty. Upon satisfaction, the ROC issues the **Certificate of Incorporation**, bringing the company into legal existence as a separate legal entity.

**Stage 3 – Capital Subscription:**
A **Prospectus** is issued to invite public subscription. The company must receive the **minimum subscription** within 120 days; otherwise, all application money must be refunded within 130 days. Allotment of shares is then made to the public.

**Stage 4 – Commencement of Business:**
A declaration (Form **INC-20A**) is filed with the ROC confirming the receipt of minimum subscription and payment of stamp duty. The ROC then issues the **Certificate of Commencement of Business**, after which the public company can fully commence its business operations and exercise borrowing powers.

> *(Students must number the stages clearly and identify the key document at each stage. Marks are awarded for the legal effect of each stage.)*

---

#### **Question B: 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – May 2022]**
> **(a)** Compare and contrast Sole Proprietorship, Partnership Firm, and Joint Stock Company as forms of business organizations. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the role, duties, and legal status of a Promoter in the formation of a company. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Comparison of Business Forms (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Sole Proprietorship | Partnership Firm | Joint Stock Company |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Business owned by a single individual. | Association of 2–50 persons sharing profits. | Registered association of members with limited liability. |
| **Legal Status** | Not a separate legal entity. | Not a separate legal entity. | Separate legal entity (Salomon v. Salomon). |
| **Liability** | Unlimited | Joint and Several (Unlimited) | Limited to unpaid share amount. |
| **Formation** | No specific Act; just a trade license. | Indian Partnership Act, 1932. | Companies Act, 2013. |
| **Capital** | Limited to proprietor's means. | Pooled capital of partners. | Unlimited — can tap public. |
| **Continuity** | Death ends business. | Dissolves on partner's death (subject to contract). | Perpetual Succession. |
| **Management** | Single-handed. | All partners (mutual agency). | Board of Directors. |
| **Taxation** | Treated as individual. | Firm is separately taxed. | Corporate tax rate. |

*Conclusion:* The Joint Stock Company offers the most robust framework for large-scale industrial enterprises due to its limited liability, perpetual succession, and capital-raising capacity.

**(b) Role, Duties, and Legal Status of a Promoter (7 Marks):**

A **Promoter** is a person who undertakes the preliminary steps for the incorporation and organization of a company.

**Roles of a Promoter:**
1. Identifies a business opportunity and conducts a feasibility study.
2. Assembles financial, technical, and human resources.
3. Engages professionals — lawyers, chartered accountants, bankers, underwriters.
4. Prepares MOA, AOA, and Prospectus.
5. Applies for name approval and files incorporation documents with the ROC.

**Duties of a Promoter:**
1. **Duty not to make secret profits** — Any commission or benefit must be disclosed to the company (Section 62).
2. **Duty of disclosure** — Full disclosure of all material facts in the prospectus to avoid liability for mis-statement.
3. **Duty of care, skill, and diligence** — Fiduciary duty towards the company.
4. **Duty to act bona fide in the interest of the company** he intends to form.
5. **Duty not to enter into binding contracts** on behalf of a non-existent company — pre-incorporation contracts are void unless ratified.

**Legal Status:**
- The promoter is **not a trustee** of the company (per the strict English position) but is treated as one in Indian law for secret profits.
- The promoter is **not an agent** of the company (since the company does not yet exist).
- The promoter is **not a servant or employee** of the company.
- On incorporation, the company can **adopt or ratify** the preliminary contracts entered into by the promoter.

**Liability of Promoter:**
- Liable to compensate the company for any breach of duty.
- Liable to refund any secret profit.
- Personally liable for pre-incorporation contracts unless the company takes them over.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating the Certificate of Incorporation with the Certificate of Commencement:** These are two *distinct* documents. Marks are deducted if students claim a Private Company needs a Certificate of Commencement.
> 2. **Confusing MOA with AOA:** MOA is the *charter* (defines scope and objectives), AOA is the *rule book* (defines internal management). Marks are lost if roles are swapped.
> 3. **Ignoring the Doctrine of Ultra Vires:** A company acting beyond its MOA objects clause makes the act void; even all shareholders cannot ratify an ultra vires act.
> 4. **Skipping the Nominee in OPC:** Whenever OPC is asked, the **mandatory nominee** concept must be mentioned — failure to do so costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Misidentifying Perpetual Succession as "Immortal":** A company has perpetual succession *until* it is legally wound up — it is not literally immortal.
> 6. **Wrongly stating the minimum members of a Public Company as "any number":** The correct minimum is **7**.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Sole Proprietorship:** Single owner, unlimited liability, no separate legal entity, no registration under any specific Act.
- **Partnership Firm:** 2 to 50 partners, joint and several unlimited liability, governed by the Indian Partnership Act, 1932.
- **Private Company:** 2 to 200 members, limited liability, name must end with **"Private Limited"**, **no public invitation**.
- **Public Company:** Minimum 7 members, no upper limit, limited liability, name must end with **"Limited"**, **public invitation via Prospectus** is allowed.
- **One Person Company (OPC):** Introduced by Companies Act, 2013 — 1 member + 1 nominee, limited liability, **exempted from capital subscription and commencement stages**.
- **Limited Liability Partnership (LLP):** Hybrid — partnership flexibility + corporate limited liability.
- **Co-operative Society:** Service motive, **one-member-one-vote** principle, suitable for agricultural/dairy/credit sectors.
- **Memorandum of Association (MOA):** Charter of the company; contains 6 clauses — Name, Registered Office, Objects, Liability, Capital, Association.
- **Articles of Association (AOA):** Internal rules and regulations for day-to-day management.
- **Prospectus:** Invitation to the public; must comply with Section 26 and Schedule I disclosures.
- **Promoter:** Undertakes preliminary work; duty-bound to disclose secret profits and ensure prospectus accuracy.
- **Four Stages of Public Company Formation:** Promotion → Incorporation → Capital Subscription → Commencement of Business.
- **Private Company / OPC Exemption:** Skip stages 3 and 4 — can begin business right after Certificate of Incorporation.
- **Minimum Subscription Rule:** Must be raised within **120 days**; otherwise refund within **130 days**.
- **Certificate of Incorporation:** Issued by ROC — conclusive evidence of legal existence; the company becomes a separate legal entity.
- **Certificate of Commencement:** Required **only for public companies** after filing INC-20A declaration.
- **Salomon v. Salomon (1897):** The foundation case for the doctrine of separate legal entity — company is a person distinct from its members.
- **Doctrine of Ultra Vires:** Acts beyond the objects clause of MOA are void ab initio; cannot be ratified even by unanimous shareholders.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Final Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer with **headings, sub-points, and tables**. Examiners reward visual clarity. For 3-mark questions, use **bullet points and crisp definitions** — avoid long paragraphs. Tie every answer to the **Companies Act, 2013** wherever applicable — section numbers earn extra marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Productivity and Work Study: Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Productivity and Work Study: Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Productivity — Formal KTU Definition

In the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus, **Productivity** is formally defined as the quantitative relationship between what is produced (output) and what is required to produce it (input). It is a dimensionless measure of efficiency that reflects how effectively an organization converts its resources (inputs) into useful goods and services (outputs).

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Productivity is **not** the same as production. Production refers to the *absolute volume* of output generated, whereas productivity measures the *efficiency* of conversion between output and input. A factory may produce more units, yet still be less productive if its inputs (man-hours, materials, energy) increased disproportionately.

### Classification of Productivity (KTU Standard)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly classifies productivity into the following categories:

| Category | Numerator | Denominator | KTU Exam Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Productivity** | Total Output (all goods & services) | Total Input (all resources) | Macro-level measure |
| **Partial Productivity** | Output of one type | One class of input (e.g., labour) | Most commonly used |
| **Factor Productivity** | Output | Single specific input (e.g., machine hours) | Micro/equipment level |
| **Multifactor Productivity** | Output | Two or more aggregated inputs (e.g., labour + capital) | Used for departments |

> [!NOTE]
> **Partial productivity of labour** is the most frequently asked variant in KTU ESE. Its formula is:
> $$\text{Labour Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for Students

Imagine two auto-rickshaw drivers, **Ravi** and **Suresh**, both working 8 hours in a day.

- **Ravi** earns ₹1,600 in 8 hours → **₹200/hour**.
- **Suresh** earns ₹1,200 in 8 hours → **₹150/hour**.

Both are working the *same number of hours* (same input), but Ravi generates **more output (money)** per unit of input. Ravi is therefore **more productive**.

**Industrial Engineering Takeaway:** When we examine a factory, we are doing exactly the same thing — comparing the *value of goods produced* against the *resources consumed*. The driver earning more per hour is analogous to the factory producing more per man-hour, per machine-hour, or per rupee of material.

## 1.3 Work Study — Formal KTU Definition

**Work Study** is a generic term used in Industrial Engineering to encompass the techniques of **Method Study** and **Work Measurement**. These two techniques are concerned primarily with the *systematic examination of human work* in order to determine the most efficient method of performing it and to establish the standard time for its completion.

$$\text{Work Study} = \text{Method Study} + \text{Work Measurement}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Style):** *"Work Study is the systematic, objective, and critical examination of all the factors governing the operational efficiency of a specified activity in order to effect improvement."*

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Work Study Intuition

Think of how an engineering student prepares tea for 10 guests at a hostel room:

1. Initially, the student wastes 25 minutes (boiling water twice, walking around searching for sugar, washing cup repeatedly).
2. After observing the process (Method Study), the student rearranges the steps: keeps sugar ready, fills water once, uses one clean pot.
3. Now the same task takes **12 minutes** (Work Measurement: standard time established).
4. Result: **Productivity doubled.**

This is exactly the **Work Study** cycle: **examine → improve → time → apply standard**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Productivity — Underlying Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Components of Productivity

Productivity measurement in Industrial Engineering is built upon **three fundamental dimensions**:

1. **Output Component** — measured in:
   - Units (pieces, kg, tonnes)
   - Monetary value (₹, $)
   - Service units (patients treated, calls answered)

2. **Input Component** — categorized under the classical **3 Ms** of a manufacturing organization:
   - **Man** (human resources / labour hours)
   - **Machine** (equipment hours, depreciation cost)
   - **Material** (raw material consumed in ₹ or kg)

3. **Time Frame Component** — measured per:
   - Hour (man-hour productivity)
   - Day (daily output per worker)
   - Month / Year (aggregate productivity index)

### 2.1.2 The Productivity Equation — Why It Works

The productivity equation is fundamentally rooted in the **conservation of efficiency principle**. If output increases while input remains constant, productivity rises proportionally. Conversely, if input decreases while output remains constant, productivity rises as well.

$$\uparrow \text{Output} \lor \downarrow \text{Input} \implies \uparrow \text{Productivity}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Productivity indices are used by:
> - The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) for **national productivity comparisons**.
> - The International Labour Organization (ILO) for **labour efficiency benchmarks**.
> - ISO 22400 standards for **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** in manufacturing.

## 2.2 Work Study — Theoretical Architecture

### 2.2.1 The Eight-Step Procedure (KTU Standard)

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates the following **eight sequential steps** for any Work Study project:

1. **Define the problem** to be studied (scope, boundaries).
2. **Record** all relevant facts using observation charts.
3. **Examine** the recorded facts critically (using questioning technique).
4. **Develop** the most economical method (ideation).
5. **Evaluate** the new method (cost-benefit analysis).
6. **Define** the new method (write a standard operating procedure).
7. **Install** the new method (training, supervision, rollout).
8. **Maintain** the new method (periodic audits, reviews).

### 2.2.2 Method Study vs. Work Measurement

| Dimension | Method Study | Work Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | HOW the work is done | HOW LONG it takes |
| **Primary Question** | Is there a better way? | What is the standard time? |
| **Outcome** | Improved procedure | Time standard / output standard |
| **Techniques** | Process charts, flow diagrams, string diagrams, multiple activity charts | Stopwatch time study, predetermined motion time systems (PMTS), work sampling, analytical estimating |
| **Stage in Work Study** | First (the "WHAT" and "HOW") | Second (the "HOW MUCH") |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every productivity and work study formula required for the KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | KTU Exam Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Productivity Index | $P = \dfrac{O}{I}$ | dimensionless ratio | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Labour Productivity | $P_L = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Man-hours}}$ | units / hr | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Machine Productivity | $P_M = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Machine-hours}}$ | units / hr | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Material Productivity | $P_{Mat} = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Material consumed}}$ | units / kg | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Multifactor Productivity | $P_{MF} = \dfrac{O}{L + M + C}$ | ratio | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Productivity Improvement % | $\Delta P\% = \dfrac{P_{new} - P_{old}}{P_{old}} \times 100$ | percent | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Standard Time | $ST = NT \times (1 + \text{Allowance})$ | minutes / units | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Normal Time | $NT = \text{Observed Time} \times \text{Performance Rating}$ | minutes | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Performance Rating | $R = \dfrac{\text{Actual Performance}}{\text{Standard Performance}} \times 100$ | percent | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | Work Sampling (sample size) | $N = \dfrac{4 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{S^2}$ | samples | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 11 | Output per hour (in standard time) | $OPH = \dfrac{60}{ST}$ | units / hr | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the relationship $ST = NT(1 + A)$ where $A$ is the allowance fraction (e.g., $0.15$ for $15\%$). This single line alone can solve 4-5 marks of typical Part B sub-questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Productivity Improvement Percentage

The productivity improvement formula is derived by treating productivity as a simple ratio and computing its *relative change*. We begin with the basic identity:

$$P_{old} = \frac{O_{old}}{I_{old}}$$

**Step 1:** Define the *change in productivity* as the difference between the new productivity index and the old one.

$$\Delta P = P_{new} - P_{old}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the ratio definitions for both terms.

$$\Delta P = \frac{O_{new}}{I_{new}} - \frac{O_{old}}{I_{old}}$$

**Step 3:** To express this as a *percentage improvement*, divide the change by the baseline (old) productivity value.

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{\Delta P}{P_{old}} = \frac{P_{new} - P_{old}}{P_{old}}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $100$ to convert the fraction into a percentage.

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{P_{new} - P_{old}}{P_{old}} \times 100$$

This final equation is the universally applied productivity change indicator used in KTU board examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation of Standard Time from Observed Time

The standard time derivation flows through three logical transitions:

**Step 1 — Capture the observed time:** A trained time-study observer using a stopwatch records the actual time taken by a qualified worker, denoted $OT$ (Observed Time).

$$NT = OT \times R$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Performance Rating:** Since workers do not always perform at exactly standard pace, the observed time is normalized using a dimensionless rating factor $R$ (typically expressed as a decimal, e.g., $1.10$ for a $10\%$ fast worker).

$$NT = OT \times R$$

**Step 3 — Apply Relaxation Allowances:** Real jobs require rest, personal time, and contingencies. We add an allowance fraction $A$ to the normal time to obtain the standard time.

$$ST = NT \times (1 + A)$$

Expanding fully:

$$ST = OT \times R \times (1 + A)$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style — 14 Mark Pattern)

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Question):**

In a small-scale manufacturing unit, **8 workers** produce **240 units per day** working **8 hours per day**. After implementing a Work Study-based Method Study improvement, the same workers produce **360 units per day** without any change in hours worked.

**Required:**

(a) Calculate the labour productivity before and after the improvement. *(7 marks)*
(b) Determine the percentage improvement in productivity. *(7 marks)*

### Solution to Part (a)

**Given Data (Extract):**
- Number of workers: $W = 8$
- Hours per day: $H = 8$
- Output before: $O_1 = 240$ units/day
- Output after: $O_2 = 360$ units/day

**Step 1 — Compute Man-hours per day (input):**

$$\text{Man-hours per day} = W \times H = 8 \times 8 = 64 \text{ man-hours}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Labour Productivity before improvement:**

$$P_1 = \frac{O_1}{\text{Man-hours}} = \frac{240}{64}$$

$$P_1 = 3.75 \text{ units per man-hour}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Labour Productivity after improvement:**

$$P_2 = \frac{O_2}{\text{Man-hours}} = \frac{360}{64}$$

$$P_2 = 5.625 \text{ units per man-hour}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both productivities are expressed in the same units (units / man-hour), allowing direct comparison.

### Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Apply the productivity improvement formula derived in Section 3.1:**

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{P_1} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the computed values:**

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{5.625 - 3.75}{3.75} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator difference:**

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{1.875}{3.75} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Final division:**

$$\Delta P_{\%} = 0.5 \times 100 = 50\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The Method Study-based improvement resulted in a **50% increase in labour productivity** without any additional inputs (no extra workers, no extra hours). This is the exact value of *Work Study* in manufacturing — it is the only technique that delivers productivity gains at zero additional cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of Work Study

The following block diagram illustrates the formal architecture of a Work Study programme as prescribed by KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Identify Problem Area] --> A2[Record Existing Method]
    A2 --> A3[Examine Using Questioning Technique]
    A3 --> A4[Develop Improved Method]

    A5 --> B1[Select Worker for Time Study]
    B1 --> B2[Record Observed Times]
    B2 --> B3[Apply Performance Rating]
    B3 --> B4[Compute Normal Time]
    B4 --> B5[Add Allowance Fraction]
    B5 --> B6[Standard Time Established]
    B6 --> B7{Work Measurement Complete}
    A5 --> C1[Install New Method]
    B7 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Train Workers]
    C2 --> C3[Supervise Implementation]
    C3 --> C4[Periodic Audit]
    C4 --> D1[Improved Productivity Achieved]
    style A1 fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1
    style B6 fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D

```

## 4.2 Productivity Classification Hierarchy

The diagram below shows the hierarchy of productivity classification under the KTU 2024 framework.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Productivity = Output per Input]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: From Method Study to Productivity Gain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[As Is Method] --> F2[Critical Examination]
    F2 --> F3[Question Technique: 5W1H + ECRS]
```

## 4.4 Productivity Measurement System (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I1[Man Input] --> C[Compute Ratio]
    I2[Machine Input] --> C
    I3[Material Input] --> C
    I4[Capital Input] --> C
    O1[Output in Units] --> C
    O2[Output in Value] --> C
    C --> R1[Labour Productivity]
    C --> R2[Machine Productivity]
    C --> R3[Material Productivity]
    C --> R4[Multifactor Productivity]
    R1 --> D[Decision Making and Benchmarking]
    R2 --> D
    R3 --> D
    R4 --> D

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each yellow / green / blue node represents a distinct phase of the KTU Work Study framework. Tracing from top to bottom in Diagram 4.1 reveals the exact eight-step procedure expected by KTU examiners for full-mark answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Productivity. Distinguish it from Production. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (Expected Key):**

- **Productivity:** The ratio of output to input, expressing the efficiency with which an organization converts resources into goods and services.
- **Production:** The absolute volume of output generated, irrespective of the resources consumed.
- **Distinction:** A factory producing 1000 units in 10 hours is *producing more* than one producing 800 units in 8 hours, but the latter is *more productive* per hour.

---

### Question 2: List any four techniques used in Method Study. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (Expected Key):**

1. **Process Charts** (Operation, Inspection, Transportation, Delay, Storage)
2. **Flow Process Charts**
3. **String Diagrams**
4. **Multiple Activity Charts**
5. **Travel Charts** (any four)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Comprehensive Work Study Analysis `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Define Work Study. Explain its two components with their objectives. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** A worker takes 1.2 minutes to complete an operation as observed by a time study analyst. The performance rating assigned is 110%. The allowance fraction applicable is 18%. Calculate the standard time and the expected output per hour. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Definition and Components:**

**Definition:** Work Study is a systematic, objective, and critical examination of all factors governing the operational efficiency of a specified activity in order to effect improvement. It comprises two complementary techniques.

**Component 1 — Method Study:**

- **Definition:** The systematic recording and critical examination of existing and proposed ways of doing work to develop and apply easier and more effective methods.
- **Objectives:** Reduce unnecessary work, simplify process, eliminate delays, improve layout, and reduce fatigue.

**Component 2 — Work Measurement:**

- **Definition:** The application of techniques designed to establish the time required for a qualified worker to perform a specified task at a defined level of performance.
- **Objectives:** Establish standard times, plan production, determine costs, motivate workers, and balance workloads.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Observed Time: $OT = 1.2 \text{ minutes}$
- Performance Rating: $R = 110\% = 1.10$
- Allowance Fraction: $A = 18\% = 0.18$

**Step 1 — Compute Normal Time:**

$$NT = OT \times R = 1.2 \times 1.10 = 1.32 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Standard Time:**

$$ST = NT \times (1 + A) = 1.32 \times (1 + 0.18)$$

$$ST = 1.32 \times 1.18$$

$$ST = 1.5576 \text{ minutes per unit}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Output per Hour:**

$$OPH = \frac{60 \text{ minutes}}{ST} = \frac{60}{1.5576}$$

$$OPH \approx 38.52 \text{ units per hour}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Standard Time = **1.5576 minutes**, Output per Hour ≈ **38.52 units/hr**.

---

### Question B: Productivity Improvement Numerical `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** A manufacturing unit had a labour productivity of 4 units per man-hour in 2023. After a Method Study improvement, the productivity rose to 6 units per man-hour in 2024. Calculate the percentage improvement in productivity. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** Discuss the significance of Work Study in modern manufacturing industries. List the eight steps of a Work Study programme. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Percentage Improvement Calculation:**

**Given Data:**
- Old productivity: $P_1 = 4$ units / man-hour
- New productivity: $P_2 = 6$ units / man-hour

**Step 1 — Apply the productivity improvement formula:**

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{P_1} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{6 - 4}{4} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$\Delta P_{\%} = \frac{2}{4} \times 100 = 0.5 \times 100 = 50\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Percentage improvement = **50%**.

---

**Part (b) — Significance and Eight Steps:**

**Significance of Work Study:**
- **Productivity boost** without additional capital expenditure.
- **Cost reduction** through elimination of wasteful activities.
- **Improved worker safety** by removing hazardous motions.
- **Better ergonomics** leading to reduced fatigue.
- **Standardization** enabling reliable planning and scheduling.
- **Foundation for incentive wage systems.**

**Eight Steps of Work Study:**
1. Define the problem
2. Record the facts
3. Examine the facts critically
4. Develop the improved method
5. Evaluate the new method
6. Define the new method
7. Install the new method
8. Maintain the new method

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Output with Productivity:** Writing *"Production increased by 50%"* instead of *"Productivity increased by 50%"* — KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this in Part B.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Rating Factor in Time Study:** Students often compute $ST = OT \times (1 + A)$ directly, skipping $NT = OT \times R$. This is a **3-mark deduction** because the rating step is mandatory.
>
> 3. **Wrong Unit Notation:** Writing "output per worker" instead of "output per man-hour" loses partial credit.
>
> 4. **Incomplete Eight Steps:** Listing only 5-6 steps of Work Study loses **2-3 marks**; KTU examiners expect all 8 for full credit.
>
> 5. **Mixing Productivity Types:** Writing multifactor productivity without specifying which two or more inputs are aggregated is a common deductable error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Productivity = Output / Input** is the foundational equation; commit it to memory verbatim.
- Productivity is **dimensionless** when expressed as a ratio; units appear only for partial/factor productivity.
- **Partial Productivity** (e.g., labour) is the most tested variant in KTU ESE.
- **Work Study = Method Study + Work Measurement** — both are mandatory, neither can be skipped.
- The **eight-step procedure** is the universal methodology expected in 14-mark questions.
- **Method Study** answers "HOW?" while **Work Measurement** answers "HOW LONG?".
- Standard Time equation: $ST = OT \times R \times (1+A)$.
- Output per Hour: $OPH = 60 / ST$.
- Productivity improvement % = $(P_{new} - P_{old}) / P_{old} \times 100$.
- Real-world applications: automotive assembly, garment manufacturing, hospital operations, banking services, software testing — productivity and work study apply wherever measurable work exists.
- Distinguish clearly between **production** (volume) and **productivity** (efficiency).
- Always specify **units** in productivity answers to avoid KTU mark-loss.
- The questioning technique **5W1H + ECRS** (Eliminate, Combine, Rearrange, Simplify) is the heart of Method Study.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods Study and Work Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Methods Study and Work Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Methods Study — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Methods Study** is the systematic recording, analysis, and critical examination of existing and proposed ways of doing work, with the objective of developing **more efficient methods** and thereby reducing the overall cost of production.

According to the British Standards Institution (BSI) — adopted universally in KTU 2024 syllabus — Methods Study is a **technique for improving working methods** that combines **procedure analysis** (the *what* and *why*) with **motion study** (the *how*).

The **core objective** of Methods Study is the *one best way* of performing a job, achieved by eliminating unnecessary operations, combining operations, rearranging sequence, and simplifying the remaining work.

### 1.2 Work Measurement — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Measurement** is the application of techniques designed to establish the **time required for a qualified worker** to perform a specified task at a **defined level of performance** (the standard rating).

The output of Work Measurement is the **Standard Time (ST)** — the benchmark time used for production planning, scheduling, incentive wage systems, and cost estimation.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

**Methods Study Analogy — "The Home Cooking Optimization"**

Imagine a mother preparing chai every morning. Initially, she boils water, then adds tea, then milk, then sugar — moving back and forth across the kitchen. **Methods Study is like a video analysis** of her routine: recording every step, finding that she walks 12 meters per cup unnecessarily, rearranging utensils so sugar and tea are placed together, and combining the boiling of water and milk into a single vessel. The output is a **leaner recipe method** — the work is unchanged, but the *waste* is removed.

**Work Measurement Analogy — "The Stopwatch on the Athlete"**

If Methods Study is *what* to run and *how* to run it, Work Measurement is **timing the runner** to determine the standard time. A coach uses a stopwatch to record 100 m sprint times under normal conditions, adjusts for a "windy day" (allowance), and then sets a **standard time** for every athlete in the training squad. That standard time becomes the benchmark.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU 2-markers):**
> - **Methods Study** answers: *"What is the BEST way to do the work?"*
> - **Work Measurement** answers: *"HOW LONG should the BEST way take?"*
> Together they are called the **Work Study** framework (also termed *Time and Motion Study*).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Conventions

- **Standard Rating (SR)**: The **British Standard Rating (BSR)** of **100 = Normal pace** of a qualified, trained worker working at a sustainable speed.
- **Working Day**: **8 hours = 480 minutes** of standard time.
- **Performance Rating Factor (PRF)**: Expressed as a percentage of standard (e.g., 110 = brisk worker, 90 = slow worker).
- **Allowance Factor (AF)**: Expressed as a **percentage** of Normal Time (e.g., 15%, 20%).
- **Confidence Level (Z)** for Work Sampling: Z = 1.96 at **95% confidence**, Z = 2.58 at **99% confidence**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Methods Study — Step-by-Step Logic (The 6/7-Step Procedure)

The universally accepted procedure (ILO standard, mandatory in KTU 2024 syllabus):

1. **SELECT** — Identify the job to be studied (use the *Activity Sampling* or *Stop-watch* approach to find the bottleneck job).
2. **RECORD** — Collect all relevant facts using charts and diagrams.
3. **EXAMINE** — Subject each fact to **critical examination** using the four-line of attack: *Purpose, Place, Sequence, Person*.
4. **DEVELOP** — Devise the most economic method based on the examination.
5. **DEFINE** — Present the proposed method clearly so it can be understood by all.
6. **INSTALL** — Put the new method into practice, with the cooperation of the workforce.
7. **MAINTAIN** — Periodically audit the method to ensure compliance and continuous improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Examination — The Four Heuristic Questions** (Examiner's favourite):
> - **Purpose** — What is done? *Is it necessary? Why?*
> - **Place** — Where is it done? *Is this the best place?*
> - **Sequence** — When is it done? *Is this the right time?*
> - **Person** — Who does it? *Is this the right person?*

### 2.2 Methods Study — Recording Techniques (Chart Family)

| Technique | Symbol Set | What it Records | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Outline Process Chart** | $\bigcirc$ Operation, $\Rightarrow$ Transport, $\square$ Inspection, $\nabla$ Storage, $D$ Delay | Overall process at macro level | 2-mark definition questions |
| **Flow Process Chart** | Same 5 symbols + combined symbols | Material OR worker flow (separate charts) | 7-mark procedure tracing |
| **Two-Handed Process Chart** | Operations of both hands on a common time scale | Micro-motion study of two hands | 14-mark cycle time analysis |
| **String Diagram** | Pins + continuous thread on a scaled layout | Path of worker/material up to ~5 workers | Layout optimization |
| **Multiple Activity Chart** | Common time scale, multiple workers | Idle and working time of several workers | Crew balancing |
| **Cycle Graph** | Path traced by limb; speed shown by line thickness | Magnitude of motion | Ergonomic redesign |
| **Chronocycle Graph** | Time-distance graph of motion | Time & distance covered in a cycle | Training aids |

### 2.3 Work Measurement — Hierarchy of Techniques

```
Work Measurement Techniques
├── Direct Measurement
│   ├── Stopwatch Time Study (Pioneered by F.W. Taylor, 1881)
│   └── Activity Sampling / Work Sampling (L. Tippet, 1935)
├── Predetermined Time Standards
│   ├── Methods Time Measurement (MTM)
│   ├── Work Factor (WF)
│   └── Predetermined Motion Time System (PMTS)
├── Standard Data Systems
│   ├── Standard Element Times
│   └── Standard Data Banks
└── Analytical Estimating
    └── Use of historical data + estimating formulas
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus**: Stopwatch Time Study and Work Sampling are the two **most-tested** techniques. They form the bulk of 14-mark numerical problems.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains the **complete formula set** required for any Methods Study or Work Measurement numerical in KTU 2024 ESE.

| Quantity | Formula | Definition of Symbols | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal Time (NT)** | $NT = OT \times \dfrac{R}{100}$ | $OT$ = Observed Time; $R$ = Performance Rating | min |
| **Basic Time (BT)** | $BT = OT \times \dfrac{R}{100}$ | Same as Normal Time (Taylor) | min |
| **Standard Time (ST)** | $ST = \dfrac{NT}{1 - AF}$ | $AF$ = Allowance Factor (decimal) | min |
| **Standard Time (Alt)** | $ST = NT \times (1 + AF_{pct})$ | $AF_{pct}$ = Allowance percentage | min |
| **Relaxation Allowance (RA)** | $RA = \dfrac{NT \times AF_{pct}}{100 + AF_{pct}}$ | Used to get Relaxed Time from NT | min |
| **Relaxed Time (RT)** | $RT = NT - RA$ | Time after relaxation deduction | min |
| **Work Sampling Sample Size (n)** | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$ | $Z$ = confidence coefficient; $p$ = proportion; $e$ = relative error | dimensionless |
| **WS Sample Size (worst case)** | $n = \dfrac{4 \cdot Z^2}{e^2}$ | When $p = 0.5$ (maximum variance) | dimensionless |
| **Total Random Observations (N)** | $N = \dfrac{n}{r}$ | $n$ = sample size; $r$ = no. of observations per day | count |
| **Number of Days (D)** | $D = \dfrac{N}{d}$ | $d$ = observations possible per day | days |
| **Westinghouse Rating Multiplier** | $R = R_s \times R_q \times R_e \times R_c$ | Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency | dimensionless |
| **Performance Rating (PRF)** | $PRF = \dfrac{Observed\ Speed}{Normal\ Speed} \times 100$ | Ratio of worker to standard | % |
| **Standard Output / Hour** | $Output = \dfrac{60}{ST}$ | Pieces per hour | pcs/hr |
| **Standard Output / Day** | $Output = \dfrac{480}{ST}$ | Pieces per 8-hr day | pcs/day |
| **Utilisation Factor** | $\eta = \dfrac{Working\ Time}{Available\ Time}$ | Machine/operator efficiency | % |

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Methods Study** drives **Kaizen** (continuous improvement), **Lean Manufacturing** (waste elimination: *Muda, Mura, Muri*), and **Toyota Production System** philosophy.
- **Work Measurement** is the foundation of **Time-Based Incentive Wage Systems** (Halsey Plan, Rowan Plan, Bedaux Plan) and **Capacity Planning** in ERP systems (SAP PP module uses work measurement-based standard times).
- Together they form the **Work Study** discipline that delivers **Productivity = Output / Input** improvement, the primary KPI of every manufacturing enterprise.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation — Standard Time from Observed Time

This is the **core derivation** that every KTU 2024 student must be able to reproduce on the answer script.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: The investigator records the stopwatch time} &\quad OT \\
\text{Step 2: The worker is not necessarily "normal" — apply Performance Rating} &\quad R \\
\text{Step 3: Normalise the observed time} &\quad NT = OT \times \frac{R}{100} \\
\text{Step 4: Add Rest & Personal Allowances (RA + PA) to the Normal Time} &\quad ST = NT + Allowances \\
\text{Step 5: Allowances are conventionally expressed as a fraction of ST} &\quad AF = \frac{Allowances}{ST} \\
\text{Step 6: Substituting} &\quad ST = NT + AF \cdot ST \\
\text{Step 7: Rearranging} &\quad ST - AF \cdot ST = NT \\
\text{Step 8: Factorising} &\quad ST \cdot (1 - AF) = NT \\
