\text{Step 9: Solving for ST} &\quad ST = \frac{NT}{1 - AF} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Pattern Problem (Part B, 7 marks)

> **[Problem]** A stopwatch time study gave an average observed cycle time of **0.80 min**. The worker's performance was rated at **120**. The allowances for rest and personal are **15%** of normal time, and **10%** of standard time is added for contingencies. Calculate: (i) Normal Time, (ii) Total Allowances, (iii) Standard Time per piece, (iv) Standard Output per 8-hour day.

#### Solution

**Given:**
- $OT = 0.80$ min
- $R = 120\%$
- Rest & Personal Allowance (RA) = **15% of NT** → applied to NT to get *Base Standard Time*
- Contingency Allowance (CA) = **10% of ST** → applied to the *Base Standard Time* to get final ST

**Step 1 — Normal Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= OT \times \frac{R}{100} \\
   &= 0.80 \times \frac{120}{100} \\
   &= 0.80 \times 1.20 \\
   &= 0.96 \text{ min/piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Base Standard Time (with RA):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Allowance value} &= NT \times \frac{15}{100} = 0.96 \times 0.15 = 0.144 \text{ min} \\
\text{Base ST} &= NT + RA = 0.96 + 0.144 = 1.104 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final Standard Time (with CA applied to Base ST):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Contingency} &= \text{Base ST} \times \frac{10}{100} = 1.104 \times 0.10 = 0.1104 \text{ min} \\
ST_{final} &= 1.104 + 0.1104 = 1.2144 \text{ min/piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Standard Output per 8-hr Day (480 min):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Output &= \frac{480}{ST_{final}} = \frac{480}{1.2144} \\
       &\approx 395.2 \text{ pieces/day} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Answer Script**: A worker of 120% rating is allowed to produce 395 pieces per day after all allowances. If the observed worker is *above 100%*, the standard time is *strictly longer* than the observed time — this rewards the *faster* worker but protects the *standard* from becoming unrealistic.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Work Sampling (Part B, 7 marks)

> **[Problem]** In a work-sampling study of a drilling machine, a preliminary sample of **100 random observations** showed the machine **idle** in **40** observations. Determine the additional number of random observations required to estimate the *true proportion of idle time* within **±4%** at **95% confidence**.

#### Solution

**Given:**
- $Z_{0.95} = 1.96$
- Preliminary proportion $p = 40/100 = 0.40$
- Relative error $e = 0.04$
- Existing observations $n_0 = 100$

**Step 1 — Required total sample size using $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.40 \times (1 - 0.40)}{(0.04)^2} \\
  &= \frac{3.8416 \times 0.40 \times 0.60}{0.0016} \\
  &= \frac{3.8416 \times 0.24}{0.0016} \\
  &= \frac{0.9220}{0.0016} \\
  &= 576.25 \approx 577 \text{ observations}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Additional observations required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{additional} &= n - n_0 = 577 - 100 = 477 \text{ observations} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Allowance Categories (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The five standard allowance categories, each as a **percentage of Normal Time**, are required knowledge for KTU 2024 ESE.

| Allowance | Typical Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Personal Allowance (PA)** | **5% – 7%** | Personal hygiene, water, restroom |
| **Rest / Fatigue Allowance (RA / FA)** | **10% – 20%** | Recovery from physical/mental fatigue |
| **Contingency Allowance (CA)** | **2% – 5%** | Unforeseen delays (tool breakage, etc.) |
| **Process Allowance (PrA)** | **5% – 10%** | Inherent to process (e.g., drying time) |
| **Special Allowance (SA)** | **Variable** | Job-specific (e.g., protective equipment wear) |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Time Study Equipment & Stopwatch Methods

| Method | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Timing** | Stopwatch runs throughout; elements are timed cumulatively, then read off | High accuracy, prevents timing errors |
| **Snap-Back Timing** | Watch reset to zero at the end of each element | Simpler, but introduces backlash error (~5%) |
| **Cumulative Timing** | Two watches; one continuous, one reset | Reduces backlash |
| **Repetitive Timing** | Element timed repeatedly; times added | Statistical averaging |
| **Fly-Back Timing** | Watch flies back to zero on click | Not commonly used |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Methods Study — Procedural Flow (7-Step Procedure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEP 1 SELECT THE JOB"] --> B["STEP 2 RECORD ALL FACTS USING CHARTS"]
    B --> C["STEP 3 EXAMINE CRITICALLY 4 HEURISTICS"]
    C --> D["STEP 4 DEVELOP THE BEST METHOD"]
    D --> E["STEP 5 DEFINE THE NEW METHOD"]
    E --> F["STEP 6 INSTALL AND IMPLEMENT"]
    F --> G["STEP 7 MAINTAIN AND AUDIT"]
    G --> H{"METHOD STILL OPTIMAL"}
    H -- NO --> B
    H -- YES --> I["OPERATIONAL EXCELLENCE"]
```

### 4.2 Work Measurement — Technique Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WORK MEASUREMENT REQUIRED"] --> B{"REPETITIVE LONG CYCLE JOB"}
    B -- YES --> C{"STOPWATCH AVAILABLE"}
    B -- NO --> D["STANDARD DATA OR PMTS"]
    C -- YES --> E["STOPWATCH TIME STUDY"]
    C -- NO --> F["WORK SAMPLING ACTIVITY SAMPLING"]
    E --> G["NORMAL TIME CALCULATION"]
    G --> H["APPLY ALLOWANCES"]
    F --> H
    D --> H
    H --> I["STANDARD TIME ST"]
```

### 4.3 Time Study — Standard Time Calculation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OT["OBSERVED TIME OT"] --> RAT["APPLY PERFORMANCE RATING R"]
    RAT --> NT["NORMAL TIME NT"]
    NT --> ALW["ADD ALLOWANCES RA PA CA"]
    ALW --> ST["STANDARD TIME ST"]
    ST --> CAP["CAPACITY PLANNING"]
    ST --> INC["INCENTIVE WAGES"]
    ST --> CST["COST ESTIMATION"]
    ST --> SCH["SCHEDULING"]
```

### 4.4 Symbols of Process Chart (Methods Study Recording)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OP["CIRCLE OPERATION DONE"] --> TR["ARROW TRANSPORT MOVED"]
    TR --> IN["SQUARE INSPECTION CHECKED"]
    IN --> ST["TRIANGLE INVERTED STORAGE"]
    ST --> DL["D SHAPE DELAY WAIT"]
    DL --> OP2["COMBINED OPERATION PLUS INSPECTION"]
```

### 4.5 Work Sampling — Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DEFINE ACTIVITY CATEGORIES"] --> B["PILOT STUDY 100 OBSERVATIONS"]
    B --> C["ESTIMATE PILOT PROPORTION P"]
    C --> D["SET CONFIDENCE Z AND ERROR E"]
    D --> E["CALCULATE SAMPLE SIZE N"]
    E --> F["PLAN RANDOM OBSERVATION SCHEDULE"]
    F --> G["CONDUCT FINAL STUDY"]
    G --> H["COMPUTE FINAL PROPORTIONS"]
    H --> I["STANDARD TIME DERIVATION"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Methods Study and Motion Study. Why is the broader discipline called "Work Study"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Methods Study | Motion Study |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Macro-level: *what*, *why*, *where*, *when* | Micro-level: *how* (the *therbligs*) |
| **Focus** | Process design, layout, sequence | Detailed hand-and-body motions |
| **Originator** | Currie (process analysis) | Gilbreth (motion economy) |
| **Output** | Improved method | Reduced motion fatigue & time |

**Work Study** = Methods Study + Work Measurement. It combines the *qualitative* (improvement of method) with the *quantitative* (time standardisation) to deliver productivity gains. **Work Study was coined by the ILO in 1945.** *(1 mark for the definition pair, 1 mark for motion-study origin, 1 mark for ILO definition of Work Study.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "Standard Time" and list any THREE allowances added to Normal Time to compute it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Standard Time (ST)** is the total time in which a job *should* be completed by a *qualified worker* working at a *normal pace*, including allowances for rest, personal needs, and contingencies.

**Three Allowances:**
1. **Personal Allowance (PA)** — typically 5–7% of NT — for personal hygiene and rest.
2. **Fatigue / Rest Allowance (RA / FA)** — typically 10–20% of NT — to recover from physical or mental exertion.
3. **Contingency Allowance (CA)** — typically 2–5% of NT — to cover unforeseen delays.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any three allowances with typical range.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choose EITHER Q-A OR Q-B; 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the *step-by-step procedure* of Methods Study with a neat flowchart. What is *Critical Examination* and what are its four heuristic questions?

**Model Solution:**

The Methods Study procedure (ILO 7-step standard) is:

1. **Select** the job — choose jobs with high frequency, high cost, bottleneck status, or complaints.
2. **Record** the present method — use *process charts*, *flow diagrams*, *string diagrams*, *two-handed process charts*, *cycle graphs*, or *chronocycle graphs*.
3. **Examine** the recorded facts critically — apply the *four-line-of-attack* (described below).
4. **Develop** the most economic method by eliminating, combining, rearranging, or simplifying.
5. **Define** the proposed method with a written standard, training aid, and clear instructions.
6. **Install** the new method — sell it to the workforce, train them, and start production.
7. **Maintain** the method with periodic audits and continuous improvement.

**Critical Examination** is the *third* step, where every recorded fact is questioned using the four heuristics:

| Heuristic | Question Asked | Sub-Question |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | *What is actually done?* | *Why is it done?* *Is it necessary?* |
| **Place** | *Where is it done?* | *Is this the best place? Can it be combined with another location?* |
| **Sequence** | *When is it done?* | *Is this the right time? Should it be done earlier/later/in parallel?* |
| **Person** | *Who does it?* | *Is this the right person? Can the operator be re-assigned?* |

*(1 mark each for any 6 steps; 1 mark for naming the four heuristics.)*

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Draw the symbols of a *Flow Process Chart* and explain each. How does a *Two-Handed Process Chart* differ from a *Flow Process Chart*?

**Model Solution:**

**Five Standard Process Chart Symbols:**

| Symbol | Name | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\bigcirc$ | **Operation** | Work is performed (a product is made/finished) | Drilling a hole, assembling parts |
| $\Rightarrow$ | **Transport** | Movement of material or worker | Carrying a part from store to machine |
| $\square$ | **Inspection** | Verification of quality or quantity | Checking the diameter of a shaft |
| $\nabla$ | **Storage** | Holding of material in a controlled state | Placing in finished goods store |
| $D$ | **Delay** | Waiting; no useful activity | Material waiting for next operation |

A **Combined Symbol** shows simultaneous activities, e.g., a circle-inside-square indicates *Operation + Inspection* at the same time.

**Two-Handed Process Chart vs. Flow Process Chart:**

| Aspect | Flow Process Chart | Two-Handed Process Chart |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject** | Material OR worker (separately) | **Both** left & right hands together |
| **Scale** | Macro (whole process) | **Micro** (one work station / one cycle) |
| **Time axis** | Sequence only | **Common time scale** for both hands |
| **Output** | Identify waste across the plant | Identify hand idle time & asymmetric motion |
| **Typical use** | Process re-engineering | Workstation design & ergonomic improvement |

*(1 mark per symbol × 5 = 5 marks; 2 marks for the difference comparison.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** What is *Performance Rating*? Explain the *Westinghouse System* of rating in detail. A worker's observed time on a job is **0.50 min** and the rating factors for skill, effort, conditions, and consistency are **+0.06, +0.04, -0.03, +0.02** respectively. Calculate the **Normal Time**.

**Model Solution:**

**Performance Rating** is the process of comparing the *observed speed of a worker* with the *normal pace* of a trained, qualified operator under standard conditions. It is expressed as a percentage; **100% = BSR normal**.

**Westinghouse System of Rating:**
The Westinghouse Electric Company developed a 4-factor system in 1928, where the final rating multiplier is the **product** of four sub-ratings:

$$
R = R_s \times R_q \times R_e \times R_c
$$

where $R_s$ = Skill rating, $R_q$ = Quality / Effort rating, $R_e$ = Effort rating, $R_c$ = Consistency rating.

Each sub-rating has a tabular value:

| Factor | Categories (Examples) | Multiplier Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Skill (Rs)** | Superskill, Excellent, Good, Average, Fair, Poor | +0.15 to -0.22 |
| **Effort (Re)** | Excessive, Excellent, Good, Average, Fair, Poor | +0.13 to -0.17 |
| **Conditions (Rq)** | Ideal, Excellent, Good, Average, Fair, Poor | +0.06 to -0.07 |
| **Consistency (Rc)** | Perfect, Excellent, Good, Average, Fair, Poor | +0.04 to -0.07 |

**Numerical Solution:**

The given rating factors are *increments* to the base 1.00 multiplier:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_s &= 1 + 0.06 = 1.06 \\
R_e &= 1 + 0.04 = 1.04 \\
R_q &= 1 - 0.03 = 0.97 \\
R_c &= 1 + 0.02 = 1.02 \\
R &= 1.06 \times 1.04 \times 0.97 \times 1.02 \\
  &= 1.06 \times 1.04 = 1.1024 \\
  &= 1.1024 \times 0.97 = 1.069328 \\
  &= 1.069328 \times 1.02 = 1.0907 \\
\text{Normal Time} &= OT \times R = 0.50 \times 1.0907 \\
                  &= 0.5454 \text{ min} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) [7 marks]** What is *Work Sampling*? A preliminary work-sampling study of 800 observations of a lathe showed it to be running (productive) in 500 observations. The desired precision is **±5%** with **95% confidence**. Calculate (i) the actual proportion running and (ii) the total sample size needed. (Z₀.₉₅ = 1.96)

**Model Solution:**

**Work Sampling** (also called *Activity Sampling* or *Ratio-delay Study*) is a statistical technique in which the *proportion* of time spent by workers, machines, or processes on various activities is determined by taking a large number of *random, instantaneous observations*.

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N_{pilot} = 800$ observations
- Productive observations = 500
- $Z = 1.96$ (95% confidence)
- $e = 0.05$ (relative error)

**Part (i) — Actual Proportion Running:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \frac{500}{800} = 0.625 \\
q &= 1 - p = 0.375
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Total Sample Size Required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot q}{e^2} \\
  &= \frac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.625 \times 0.375}{(0.05)^2} \\
  &= \frac{3.8416 \times 0.234375}{0.0025} \\
  &= \frac{0.9006}{0.0025} \\
  &\approx 360 \text{ observations}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since the pilot study already gathered **800 > 360** observations, the precision target of **±5%** is already achieved — no further observations are required. *(3 marks for definition, 2 marks for p calculation, 2 marks for n calculation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Basic Time with Normal Time** — In some texts BT = NT, but in KTU's preferred convention (Indian ILO/IS 12216), **BT excludes the relaxation allowance** and **NT includes the relaxation allowance**. The examiner expects $BT \le NT$.
> 2. **Allowance base error** — A common error: applying the Rest Allowance (15%) and Contingency Allowance (10%) **both to the NT** and *adding* them linearly. The correct treatment is: add RA to NT → get Base ST → apply CA to Base ST (sequential application, not additive).
> 3. **Work Sampling "Z" value** — Students frequently use Z = 1.645 (90% confidence) instead of 1.96 (95%). Always verify the confidence level stated in the problem.
> 4. **Forgetting $p(1-p)$** — When $p$ is given, the *other* category proportion is $(1-p)$; using $p^2$ instead of $p(1-p)$ leads to grossly wrong sample sizes.
> 5. **Performance Rating misuse** — Westinghouse factors are *multipliers around 1.00*, not percentages. Writing $R = 1.06 \times 100 = 106$ is a classic mark-loss error.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Methods Study (ILO 7-step)**: **S**elect → **R**ecord → **E**xamine → **D**evelop → **D**efine → **I**nstall → **M**aintain.
- **Four Heuristics of Critical Examination**: **P**urpose, **P**lace, **S**equence, **P**erson (2 P's, 1 S, 1 P).
- **Five Process Chart Symbols**: **O**peration $\bigcirc$, **T**ransport $\Rightarrow$, **I**nspection $\square$, **S**torage $\nabla$, **D**elay $D$.
- **Standard Time** is *not* Normal Time. Standard Time = Normal Time + *all* allowances.
- **Westinghouse Rating** is a **product**, not a sum: $R = R_s \cdot R_q \cdot R_e \cdot R_c$.
- **Work Sampling** uses **$p(1-p)$** for sample size; for *unknown* p, use **p = 0.5** → $n = 4Z^2/e^2$.
- **95% Confidence** → **Z = 1.96**; **99% Confidence** → **Z = 2.58**; **90% Confidence** → **Z = 1.645**.
- **Performance Rating of 100** = British Standard Rating (BSR) = Normal pace.
- **Standard Output per Day** = $\dfrac{480}{ST}$ (when working day = 8 hours = 480 minutes).
- **Allowances are always positive additions to NT** — they make ST *longer*, not shorter.
- **Methods Study** is *qualitative* (what & how); **Work Measurement** is *quantitative* (how long).
- **Work Study** = Methods Study + Work Measurement (the umbrella discipline).
- **Work Sampling** is a *discrete-event* technique (snapshot-based); **Time Study** is a *continuous-event* technique (stopwatch-based).
- **PMTS** systems (MTM, WF) need no rating since motion times are pre-determined for *all* normal workers.
- **Standard data** can be built from past time studies and reused for new jobs with similar elements.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Methods Study and Work Measurement**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Charts and Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Use of Charts and Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Engineering and Management (IE&M)**, *charts and diagrams* constitute a family of **graphical representation tools** employed to systematically record, analyse, communicate, and control information related to processes, production systems, manpower, materials, and management functions. They convert raw numerical or descriptive data into visual forms that allow engineers and managers to identify patterns, bottlenecks, deviations, and relationships at a glance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1 – IE&M):**
> Charts and diagrams are *visual aids* used by Industrial Engineers to study workflows, time relationships, process sequences, cost structures, and organizational hierarchies. They form the foundation of **Work Study**, **Method Study**, and **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)**.

## 1.2 Classification of Charts and Diagrams

Charts and diagrams in IE are broadly classified into two super-categories:

### A. Display & Presentation Charts
These communicate *quantitative data* to a wider audience (management, clients, workers).

| Sub-type | Primary Use |
|----------|------------|
| **Bar / Column Chart** | Comparing discrete categories |
| **Pie Chart** | Showing parts of a whole (composition) |
| **Line Graph** | Displaying trends over time |
| **Histogram** | Showing frequency distribution of continuous data |
| **Pareto Chart** | Separating vital few from trivial many |
| **Gantt Chart** | Project scheduling and progress tracking |
| **Control Chart** | Monitoring process variation (SQC) |
| **Scatter Diagram** | Studying correlation between two variables |

### B. Process & Operation Charts
These analyse *workflow* and *human/machine activity*.

| Sub-type | Primary Use |
|----------|------------|
| **Flow Process Chart** | Sequence of operations/inspections/transports/delays/storage |
| **Man-Machine Chart** | Idle vs. working time of operator and machine |
| **SIMO Chart** | Simultaneous-Motion chart for multi-cycle operations |
| **String Diagram** | Path tracing in a work area |
| **Travel Chart** | Movement of materials between work centres |
| **Cycle Graph / Chronocyclegraph** | Motion study with photographic timing |
| **Organization Chart** | Reporting hierarchy |
| **Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa) Diagram** | Root-cause analysis of problems |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Doctor's Stethoscope for a Factory"**
> Just as a doctor uses a stethoscope to *listen* to what is happening inside the human body without cutting it open, an industrial engineer uses charts and diagrams to *see* what is happening inside a production system without dismantling it. A bar chart is like a **thermometer** (it shows a single temperature reading), a flow process chart is like a **road map of a patient's journey** through a hospital (admission → tests → diagnosis → treatment → discharge), and a Pareto chart is like a **triage list** (treating the most critical 20% of wounds that cause 80% of the bleeding). Without these visuals, an engineer would be diagnosing a complex factory "disease" using only raw numbers — slow, error-prone, and unintuitive.

## 1.4 Why Charts and Diagrams Are Essential in IE

1. **Visual Compression** — A single A3-size flow process chart can replace 5 pages of written description.
2. **Pattern Recognition** — Humans process images **60,000× faster** than text.
3. **Standardization** — Provides a common language across departments.
4. **Decision Support** — Management decisions on layout, scheduling, and quality rest heavily on these visuals.
5. **Documentation** — Acts as a permanent record for ISO/audit compliance.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme Part-B (14-mark) questions, examiners frequently combine a *descriptive answer* (definition + symbols + procedure) with a *sketch* of the diagram. Marks are split roughly: **6 marks description + 8 marks diagram & analysis** (or vice versa). Always draw neat diagrams with a ruler and label all axes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Each Major Chart

### 2.1.1 Bar Chart (Column Chart)

- **Definition:** Rectangular bars whose lengths are proportional to the values they represent, plotted on a common baseline.
- **Types:** Simple, multiple/grouped, component/sub-divided, and percentage bar charts.
- **Construction Rule:** The width of all bars is identical; only the height varies. A uniform gap (~50% of bar width) is maintained between bars.
- **Why used:** To compare production output, sales, wages, costs across departments/years.

### 2.1.2 Pie Chart (Sector / Circle Chart)

- **Definition:** A circle divided into sectors whose angles are proportional to the relative magnitudes of the components.
- **Why used:** Best for showing *composition* (e.g., break-up of factory expenses, time distribution, labour allocation).

### 2.1.3 Flow Process Chart

- **Definition:** A diagrammatic representation of all events that occur during a process, using five standardized symbols.
- **Five Symbols (Gilbreth's Therbligs-Process Symbols):**

| Symbol | Shape | Meaning |
|--------|-------|---------|
| Operation | ● (circle) | A productive step (machining, assembly) |
| Inspection | ◐ (square) | Checking quality/quantity |
| Transport | → (arrow) | Movement of material |
| Delay | D (capital D) | Material waiting |
| Storage | ▽ (inverted triangle) | Material held in store |

### 2.1.4 Man-Machine Chart

- **Definition:** A two-line chart where one line represents the **operator's activity** and the other represents the **machine's activity**, plotted against time.
- **Purpose:** Identifies idle time of operator/machine so that work can be balanced and a second machine/operation assigned to the operator.
- **Key concept:** A *one-worker / one-machine* chart shows poor utilization; a *one-worker / two-machine* chart (achieved after balancing) is the ideal.

### 2.1.5 SIMO Chart (Simultaneous Motion Chart)

- **Definition:** A micro-level motion chart that records the simultaneous movements of different body parts (both hands, sometimes both feet) of an operator during **one complete cycle** of operation at a *fractional scale* (e.g., 1 sec = 0.1 cm).
- **Symbol:** Uses 18 Gilbreth therbligs (e.g., Grasp, Transport Loaded, Position, Release, Search, etc.).
- **Purpose:** Locates wasteful motions and helps design the *best method* (motion economy).

### 2.1.6 String Diagram

- **Definition:** A scale plan of the work area on which a thread/string is run to trace the exact path of a worker or material.
- **Why used:** To study back-tracking, cross-traffic, and unnecessary movement; helps redesign layout.

### 2.1.7 Travel Chart

- **Definition:** A matrix in which rows and columns represent work centres/machines, and entries show the number of trips (or volume moved) between any pair.
- **Why used:** To optimize inter-departmental material flow; basis for *From-To* analysis and plant layout decisions.

### 2.1.8 Pareto Chart

- **Definition:** A dual chart combining a **descending bar chart** (frequency) with a **cumulative percentage line** (cumulative frequency).
- **Underlying Principle (Pareto's 80/20 Rule):** Roughly 80% of the problems are caused by 20% of the causes.
- **Steps to construct:**
  1. List causes in descending order of frequency.
  2. Compute cumulative frequency.
  3. Plot bars (left Y-axis) and cumulative % line (right Y-axis).
  4. Identify the "vital few" up to ~80%.

### 2.1.9 Gantt Chart

- **Definition:** A horizontal bar chart where each bar represents a *task* and its length represents its *duration* against a time scale.
- **Why used:** Project scheduling, machine loading, production planning, progress reporting.
- **Key feature:** Bars for *planned* vs. *actual* progress are often superimposed with shading.

### 2.1.10 Control Chart (Statistical Process Control)

- **Definition:** A time-series chart with three horizontal lines — **Upper Control Limit (UCL)**, **Central Line (CL)**, and **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** — based on ±3σ.
- **Purpose:** Distinguishes *common-cause* (inherent) variation from *special-cause* (assignable) variation.

### 2.1.11 Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa / Fishbone) Diagram

- **Structure:** A horizontal "spine" with branches categorized under the **6M's** of industry: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment).

### 2.1.12 Histogram vs. Bar Chart — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | Histogram | Bar Chart |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| X-axis | Continuous numerical intervals (bins) | Discrete categories |
| Bars | Touch each other (no gap) | Gaps between bars |
| Purpose | Distribution of variation | Comparison of magnitudes |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Chart / Concept | Key Formula / Rule | Typical Use |
|---|----------------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Pie Chart sector angle | $\theta = \dfrac{\text{Component Value}}{\text{Total}} \times 360^\circ$ | Converting % to angle |
| 2 | Pareto Cumulative % | $\text{Cum.\%}_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j}{N} \times 100$ | Building Pareto line |
| 3 | Control Chart UCL | $UCL = \bar{X} + 3\sigma$ (for $\bar{X}$-chart) | SQC |
| 4 | Control Chart LCL | $LCL = \bar{X} - 3\sigma$ | SQC |
| 5 | R-chart UCL | $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | SQC (range) |
| 6 | R-chart LCL | $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | SQC (range) |
| 7 | SIMO Chart time scale | $\text{1 sec} = 0.1\ \text{cm}$ (typical) | Micro-motion study |
| 8 | String Diagram scale | $1\ \text{cm} = 1\ \text{m}$ (typical) | Layout analysis |
| 9 | % Idle Time (Man-Machine) | $\%\ Idle = \dfrac{\text{Idle Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100$ | Balancing |
| 10 | Bar Chart scaling | $Y\text{-axis: 1 cm} = k\ \text{units}$ (choose k for clear plot) | Visual clarity |
| 11 | Mean (for X-bar) | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum X_i}{n}$ | Central tendency |
| 12 | Std. Deviation (sample) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ | Process variation |
| 13 | Gantt Slack | $\text{Slack} = LS - ES$ | Project scheduling |
| 14 | From-To Trip Count | $T_{ij} = \sum$ trips from machine $i$ to machine $j$ | Travel chart |
| 15 | Process Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100$ | Lean IE |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary / Special Conditions**
> - For *R-chart*: $D_3 = 0$ for $n \le 6$; $D_4 = 3.267$ for $n = 5$.
> - For *p-chart* (defective items): $UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$.
> - For *c-chart* (defects/unit): $UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$.

## 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Toyota Production System** uses *Yamazumi charts* (a stacked-bar variant of man-machine charts) to balance operator cycles.
- **Hospitals** use *flow process charts* to reduce patient waiting time (Lean Healthcare).
- **Software firms** use *Ishikawa diagrams* during post-mortem meetings to identify the root cause of production bugs.
- **Construction industry** uses *Gantt charts* (MS Project, Primavera) for civil project scheduling.
- **Six Sigma Black Belts** use *Pareto + Ishikawa + Control Charts* as the **Magnificent Seven** IE tools.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Core Computation Steps

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Pie Chart Sector Angle

**Statement:** Given component values $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n$ with total $T = \sum v_i$, derive the sector angle $\theta_i$ for the $i^{th}$ component.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Angle of full circle} &= 360^\circ \\
\text{Angle per unit value} &= \frac{360^\circ}{T} \\
\text{Therefore, angle of component } i &= \frac{v_i}{T} \times 360^\circ \\
\therefore \theta_i &= \frac{v_i}{T} \times 360^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

*Data:* Factory expenses — Materials ₹4,00,000; Labour ₹2,00,000; Overheads ₹2,00,000; Profit ₹2,00,000 (Total = ₹10,00,000).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{Materials}} &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 360^\circ = 0.4 \times 360^\circ = 144^\circ \\
\theta_{\text{Labour}} &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 360^\circ = 0.2 \times 360^\circ = 72^\circ \\
\theta_{\text{Overheads}} &= 72^\circ \\
\theta_{\text{Profit}} &= 72^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $144 + 72 + 72 + 72 = 360^\circ$ ✓

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Control Chart Limits (X-bar Chart)

**Assumption:** Quality characteristic $X$ follows a Normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. Samples of size $n$ are drawn; their means $\bar{X}$ follow $N(\mu, \sigma/\sqrt{n})$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Central Line: } CL &= \bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k} \\
UCL &= \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\
LCL &= \bar{\bar{X}} - 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $\sigma$ is unknown, it is estimated from the average range $\bar{R}$:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}
$$

where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant (e.g., $d_2 = 2.326$ for $n = 5$). The control limits then become:

$$
UCL = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad LCL = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
$$

with $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Pareto Cumulative Percentage

**Step-by-step transition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frequency of cause } i &= f_i \\
\text{Total observations } N &= \sum_{j=1}^{m} f_j \\
\text{Relative frequency of } i &= r_i = \frac{f_i}{N} \\
\text{Cumulative frequency up to } i &= F_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j \\
\text{Cumulative percentage up to } i &= P_i = \frac{F_i}{N} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example (5 defect types):**

| Defect | $f_i$ | $F_i$ | $P_i$ (%) |
|--------|-------|-------|-----------|
| A | 42 | 42 | 52.5 |
| B | 18 | 60 | 75.0 |
| C | 10 | 70 | 87.5 |
| D | 6 | 76 | 95.0 |
| E | 4 | 80 | 100.0 |
| **Total** | **80** | — | — |

*Interpretation:* Defects A + B together account for **75%** — these are the "vital few".

### 3.1.4 Derivation of Process Efficiency from a Flow Process Chart

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= \sum(\text{Operation} + \text{Inspection} + \text{Transport} + \text{Delay} + \text{Storage}) \\
T_{\text{value-added}} &= \sum(\text{Operation time}) \\
\eta &= \frac{T_{\text{value-added}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Chart

| Problem Type | Best Chart |
|--------------|------------|
| Compare 5 departments' output | Bar chart |
| Show % break-up of costs | Pie chart |
| Trend of sales 2018–2024 | Line graph |
| Frequency of defect sizes | Histogram |
| Find root cause of a problem | Ishikawa + Pareto |
| Schedule a project | Gantt chart |
| Monitor machine setting variation | Control chart |
| Study operator–machine balance | Man-machine chart |
| Redesign hand-motion sequence | SIMO chart |
| Trace worker path in shop | String diagram |
| Plan inter-move frequency | Travel chart |
| Show reporting structure | Organization chart |
| Correlation between temp & rejection | Scatter diagram |
| Two-variable trend overlay | Multiple bar / line chart |
| Time-share of 6M's of a problem | Pie chart |

## 3.2 Mini Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A machine produces components in batches. The number of defects observed in 6 samples of size 5 each is: 2, 0, 3, 1, 4, 2. Construct an $np$-chart and decide if the process is in control. (Given $n = 5$, $A_2$ is not needed; $3\sigma_{np} = 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$.) *(Part-A illustrative, not full marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{np} &= \frac{2+0+3+1+4+2}{6} = \frac{12}{6} = 2 \\
\bar{p} &= \frac{12}{6 \times 5} = 0.4 \\
\sigma_{np} &= \sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} = \sqrt{5 \times 0.4 \times 0.6} = \sqrt{1.2} = 1.095 \\
UCL &= 2 + 3(1.095) = 5.29 \\
LCL &= 2 - 3(1.095) = -1.29 \to 0 \\
\text{Since no } np_i \text{ exceeds 5.29, process is IN CONTROL.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU Part-A (3-mark) numerical questions on **Pie chart angle** and **Pareto cumulative %** are extremely common (≥ 90% probability of appearance in any Module-1 IE paper). Master them.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Charts and Diagrams

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Charts and Diagrams in IE] --> B[Display Charts]
    A --> C[Process and Motion Charts]
    A --> D[Project and Control Charts]
    A --> E[Analysis Charts]

    B --> B1[Bar Chart]
    B --> B2[Pie Chart]
    B --> B3[Line Graph]
    B --> B4[Histogram]
    B --> B5[Scatter Diagram]

    C --> C1[Flow Process Chart]
    C --> C2[Man-Machine Chart]
    C --> C3[SIMO Chart]
    C --> C4[String Diagram]
    C --> C5[Travel Chart]

    D --> D1[Gantt Chart]
    D --> D2[Control Chart]
    D --> D3[Organization Chart]

    E --> E1[Pareto Chart]
    E --> E2[Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram]
    E --> E3[Cycle Graph and Chronocyclegraph]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b2a47,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Charts and IE Functions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RawData[Raw Production Data] -->|Feed| ChartEngine[Charts and Diagrams]
    ChartEngine -->|Analyse| WorkStudy[Work Study and Method Study]
    ChartEngine -->|Schedule| ProjectMgmt[Project Management]
    ChartEngine -->|Monitor| QualityControl[Statistical Quality Control]
    ChartEngine -->|Optimize| Layout[Plant Layout and Design]
    ChartEngine -->|Communicate| Management[Top Management Decisions]
    WorkStudy -->|Improve| Productivity
    ProjectMgmt -->|Deliver| Productivity
    QualityControl -->|Reduce Defects| Productivity
    Layout -->|Reduce Movement| Productivity
    Management -->|Strategic Plan| Productivity
    Productivity --> Output[Higher Productivity and Profit]
    style ChartEngine fill:#ff6f00,color:#ffffff,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:3px
    style Productivity fill:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Man-Machine Chart — Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start of Cycle]) --> Op1[Operator loads blank on Machine A]
    Op1 --> M1Run[Machine A machines for 4 min]
    M1Run --> OpIdle1[Operator idle 4 min]
    OpIdle1 --> Op2[Operator unloads A, loads Machine B]
    Op2 --> M1Stop[Machine A idle 1 min]
    M1Stop --> M2Run[Machine B runs 5 min]
    M2Run --> OpIdle2[Operator idle 1 min during M2 run]
    OpIdle2 --> UnloadB[Operator unloads B, inspects]
    UnloadB --> End([End of Cycle Total 10 min])
    style M1Run fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff
    style M2Run fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style OpIdle1 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style OpIdle2 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading of the chart:**
- Machine A utilisation = (4+1+0)/10 = need recompute ⇒ (4 min active + 1 min idle + 5 min waiting for B to finish) → in 10-min cycle, A works 4 min, idle 6 min.
- Operator works 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4 min approx, idle 6 min.
- **Improvement:** Assign operator a second Machine C to fill idle time → *one-operator, two-machine* balanced chart.

## 4.4 Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram — Block Schematic (6M's)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Head[Problem: High Rejection Rate] --> SpineMain[Cause and Effect Spine]
    SpineMain --> M1[Man: skill fatigue training]
    SpineMain --> M2[Machine: wear calibration vibration]
    SpineMain --> M3[Material: hardness variability supplier]
    SpineMain --> M4[Method: speed feed incorrect sequence]
    SpineMain --> M5[Measurement: gauge error sampling]
    SpineMain --> M6[Environment: temperature humidity lighting]
    style Head fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f0000,stroke-width:2px
    style SpineMain fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.5 Gantt Chart — Block Schematic of a 5-Task Project

```mermaid
gantt
    title Gantt Chart for Module1 Project Tasks
    dateFormat  YYYY-MM-DD
    axisFormat  %m-%d
    section Planning
    Task1 Requirements Analysis    :a1, 2025-01-01, 5d
    section Design
    Task2 System Design           :a2, after a1, 4d
    section Implementation
    Task3 Coding Phase 1          :a3, after a2, 6d
    Task4 Coding Phase 2          :a4, after a3, 5d
    section Closure
    Task5 Testing and Delivery    :a5, after a4, 4d
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading hint for KTU 2024:** In a Gantt question, students are usually asked to compute *total project duration* and identify the *critical path*. Here, duration = 5 + 4 + 6 + 5 + 4 = **24 days**; the path through all 5 tasks is critical.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern Followed:**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - **Part B:** 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice a OR b sub-parts) = 14 marks
> - **Module-1 share:** ~20–25% of total marks

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Flow Process Chart. List its five symbols. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Flow Process Chart is a diagrammatic representation of all activities that take place during a process, using standardized symbols. It helps in identifying delays, transport, and unnecessary operations, and is a fundamental tool of **Method Study** in Work Study.

**Five Symbols:**

| Symbol | Shape | Meaning |
|--------|-------|---------|
| Operation | ● | Produces/achieves something |
| Inspection | ◐ | Checks quality/quantity |
| Transport | → | Material is moved |
| Delay | D | Material waits |
| Storage | ▽ | Material held in store |

(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the 5-symbol table — examiners prefer a neat tabular form.)

---

### Q2. What is a Pareto Chart? State the 80/20 principle. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Pareto Chart is a dual graph that combines a **descending bar chart** of defect frequencies with a **cumulative percentage curve** on a second Y-axis.

**80/20 Principle (Pareto Principle):** Roughly **80% of the problems** (effects) are caused by only **20% of the causes**. Hence, the longest bars on the left (the "vital few") should be tackled first to obtain the maximum benefit.

(1 mark definition + 1 mark 80/20 statement + 1 mark example/utility.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the procedure for constructing a **Pareto Chart** with a suitable industrial example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between a **Bar Chart** and a **Histogram**. When would you prefer a Pie Chart over a Bar Chart? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** A Pareto Chart combines a descending bar chart and a cumulative % curve. It is used in Quality Control to identify the "vital few" causes that produce most of the defects.

**Step 2 — Procedure to Construct (3 marks):**
1. Collect data on the type and frequency of defects over a defined period.
2. Arrange the causes in **descending order** of frequency.
3. Calculate the **cumulative frequency** and **cumulative percentage**.
4. Draw the X-axis with cause categories.
5. Draw the Y-axis (left) for frequency; right Y-axis for cumulative %.
6. Plot bars for each cause.
7. Plot the cumulative % line graph.
8. Identify the "vital few" causes up to 80% cumulative.

**Step 3 — Example (2 marks):**

| Cause | Frequency | Cumulative | Cum. % |
|-------|-----------|------------|--------|
| Burrs | 45 | 45 | 56.25 |
| Scratches | 25 | 70 | 87.50 |
| Dents | 6 | 76 | 95.00 |
| Cracks | 3 | 79 | 98.75 |
| Others | 1 | 80 | 100.00 |

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):** Burrs + Scratches (top 2 of 5 causes = 40%) account for 87.5% of defects → "vital few". Focus improvement efforts here.

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Differentiation Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | Bar Chart | Histogram |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Data type | Discrete/categorical | Continuous numerical |
| X-axis | Categories (e.g., Dept A, B, C) | Numerical intervals (e.g., 10–20, 20–30) |
| Bars | Gaps between bars | Bars touch each other |
| Order of bars | Can be rearranged | Cannot be rearranged |
| Purpose | Compare values | Show distribution shape |

**When Pie Chart is preferred over Bar Chart (2 marks):**
- When showing **composition / parts of a whole** (e.g., % of total cost, time distribution across activities).
- When there are **5 or fewer categories** (more makes the pie unreadable).
- When emphasizing **percentage share** is more important than absolute comparison.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** What is a **Man-Machine Chart**? With a neat sketch, explain how it is used to balance the workload between an operator and a machine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and uses of a **String Diagram** and a **Travel Chart** in plant layout studies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Definition (2 marks):** A Man-Machine Chart is a two-line graphical representation plotting, against a common time scale, the working and idle periods of an operator (top line) and a machine (bottom line) during a complete work cycle. It is a key tool in **Method Study** for eliminating idle time.

**Sketch (3 marks — examiner insists on a sketch):**

```
Time → 0    1    2    3    4    5    6    7    8   (min)
MAN     |████|    |████|             |████|    |████|
        Work     Work              Work      Work
MACHINE |        |──────────|         |        |──────|
        Idle    Machining          Idle    Machining
```

**Workload Balancing Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Construct the chart for the existing one-worker, one-machine operation.
2. Identify the **idle period of the operator** (e.g., 4–6 min in the sketch).
3. Reassign a second machine (or auxiliary task) to fill this idle time.
4. Construct a new chart — *one-worker, two-machine* — to verify reduced idle time.
5. Iterate until idle time is minimized.

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**String Diagram (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A scale drawing of the work area on which a thread is laid to trace the path of a worker or material.
- **Construction:** Use a scale of 1 cm = 1 m (typical). Mark work centres, machines, and storage. Pin a thread at the start point and trace the actual path, marking each change of direction with a pin.
- **Uses:** Identifies backtracking, cross-traffic, long travel distances; helps propose a **better layout** that reduces total distance travelled.

**Travel Chart (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A matrix in which rows and columns represent work centres and entries show the **number of trips** between each pair.
- **Construction:** List all machines/work centres on both axes. Count and enter the trips between each pair.
- **Uses:** Highlights the most frequent inter-moves, guides decisions on *adjacency* in **plant layout**, supports **From-To analysis**, reduces material handling cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the diagram** (you can lose 4–6 marks out of 14 even with a perfect description).
> 2. **Not labelling axes and time scale** in Gantt / Man-Machine charts — examiner awards 0 for an unlabelled sketch.
> 3. **Confusing histogram with bar chart** — this is a *favourite trap* question. Memorize the difference (continuous vs. discrete; bars touch vs. bars have gaps).
> 4. **Forgetting LCL = max(0, computed value)** for control charts with low $n$ — negative LCL must be reported as 0.
> 5. **Not stating the 6M's** in an Ishikawa question — partial marks will be cut.
> 6. **Pie chart angle not summing to 360°** — always verify.
> 7. **Writing 18 therbligs** for SIMO chart but **only 5 process symbols** for flow process chart — mixing them up is a common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize Before Exam)**

- ☐ **Flow Process Chart** has **5 symbols**: O, I, →, D, ▽.
- ☐ **SIMO Chart** has **18 therbligs** (Gilbreth).
- ☐ **Pie Chart** angle = $(v_i / T) \times 360°$.
- ☐ **Pareto Chart** = Bars (descending) + Cumulative % line on second Y-axis.
- ☐ **80/20 Rule**: 80% problems from 20% causes.
- ☐ **Gantt Chart** = Horizontal bars on a time scale (scheduling).
- ☐ **Control Chart** = $\bar{X} \pm 3\sigma$ (3-sigma control limits).
- ☐ **Ishikawa (Fishbone)** = 6M's: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature.
- ☐ **Histogram** = continuous data, bars touch, no rearranging.
- ☐ **Bar Chart** = discrete categories, gaps, can rearrange.
- ☐ **Man-Machine Chart** = 2 parallel lines (man & machine) vs. time.
- ☐ **String Diagram** = scale plan + thread (path tracing).
- ☐ **Travel Chart** = matrix of trips between work centres.
- ☐ **Organization Chart** = reporting hierarchy (vertical/horizontal).
- ☐ **Scatter Diagram** = correlation study between 2 variables.
- ☐ **Cycle Graph / Chronocyclegraph** = motion study with camera + light.
- ☐ **Always label axes, units, and time scale** in every diagram.
- ☐ **Use a ruler and pencil** for all sketches — first impression matters.
- ☐ **Total of pie angles must equal 360°** (verification step).
- ☐ **Process is in control** only when all points lie within UCL & LCL on a control chart.
- ☐ **Aim of every chart**: reduce waste, improve communication, support decision-making.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** In KTU 2024 14-mark Part-B questions on "Use of Charts and Diagrams", the safest strategy is — **(i)** write a precise 3–4 line definition, **(ii)** draw a neat labelled diagram (3–5 marks reserved), **(iii)** list 4–6 key advantages / uses, and **(iv)** if numerical, show the full derivation. This guarantees a minimum of 10–11 marks even if the examiner is strict.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Rating and Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Performance Rating and Methods ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Performance Rating (PR)** is a **qualitative judgmental technique** used in Time Study under Work Measurement to evaluate the **effective speed of a qualified worker** against the **concept of "normal" performance**, and to express this evaluation as a numerical **rating factor** (or percentage) that converts the *Observed Time* into the *Normal Time*. In simpler KTU-board-friendly language: $$\text{Normal Time (NT)} = \text{Observed Time (OT)} \times \text{Performance Rating Factor (RF)}$$ where the **Rating Factor** is expressed as a decimal (e.g., 1.10 for 110%) or a percentage (e.g., 110%). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Marathon Referee:** Imagine a referee timing a 100 m sprint. A casual stopwatch gives the *raw* time the runner actually took. But a trained referee also looks at *how hard* the runner was pushing — was the runner jogging, sprinting, or barely moving? The referee mentally "adjusts" the stopwatch to what the time *would have been* if the runner was running at a "standard" competitive pace. **Performance Rating is exactly this mental adjustment** in time study — the stopwatch gives Observed Time, the IE (Industrial Engineer) gives the Rating, and the product is the Normal Time. ### 1.3 Why Rating is Necessary > [!TIP] > Two workers performing the **same operation** will almost never take **exactly the same observed time** because human effort, skill, motivation, and working conditions differ. To get a **fair, standardised, and comparable** work content (Normal Time), we must neutralise these personal/environmental variations. This neutralisation IS performance rating. ### 1.4 Standard Benchmarks Used (KTU Must-Know) | Benchmark | Pace Equivalence | Rating Factor | |---|---|---| | **Normal Pace** (BS 3138: 1979) | A person walking **75 m/min** without load | **1.00 (100%)** | | Standard Performance | A qualified worker working at **steady pace** under supervision | **1.00 (100%)** | | Fast Pace | Worker moving **briskly** but without strain | **1.20 – 1.33** | | Slow Pace | Worker sluggish, deliberate | **0.50 – 0.80** | > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners specifically test: *"Define normal pace as a person walking 75 m/min carrying a 1 kg load, returning empty, and covering 30 m in 23 seconds."* Memorise this verbal description. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Conceptual Logic Flow (Step-Wise) 1. **The Time-Study Stopwatch gives Observed Time (OT).** - This is raw data — depends entirely on who worked and how fast they worked. 2. **The Industrial Engineer (IE) observes the worker's pace live.** - Skill of the worker, effort exerted, conditions, and consistency are all noted. 3. **The IE assigns a Rating Factor (RF).** - RF is a multiplier that "normalises" the pace to the standard 100% benchmark. 4. **Normal Time (NT) is computed.** - $NT = OT \times RF$ - NT represents the time a *qualified worker* would take at *normal pace* under *standard conditions*. 5. **Allowances are added to get Standard Time (ST).** - $ST = NT \times (1 + \text{Allowance factor})$ - Allowances cover personal needs, fatigue, and contingencies. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **every high-yield formula** for this topic. **Memorise these** — they appear in almost every KTU ESE question. | # | Concept | Formula | Symbols / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Normal Time | $NT = OT \times RF$ | $OT$ in min/s, $RF$ dimensionless | | 2 | Basic Time | $BT = OT \times RF \times \text{Performance Index}$ | Sometimes used interchangeably with NT | | 3 | Standard Time | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ | $A$ = total allowance as decimal | | 4 | Standard Time (alt) | $ST = NT + \text{Absolute Allowance}$ | Absolute allowance in same time unit | | 5 | Rating on Speed | $RF_{speed} = \dfrac{\text{Observed Speed}}{\text{Standard Speed}}$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | Rating on Effort | $RF_{effort} = \dfrac{\text{Observed Effort}}{\text{Standard Effort}}$ | Dimensionless | | 7 | Westinghouse RF | $RF = 1 + S + E + C + I$ | $S,E,C,I$ are skill/effort/conditions/consistency numerics | | 8 | Output Method | $RF = \dfrac{\text{Standard Output}}{\text{Observed Output}} \times 100\%$ | Dimensionless | | 9 | Allowance Factor | $A = P + F + C$ | $P$=personal, $F$=fatigue, $C$=contingency | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Safety Rule Applied:** No vertical pipes inside table cells — all absolute value / normalisation uses $\vert \cdot \vert$ or "divided by" notation. This preserves table rendering on KTU's online portal. ### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Cost Estimation & Quoting:** Standard Time is the foundation of piece-rate wages, labour costing, and tender pricing. - **Production Planning:** Standard Time × Batch Size = Routing Lead Time for an operation. - **Workforce Balancing:** Standard Times across stations are used to balance assembly lines. - **Incentive Wage Systems (Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux, Taylor):** All require NT and ST as the **base reference** — and both depend on correct Rating. - **Lean & Six Sigma:** Cycle-time standards in *takt-time* calculations trace back to a rated time study. ### 2.4 The Six Major Performance Rating Methods > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU boards (Module 1, Work Study chapter) **expect** students to know the following six methods and be able to *contrast* them: 1. **Speed Rating** (or Pace Rating) — uses worker's speed of limb/arm movement vs. standard. 2. **Effort Rating** — uses depth of worker's exertion (mental/physical) vs. standard. 3. **Westinghouse System of Rating** — analytical, 4-factor (Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency). 4. **Objective Rating** (Lowry–Maynard–Stegemerten) — uses a 100 = normal speed scale. 5. **Synthetic Rating** — compares the operation with previously rated standard jobs. 6. **Activity Rating** — measures pace against a "synthetic" measure of effort/activity. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Master Derivation: From Stopwatch Reading to Standard Time > [!NOTE] > The following **six-line derivation** is the *single most asked* chain in KTU Work Study problems. Practice it until you can write it without thinking. **Step 1 — Define the Observed Time.** $$OT = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i \;/\; n$$ where $T_i$ is the i-th stopwatch reading of the operation and $n$ is the number of cycles timed. This is the **raw average time** the worker actually took. **Step 2 — Apply the Rating Factor (the "normalisation" step).** $$NT = OT \times RF$$ The IE multiplies the raw time by a rating factor that represents the worker's pace relative to standard. This **strips out** the worker's personal speed/skill effect and gives the *time a standard worker would take*. **Step 3 — Convert the Rating Factor to its decimal form for calculation.** $$RF_{decimal} = \dfrac{RF_{percentage}}{100}$$ If the IE rates the worker at 110%, then $RF = 1.10$. If at 80%, then $RF = 0.80$. **Step 4 — Apply the total allowance factor.** $$A = P + F + C$$ where $P$ = personal allowance (typically 5–12%), $F$ = fatigue allowance (4–25%), $C$ = contingency (1–10%). Standard $A$ in KTU examples is often 0.20 (20%). **Step 5 — Compute Standard Time using the multiplicative form.** $$ST = NT \times (1 + A)$$ **Step 6 — Compute Standard Time using the additive (absolute) form.** $$ST = NT + (NT \times A) = NT + \text{Absolute Allowance}$$ These two forms are **algebraically identical**; pick whichever is convenient for the problem. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 (Basic) > [!TIP] > **Problem:** An operation is timed 5 times with a stopwatch. The recorded times (in minutes) are: 0.50, 0.52, 0.48, 0.51, 0.49. The IE rates the worker's performance at 110%. Personal allowance = 5%, Fatigue allowance = 10%, Contingency = 5%. Compute Standard Time. **Step 1 — Compute Observed Time (OT).** $$OT = \frac{0.50 + 0.52 + 0.48 + 0.51 + 0.49}{5} = \frac{2.50}{5} = 0.50 \;\text{min}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Rating Factor (RF = 1.10).** $$NT = 0.50 \times 1.10 = 0.55 \;\text{min}$$ **Step 3 — Sum Allowances.** $$A = 0.05 + 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.20 \;(20\%)$$ **Step 4 — Compute Standard Time (multiplicative).** $$ST = 0.55 \times (1 + 0.20) = 0.55 \times 1.20 = 0.66 \;\text{min per cycle}$$ **Step 5 — Verify (additive form).** $$ST = 0.55 + (0.55 \times 0.20) = 0.55 + 0.11 = 0.66 \;\text{min} \;\checkmark$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 (Westinghouse Rating System) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** During time study, the IE assigns: Skill = B2 (worth −0.04), Effort = C1 (worth +0.02), Conditions = D (worth 0.00), Consistency = 1 (worth +0.01). OT = 1.20 min. Allowance = 15%. Compute ST. **Step 1 — Westinghouse Rating Factor.** $$RF = 1 + S + E + C + I = 1 + (-0.04) + 0.02 + 0.00 + 0.01 = 0.99$$ **Step 2 — Normal Time.** $$NT = 1.20 \times 0.99 = 1.188 \;\text{min}$$ **Step 3 — Standard Time.** $$ST = 1.188 \times 1.15 = 1.3662 \;\text{min}$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Rating Methods > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **examination-ready**. KTU frequently asks for a "Compare any two rating methods" question worth 7 marks. | Aspect | Speed Rating | Effort Rating | Westinghouse | Objective (LMS) | Synthetic | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Basis** | Speed of movement | Depth of effort | 4 numerical sub-factors | Speed of motion | Comparison to rated jobs | | **Subjective?** | Highly | Highly | Partly (numerical) | Mostly objective | Objective | | **Rater Bias** | High | High | Lower | Low | Lowest | | **Experience Needed** | High | High | Moderate | Moderate | Very high | | **Best For** | Repetitive manual work | Variable-effort work | Mixed industrial jobs | Repetitive, visible work | New or non-standard jobs | | **Numerical Output** | % of standard speed | % of standard effort | 1 + sum of factors | Speed number | Match with prior job rating | | **Time Required** | Quick | Quick | Moderate (table lookup) | Moderate | Quick (if library exists) | | **KTU Frequency** | High | High | Very High | Moderate | Moderate | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Flow Diagram: Stopwatch-to-Standard-Time Pipeline > [!TIP] > The Mermaid diagram below maps the **complete time-study data flow**, showing exactly where Performance Rating intervenes. The graph is purely node-and-edge so it renders cleanly on the KTU portal and inside any Markdown viewer. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Qualified Worker
on Assigned Job] --> B[Industrial Engineer
starts Stopwatch] B --> C[Record Multiple
Observed Times T1 T2 T3 ... Tn] C --> D[Compute Average
Observed Time OT] D --> E[IE Observes Worker Pace
Skill Effort Conditions Consistency] E --> F{Choose Rating
Method} F --> G1[Speed Rating] F --> G2[Effort Rating] F --> G3[Westinghouse
System] F --> G4[Objective Rating
LMS] F --> G5[Synthetic Rating] G1 --> H[Rating Factor RF] G2 --> H G3 --> H G4 --> H G5 --> H H --> I[Normal Time
NT equals OT times RF] I --> J[Add Personal Fatigue
Contingency Allowances] J --> K[Standard Time ST
Final Output] K --> L[Costing Wages Planning
Line Balancing Incentives] ``` ### 4.2 Block Architecture: Westinghouse Four-Factor Rating > [!NOTE] > This block diagram decomposes the Westinghouse system into its four constituents, each contributing a numerical adjustment to the base rating of **1.00**. ```mermaid flowchart LR BASE[Base Rating equals 1.00] --> S[Skill Factor S
6 classes A1 to A6] BASE --> E[Effort Factor E
6 classes B1 to B6] BASE --> C[Conditions Factor C
4 classes D1 to D4] BASE --> I[Consistency Factor I
3 classes] S --> SUM[Total RF equals
1 plus S plus E plus C plus I] E --> SUM C --> SUM I --> SUM SUM --> OUT[Apply to OT
NT equals OT times RF] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree: Which Rating Method to Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD START[New Time Study
Required] --> Q1{Is there a
rated job library?} Q1 -- Yes --> SYNTH[Use Synthetic Rating] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is motion
clearly visible?} Q2 -- Yes --> OBJ[Use Objective Rating
LMS Scale] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Repetitive
manual work?} Q3 -- Yes --> SPD[Use Speed Rating] Q3 -- No --> Q4{Need detailed
audit trail?} Q4 -- Yes --> WEST[Use Westinghouse
System] Q4 -- No --> EFF[Use Effort Rating] ``` ### 4.4 Performance Rating Position in Work Study Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB WS[Work Study] --> MS[Method Study] WS --> WM[Work Measurement] WM --> TS[Time Study] WM --> PWS[Predetermined
Motion Time Systems] WM --> WS2[Work Sampling] TS --> ST1[Stopwatch
Procedure] TS --> ST2[Performance
Rating] ST1 --> ST3[Observed Time] ST2 --> ST3 ST3 --> ST4[Normal Time] ST4 --> ST5[Add Allowances] ST5 --> ST6[Standard Time] ``` ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the term **"Normal Pace"** as used in performance rating. What is its standard walking equivalent? > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Normal pace is the speed of movement at which a **qualified worker** performs a task under **standard conditions** without overexertion, corresponding to a rating factor of **1.00 (100%)**. It is physically equivalent to a person **walking 75 m per minute** (or 4.5 km/h) on level ground without load. This benchmark is the foundation on which all rating factors are calibrated. > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2022]** List **any three** allowances that are added to Normal Time to obtain Standard Time. > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > (i) **Personal allowance** — for personal needs such as drinking water, restroom use, washing; typical value **5%–12%**. > (ii) **Fatigue allowance** — to compensate for physiological and psychological tiredness; typical value **4%–25%**, basic 4% plus variable. > (iii) **Contingency allowance** — for unforeseen delays, minor breakdowns, irregular supplies; typical value **1%–10%**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, KTU Internal Choice Pattern) > **Q. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Q.2]** > **(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **Westinghouse System of Performance Rating**, listing all four sub-factors, their classes, and how the rating factor is computed. > > **(b) [7 marks]** A worker is observed on a machining operation for 6 cycles. The stopwatch readings (in min) are: 1.40, 1.32, 1.45, 1.38, 1.35, 1.42. The IE assigns a rating of 120% using the **Objective Rating** method. Total allowance is 18%. Compute the **Standard Time per piece** and the **Standard Output per 8-hour shift**. --- ### 5.3 Model Solution — Part (a): Westinghouse System > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 mark):** The Westinghouse System is an **analytical rating method** developed by the Westinghouse Electric Corporation. Instead of giving one overall rating, the IE evaluates **four separate sub-factors** and combines them arithmetically. **The Four Sub-Factors:** **(i) Skill (S) — 6 Classes (1 mark):** | Class | Description | Adjustment | |---|---|---| | A1 | Superskill | +0.15 | | A2 | Excellent | +0.11 | | A3 | Good | +0.06 | | B1 | Average | +0.03 | | B2 | Fair | 0.00 | | C1 | Poor | −0.05 | **(ii) Effort (E) — 6 Classes (1 mark):** | Class | Description | Adjustment | |---|---|---| | A1 | Excessive | +0.13 | | A2 | Excellent | +0.10 | | A3 | Good | +0.05 | | B1 | Average | +0.02 | | B2 | Fair | 0.00 | | C1 | Poor | −0.04 | **(iii) Conditions (C) — 4 Classes (1 mark):** | Class | Description | Adjustment | |---|---|---| | A | Ideal | +0.06 | | B | Excellent | +0.02 | | C | Good | 0.00 | | D | Fair | −0.03 | **(iv) Consistency (I) — 3 Classes (1 mark):** | Class | Description | Adjustment | |---|---|---| | A | Excellent | +0.04 | | B | Average | 0.00 | | C | Poor | −0.04 | **Final Rating Factor Formula (1 mark):** $$RF = 1 + S + E + C + I$$ **Significance (1 mark):** The system minimises rater bias by forcing the IE to evaluate four independent dimensions rather than one holistic impression. It is **semi-objective** and suitable for shop-floor documentation and audit. --- ### 5.4 Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical Computation **Step 1 — Compute Observed Time.** $$OT = \frac{1.40 + 1.32 + 1.45 + 1.38 + 1.35 + 1.42}{6}$$ $$OT = \frac{8.32}{6} = 1.3867 \;\text{min}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Rating Factor (120% → 1.20).** $$NT = 1.3867 \times 1.20 = 1.6640 \;\text{min}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Total Allowance (18%).** $$ST = 1.6640 \times (1 + 0.18) = 1.6640 \times 1.18$$ $$ST = 1.9635 \;\text{min per piece}$$ **Step 4 — Standard Output per 8-Hour Shift.** $$\text{Shift duration} = 8 \times 60 = 480 \;\text{min}$$ $$\text{Output} = \frac{480}{1.9635} \approx 244.5 \;\text{pieces per shift}$$ Rounding to a whole piece: **244 pieces per 8-hour shift**. --- ### 5.5 Alternative Internal-Choice Question (14 Marks) > **Q. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 marks]** > **(a) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **Speed Rating** and **Effort Rating** methods. Under what conditions would you prefer one over the other? > > **(b) [7 marks]** During a time study, 4 readings of a drilling operation are: 0.85, 0.90, 0.88, 0.92 min. The IE rates the worker as 90% (Speed Rating). Personal = 5%, Fatigue = 10%, Contingency = 5%. Determine the **Standard Time** and the **time saved per piece** compared to the observed time. **Quick Model Solution Outline:** - $OT = 0.8875$ min, $RF = 0.90$, $NT = 0.7988$ min, $A = 0.20$, $ST = 0.9585$ min/piece. - "Time saved" = $OT - ST = 0.8875 - 0.9585 = -0.071$ min (i.e., standard is **slower** because allowance is added and rating is below 100%). - Discuss: in a sub-100% rating scenario, Standard Time is *greater* than Observed Time because allowances are added and the time is normalised to standard pace. --- ### 5.6 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake 1 — Forgetting to add allowances.** > Many students stop at Normal Time. **Standard Time = Normal Time + Allowances.** If you miss the allowance step, you lose **3–4 marks** instantly. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake 2 — Rating as percentage vs. decimal.** > If the IE says "110%", you must write $RF = 1.10$, NOT 110. Using 110 in $NT = OT \times RF$ gives a wildly wrong answer. **Always divide percentage by 100.** > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake 3 — Confusing Speed Rating with Effort Rating definitions.** > Speed Rating judges **how fast** the limbs move. Effort Rating judges **how much exertion/depth of work** is going on. They are **not the same** even though both yield an RF. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake 4 — Westinghouse table memorisation slip.** > KTU's "Compare Westinghouse with Objective Rating" question requires **specific class numbers** (Skill: 6 classes, Effort: 6 classes, Conditions: 4, Consistency: 3). If you write "4 sub-factors" but skip the class counts, you lose 2 marks. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake 5 — Confusing Normal Time with Basic Time.** > In the **"with rest"** time study, $BT = OT \times RF$ and $NT = BT \times (1 + \text{Personal Allowance})$. In the **"continuous"** time study, Personal Allowance is not added between Normal and Basic. **Read the question carefully.** --- ### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorise These Bullets:** - **Definition:** Performance Rating is the IE's **judgement of worker pace** vs. standard pace, expressed as a numerical **Rating Factor (RF)**. - **Standard Benchmark:** Normal pace = **100% (RF = 1.00)** = walking **75 m/min**. - **Core Equation 1:** $NT = OT \times RF$ - **Core Equation 2:** $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ - **Core Equation 3:** $A = P + F + C$ (Personal + Fatigue + Contingency). - **Westinghouse has 4 sub-factors:** Skill (6), Effort (6), Conditions (4), Consistency (3). $RF = 1 + S + E + C + I$. - **Six Rating Methods:** Speed, Effort, Westinghouse, Objective (LMS), Synthetic, Activity. - **Speed Rating = Pace Rating:** judges speed of movement. - **Effort Rating:** judges depth of exertion. - **Synthetic Rating** is most useful when a **library of previously rated jobs** exists. - **Objective Rating (LMS)** uses a numerical speed scale and is the **least subjective**. - **Most rated value in KTU problems:** $RF = 1.10$ to $1.20$ and $A = 0.20$ (20%). - **Output formula:** Output per shift = Shift minutes / Standard Time. - **Always** state **units** (min/piece, pieces/shift) — losing units costs ½ mark. - **Time Study procedure order:** Inform worker → Record job data → Observe & record times → Rate performance → Add allowances → Compute Standard Time. - **Remember:** Rating is a **skill** that improves with experience; new IEs typically have ±10% bias.

topics.title: Types of Allowances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Allowances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
In Work Measurement and Time Study (a core sub-discipline of Industrial Engineering), an **Allowance** is a formal addition of time to the *Normal Time* of a job/task to compensate the worker for legitimate, non-productive delays, rest needs, and personal requirements that are inherent to any realistic human work cycle. 

Mathematically, it is the difference between the **Standard Time** and the **Normal Time**, expressed either as an absolute time value (minutes/seconds) or, more commonly, as a **percentage (%) of Normal Time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Work Study):**
> Allowances are the third pillar of the **Standard Time Formula**. Without correctly classifying and adding allowances, a "normal time" alone is operationally useless in industry because it represents a theoretical 100% effort pace with zero rest — a condition no human worker can sustain across an 8-hour shift.

### The Universal Standard Time Equation
The foundational relationship in Industrial Engineering for converting observed/rated times into a workable production standard is:

$$\text{Standard Time (ST)} = \text{Normal Time (NT)} + \text{Allowance}$$

Where:

$$\text{Allowance} = \text{Normal Time} \times \text{Allowance Factor (\%)}$$

Equivalently, the compact form widely used in industry is:

$$\text{ST} = \text{NT} \times \left(1 + \frac{\text{Allowance \%}}{100}\right)$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are a cricket coach timing your fastest batsman. His absolute fastest sprint between the wickets is **3.0 seconds**. If you build the team's play-book using *only* this 3.0 s figure, your team will collapse within an hour because:
- The player needs water breaks (**Personal Allowance**).
- He gets tired after 6 overs and his sprint slows (**Fatigue Allowance**).
- The third umpire occasionally calls for a referral, causing unavoidable waiting (**Contingency Allowance**).
- The pitch occasionally needs rolling mid-innings (**Process/Policy Allowance**).

So the coach's *realistic* play-book entry becomes "**3.4 seconds per sprint**" — that extra 0.4 s is the **Allowance**. The batsman, the team, and the match officials all *expect* this buffer; removing it would be unfair and unsustainable.

In industry, allowances serve the **same exact purpose** for a machine operator, an assembly worker, or a fitter on a shop floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction Students Often Confuse:**
> - **Normal Time** = the time a *qualified worker* takes at a *normal/standard pace (100% rating)*. It is a **mathematical construct** (observed time × Performance Rating).
> - **Standard Time** = the time a worker should be *allowed* to complete the job in real working conditions. **This is what goes into the production planning system.**

### Why Allowances Are Non-Negotiable in Engineering Practice
1. **Human Sustainability:** A standard with 0% allowance would require continuous 100% effort — physiologically impossible for an 8-hour shift.
2. **Quality Assurance:** Rushed, fatigued workers produce defective items. Allowances indirectly protect product quality.
3. **Worker Morale & Labour Relations:** ILO and labour-law norms in India (Factories Act 1948, Section 51–59) implicitly recognize the need for rest intervals.
4. **Realistic Costing & Quota Setting:** Standard times feed into wage-incentive plans. Undersized allowances cause worker grievances; oversized allowances inflate labour costs.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Master Classification Tree of Allowances
Allowances in Industrial Engineering are classified into **FIVE primary categories** under the British Standard Institution (BS 3138: 1979) and ILO conventions, which KTU follows:

#### A. Relaxation Allowance (RA) — *The Largest Single Component*
This is time given to the worker to recover from the **physiological and psychological effects of work**. It is the sum of three sub-components:

| Sub-Component | What it Covers | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Personal Allowance (PA)** | Time for natural human needs: drinking water, visiting toilet, washing hands, blowing nose, adjusting clothing. | **5% – 7%** (constant for almost all jobs) |
| **Fatigue Allowance (FA — Basic)** | Recovery from the basic energy expenditure of simply *being at work* (standing, minor movements, light mental effort). | **4%** (constant baseline added to all jobs) |
| **Fatigue Allowance (FA — Variable)** | Extra fatigue caused by *specific* working conditions: heavy lifting, awkward posture, monotony, high temperature, noise, poor lighting, eye strain, mental concentration. | **0% – 40%+** (varies with job severity) |

$$\text{Total Relaxation Allowance} = PA + FA_{basic} + FA_{variable}$$

#### B. Contingency Allowance (CA)
A small fixed allowance (typically **2% – 5%**) to compensate for **irregular delays** that the time-study analyst could not foresee — for example: a brief tool slip, a mis-fed part that requires realignment, a momentary supervisor query, or a small material jam. It is essentially an **insurance buffer** built into the standard.

> [!NOTE]
> Contingency allowance should NOT be used to cover **avoidable** problems (like a chronically faulty machine). Those must be solved through **Method Study**, not buried in the time standard.

#### C. Process Allowance (also called Special / Unavoidable Delay Allowance)
Time required for elements of work that are **outside the operator's control** but are a natural part of the process cycle. Classic examples:
- Time for a forging to cool before the next handling.
- Time for paint to dry in a spray-painting booth.
- Time for a furnace to reach operating temperature.
- Time for cement/concrete to set.

These are *synchronous* with the work cycle — the operator cannot do anything else during them, so they are kept in the standard time.

#### D. Policy Allowance
An allowance granted at the **discretion of management**, not derived from the time study itself. Examples:
- An extra 5% given to a new/trainee worker.
- An extra 3% given to a worker returning from long medical leave.
- Concessions as part of a labour-union agreement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Policy allowances are **NOT engineering-derived**. They are management decisions. Always state them separately in the standard-time card so that engineering revisions do not silently erase them.

#### E. Activity Allowance (also called Interference / Occasional Allowance)
Time given to the operator to perform **auxiliary or occasional duties** that fall outside the main repetitive cycle but belong to the job. Examples:
- Re-sharpening a tool periodically.
- Cleaning the workstation at the end of shift.
- Filling the coolant tank.
- Helping a colleague on a stalled machine.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is exam-portable. Memorize the formulas and units — they appear almost every KTU examination cycle.

| # | Formula | Symbolic Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Observed Time → Normal Time | $NT = OT \times \dfrac{R}{100}$ | minutes | After Performance Rating |
| 2 | Allowance in absolute time | $A = NT \times \dfrac{a}{100}$ | minutes | Convert % to time |
| 3 | Standard Time (additive form) | $ST = NT + A$ | minutes | Most direct form |
| 4 | Standard Time (compact form) | $ST = NT \times \left(1 + \dfrac{a}{100}\right)$ | minutes | Industry-standard |
| 5 | Standard Time (alternative) | $ST = \dfrac{NT}{1 - \dfrac{a}{100}}$ | minutes | When allowance is given as a *fraction of Standard Time* (not Normal Time) |
| 6 | Total Allowance % | $a_{total} = PA + FA + CA + PrA + PolA + AcA$ | % | Final allowance to add |

*Where:* OT = Observed Time, R = Rating Factor, NT = Normal Time, ST = Standard Time, a = Allowance %.

> [!WARNING]
> **Formulas (4) and (5) are NOT identical.** Formula (4) assumes the allowance is a *percentage of Normal Time*. Formula (5) assumes the allowance is a *percentage of Standard Time*. KTU questions frequently test this exact difference. Read the wording carefully!

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Garment Industry (Tirupur, Kerala, TN):** Standard times for stitching operations use **~15%** total allowance (7% personal, 4% basic fatigue, 2% contingency, 2% policy).
- **Heavy Steel Foundry:** Workers handling molten metal may get **30%–50%** allowance due to extreme heat and lifting.
- **CNC Machine Shops:** Allowance is often low (10%–12%) because the machine does the heavy work — operator fatigue is minimal.
- **Software / Knowledge Work:** Less standardized, but typical productivity buffers are 15%–25% of nominal task time.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Observed Time to Standard Time
This is the **complete five-step pipeline** that every KTU question on allowances will ultimately test. We will derive it algebraically and then work a numerical example.

#### Step 1 — Record the Observed Time (OT)
Using a stopwatch (or electronic time-study board), the analyst records the actual time the worker took to complete one cycle of the job, under the actual working conditions.

$$OT = \text{(Recorded wall-clock time per cycle)}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the Performance Rating
The analyst judges the worker's pace against the "normal" pace (defined as 100 on the BSI scale, or 60 on the Westinghouse 100-point scale) and assigns a Rating factor *R*.

$$NT = OT \times \left(\frac{R}{100}\right)$$

*Reasoning:* If a worker is working at 120% of normal pace, then the "normalized" time he would have taken at 100% pace is smaller. Hence we divide by the rating.

#### Step 3 — Determine the Allowance Percentage
The analyst identifies which categories of allowance apply (Personal, Fatigue basic, Fatigue variable, Contingency, Process, Policy, Activity) and sums the percentages.

$$a_{total} = PA + FA_{basic} + FA_{variable} + CA + PrA + PolA + AcA$$
