<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — MATERIAL FLOW PATTERN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> A **Material Flow Pattern** is the standardised physical route or path along which raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods travel through a manufacturing or service facility, governed by the production sequence, the relative positioning of workstations, and the materials handling equipment deployed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (OEIET722 — Module 2)**
> Material flow patterns are a *layout-selection tool*. Before drawing aisles, machines, or buildings, the industrial engineer must first decide the **skeleton (pattern)** through which materials will move. The wrong pattern can permanently lock a plant into excessive travel, backtracking, and congestion.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The River and the City"

Imagine a city being built. Before laying down roads, the city planners decide **where the river will flow** — North-to-South, U-shaped around a hill, branching into tributaries, etc. The roads, bridges, and houses are then *forced* to follow that water path.

A factory works identically:
- The **river** = the sequence of operations the product must go through.
- The **roads** = aisles, conveyors, forklift paths.
- The **houses** = machines, workstations, storage racks.

The **Material Flow Pattern** is the *shape* of that river inside the factory walls. If the river has waterfalls going backwards (backtracking), the city spends extra energy carrying goods uphill — that's wasted material handling cost.

### 1.2 Why Material Flow Pattern Matters

> [!NOTE]
> A well-chosen material flow pattern minimises:
> 1. **Total travel distance** (moved × distance)
> 2. **Backtracking** (movement opposite to the main flow direction)
> 3. **Cross-traffic** and congestion
> 4. **Handling cost** (often **20–30%** of total manufacturing cost in discrete manufacturing)
> 5. **Production lead time**

The eight classical patterns taught under the KTU 2024 scheme are: **I (Straight), L, U, S/Zig-Zag, Circular/Oval, Branching, Spine, and Comb**. Each is a *template*; the engineer selects the template whose geometry best fits the operation sequence.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Standard Material Flow Patterns

### 2.1.1 I-Pattern (Straight Line)
- **Geometry:** Operations in a perfectly straight line, entry at one end, exit at the other.
- **Flow Logic:** Material enters at point A → visits workstations 1, 2, 3 … n in sequence → leaves at point B.
- **Why it works:** Zero backtracking, shortest theoretical path.
- **Limitations:** Requires long, narrow building; unsuitable when a single product is mixed with side processes (storage, inspection, sub-assembly).
- **Real-world use:** Assembly lines for automobiles, bottling plants, food processing.

### 2.1.2 L-Pattern
- **Geometry:** Operations arranged along two perpendicular walls forming an "L".
- **Flow Logic:** Material enters at one end of the horizontal arm, turns 90° at the corner, exits at the end of the vertical arm.
- **Why it works:** Fits a rectangular plot where one dimension is limited.
- **Use case:** Machine shops with one large machine at the corner acting as the process hub.

### 2.1.3 U-Pattern
- **Geometry:** Entry and exit are on the **same side** of the building; the line bends back on itself.
- **Flow Logic:** Material enters → goes forward → turns 180° → returns to the starting wall.
- **Why it works:** Allows loading and unloading at the same dock (huge advantage in truck scheduling), supervisor can oversee the entire loop from one point, and inbound/outbound materials share a single gate.
- **Use case:** Car assembly, electronics assembly, any cellular manufacturing cell.
- **KTU favourite:** This is the most-cited pattern in university questions.

### 2.1.4 Zig-Zag / S-Pattern
- **Geometry:** Operations alternate between two parallel walls.
- **Flow Logic:** Material snakes back and forth, like a serpent.
- **Why it works:** Achieves long production sequence inside a compact square plot.
- **Use case:** Garment manufacturing, PCB assembly, small-batch machine shops.

### 2.1.5 Circular / Oval Pattern
- **Geometry:** Workstations arranged around a central point (e.g., a central store or rotating table).
- **Flow Logic:** Material moves in a continuous loop.
- **Why it works:** Excellent for processes that return to a common starting material (recycling, casting foundry with sand reclamation).
- **Use case:** Casting foundries, paint shops with rotating tables.

### 2.1.6 Branching (Tree) Pattern
- **Geometry:** One raw-material source feeds several sub-assembly lines, which then converge into final assembly.
- **Why it works:** Mirrors the **BOM (Bill of Materials)** structure exactly — sub-assemblies develop in parallel branches.
- **Use case:** Furniture manufacturing, aircraft assembly, any product with a multi-level BOM.

### 2.1.7 Spine Pattern
- **Geometry:** A main central aisle (the "spine") runs the length of the plant; workstations branch off to either side.
- **Why it works:** Centralised materials handling (e.g., automated conveyor spine) with flexible side-cells.
- **Use case:** Modern automotive plants, mail-sorting centres.

### 2.1.8 Comb Pattern
- **Geometry:** Like the spine pattern, but each side-branch is dedicated to a single operation.
- **Why it works:** Combines the compactness of a spine with the sequential clarity of an I-pattern on each branch.
- **Use case:** Heavy fabrication shops, shipyards.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the quantitative tools examiners use to test your understanding of flow patterns. Memorise units and assumptions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Variables & Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Material Handling Cost | $C_H \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \cdot C_i \cdot D_i$ | $N_i$ = number of trips between dept $i$ and $i+1$ per period, $C_i$ = cost per trip (₹/trip), $D_i$ = distance (m) | Comparing two alternative layouts |
| 2 | Total Distance Travelled | $TD \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} N_i \cdot D_i$ | $TD$ in metre-trips | Layout cost minimisation |
| 3 | Backtracking Percentage | $B\% \;=\; \dfrac{D_{\text{back}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $D_{\text{back}}$ = distance moved against net flow direction, $D_{\text{total}}$ = total distance moved | Diagnosing an I-pattern violation |
| 4 | Flow Efficiency | $\eta_f \;=\; \dfrac{D_{\text{net}}}{D_{\text{actual}}} \times 100$ | $D_{\text{net}}$ = straight-line ideal, $D_{\text{actual}}$ = travelled path | Comparing patterns (I-pattern ⇒ highest $\eta_f$) |
| 5 | Number of Loads Between Departments | $N_{ij} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{ij}}{C_v}$ | $P_{ij}$ = total material flow per period (kg/units), $C_v$ = capacity per vehicle/container (kg/units) | Computing $C_H$ |
| 6 | Handling Cost per Unit | $c_u \;=\; \dfrac{C_H}{Q}$ | $Q$ = units produced per period | Unit-cost comparison of patterns |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** All distances must be measured along the **actual travelled path**, not the straight-line "as-the-crow-flies" distance. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for ignoring this.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Material Handling Cost for a Given Flow Pattern

We start from first principles. Let a plant have $n$ departments arranged according to a chosen flow pattern. Material must travel from department $i$ to department $i+1$ for $N_i$ trips per production period. Each trip costs a linear amount $C_i$ per metre travelled.

**Step 1 — Define the cost of a single trip between departments $i$ and $i+1$.**

A single trip generates handling cost proportional to the distance covered:

$$c_{i,\text{single}} \;=\; C_i \cdot D_i$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the number of trips per period.**

If $N_i$ trips are made between the same pair, total cost for that pair is:

$$C_{i,\text{total}} \;=\; N_i \cdot C_i \cdot D_i$$

**Step 3 — Sum over all $n-1$ inter-departmental pairs (or all $k$ pairs in a non-sequential flow).**

For a linear (I, L, U, S) pattern, departments are connected in sequence, giving $n-1$ links:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_H &= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} N_i \cdot C_i \cdot D_i \\
&= N_1 C_1 D_1 \;+\; N_2 C_2 D_2 \;+\; \dots \;+\; N_{n-1} C_{n-1} D_{n-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Specialisation for an I-pattern with uniform cost and uniform trips.**

If every inter-departmental cost-per-metre is the same constant $C$ and every pair carries the same number of trips $N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_H &= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} N \cdot C \cdot D \\
&= N \cdot C \cdot (n-1) \cdot d
\end{aligned}
$$

where $d$ is the uniform distance between adjacent departments. This is the simplest **closed-form expression** and is the one most often asked in KTU 14-mark problems.

**Step 5 — Generalise to a non-sequential (branching / spine) pattern.**

For a branching pattern, the same summation applies, but the index runs over **all $k$ transport links**, not just $n-1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_H &= \sum_{j=1}^{k} N_j \cdot C_j \cdot D_j
\end{aligned}
$$

In a tree pattern with $r$ branches, the total number of links is $k = (n-1)$ for a fully connected tree, identical in count to a linear chain — but the *spatial distances* $D_j$ are smaller because branches are short.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The branching pattern is therefore often the *minimum-cost* topology when the BOM is branching and each sub-assembly is short — a classic IE optimisation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Backtracking Percentage for a U-Pattern

A common KTU question asks: "In a U-pattern, what percentage of total movement is backtracking?" Let us derive it.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**

Consider a rectangular plant of length $L$ and width $W$, with operations starting at the bottom-left, going up the left wall (length $L$), across the top (width $W$), and back down the right wall (length $L$).

**Step 2 — Identify net flow direction.**

The "useful" direction is the long axis, say the $y$-axis. Material enters moving in $+y$, but on the return leg of the U it moves in $-y$ — this is backtracking.

**Step 3 — Compute total actual path length.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{actual}} &= L + W + L \\
&= 2L + W
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the backtracking component.**

The return leg of length $L$ is entirely in the $-y$ direction, so it counts as backtracking:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{back}} &= L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the backtracking percentage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B\% &= \frac{D_{\text{back}}}{D_{\text{actual}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{L}{2L + W} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Limiting case check.**

If $W \to 0$ (degenerate U becomes a line going and coming back), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B\% &\to \frac{L}{2L} \times 100 = 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

If $W \to \infty$, then $B\% \to 0$. Both limits are physically sensible.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pattern Selection Guide

| Decision Criterion | I | L | U | S/Zig-Zag | Circular | Branching | Spine | Comb |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shortest path | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★ |
| Same-side entry/exit | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | depends | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Handles branching BOM | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓★★ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Compactness of building | ✗ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★ |
| Ease of supervision | ★★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★ |
| Best for assembly line | ✓★★ | ✓ | ✓★★ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Best for process layout | ✗ | ★ | ✗ | ★ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> ★★★ = excellent, ★★ = good, ★ = acceptable, ✗ = unsuitable. This matrix is examiner-favourite for "compare any two patterns" questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All diagrams use the Mermaid `flowchart` syntax with strictly alphanumeric node IDs. Labels are kept clean — no markdown formatting inside quoted strings.

## 4.1 Master Decision Map — Which Pattern to Select?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Product and BOM known] --> B{Single product linear sequence?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Same side entry and exit required?}
    B -- No --> D{BOM has parallel sub-assemblies?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Choose U Pattern]
    C -- No --> F{Limited building width?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Choose L Pattern]
    F -- No --> H[Choose I Pattern]
    D -- Yes --> I{Centralised handling spine feasible?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Choose Spine or Comb Pattern]
    I -- No --> K[Choose Branching Tree Pattern]
    D -- No --> L{Process returns to raw material?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Choose Circular Pattern]
    L -- No --> N{Compact square building?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Choose S or Zig-Zag Pattern]
    N -- No --> P[Re-evaluate product and process mix]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Comparison of I, L, U Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IPAT[I Pattern Straight Line]
        I1[Raw Material In] --> I2[Operation 1] --> I3[Operation 2] --> I4[Operation 3] --> I5[Finished Goods Out]
    end

    subgraph LPAT[L Pattern]
        L1[Raw Material In] --> L2[Operation 1] --> L3[Operation 2] --> L4[Operation 3] --> L5[Finished Goods Out]
    end

    subgraph UPAT[U Pattern]
        U1[Raw Material In] --> U2[Operation 1] --> U3[Operation 2] --> U4[Operation 3] --> U5[Finished Goods Out same side]
    end
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Branching and Spine Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BPAT[Branching Tree Pattern]
        B0[Central Store RM] --> B1A[Sub Assembly A]
        B0 --> B1B[Sub Assembly B]
        B0 --> B1C[Sub Assembly C]
        B1A --> B2[Final Assembly]
        B1B --> B2
        B1C --> B2
        B2 --> B3[Despatch]
    end

    subgraph SPAT[Spine Pattern]
        S0[Central Conveyor Spine] --> S1A[Workstation A1]
        S0 --> S1B[Workstation A2]
        S0 --> S1C[Workstation B1]
        S0 --> S1D[Workstation B2]
        S1A --> SOUT[Collection Spine]
        S1B --> SOUT
        S1C --> SOUT
        S1D --> SOUT
    end
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow — U-Pattern Showing Backtracking Leg

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Entry Dock] --> P2[Op 1 left wall] --> P3[Op 2 top wall] --> P4[Op 3 right wall] --> P5[Exit Dock same side as entry]
    P3 -. backtracking leg .-> P4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OEIET722]`
**"Define material flow pattern. List any four standard patterns used in plant layout."**

**Model Answer:**
A material flow pattern is the standardised physical route through which raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods move between workstations, governed by the production sequence and the chosen layout topology. The four standard patterns are: (1) **I-Pattern** (straight line), (2) **L-Pattern**, (3) **U-Pattern**, and (4) **Zig-Zag / S-Pattern**. Other patterns include circular, branching, spine, and comb. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the four patterns.)*

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OEIET722]`
**"Why is the U-pattern preferred over the I-pattern in modern automobile assembly plants? Give two reasons."**

**Model Answer:**
The U-pattern is preferred because: (1) **Loading and unloading share a single dock**, simplifying inbound and outbound material scheduling and reducing transport-vehicle idle time; (2) **The supervisor can oversee the entire assembly loop from one central point**, improving visual control and quick response to line stoppages. A third reason sometimes cited is reduced building footprint for a given line length. *(3 marks: 1.5 for each reason.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme regulations, every Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternative choices are provided below.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OEIET722]`

**(a)** Explain any **four** material flow patterns with neat sketches. State **two advantages** of each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. I-Pattern (Straight Line):** Material flows in a single straight line from raw-material entry to finished-goods exit. *Sketch:* RM → Op1 → Op2 → Op3 → FG arranged in a straight horizontal line.
*Advantages:* (i) Shortest possible material path, hence minimum handling cost. (ii) Easy to plan, balance, and supervise because the sequence is unambiguous.

**2. U-Pattern:** Entry and exit are on the same side; the line bends 180° and returns. *Sketch:* RM at bottom-left → up the left wall → across the top → down the right wall → FG at bottom-right.
*Advantages:* (i) Same-side loading and unloading simplifies material handling. (ii) Compact use of a square or near-square building.

**3. L-Pattern:** Material flows along two perpendicular walls forming an L. *Sketch:* RM at one end of horizontal arm → operations along horizontal arm → turn 90° at the corner → operations along vertical arm → FG at the far end.
*Advantages:* (i) Fits a rectangular plot where one dimension is restricted. (ii) Useful when a large machine at the corner acts as the process hub.

**4. Branching / Tree Pattern:** One raw-material source feeds multiple sub-assembly branches which then converge. *Sketch:* RM at root → branches A, B, C → sub-assemblies → convergence at final assembly → FG.
*Advantages:* (i) Mirrors the BOM structure, reducing WIP movement. (ii) Enables parallel processing of sub-assemblies, shortening lead time.

> [!NOTE]
> *Mark split:* 1.25 marks per pattern + 0.5 per advantage × 2 = 1.0. Total = (1.25 × 4) + (0.5 × 2 × 4) = 5 + 4 = 9, capped at 7 by the rubric. Examiners give 7/7 for any four patterns explained with two valid advantages each.

**(b)** A manufacturing plant has **five departments** arranged in a **U-pattern** within a rectangular building of length **40 m** and width **20 m**. The number of loads moved between successive departments per month is given in the table below. The handling cost is **₹8 per load-metre**. Calculate the **total monthly material handling cost**.

| From → To | Loads per month | Distance (m) |
|---|---|---|
| Dept 1 → Dept 2 | 200 | 12 |
| Dept 2 → Dept 3 | 180 | 12 |
| Dept 3 → Dept 4 | 220 | 12 |
| Dept 4 → Dept 5 | 150 | 12 |

*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the handling cost formula.**

$$C_H \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} N_i \cdot C_i \cdot D_i$$

Here $C_i$ is the per-load-per-metre cost = ₹8.

**Step 2 — Compute each link's contribution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{12} &= 200 \times 8 \times 12 = ₹19{,}200 \\
C_{23} &= 180 \times 8 \times 12 = ₹17{,}280 \\
C_{34} &= 220 \times 8 \times 12 = ₹21{,}120 \\
C_{45} &= 150 \times 8 \times 12 = ₹14{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_H &= 19{,}200 + 17{,}280 + 21{,}120 + 14{,}400 \\
&= ₹72{,}000 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> *Mark split:* 2 marks for formula, 4 marks for computation (1 each), 1 mark for final answer with units. **Total: ₹72,000 per month.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare the **I-Pattern** and **U-Pattern** of material flow on the basis of: (i) entry-exit position, (ii) backtracking, (iii) supervision ease, (iv) building shape suitability. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | I-Pattern | U-Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Entry-Exit Position | Entry and exit on **opposite walls** of the building | Entry and exit on the **same wall** |
| (ii) Backtracking | **Zero** backtracking; material always moves forward along the line | **Significant** backtracking on the return leg (≈ 50% in a long narrow U) |
| (iii) Supervision Ease | Moderate — supervisor must walk the length of the line | **High** — entire loop is visible from a single central point |
| (iv) Building Shape Suitability | Requires a **long, narrow** building; uneconomical for square plots | Fits **square or near-square** plots efficiently |

> [!NOTE]
> *Mark split:* 1.5 marks per criterion × 4 = 6 + 1 mark for the introductory sentence = 7.

**(b)** A plant uses a **U-pattern** in a building of length **L = 50 m** and width **W = 20 m**. Compute the **backtracking percentage** of the total material movement. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the U-pattern geometry.**

Total actual path length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{actual}} &= L + W + L \\
&= 2L + W \\
&= 2(50) + 20 \\
&= 120 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the backtracking component.**

The return leg of length $L$ runs opposite to the entry direction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{back}} &= L = 50 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the backtracking formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B\% &= \frac{D_{\text{back}}}{D_{\text{actual}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{50}{120} \times 100 \\
&= 41.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> *Mark split:* 2 marks for geometry, 2 marks for computing $D_{\text{actual}}$, 1 mark for $D_{\text{back}}$, 2 marks for the final percentage with correct units. **Answer: ≈ 41.67% backtracking.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Read Carefully)**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Handling cost formulas expect **load-metres** as the compound unit. Writing "₹8/m" instead of "₹8 per load-metre" loses 1 mark in part-(b) computations.
> 2. **Path vs. Straight-Line Distance:** The total distance $D$ must be the **actual path** (along aisles), not the straight-line Euclidean distance between department centres. Many students write $D = \sqrt{L^2 + W^2}$ for a U-pattern, which is **wrong** for material handling cost.
> 3. **Backtracking Definition:** Backtracking is movement **against the net flow direction**, not just any non-forward movement. A sideways cross-traffic is not backtracking; it is a cross-flow.
> 4. **Pattern Sketches:** A "sketch" without arrows showing material flow direction gets **zero** credit for that pattern. Always include directional arrows.
> 5. **Choice of Pattern:** Examiners expect you to **justify** the choice of a pattern by linking it to the **BOM structure** and the **building shape**, not merely to state it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Material Flow Patterns**
> - A material flow pattern is the **route skeleton** of the plant; choose it *before* drawing the layout.
> - The **eight standard patterns** are: I, L, U, S/Zig-Zag, Circular, Branching, Spine, Comb.
> - **I-Pattern** = shortest path, zero backtracking, needs a long narrow building.
> - **U-Pattern** = same-side entry/exit, best for supervision, has backtracking on the return leg.
> - **L-Pattern** = fits rectangular plots with one limited dimension.
> - **S/Zig-Zag** = compact for long sequences in a square plot.
> - **Circular** = ideal when the process loops back to raw material (recycling, casting).
> - **Branching** = mirrors a multi-level BOM; enables parallel sub-assembly.
> - **Spine** = centralised conveyor with side-workstations; high flexibility.
> - **Comb** = spine with dedicated single-operation branches; very long sequences.
> - **Key formulas** to memorise:
>   $C_H = \sum N_i C_i D_i$
>   $B\% = (D_{\text{back}} / D_{\text{actual}}) \times 100$
>   $\eta_f = (D_{\text{net}} / D_{\text{actual}}) \times 100$
> - **Always** use the **actual travelled path** for $D$, never the Euclidean distance.
> - **Justify** every pattern choice with reference to BOM structure, building shape, and supervision needs — this is what examiners reward in 14-mark answers.
> - The U-pattern backtracking limit: as $W \to 0$, $B\% \to 50\%$; as $W \to \infty$, $B\% \to 0\%$.
> - Handling cost typically represents **20–30%** of total manufacturing cost in discrete industries — pattern selection has a major financial impact.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Layout Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Layout Planning (Plant Layout / Facility Layout)** is the systematic and pre-planned physical arrangement of industrial facilities, including machines, equipment, workstations, storage areas, aisles, and service points, to promote the efficient flow of materials, workers, and information, while ensuring optimal utilization of space, safety, flexibility, and worker comfort.

As per the **KTU 2024 OEIET722 syllabus**, Layout Planning is treated as a strategic decision-making function within Industrial Engineering that directly affects **productivity, cost, lead time, and worker morale**. A layout decision is generally considered a **long-term, capital-intensive, and irreversible** managerial commitment, since relocating a fully installed plant may cost up to **50% of the original installation cost**.

The classical definition by **James A. Tompkins** describes facility layout as the process of determining the placement of departments, work groups within departments, workstations, machines, and stock-holding points within a facility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – Module 2 Focus:** Layout Planning is positioned at the intersection of **Production Management, Ergonomics, and Operations Research**. Students must master both qualitative principles (e.g., 5S, integration, minimum travel) and quantitative tools (e.g., CRAFT, ALDEP, CORELAP, From-To charts).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of your **kitchen at home**. When you cook, you instinctively place the stove next to the gas cylinder, keep the plates and cutlery close to the dining table, and ensure the water tap is near the washing area. You don't put the fridge in the bathroom!

A factory floor works exactly the same way. **Layout Planning is the art and science of placing "the right item, in the right place, so that the right work can be done with minimum wasted movement, time, and effort."**

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Rule of Thumb:** If a material travels a long way before becoming a finished product, the layout is **bad**. If workers walk a lot between machines, the layout is **bad**. If there is congestion, safety risk, or excess inventory, the layout is **bad**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics of a Good Layout

The success of any layout can be objectively measured using the following KTU-accepted metrics:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Benchmark Goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material Handling Cost | MHC | **Rupees per unit** | Minimize |
| Backtracking Ratio | BTR | **Dimensionless (%)** | Less than 10% |
| Floor Space Utilization | FSU | **% of total area** | **70% – 80%** |
| Travel Distance | TD | **Metres (m)** | Minimize |
| Production Lead Time | PLT | **Hours / Days** | Minimize |
| Workstation Balance Delay | WBD | **Dimensionless (%)** | Less than **10%** |
| Flexibility Index | FI | **Qualitative (1–5 scale)** | Maximize |

> [!WARNING]
> A common student mistake is confusing **Plant Layout** (macro-level — entire factory) with **Layout Planning** (decision-making process). Plant layout is the **output**, layout planning is the **process**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of Layout Planning

The objectives follow a strict hierarchy in KTU evaluation. A student is expected to list them in **descending order of operational priority**:

1. **Integration of Operations** – Connect men, machines, and materials into a single productive unit.
2. **Minimum Material Movement (Minimum Travel)** – Reduce the distance covered by the work-piece.
3. **Minimum Material Handling Cost (MHC)** – Avoid back-tracking and delays.
4. **Optimum Use of Available Floor Space** – Eliminate wasted or non-productive area.
5. **Smooth Flow of Production** – Maintain uninterrupted workflow.
6. **Worker Comfort, Safety, and Welfare** – Reduce fatigue, ensure ergonomic compliance.
7. **Flexibility and Adaptability** – Allow future expansion or product changes.
8. **Effective Supervision and Control** – Allow clear visibility of operations.
9. **Minimum Capital Investment** – Avoid unnecessary civil works.
10. **Reduction in Work-in-Progress (WIP)** – Lesser waiting time between processes.

## 2.2 Principles of Layout (KTU High-Frequency Topic)

The Eight Classic Principles, as standardized in KTU textbooks:

| # | Principle | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Principle of Integration** | All production factors must be integrated into one balanced plan. |
| 2 | **Principle of Minimum Distance** | Travel path between successive operations must be minimum. |
| 3 | **Principle of Flow** | Layout must support a continuous, unidirectional flow. |
| 4 | **Principle of Cubic Space Utilization** | Use **3D space** (vertical + horizontal), not just floor area. |
| 5 | **Principle of Satisfaction** | Use the first **satisfactory** (not necessarily optimal) layout. |
| 6 | **Principle of Flexibility** | Layout must adapt to future changes in product or volume. |
| 7 | **Principle of Safety, Security, and Comfort** | Zero accident policy; ensure ventilation, lighting, noise < **85 dB**. |
| 8 | **Principle of Minimum Handling** | Handle materials as few times as possible. |

## 2.3 Types of Layout (KTU Core Syllabus)

### 2.3.1 Product Layout (Line Layout)
- **Setup:** Machines arranged in the **sequence of operations** required for a specific product.
- **Used for:** Mass production, continuous flow (e.g., car assembly).
- **Strength:** Low MHC, high throughput, short cycle time.
- **Weakness:** Low flexibility, high breakdown impact.
- **Best Metric:** **Line Balancing Efficiency (LBE)**.

### 2.3.2 Process Layout (Functional Layout)
- **Setup:** Similar machines grouped together (all lathes in one section, all milling in another).
- **Used for:** Job-shop production, low volume, high variety.
- **Strength:** High flexibility, good machine utilization.
- **Weakness:** Long travel distance, complex scheduling.
- **Best Metric:** **Total Travel Distance (TD)**.

### 2.3.3 Fixed Position Layout
- **Setup:** The **product remains stationary**; men, machines, and materials move to it.
- **Used for:** Ship-building, aircraft, large boilers, construction.
- **Strength:** Eliminates product movement.
- **Weakness:** High MHC, low throughput, scheduling nightmare.

### 2.3.4 Cellular / Group Technology Layout
- **Setup:** Parts with similar processing requirements are grouped into **part-families**; machines form a **cell**.
- **Used for:** Batch production, medium variety.
- **Strength:** Combines benefits of product and process layout.

### 2.3.5 Hybrid (Combined) Layout
- **Setup:** Combination of two or more basic layouts in different sections of the same plant.
- **Real-world Example:** Most KTU industrial visits will feature this.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule (KTU Favorite Question):**
> - **High Volume + Low Variety** → Product Layout
> - **Low Volume + High Variety** → Process Layout
> - **Very Large / Heavy Product** → Fixed Position Layout
> - **Batch Production with Part Families** → Cellular Layout

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following formulas are **verified from classical Industrial Engineering references** and form the high-yield numerical set for KTU ESE:

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Material Handling Cost (TMHC)** | $TMHC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_i \times D_i)$ | $C_i$ = cost per unit distance (Rs/m), $D_i$ = distance (m) |
| 2 | **Line Balancing Efficiency (LBE)** | $LBE = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{n \times t_{max}} \times 100$ | $t_i$ = task time, $n$ = stations, $t_{max}$ = cycle time |
| 3 | **Balance Delay (BD)** | $BD = (1 - LBE) \times 100$ | Expressed in **%** |
| 4 | **Number of Workstations (Minimum)** | $N_{min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\sum t_i}{t_{c}} \right\rceil$ | $t_c$ = desired cycle time |
| 5 | **Cycle Time** | $t_c = \dfrac{\text{Available Time per Day}}{\text{Demand per Day}}$ | Units: **minutes/unit** |
| 6 | **Output of Line** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{t_{max}}$ | Units: **units/day** |
| 7 | **Backtracking Ratio** | $BTR = \dfrac{\text{Backtracked Distance}}{\text{Total Distance}} \times 100$ | Expressed in **%** |
| 8 | **Floor Space Index (FSI)** | $FSI = \dfrac{\text{Built-up Area}}{\text{Total Plot Area}}$ | Standard range: **1.0 – 1.5** |
| 9 | **Idle Time of a Station** | $IT_i = t_{max} - t_i$ | Units: **minutes** |
| 10 | **Overall Effectiveness** | $OE = LBE \times U \times Q$ | $U$ = utilization, $Q$ = quality rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** For absolute value expressions, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — **never** use the bare pipe symbol `|x|` inside markdown tables, as it breaks table syntax.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Layout Planning

- **Manufacturing Engineering:** Determines capital cost, cycle time, bottleneck formation.
- **Logistics & SCM:** Influences warehouse slotting, dock design, AGV paths.
- **Safety Engineering:** Dictates emergency exits, fire lanes, machine guarding.
- **Ergonomics & HSE:** Affects noise zones, lighting design, fatigue index.
- **Production Planning:** Defines routing, lead-time, WIP build-up.
- **Cost Accounting:** Forms the basis for **overhead allocation** and **activity-based costing (ABC)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Material Handling Cost (TMHC) of a Layout

The Material Handling Cost function forms the **objective function** of every layout optimization algorithm (CRAFT, COFAD, etc.). The derivation is shown below.

### 3.1.1 Statement

For a set of $n$ departments carrying out $m$ material flows, the total MHC is the sum of (cost per unit flow per unit distance) multiplied by the flow volume and the distance between each pair.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:

- $f_{ij}$ = Number of material flow units moved from department $i$ to department $j$ (loads/month).
- $d_{ij}$ = Distance between department $i$ and department $j$ (m).
- $c_{ij}$ = Material handling cost per unit flow per unit distance (Rs per load per m).

The cost of moving material between any single pair $(i, j)$ is:

$$
\text{MHC}_{ij} = f_{ij} \times d_{ij} \times c_{ij}
$$

For a layout with $n$ departments, the **total Material Handling Cost** is the sum over all ordered pairs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TMHC &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{ij} \times d_{ij} \times c_{ij} \\[6pt]
     &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{ij} \cdot c_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the cost is uniform across all flows ($c_{ij} = c$, a constant), it can be pulled out:

$$
TMHC = c \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}
$$

Since $d_{ij} = d_{ji}$ (distance is symmetric), and the flows are one-way:

$$
TMHC = c \cdot \sum_{i < j} (f_{ij} + f_{ji}) \cdot d_{ij}
$$

This is the **fundamental cost equation** used by the CRAFT algorithm.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

A 3-department factory has the following data (cost constant $c = 1$ Rs/load/m):

| Flow (i → j) | $f_{ij}$ (loads) | $d_{ij}$ (m) | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | 10 | 4 | $10 \times 4 \times 1 = 40$ |
| 2 → 3 | 8 | 6 | $8 \times 6 \times 1 = 48$ |
| 1 → 3 | 5 | 9 | $5 \times 9 \times 1 = 45$ |
| 3 → 2 | 7 | 6 | $7 \times 6 \times 1 = 42$ |
| 3 → 1 | 3 | 9 | $3 \times 9 \times 1 = 27$ |
| 2 → 1 | 2 | 4 | $2 \times 4 \times 1 = 8$ |

$$
\begin{aligned}
TMHC &= 40 + 48 + 45 + 42 + 27 + 8 \\[4pt]
     &= 210 \text{ Rs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always tabulate pairwise contributions; summing on paper invites arithmetic errors. A 2-mark bonus is given for clean tabulation in KTU ESE.

## 3.2 Derivation: Line Balancing Efficiency (LBE)

### 3.2.1 Definition of LBE

Line balancing efficiency is the ratio of **total task time assigned to all stations** to the **sum of maximum (cycle) times of all stations**, expressed as a percentage.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let a line have $n$ workstations, each with a cycle time $t_{max}$.

Total available time across the line = $n \times t_{max}$.

Total productive (work) time assigned = $\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$, where $t_i$ is the sum of task times at station $i$.

By definition:

$$
LBE = \dfrac{\text{Productive Time}}{\text{Available Time}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LBE &= \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n \times t_{max}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{T}{n \times t_{max}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

where $T = \sum t_i$ is the **total work content** of the assembly.

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example

A 4-station assembly line has the following station times: 5, 6, 4, 7 minutes. The bottleneck (max) time = **7 min**.

Total work content:

$$
T = 5 + 6 + 4 + 7 = 22 \text{ min}
$$

Line balancing efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LBE &= \dfrac{22}{4 \times 7} \times 100 \\[4pt]
    &= \dfrac{22}{28} \times 100 \\[4pt]
    &= 78.57\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Balance delay:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BD &= (1 - 0.7857) \times 100 \\[4pt]
   &= 21.43\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Layout vs. Production Parameters

| Parameter | Product Layout | Process Layout | Fixed Position | Cellular Layout |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Volume | **High** | Low | Very Low | Medium |
| Variety | **Low** | High | One-of-a-kind | Medium (Families) |
| Lead Time | **Short** | Long | Very Long | Medium |
| WIP | **Low** | High | Very High | Medium |
| Capital | **High** | Low | Variable | Medium |
| Skill | Low | **High** | Variable | Medium |
| Equipment Utilization | Low | **High** | Low | Medium |
| Best Suited Industry | Auto, FMCG | Tool room | Ship-yard | Batch manufacturing |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **guaranteed KTU question** in some form. Memorize the **bold** entries.

## 3.4 Layout Planning Procedure (Sequential Steps)

1. **Define the objective** (e.g., minimize MHC).
2. **Collect base data** — production volume, sequence, product mix.
3. **Prepare a From-To Chart** (or Activity Relationship Chart).
4. **Determine space requirements** for each department.
5. **Develop activity relationships** (REL chart by Muther).
6. **Generate block layout** (use templates / computer algorithm).
7. **Evaluate alternatives** (CRAFT, CORELAP, ALDEP).
8. **Select the best layout** using the **principle of satisfaction**.
9. **Detail the layout** — exact machine positions, aisles, utilities.
10. **Implement and review** with a 5S and continuous-improvement loop.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Layout Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["FACTORY LAYOUT"] --> B["PRODUCT LAYOUT"]
    A --> C["PROCESS LAYOUT"]
    A --> D["FIXED POSITION LAYOUT"]
    A --> E["CELLULAR LAYOUT"]
    A --> F["HYBRID LAYOUT"]
    B --> B1["MASS PRODUCTION"]
    B --> B2["ASSEMBLY LINE"]
    C --> C1["JOB SHOP"]
    C --> C2["BATCH SHOP"]
    D --> D1["SHIPYARD"]
    D --> D2["AIRCRAFT ASSEMBLY"]
    E --> E1["GROUP TECHNOLOGY"]
    E --> E2["PART FAMILY CELL"]
    F --> F1["PROCESS + PRODUCT MIX"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Layout Planning Process Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["STEP 1: STUDY PRODUCTION PLAN"] --> P2["STEP 2: BUILD FROM-TO CHART"]
    P2 --> P3["STEP 3: PREPARE ACTIVITY CHART"]
    P3 --> P4["STEP 4: ESTIMATE SPACE"]
    P4 --> P5["STEP 5: GENERATE BLOCK PLAN"]
    P5 --> P6["STEP 6: APPLY CRAFT ALGORITHM"]

```

## 4.3 Product Layout vs. Process Layout Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PL["PRODUCT LAYOUT"]
        P1A["CUTTING"] --> P1B["DRILLING"] --> P1C["GRINDING"] --> P1D["ASSEMBLY"] --> P1E["TESTING"]
    end
    subgraph PR["PROCESS LAYOUT"]
        PR1["LATHE 1"] --> PR2["LATHE 2"]
        PR3["MILL 1"] --> PR4["MILL 2"]

    end
```

## 4.4 CRAFT Algorithm Flow (Quantitative Layout Tool)

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1["INPUT INITIAL LAYOUT + FROM-TO DATA"] --> C2["CALCULATE INITIAL TMHC"]
    C2 --> C3["SWAP TWO DEPARTMENTS"]
    C3 --> C4["RECALCULATE NEW TMHC"]
    C4 --> C5{"IS NEW TMHC LOWER"}
    C5 -->|"YES"| C6["ACCEPT SWAP"]
    C5 -->|"NO"| C7["REJECT SWAP"]
    C6 --> C8["MORE PAIRS REMAINING"]
    C7 --> C8
    C8 -->|"YES"| C3
    C8 -->|"NO"| C9["OUTPUT OPTIMIZED LAYOUT"]
```

## 4.5 Activity Relationship Diagram (Muther's REL Chart Symbols)

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1["A ABSOLUTELY NECESSARY"] --> R2["E ESPECIALLY IMPORTANT"]
    R2 --> R3["I IMPORTANT"]
    R3 --> R4["O ORDINARY CLOSENESS OK"]
    R4 --> R5["U UNIMPORTANT"]
    R5 --> R6["X NOT DESIRABLE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard:** The letters A, E, I, O, U, X and their corresponding numerical closeness values (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, -1) are the KTU-approved symbols for the **Muther's Activity Relationship Chart**. Memorize them.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define Plant Layout. List any four objectives of a good plant layout.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Plant layout is the physical arrangement of industrial facilities such as machines, equipment, workstations, storage, and service areas to ensure efficient and economic flow of materials, men, and information through the production system.

**Four Objectives (1.5 Marks – 0.4 each + 0.1 for sequencing):**

1. **Integration of men, machines, and materials** into a single coordinated system.
2. **Minimum material movement** between successive operations.
3. **Effective and optimum use of available floor space.**
4. **Provision of safety, comfort, and welfare** to workers.

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
> **Differentiate between Product Layout and Process Layout on any three parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks – 1 mark per parameter):**

| Parameter | Product Layout | Process Layout |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis of Grouping** | Machines arranged in **sequence of operation** for a specific product. | Similar machines / processes grouped together. |
| **Production Volume & Variety** | **High volume, low variety.** | **Low volume, high variety.** |
| **Material Flow** | **Smooth, linear, and continuous.** | **Complex and criss-cross.** |
| **Work-in-Progress** | **Low** (line flow). | **High** (waiting between sections). |
| **Capital Investment** | **High** (special-purpose machines). | **Lower** (general-purpose machines). |

> **Examiner's Note:** Always tabulate the differences; it secures full 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Module 2 Internal Choice)

> ### Question A (14 Marks)
> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
> **(a) Explain the different types of plant layouts with neat sketches. Highlight their suitability and limitations.** (7 Marks)
> **(b) The following data refers to 4 workstations in an assembly line. Calculate the Line Balancing Efficiency and Balance Delay.**
>
> | Station | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Time (min) | 6 | 8 | 5 | 9 |

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Types of Plant Layouts (7 Marks)

**1. Product Layout (1.5 Marks):**
Machines are arranged in the **sequence of operations** required to manufacture a specific product. The material flows in a **straight line** from raw material to finished product. Suited for **mass production, assembly lines, continuous flow industries** (e.g., car assembly, FMCG bottling).
*Limitation:* Highly **inflexible**; breakdown at one station halts the entire line.

**2. Process Layout (1.5 Marks):**
Similar machines and processes are **grouped together** (all drilling machines in one section, all lathes in another). Suitable for **job-shop and batch production** with high product variety.
*Limitation:* Long travel distances, complex scheduling, high WIP.

**3. Fixed Position Layout (1.5 Marks):**
The **product remains stationary** at one location, while men, machines, and materials are brought to it. Used for **very large products** like ships, aircraft, and large boilers.
*Limitation:* Extremely high material handling cost; difficult coordination.

**4. Cellular (Group Technology) Layout (1.5 Marks):**
Parts with **similar processing requirements** are grouped into **part-families**. Machines required to process a family are arranged in a **cell**. Combines the **efficiency of product layout** with the **flexibility of process layout**.

**5. Hybrid Layout (1 Mark):**
A combination of two or more basic layouts in different sections of the same plant. Most real-world factories use this.

### Part (b) — Line Balancing Calculation (7 Marks)

Given:

- $t_1 = 6$ min, $t_2 = 8$ min, $t_3 = 5$ min, $t_4 = 9$ min.
- $n = 4$ stations.

**Step 1:** Total work content.

$$
T = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 = 6 + 8 + 5 + 9 = 28 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify bottleneck (maximum station time).

$$
t_{max} = 9 \text{ min (Station 4)}
$$

**Step 3:** Line Balancing Efficiency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
LBE &= \dfrac{T}{n \times t_{max}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
    &= \dfrac{28}{4 \times 9} \times 100 \\[4pt]
    &= \dfrac{28}{36} \times 100 \\[4pt]
    &= 77.78\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Balance Delay.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BD &= (1 - LBE) \times 100 \\[4pt]
   &= (1 - 0.7778) \times 100 \\[4pt]
   &= 22.22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B):**
> - **Don't forget to multiply by 100** when expressing LBE and BD as percentages. Many students write the decimal value and lose **1 mark**.
> - **Don't confuse Balance Delay with Bottleneck** — Balance Delay is a *global* measure of imbalance, not a single station.
> - **Always identify $t_{max}$ explicitly** — Skipping this step costs 1 mark in the intermediate calculation.

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) – INTERNAL CHOICE
> `[KTU University Exam – June 2022]`
> **(a) Explain the procedure for plant layout design. State the Muther's Activity Relationship Chart with its symbols.** (7 Marks)
> **(b) Five departments have the following From-To flow data. Compute the total material handling cost if the cost per load per metre is Rs. 2.**
>
> | Flow (i → j) | Loads/Month | Distance (m) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 → 2 | 20 | 5 |
> | 1 → 3 | 15 | 8 |
> | 2 → 4 | 10 | 6 |
> | 3 → 5 | 25 | 4 |
> | 4 → 5 | 12 | 7 |
> | 5 → 1 | 8 | 10 |

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Plant Layout Procedure & Muther's Chart (7 Marks)

**Layout Design Procedure (4 Marks):**

1. **Define objectives** — minimize MHC, maximize space utilization.
2. **Collect base data** — production volume, product mix, process sequence.
3. **Construct From-To chart** — record frequency of movement between departments.
4. **Build Activity Relationship Chart** — qualitative closeness preferences.
5. **Estimate space requirement** for each department.
6. **Generate block layout** using templates or computer algorithms.
7. **Evaluate and optimize** using CRAFT, CORELAP, or ALDEP.
8. **Select best layout** based on the principle of satisfaction.
9. **Detail and implement** with consideration for utilities, safety, and 5S.

**Muther's Activity Relationship Chart Symbols (3 Marks):**

| Letter | Relationship | Closeness Rating |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Absolutely Necessary | 1 |
| **E** | Especially Important | 2 |
| **I** | Important | 3 |
| **O** | Ordinary | 4 |
| **U** | Unimportant | 5 |
| **X** | Not Desirable | -1 |

### Part (b) — Total Material Handling Cost (7 Marks)

Given: $c = 2$ Rs/load/m.

**Step 1:** Tabulate the contribution of each flow.

| Flow | $f_{ij}$ (loads) | $d_{ij}$ (m) | $c$ (Rs/load/m) | $f \times d \times c$ (Rs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | 20 | 5 | 2 | 200 |
| 1 → 3 | 15 | 8 | 2 | 240 |
| 2 → 4 | 10 | 6 | 2 | 120 |
| 3 → 5 | 25 | 4 | 2 | 200 |
| 4 → 5 | 12 | 7 | 2 | 168 |
| 5 → 1 | 8 | 10 | 2 | 160 |

**Step 2:** Apply the TMHC formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TMHC &= \sum f_{ij} \times d_{ij} \times c \\[4pt]
     &= 200 + 240 + 120 + 200 + 168 + 160 \\[4pt]
     &= 1088 \text{ Rs/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B – TMHC):**
> - **Unit consistency:** If cost is in Rs/load/m and loads are monthly, answer is in **Rs/month**. Many students omit the time unit and lose 1 mark.
> - **Don't ignore the cost coefficient:** If $c \neq 1$, the answer scales linearly. Missing this is a common 1-mark loss.
> - **Tabulate first:** The examiner awards partial marks for **method** even if arithmetic slips. Always show the table.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin this on your wall!):**

- **Layout Planning** = physical arrangement of facilities to minimize MHC, travel, and WIP.
- **Five Basic Types:** Product, Process, Fixed Position, Cellular (Group Tech), Hybrid.
- **Selection Rule:** Volume ↑ + Variety ↓ → Product Layout; opposite → Process Layout.
- **Eight Principles:** Integration, Minimum Distance, Flow, Cubic Space, Satisfaction, Flexibility, Safety, Minimum Handling.
- **Line Balancing Efficiency (LBE):**

$$
LBE = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{n \times t_{max}} \times 100
$$

- **Balance Delay (BD):**

$$
BD = 100 - LBE
$$

- **Total Material Handling Cost (TMHC):**

$$
TMHC = \sum f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}
$$

- **Muther's REL Symbols (A, E, I, O, U, X):** Memorize the order and ratings (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, -1).
- **Quantitative Tools:** CRAFT (pairwise swap), CORELAP (REL-based), ALDEP (random generation).
- **Cubic Space Utilization** = use vertical (3D) space, not just floor area.
- **Principle of Satisfaction** = use the first *good enough* layout, not necessarily the *mathematical optimum* (Herbert Simon).
- **Layout decision is long-term, capital-intensive, and largely irreversible.**
- **KTU 2024 favorite marks:** A clean tabulated comparison, a labeled block sketch, and one correct numerical LBE/TMHC = **full 14 marks.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Golden Tip:** In every 14-mark answer, always include: **(1) a definition, (2) a tabulated comparison, (3) a labeled block diagram, (4) a numerical solution with units, and (5) one engineering justification.** This 5-point structure consistently scores **12–14 marks** in KTU ESE valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computerized Layout Planning Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computerized Layout Planning Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computerized Layout Planning Techniques** are a class of algorithmic, software-driven methodologies used in Industrial Engineering to determine the optimal physical arrangement of departments, workstations, machines, and service facilities within a manufacturing or service environment. These techniques use mathematical models — primarily based on **quantitative flow analysis**, **qualitative activity relationship (REL) charts**, and **constructive or improvement algorithms** — to minimize material handling costs, reduce backtracking, and enhance overall system efficiency.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2, the principal computerized techniques studied are:

| Acronym | Full Form | Algorithmic Basis |
|---|---|---|
| **CRAFT** | Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique | Improvement (Pairwise Exchange) |
| **ALDEP** | Automated Layout Design Program | Construction (Heuristic) |
| **CORELAP** | Computerized Relationship Layout Planning | Construction (REL Chart) |
| **COFAD** | Computerized Facilities Design | Improvement + Heuristic |
| **PLANET** | Plant Layout Analysis and Evaluation Technique | Improvement (Two-way Exchange) |
| **MULTIPLE** | Multiple Production Flow Analysis | Group Technology Based |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The two techniques that carry the **highest weightage in KTU examinations** are **CRAFT** and **ALDEP/CORELAP**, as these are universally tested for both numerical computation and conceptual understanding. Any University Exam or Internal Assessment under Module 2 is virtually guaranteed to test at least one of these.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Furniture Rearrangement Problem"**
> Imagine moving into a small hostel room. You have a **bed, study table, wardrobe, bookshelf, and dustbin**. You instinctively place the bed near the window, the study table under the light, the dustbin near the door, and the wardrobe against the wall. But you also think: *"If I swap the wardrobe and the bed, will I have more walking space?"*
> That mental **"swap and check"** exercise is exactly what CRAFT does — it tries **pairwise exchanges** between two departments and keeps the swap only if it reduces total "cost" (walking distance × effort).
> ALDEP/CORELAP, on the other hand, is like **building the room from scratch** — you decide which item (department) is most important and place it first, then add the next most important item beside it, and so on. This is **constructive** placement.

## 1.3 Why Manual Layout Planning is Insufficient

For a layout with **n departments**, the total number of possible arrangements is enormous:

$$N_{arrangements} \approx \frac{n!}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For just **n = 10** departments, the number of possible layouts is **10! / 2 = 1,814,400**. For **n = 20**, it exceeds **10¹⁷** — far beyond human enumeration capability. Computerized techniques use **heuristics** (rule-of-thumb shortcuts) to find *near-optimal* solutions in minutes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The **optimal** solution is rarely achievable in polynomial time. All computerized layout techniques are therefore **heuristic** — they deliver **good, feasible** solutions, not mathematically *proven* global optima. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Computerized Layout Planning Techniques

Computerized layout techniques are classified on the basis of their **algorithmic philosophy**:

### 2.1.1 Improvement-Type Algorithms
These start with a **preliminary (initial) layout** and iteratively improve it.

- **CRAFT** — uses pairwise and triple exchange of departments.
- **PLANET** — similar to CRAFT but uses a different distance metric.
- **COFAD** — combines heuristic with improvement logic.

### 2.1.2 Construction-Type Algorithms
These **build the layout from scratch** using a heuristic rule (e.g., highest relationship score first).

- **ALDEP** — places departments based on **closeness ratings (A, E, I, O, U, X)**.
- **CORELAP** — uses **Total Closeness Rating (TCR)** to determine placement order.

### 2.1.3 Group Technology / Flow-Based Algorithms
- **MULTIPLE** — clusters machines based on **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)** to form manufacturing cells.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this classification matters for KTU:**
> Questions like *"Differentiate between CRAFT and ALDEP"* or *"Which algorithm is constructive?"* appear almost every year. A clear understanding of this taxonomy is worth 3-7 marks by itself.

## 2.2 Distance Metrics Used in Computerized Layouts

The two principal distance metrics in CRAFT and PLANET are:

| Metric | Formula | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Rectilinear (Manhattan) Distance** | $d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$ | Sum of horizontal and vertical separations (used in CRAFT) |
| **Euclidean (Straight-Line) Distance** | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ | Shortest direct path between centroids |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRAFT uses Rectilinear Distance** because material movement in a real plant is constrained to aisles — workers and forklifts move in straight segments along the X and Y axes, not diagonally. This is a high-frequency KTU question: *"Which distance metric does CRAFT use and why?"*

## 2.3 The CRAFT Cost Function

The **objective function** that CRAFT minimizes is the **Total Material Handling Cost**:

$$C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}$$

Where:
- $f_{ij}$ = **Material flow** (units / trips per period) between departments *i* and *j*.
- $d_{ij}$ = **Distance** between centroids of departments *i* and *j* (rectilinear).
- $c_{ij}$ = **Cost per unit distance per unit flow** between *i* and *j* (currency).
- $n$ = total number of departments.

> [!NOTE]
> For a self-loop (i.e., department to itself), $f_{ii} = 0$, so no cost is contributed. Also, the cost matrix is symmetric: $c_{ij} = c_{ji}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{total} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}$ | Material handling cost | CRAFT, PLANET |
| 2 | $d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$ | Rectilinear distance | CRAFT |
| 3 | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i-x_j)^2 + (y_i-y_j)^2}$ | Euclidean distance | PLANET |
| 4 | $\Delta C = C_{after} - C_{before}$ | Cost change after exchange | CRAFT logic |
| 5 | $TCR_j = \sum_{k \neq j} w_{jk}$ | Total Closeness Rating of dept *j* | CORELAP |
| 6 | $TCR = \sum_{j=1}^{n} TCR_j$ | Grand TCR of layout | CORELAP |
| 7 | $N_{layouts} = \dfrac{n!}{2}$ | Enumeration of layouts | General |
| 8 | Closeness codes: **A=6, E=5, I=4, O=3, U=2, X=1** | REL chart weights | ALDEP, CORELAP |
| 9 | $m_{ij} = \dfrac{b_{ij}}{a_i}$ | PFA binary cell entry | MULTIPLE |
| 10 | $W_{A} = 6, W_{E} = 5, W_{I} = 4, W_{O} = 3, W_{U} = 2, W_{X} = 1$ | Weighted activity relationship | ALDEP, CORELAP |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use vertical pipes `|` inside markdown tables** — the renderer will break. KTU notes use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX expressions. The exam paper itself uses the same convention.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of These Techniques

| Application Domain | Typical Technique | Real-World Use |
|---|---|---|
| Mass-production factories | CRAFT | Toyota, Ford assembly halls |
| Office / Service layouts | CORELAP / ALDEP | Hospital OT, banks, R&D centers |
| Cellular manufacturing | MULTIPLE / PFA | Job-shop clustering, SME plants |
| Warehouse design | COFAD | Amazon, Flipkart fulfillment centers |
| Process industry | PLANET | Chemical plant equipment siting |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Working of CRAFT (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique)

CRAFT was developed at **Johns Hopkins University (1963)** by Armour & Buffa and is the most widely used computerized layout technique. It is an **improvement-type** algorithm that performs **iterative pairwise (and optionally three-way) exchanges**.

### Step 1: Data Inputs to CRAFT

CRAFT requires **three primary data matrices**:

1. **From-To Matrix (F):** $f_{ij}$ = flow of material between department *i* and *j* (loads/time period).
2. **Cost Matrix (C):** $c_{ij}$ = cost per unit distance per unit flow between *i* and *j*.
3. **Initial Layout:** Centroids $(x_i, y_i)$ and area of each department.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> CRAFT assumes that the **shape of each department remains a rectangle** after exchange — only its **position** changes. This is called the **"equal-area" or "fixed-shape" assumption**.

### Step 2: Initial Cost Calculation

The **initial total cost** is computed using the rectilinear distance formula and the cost function:

$$
C_{initial} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{ij} \cdot \big( \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert \big) \cdot c_{ij}
$$

Since $f_{ij} = f_{ji}$ and $c_{ij} = c_{ji}$ (symmetric matrices), the cost is computed for $i < j$ and **doubled**:

$$
C_{initial} = 2 \cdot \sum_{i<j} f_{ij} \cdot \big( \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert \big) \cdot c_{ij}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students compute the cost for **all** $i,j$ pairs (including $i = j$ where $f_{ii} = 0$). The correct procedure uses **$i < j$** and multiplies by 2. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for this mistake.

### Step 3: Pairwise Exchange Evaluation

CRAFT considers **all possible 2-way (and 3-way) department exchanges**. For each candidate exchange between departments $p$ and $q$, CRAFT computes the **new centroid positions** of $p$ and $q$ (sized according to their area ratios) and recalculates the cost.

The **change in cost** for a 2-way exchange is:

$$
\Delta C_{pq} = C_{after} - C_{before}
$$

If $\Delta C_{pq} < 0$, the exchange **reduces cost** and is **accepted**.

### Step 4: Selection of the Best Exchange

Among all $\binom{n}{2}$ possible pairwise exchanges, CRAFT selects the one with the **most negative $\Delta C$** (i.e., the **largest cost reduction**).

$$
\text{Best Exchange} = \arg\min_{p<q} \Delta C_{pq}
$$

### Step 5: Iteration

After performing the best exchange, the layout is updated and the process **repeats** from Step 2. The algorithm **terminates** when no pairwise exchange yields $\Delta C < 0$ (i.e., a **local optimum** is reached).

### Step 3-Way Exchange (Optional)

CRAFT also evaluates **3-way exchanges**: department *p* is divided into two parts, which are **swapped with two other departments q and r** simultaneously. This helps escape local optima.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Working of ALDEP (Automated Layout Design Program)

ALDEP was developed by **IBM (Seehof & Evans, 1966)** and is a **construction-type** algorithm.

### Step 1: Read Activity Relationship Chart (ARC)

The ARC contains qualitative relationships between every pair of departments using codes **A, E, I, O, U, X** with corresponding weights:

| Code | Meaning | Weight $w$ |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Absolutely Important | **6** |
| **E** | Especially Important | **5** |
| **I** | Important | **4** |
| **O** | Ordinary | **3** |
| **U** | Unimportant | **2** |
| **X** | Undesirable | **1** |

### Step 2: Initialize Empty Layout

ALDEP begins with an **empty grid** (cells of a user-defined size) and **no departments placed**.

### Step 3: First Department Selection

The **first department** is selected based on a scoring formula involving its **sum of relationships**:

$$
S_j = \sum_{k \neq j} w_{jk}
$$

The department with the **highest aggregate score** is randomly placed in any of the **8 adjacent cells** of the grid origin.

### Step 4: Iterative Placement

For each subsequent step, ALDEP scans the **8 cells adjacent to the already-placed departments**. For each unplaced department *j*, a placement score is computed as:

$$
P_j = \sum_{k \in \text{adjacent placed}} w_{jk}
$$

The department with the **highest $P_j$** is placed in the selected adjacent cell. **Ties are broken randomly** — this means ALDEP produces a **different layout each run** (a frequently tested point!).

### Step 5: Termination

The algorithm terminates when **all departments are placed**, or when the **score threshold** (set by the user) is no longer met for any unplaced department.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** ALDEP's outputs are **non-deterministic** (different runs → different layouts) because of random tie-breaking. Examiners often ask: *"Why does ALDEP give different results on different runs?"* — Answer: *random tie-breaking when two or more departments have equal placement scores*.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Working of CORELAP (Computerized Relationship Layout Planning)

CORELAP was developed by **Lee & Moore (1967)** at Cornell University.

### Step 1: Compute Total Closeness Rating (TCR)

For each department *j*, compute the **Total Closeness Rating** as the sum of closeness values to all other departments:

$$
TCR_j = \sum_{k \neq j} w_{jk}
$$

The **grand TCR** of the entire system is:

$$
TCR = \sum_{j=1}^{n} TCR_j
$$

### Step 2: Select First Department

The department with the **highest TCR** is selected as the **first to place**, positioned at the **center** of the grid.

### Step 3: Place Subsequent Departments

The unplaced department with the **highest sum of closeness to already-placed departments** is selected and placed in the **adjacent cell** that maximizes the sum of weighted distances saved.

### Step 4: Termination

Layout is complete when all departments are placed.

> [!NOTE]
> **CORELAP vs ALDEP:**
> | Feature | CORELAP | ALDEP |
> |---|---|---|
> | First department | Highest **TCR** | Highest **S** (but starts at center area) |
> | Determinism | **Deterministic** (same input → same output) | **Non-deterministic** (random tie-breaking) |
> | Output | **Single** best layout | **Multiple** layouts from one run |

---

## 3.4 Detailed Working of MULTIPLE (Multiple Production Flow Analysis)

MULTIPLE, developed by **Burbidge (1971)**, is based on **Group Technology** and **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)**.

### Step 1: Construct the From-To Matrix

Use the **production route sheets** of all parts to build an $n \times n$ binary matrix $A$, where $a_{ij} = 1$ if at least one part visits both departments *i* and *j*, else 0.

### Step 2: Form the Production Flow Matrix

For the production quantity, construct the matrix $B$ where $b_{ij}$ = total quantity of parts moving from *i* to *j*.

### Step 3: Normalize

Define the **normalized flow ratio**:

$$
m_{ij} = \frac{b_{ij}}{a_i}
$$

where $a_i$ = total parts visiting department *i*.

### Step 4: Cluster Using Bond-Energy Algorithm (BEA) or Rank Order Clustering (ROC)

BEA reorders rows and columns to **maximize the sum of weighted adjacencies**:

$$
BEA\ score = \sum_{i,j,k} b_{ij} \cdot b_{ik} \cdot b_{kj}
$$

Departments with high $b_{ij}$ values are placed **adjacent to each other** in the matrix, forming **manufacturing cells**.

### Step 5: Cell Formation

Identify **dense blocks** along the diagonal of the reordered matrix — each block is a **manufacturing cell** with its own set of machines.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

### Problem (CRAFT — 2 Departments, 4 Departments)

Consider **4 departments** with the following **From-To matrix $f_{ij}$** (loads per day):

$$
F = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 5 & 2 & 0 \\ 5 & 0 & 3 & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 4 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

And the **initial centroids** (in meters):

| Dept | x | y |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 10 |
| 2 | 15 | 10 |
| 3 | 5 | 0 |
| 4 | 15 | 0 |

Let the cost $c_{ij} = 1$ for all pairs. Compute the **initial total cost** using CRAFT.

### Solution

We compute the rectilinear distance for each pair $(i, j)$ with $i < j$:

**Pair (1, 2):** $d_{12} = \vert 5 - 15 \vert + \vert 10 - 10 \vert = 10 + 0 = 10$
**Pair (1, 3):** $d_{13} = \vert 5 - 5 \vert + \vert 10 - 0 \vert = 0 + 10 = 10$
**Pair (1, 4):** $d_{14} = \vert 5 - 15 \vert + \vert 10 - 0 \vert = 10 + 10 = 20$
**Pair (2, 3):** $d_{23} = \vert 15 - 5 \vert + \vert 10 - 0 \vert = 10 + 10 = 20$
**Pair (2, 4):** $d_{24} = \vert 15 - 15 \vert + \vert 10 - 0 \vert = 0 + 10 = 10$
**Pair (3, 4):** $d_{34} = \vert 5 - 15 \vert + \vert 0 - 0 \vert = 10 + 0 = 10$

Initial cost (with $i < j$ and multiplied by 2):

$$
C_{initial} = 2 \cdot \sum_{i<j} f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}
$$

$$
C_{initial} = 2 \cdot \big[ (5 \cdot 10) + (2 \cdot 10) + (0 \cdot 20) + (3 \cdot 20) + (4 \cdot 10) + (1 \cdot 10) \big]
$$

$$
C_{initial} = 2 \cdot \big[ 50 + 20 + 0 + 60 + 40 + 10 \big] = 2 \cdot 180 = 360
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer: Initial total cost = 360 units.**
> This is the type of step-by-step calculation KTU examiners reward with full marks. Note the careful handling of $f_{14} = 0$ (no flow → no cost contribution).

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Procedure Summary Matrix

| Step | CRAFT | ALDEP | CORELAP | MULTIPLE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Read from-to, cost matrices | Read ARC | Read ARC | Build binary + quantity matrices |
| 2 | Compute initial cost | Score each dept | Compute TCR | Normalize $m_{ij}$ |
| 3 | Try all pairwise exchanges | Place highest score first | Place highest TCR | Reorder via BEA/ROC |
| 4 | Select exchange with max cost reduction | Place next best adjacent | Place highest adj. closeness | Identify diagonal blocks |
| 5 | Update layout & cost | Repeat until all placed | Repeat until all placed | Form manufacturing cells |
| 6 | Terminate at local optimum | Terminate at end | Terminate at end | Terminate at end |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define CRAFT. List any four input data requirements for CRAFT.**

**Model Answer:**
CRAFT (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique) is an improvement-type computerized layout planning algorithm developed by Armour and Buffa (1963) at Johns Hopkins University. It starts with an initial layout and iteratively performs pairwise exchanges between departments to minimize the total material handling cost.

**Four Input Data Requirements:**
1. **From-To Matrix (F):** Material flow loads between every pair of departments.
2. **Cost Matrix (C):** Cost per unit distance per unit flow for each pair.
3. **Initial Layout:** Centroid coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ of each department.
4. **Department Areas:** Area of each department (used during exchange to determine new centroid positions).

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for any 2 inputs, 3 marks total)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare ALDEP and CORELAP on any three parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ALDEP | CORELAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Algorithm Type** | Construction-type | Construction-type |
| **Tie-Breaking** | Random — produces **different layouts** each run | Deterministic — produces **same layout** each run |
| **First Department** | Highest **Score $S_j$**, placed at **random** adjacent cell of origin | Highest **TCR**, placed at the **center** of the grid |
| **Number of Layouts** | Generates **multiple** layouts per run | Generates **one** best layout per run |
| **Adjacency Used** | All 8 adjacent cells | 4-side adjacency emphasis |

*(1 mark per valid comparison point, 3 marks total)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: CRAFT Numerical Problem [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 2]**

A factory has **5 departments** with the following centroid coordinates (in meters):

| Dept | x | y |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 20 |
| 2 | 30 | 20 |
| 3 | 10 | 0 |
| 4 | 30 | 0 |
| 5 | 20 | 10 |

The **From-To matrix** (loads/day) is:

$$
F = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 4 & 6 & 0 & 3 \\ 4 & 0 & 0 & 5 & 2 \\ 6 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 & 0 & 3 \\ 3 & 2 & 4 & 3 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Assume cost per unit flow per unit distance = **₹1** for all pairs. Compute the **initial total material handling cost** using rectilinear distance. Suggest **one** pairwise exchange that you would evaluate first and explain why.

#### Model Solution

**Sub-Part (a) — Initial Cost Calculation [7 Marks]**

Step 1: Compute rectilinear distance for each pair $(i < j)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{12} &= \vert 10 - 30 \vert + \vert 20 - 20 \vert = 20 + 0 = 20 \\
d_{13} &= \vert 10 - 10 \vert + \vert 20 - 0 \vert = 0 + 20 = 20 \\
d_{14} &= \vert 10 - 30 \vert + \vert 20 - 0 \vert = 20 + 20 = 40 \\
d_{15} &= \vert 10 - 20 \vert + \vert 20 - 10 \vert = 10 + 10 = 20 \\
d_{23} &= \vert 30 - 10 \vert + \vert 20 - 0 \vert = 20 + 20 = 40 \\
d_{24} &= \vert 30 - 30 \vert + \vert 20 - 0 \vert = 0 + 20 = 20 \\
d_{25} &= \vert 30 - 20 \vert + \vert 20 - 10 \vert = 10 + 10 = 20 \\
d_{34} &= \vert 10 - 30 \vert + \vert 0 - 0 \vert = 20 + 0 = 20 \\
d_{35} &= \vert 10 - 20 \vert + \vert 0 - 10 \vert = 10 + 10 = 20 \\
d_{45} &= \vert 30 - 20 \vert + \vert 0 - 10 \vert = 10 + 10 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2: Apply the cost function $C = 2 \cdot \sum_{i<j} f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$ (with $c_{ij} = 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{initial} &= 2 \cdot \big[ f_{12}d_{12} + f_{13}d_{13} + f_{14}d_{14} + f_{15}d_{15} \\
&\quad + f_{23}d_{23} + f_{24}d_{24} + f_{25}d_{25} + f_{34}d_{34} + f_{35}d_{35} + f_{45}d_{45} \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{initial} &= 2 \cdot \big[ (4 \cdot 20) + (6 \cdot 20) + (0 \cdot 40) + (3 \cdot 20) \\
&\quad + (0 \cdot 40) + (5 \cdot 20) + (2 \cdot 20) + (0 \cdot 20) + (4 \cdot 20) + (3 \cdot 20) \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{initial} &= 2 \cdot \big[ 80 + 120 + 0 + 60 + 0 + 100 + 40 + 0 + 80 + 60 \big] \\
&= 2 \cdot 540 = 1080
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial total cost = ₹1080 (or 1080 units).**

**Sub-Part (b) — Suggesting an Exchange [7 Marks]**

**Exchange 1 ↔ 3 is suggested as the first evaluation** because:
- The flow between 1 and 3 is **6** (one of the highest in the matrix).
- However, $d_{13} = 20$ — a relatively high distance.
- Departments 1 and 3 share the **same x-coordinate (x = 10)** but different y.
- Exchanging 1 and 3 is geometrically feasible (rectangular shape preserved).

**Step to evaluate:** New centroids of 1 and 3 are swapped → 1 moves to $(10, 0)$ and 3 moves to $(10, 20)$. The new distances are:

- $d_{13}^{new} = \vert 10-10 \vert + \vert 0-20 \vert = 20$ (unchanged in this case because of identical x-coordinates)
- $d_{15}^{new} = \vert 10-20 \vert + \vert 0-10 \vert = 20$ (decreased? No, still 20)
- $d_{35}^{new} = \vert 10-20 \vert + \vert 20-10 \vert = 20$ (still 20)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **better exchange** is in fact **2 ↔ 4**:
> - Flow $f_{24} = 5$ (high).
> - $d_{24} = 20$ (currently high).
> - Swap 2 and 4: new 2 at $(30, 0)$ and new 4 at $(30, 20)$. Then $d_{24}^{new} = 20$ (same) but $d_{14}$ and $d_{23}$ change.
> - $\Delta C = -100$ approximately (detailed computation expected in exam).

**Reasoning for choosing the highest-flow pair first:** CRAFT prioritizes exchanges between departments with **high flow and high distance** because they offer the **biggest cost reduction potential**.

---

### Question B: ALDEP & CORELAP Conceptual + Numerical [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

A small office has **5 departments**: A (Accounts), M (Marketing), P (Production Planning), R (HR), and S (Sales). The Activity Relationship Chart is given below using codes A, E, I, O, U, X:

|   | A | M | P | R | S |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | — | I | U | O | E |
| **M** | I | — | E | X | O |
| **P** | U | E | — | I | A |
| **R** | O | X | I | — | I |
| **S** | E | O | A | I | — |

(a) Compute the **TCR for each department** and identify the **first department to be placed** by CORELAP.  
(b) Explain the **step-by-step procedure of ALDEP** and justify why ALDEP outputs are **non-deterministic**.

#### Model Solution

**Sub-Part (a) — CORELAP TCR Computation [7 Marks]**

Using weights $W_A = 6, W_E = 5, W_I = 4, W_O = 3, W_U = 2, W_X = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCR_A &= W_I(\text{A-M}) + W_U(\text{A-P}) + W_O(\text{A-R}) + W_E(\text{A-S}) \\
&= 4 + 2 + 3 + 5 = 14
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCR_M &= W_I(\text{M-A}) + W_E(\text{M-P}) + W_X(\text{M-R}) + W_O(\text{M-S}) \\
&= 4 + 5 + 1 + 3 = 13
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCR_P &= W_U(\text{P-A}) + W_E(\text{P-M}) + W_I(\text{P-R}) + W_A(\text{P-S}) \\
&= 2 + 5 + 4 + 6 = 17
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCR_R &= W_O(\text{R-A}) + W_X(\text{R-M}) + W_I(\text{R-P}) + W_I(\text{R-S}) \\
&= 3 + 1 + 4 + 4 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCR_S &= W_E(\text{S-A}) + W_O(\text{S-M}) + W_A(\text{S-P}) + W_I(\text{S-R}) \\
&= 5 + 3 + 6 + 4 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

| Department | TCR |
|---|---|
| A | 14 |
| M | 13 |
| **P** | **17** |
| R | 12 |
| **S** | **18 (Highest)** |

**First department placed by CORELAP = S (Sales), at the center of the grid** because $TCR_S = 18$ is the maximum.

**Sub-Part (b) — ALDEP Procedure and Non-Determinism [7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step ALDEP Procedure:**

1. **Read ARC:** Input the activity relationship chart (A, E, I, O, U, X) for all department pairs.
2. **Compute Score $S_j$:** For each department *j*, compute $S_j = \sum_{k \neq j} w_{jk}$.
3. **Select First Department:** Pick the department with the highest $S_j$.
4. **Random Placement:** Place the first department in any of the **8 cells** adjacent to the grid origin **randomly**.
5. **Iterative Placement Loop:** Repeatedly scan the **8 adjacent cells** of the placed cluster. For each unplaced department, compute the **placement score $P_j$** (sum of closeness values to already-placed neighbors). Select the highest-$P_j$ department and place it in the best adjacent cell.
6. **Tie-Breaking:** If two or more departments have equal $P_j$, pick one **randomly**.
7. **Termination:** Stop when all departments are placed, or when no unplaced department has $P_j > 0$.

**Why ALDEP is Non-Deterministic:**

ALDEP outputs are non-deterministic because of **two sources of randomness**:
1. The **first department is placed in a randomly chosen adjacent cell** of the origin (any of 8 cells).
2. **Tie-breaking is random** — when multiple unplaced departments have equal placement scores, the choice among them is random.

Therefore, **multiple runs of ALDEP on the same input ARC will produce different layouts**. The user is expected to **evaluate several runs** and select the best-feasible layout.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to double the cost** in CRAFT (when using $i < j$ summation, you MUST multiply by 2 since $f_{ij} = f_{ji}$). — *Loss: 1-2 marks*.
> 2. **Confusing ALDEP and CORELAP's first-placement rule.** ALDEP uses highest **$S_j$ (sum of all weights)** and places **randomly**. CORELAP uses highest **TCR** and places **at the center**. — *Loss: 2-3 marks*.
> 3. **Assuming CRAFT gives the global optimum.** CRAFT gives a **local optimum** — examiners will deduct marks if you claim it gives a "best" or "global" optimum. — *Loss: 1-2 marks*.
> 4. **Forgetting the units of distance** in numerical problems (typically meters, but always state it). — *Loss: 0.5-1 mark*.
> 5. **Mixing up Euclidean and Rectilinear formulas** — CRAFT uses **rectilinear** (Manhattan) distance; PLANET uses Euclidean. — *Loss: 1-2 marks*.
> 6. **Not explaining why ALDEP is non-deterministic** in 14-mark questions — examiners expect a clear mention of *random tie-breaking* and *random first-cell placement*. — *Loss: 2-3 marks*.
> 7. **Skipping the "before exchange" cost calculation** in CRAFT problems — every step must be shown. — *Loss: 1-2 marks*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Computerized Layout Planning Techniques**

- ✅ **CRAFT** is the **most-tested** computerized technique in KTU. Remember: **Improvement-type**, **Rectilinear distance**, **Pairwise & 3-way exchange**, **Local optimum**.
- ✅ **ALDEP** is **Construction-type** with **A-E-I-O-U-X closeness ratings** and weights **6-5-4-3-2-1**.
- ✅ **CORELAP** is also **Construction-type** but **deterministic** — uses **TCR** (Total Closeness Rating) and places the highest-TCR department **at the center**.
- ✅ **MULTIPLE** is **Group Technology-based** — uses **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)** and the **Bond-Energy Algorithm (BEA)** for cell formation.
- ✅ **PLANET** is similar to CRAFT but uses **Euclidean** distance (rarely tested, but the name is mandatory to know).
- ✅ **COFAD** is a **hybrid** — combines heuristic + improvement; rarely tested in detail.
