- ✅ The cost function $C_{total} = 2 \cdot \sum_{i<j} f_{ij} \cdot d_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}$ is the **single most important formula** of this module.
- ✅ **Rectilinear distance:** $d_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$ — used in CRAFT.
- ✅ **Euclidean distance:** $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ — used in PLANET.
- ✅ CRAFT accepts an exchange only if **$\Delta C < 0$** (cost reduces).
- ✅ ALDEP is **non-deterministic**; CORELAP is **deterministic**.
- ✅ Always show: (i) initial cost, (ii) candidate exchange, (iii) new centroids, (iv) new cost, (v) $\Delta C$, (vi) decision.
- ✅ **CRAFT result = Local optimum, not global optimum** — a frequently asked conceptual question.
- ✅ Closeness codes: **A (6), E (5), I (4), O (3), U (2), X (1)** — memorize this **forward and backward**.
- ✅ KTU 14-mark Part B questions on this module are **numerical-heavy** for CRAFT and **conceptual-numerical hybrid** for ALDEP/CORELAP.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production Planning and Control: Functions and Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production Planning and Control: Functions and Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Production Planning and Control (PPC)** is the administrative and engineering procedure that plans, directs, schedules, and controls the material, machinery, manpower, and supporting services of an enterprise to achieve a desired quantity and quality of output at a predetermined time and at the lowest possible cost. It is the nerve center of any manufacturing organization and bridges the gap between planning (the desk job) and execution (the shop floor job).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** PPC is a system that integrates **forecasting, capacity planning, master production scheduling, material requirement planning, routing, scheduling, dispatching, expediting, and corrective action** into a unified, closed-loop control framework.

The system operates on a closed feedback loop:

$$\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Route} \rightarrow \text{Sequence} \rightarrow \text{Schedule} \rightarrow \text{Dispatch} \rightarrow \text{Execute} \rightarrow \text{Feedback} \rightarrow \text{Corrective Action}$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are organizing a **grand college fest** in your campus.

- **Planning** → Deciding what events to conduct, how many stalls, and the budget.
- **Routing** → Mapping out the exact path each volunteer must take (e.g., who sets up the stage first, who handles registration).
- **Scheduling** → Deciding that the DJ starts at 5:00 PM, the band at 6:30 PM, and food stalls open at 4:00 PM.
- **Dispatching** → Actually calling the sound engineer and saying, "Sir, please start setting up now."
- **Expediting / Follow-up** → Walking around mid-event to check if the registration desk is overwhelmed and shifting volunteers there.
- **Inspection** → Checking that the sound system is loud enough, the food is hygienic, and stage lights are working.

If any of these steps is missed, the fest collapses — delays, chaos, unhappy crowd. **PPC does exactly this for a factory**, ensuring raw materials arrive on time, machines are loaded, workers know their jobs, and the finished product leaves the gate on the promised date.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol / Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Production Lead Time (LT)** | $L$ (days) | Total time from order receipt to dispatch |
| **Throughput Time** | $T_p$ | Time a unit spends inside the system |
| **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** | $W$ | Units partially completed at a point in time |
| **Machine Utilization (η)** | $\eta$ | Ratio of productive time to available time |
| **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | $\geq 95\%$ | Industry benchmark for a healthy PPC system |
| **Setup / Changeover Time** | $T_{su}$ | Time to switch a machine from one product to another |

> [!NOTE]
> The **95% On-Time Delivery** figure and **80–85% machine utilization** as a practical ceiling are universally accepted KTU-board industry standards. Aiming for 100% utilization always backfires (starves downstream stations).

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Focus Areas (Module 2):**
> 1. **Functions of PPC** — Planning, Routing, Scheduling, Dispatching, Expediting, Inspection.
> 2. **Objectives of PPC** — Cost, quality, delivery, flexibility, productivity.
> 3. **Types of Production systems** in which PPC operates (job, batch, mass, continuous, project).
> 4. **PPC in different industries** — manufacturing vs. service.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Canonical Functions of PPC (Step-by-Step Logic)

PPC is decomposed into **six sequential, interdependent functions**. Each feeds the next; failure in one cascades downstream.

### Function 1 — **Planning (Forecasting & Capacity Planning)**
- **What:** Decide *what* to produce, *how much*, *when*, and *with what resources*.
- **Why:** Without a plan, every other function is shooting in the dark.
- **How:** Uses sales forecasts, demand aggregation, and capacity analysis (machine-hours, man-hours available vs. required).
- **Inputs:** Market demand, inventory position, customer orders, available capacity.
- **Outputs:** Master Production Schedule (MPS), resource plan, budget.

### Function 2 — **Routing**
- **What:** Determine the **path** a job will travel through the factory — machine, sequence, tools, fixtures.
- **Why:** Multiple machines can do the same job; routing picks the most efficient one.
- **How:** Process sheets, operation analysis, machine capability charts.
- **Output:** Route sheet / operation sheet for every part number.

### Function 3 — **Scheduling**
- **What:** Fix the *time* at which each operation begins and ends.
- **Why:** Resources are shared; we must avoid clashes.
- **How:** Gantt charts, machine loading charts, mathematical models (Johnson's rule, CDS algorithm, priority rules).
- **Output:** Detailed timetable for every workstation.

### Function 4 — **Dispatching**
- **What:** **Authorize** the scheduled work to actually start on the shop floor. Issue the orders, draw the materials, release the tools.
- **Why:** A schedule is a piece of paper until someone *releases* it.
- **How:** Dispatch slips, move orders, work orders, and material requisitions.
- **Output:** Active jobs running on machines.

### Function 5 — **Expediting / Follow-up**
- **What:** Track progress, chase bottlenecks, push lagging jobs, and remove obstacles.
- **Why:** Reality always deviates from the plan (machine breakdown, absenteeism, material shortage).
- **How:** Progress reports, exception reports, daily floor walks, ERP dashboards.
- **Output:** Corrective actions and updated schedules.

### Function 6 — **Inspection / Corrective Action**
- **What:** Compare actual output against the standard; identify deviations; close the loop.
- **Why:** Without feedback, the system cannot self-correct.
- **How:** Statistical Process Control (SPC), quality audits, rework/reject analysis.
- **Output:** Quality records, corrective action requests fed back to Planning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic (use in exam):** "**P**lease **R**ead **S**illy **D**escriptions **E**asily **I**nside" → **P**lanning → **R**outing → **S**cheduling → **D**ispatching → **E**xpediting → **I**nspection.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = T_{su} + T_{p} + T_{wt}$ | Lead Time = Setup + Processing + Wait Time | Delivery commitment |
| 2 | $T_p = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{i}$ | Total processing time across n operations | Throughput calculation |
| 3 | $W = R \times T_p$ (Little's Law) | WIP = Throughput Rate $\times$ Throughput Time | Flow shop analysis |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{productive}}{T_{available}} \times 100\%$ | Machine/operator efficiency | Capacity audit |
| 5 | $C_{u} = \dfrac{\text{Output Units}}{\text{Standard Hours Avail.}}$ | Capacity Utilization index | Comparing cells |
| 6 | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{Orders Delivered On Time}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100\%$ | Service level | Logistics KPI |
| 7 | $P_{balance} = \dfrac{\sum t_{i}}{n \times t_{max}}$ | Line balancing efficiency | Assembly line design |
| 8 | $\text{Cost/unit} = \dfrac{C_{f} + C_{v} \times Q}{Q}$ | Total cost per unit (fixed + variable) | Production cost analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value, **never** the raw pipe `|`. The KTU formula $\vert x - \bar{x} \vert$ will break a table if written as `|x - x̄|`.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Toyota's PPC system (kanban + heijunka level scheduling) is a globally emulated benchmark. Functions 1–6 above map exactly to Toyota's pillars.
- **Aerospace (HAL, Boeing):** Routing sheets for a single aircraft wing may run into thousands of operations — PPC coordinates them.
- **IT Service Industry:** Functions 1–6 apply verbatim to project management: Plan → Allocate Resources → Sequence → Assign Tasks → Track Progress → Quality Review.
- **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Demand forecasting (Function 1) and last-mile dispatching (Function 4) are PPC at massive scale.

---

## 2.4 Objectives of PPC — Detailed Analysis

A robust PPC system must simultaneously satisfy the **five cardinal objectives** below. The art of production management is **trading off** these objectives when they conflict.

| S.No. | Objective | What It Means | Trade-off With |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Right Product** | Produce what the customer orders, in the correct specification | — |
| 2 | **Right Quantity** | Match production volume to demand to avoid stock-out or overstock | Inventory holding vs. shortage |
| 3 | **Right Quality** | Conform to design and tolerance standards | Cost vs. quality |
| 4 | **Right Time** | Deliver on or before the promised date | Utilization vs. slack |
| 5 | **Right Cost** | Produce at the lowest feasible cost | Cost vs. service |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 R's of Production** — these are the most frequently asked KTU question under this topic. Memorize the table above verbatim.

**Additional Derived Objectives (frequently asked as 7-markers):**
- Minimize **work-in-progress** inventory.
- Maximize **machine and labor utilization** to an optimal (not maximum) level.
- Maintain **flexibility** to handle rush orders, design changes, and demand fluctuations.
- Improve **worker morale** through fair scheduling and stable workloads.
- Achieve **continuous flow** and eliminate bottlenecks.
- Provide a **decision support system** for top management.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Procedure Walkthrough

> Since PPC is a management and systems topic rather than a closed-form mathematical derivation, this section presents **exhaustive procedural analyses**, **analytical matrices**, and **closed-loop feedback derivations** of the most important sub-concepts. Every step is written out explicitly, in the spirit of a full KTU board answer.

---

## 3.1 Closed-Loop Feedback Derivation of the PPC System

A formal PPC system is mathematically equivalent to a **control system** with a setpoint, a sensor, a controller, and an actuator. Let us derive its behavior step by step.

### Step 1 — Define the Production Plan (Setpoint)
The planning function generates the Master Production Schedule (MPS):

$$MPS_t = D_t + S_{target} - I_{t-1}$$

where:
- $D_t$ = Forecasted demand in period $t$
- $S_{target}$ = Target safety stock
- $I_{t-1}$ = Inventory carried over from period $t-1$

### Step 2 — Translate MPS into Routing & Scheduling (Controller Output)
Routing fixes the operation sequence, scheduling fixes the timing. For a single product, the **load on machine $j$ in period $t$** is:

$$L_{j,t} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} p_{k,j} \times Q_{k,t}$$

where $p_{k,j}$ = processing time of product $k$ on machine $j$, $Q_{k,t}$ = quantity of product $k$ scheduled in period $t$, summed over $m$ products.

### Step 3 — Dispatch Releases Jobs to the Shop Floor (Actuator Signal)
The dispatch function authorizes the start of each operation:

$$\text{Dispatch issued at time } \tau \iff L_{j,\tau} \leq C_{j,\tau}$$

where $C_{j,\tau}$ is the available capacity of machine $j$ at time $\tau$. If load exceeds capacity, dispatch is **deferred** (and the system is flagged).

### Step 4 — Expediting Measures Actual Progress (Sensor Reading)
The expediter reads the **actual progress** $A_{j,t}$ at intervals and computes the deviation:

$$\Delta_{j,t} = A_{j,t} - P_{j,t}$$

where $P_{j,t}$ is the planned progress. If $\Delta_{j,t} < 0$, a **negative deviation** (behind schedule) is detected.

### Step 5 — Corrective Action Closes the Loop (Controller Update)
The corrective action updates the schedule and the plan:

$$MPS_{t+1} = MPS_t + K \times \Delta_{j,t}$$

where $K$ is a corrective gain (positive when behind schedule, negative when ahead to avoid overproduction). This is identical in form to a **Proportional-Integral controller** in classical control theory.

### Step 6 — Inspection Confirms Output Quality (Final Verification)
Output quality is checked against the standard $Q_{spec}$. Reject rate is fed back into planning as a yield adjustment:

$$\text{Effective Output} = Q_{produced} \times (1 - r_{reject})$$

The yield factor modifies the next MPS cycle, completing the **closed feedback loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> This control-theoretic framing of PPC is a KTU high-yield insight. Examiners award bonus marks when students explicitly map PPC functions to **setpoint → controller → actuator → sensor → feedback** in their answers.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of PPC Functions with Type of Production

| PPC Function | Job Production | Batch Production | Mass Production | Continuous Production | Project Production |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Planning** | Order-based, short horizon | Aggregate planning, MPS | Long-term, stable forecast | Continuous, steady-state | Milestone-based |
| **Routing** | New route per order | Standard route per batch family | Fixed production line | Fixed process flow | Engineering-heavy, custom |
| **Scheduling** | FCFS / priority rules | Johnson's rule, CDS | Line balancing, takt time | 24×7 uninterrupted | CPM / PERT networks |
| **Dispatching** | Manual, paper-based | Mix of manual + ERP | Highly automated | Fully automated DCS | Milestone release |
| **Expediting** | Very intense, every job | Periodic floor walks | Exception management | Statistical monitoring | Daily progress reports |
| **Inspection** | 100% inspection common | AQL sampling | SQC charts | Continuous sensors | Hold points / FAT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **PPC vs. Production Type** matrix is a top-3 favorite for KTU 14-mark questions. Master the columns and rows.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Application of Lead Time Formula

**Problem:** An order requires **3 operations** with processing times 45 min, 30 min, and 60 min respectively. The queue/wait times between operations are 20 min, 35 min. The machine setup takes 25 min. Find (a) the throughput time $T_p$, (b) the lead time $L$, and (c) the WIP at steady state if throughput rate is 2 units/hour.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Processing Time**

$$T_p = 45 + 30 + 60 = 135 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Total Wait Time**

$$T_{wt} = 20 + 35 = 55 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3 — Lead Time**

$$L = T_{su} + T_p + T_{wt} = 25 + 135 + 55 = 215 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hours**

$$L = \frac{215}{60} = 3.583 \text{ hours} \approx 3 \text{ h } 35 \text{ min}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Little's Law for WIP**

Throughput time $T_p = 135 \text{ min} = 2.25 \text{ hours}$. Throughput rate $R = 2$ units/hour.

$$W = R \times T_p = 2 \times 2.25 = 4.5 \text{ units (WIP)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At any instant, **4.5 units** are in the system being processed. This value is the key input to a kanban sizing calculation in real factories.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PPC as a Closed-Loop Control System (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Demand Forecast] --> B[Planning MPS]
    B --> C[Routing]
    C --> D[Scheduling]
    D --> E[Dispatching]
    E --> F[Shop Floor Execution]
    F --> G[Expediting Follow up]
    G --> H[Inspection Quality]
    H --> I{Deviation?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Corrective Action]
    J --> B
    I -- No --> K[Finished Goods Dispatch]
    K --> L[Customer]
    L --> A
```

**How to read this diagram for the exam:**
- The outer loop (A → L → A) is the **demand-driven planning cycle**.
- The inner loop (B → C → D → E → F → G → H → I → J → B) is the **closed-loop control cycle** of PPC.
- The decision diamond `I` is the **feedback comparator** that triggers corrective action whenever actual performance deviates from the plan.

---

## 4.2 Hierarchical Block Diagram of PPC Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PL[Planning Layer]
        P1[Demand Forecasting]
        P2[Capacity Planning]
        P3[Master Production Schedule]
        P1 --> P2 --> P3
    end
    subgraph EX[Execution Layer]
        R1[Routing Sheets]
        S1[Scheduling Gantt Charts]
        D1[Dispatch Orders]
        R1 --> S1 --> D1
    end
    subgraph CT[Control Layer]
        E1[Expediting]
        Q1[Quality Inspection]
        E1 --> Q1
    end
    P3 --> R1
    D1 --> E1
    Q1 --> P2
```

**Block-level architecture explained:**
- **Planning Layer** sits at the top — it owns the *what* and *when*.
- **Execution Layer** in the middle — it owns the *how* and *who*.
- **Control Layer** at the bottom — it owns the *did it happen as planned* and *is it right*.
- The two cross-layer arrows (`P3 → R1` and `D1 → E1`) represent the **information handoff** between layers; the reverse arrow (`Q1 → P2`) is the **feedback channel**.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Routing vs. Scheduling vs. Dispatching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Order Received]) --> Q1{What is the product?}
    Q1 --> R[Routing: Determine machine, sequence, tools]
    R --> Q2{When are machines free?}
    Q2 --> S[Scheduling: Assign time slot, calculate start and end]
    S --> Q3{Material available?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> D[Dispatching: Release work order to shop floor]
    Q3 -- No --> W[Hold and notify procurement]
    D --> M[Monitor via Expediting]
    M --> End([Job Complete])
    W --> S
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "Differentiate between Routing, Scheduling, and Dispatching," draw this exact decision flow and label each node. Examiners give full 7 marks for a labeled diagram alone.

---

## 4.4 Objectives of PPC — Radar-Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CORE[Core Objectives - The 5 Rs]
        R1[Right Product]
        R2[Right Quantity]
        R3[Right Quality]
        R4[Right Time]
        R5[Right Cost]
    end
    subgraph SEC[Secondary Objectives]
        S1[Minimize WIP]
        S2[Maximize Utilization]
        S3[Maintain Flexibility]
        S4[Improve Morale]
        S5[Enable Flow]
    end
    CORE --> SEC
    SEC --> GOAL([Customer Satisfaction and Profit])
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- The 5 Rs are the **must-haves** (primary).
- The secondary objectives are the **should-haves** (derived/enabling).
- Both layers converge to the single ultimate goal: **Customer Satisfaction and Profit**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions below are modeled strictly on the KTU 2024 Scheme pattern: **Part A (3 marks each)** and **Part B (14 marks each, internal choice between Question A and Question B)**. Each sub-question carries 7 marks.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Production Planning and Control. Mention any two of its objectives.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Model Paper 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Production Planning and Control (PPC) is the function of management that plans, directs, schedules, and controls the material, machinery, manpower, and processes of a manufacturing enterprise to produce the right product, in the right quantity, of the right quality, at the right time, and at the right cost.
- **Two objectives (0.75 marks each, total 1.5 marks):**
  1. To ensure **uninterrupted flow of production** by coordinating all resources.
  2. To **minimize work-in-progress and finished goods inventory** by synchronizing production with demand.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between **Scheduling** and **Dispatching** in PPC.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Scheduling | Dispatching |
|---|---|---|
| **What it does** | Decides *when* each operation starts and ends | *Authorizes* the start of the scheduled work |
| **Output** | A timetable / Gantt chart | Work orders, move orders, material requisitions |
| **Performed by** | PPC planner / scheduler | Floor supervisor / foreman |
| **Timing** | Done in advance (offline) | Done in real-time (online) |
| **Decision type** | Forward planning | Execution trigger |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Instructions modeled on KTU ESE pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the six major functions of Production Planning and Control in detail. Draw a neat block diagram showing their inter-relationships.**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 2, 14-mark compulsory]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Planning (1 mark):** The first and most strategic function. Involves demand forecasting, capacity planning, and preparation of the Master Production Schedule (MPS). Decides *what* to produce, *how much*, and *when*.
2. **Routing (1 mark):** Determines the path of manufacture — the machines, operations, sequence, tools, jigs, fixtures, and inspection points. Output: route sheet.
3. **Scheduling (1 mark):** Fixes the start and end times of each operation using techniques like Gantt charts, Johnson's rule, CDS algorithm, and priority rules.
4. **Dispatching (1 mark):** The action arm of PPC. Releases the orders, material, tools, and instructions to the shop floor. Output: work order, move order, tool order.
5. **Expediting / Follow-up (1 mark):** The chase function. Tracks actual progress, identifies bottlenecks, and triggers corrective action to bring the job back on schedule.
6. **Inspection (1 mark):** Compares actual output (quantity and quality) with the plan and the standard. Closes the feedback loop.
7. **Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```
Planning → Routing → Scheduling → Dispatching
                                           ↓
Customer ← Inspection ← Expediting ← Shop Floor
              ↑                              |
              └────── Corrective ────────────┘
```

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the major objectives of PPC. Why is "right cost" often in conflict with "right quality"?**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | 14-mark model]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Major Objectives (5 marks):**
1. **Right Product** — produce what the customer wants.
2. **Right Quantity** — avoid both shortage and overstock.
3. **Right Quality** — meet design specifications.
4. **Right Time** — honour delivery commitments.
5. **Right Cost** — produce economically.
6. **Minimize WIP** — keep the system lean.
7. **Maximize Utilization** — keep machines and people productive (to an *optimal* level, not 100%).
8. **Maintain Flexibility** — handle rush orders, design changes.
9. **Improve Worker Morale** — fair, predictable workload.
10. **Enable Continuous Flow** — eliminate bottlenecks.

**Cost vs. Quality Conflict (2 marks):**
- Higher quality often requires **better raw materials, tighter tolerances, more inspection, and more rework** — all of which raise cost.
- Conversely, cutting cost by using cheaper materials, skipping inspection, or speeding up machines usually degrades quality.
- PPC resolves this by finding the **economic quality level** — the point where the *sum* of (prevention cost + appraisal cost + failure cost) is minimized. This is called the **Quality Cost Model** and is a key KTU 2024 concept.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice for the student*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a flowchart, explain how PPC functions as a closed-loop control system. Compare PPC in job-shop production versus mass production.**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Alternative 14-mark]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Flowchart Explanation (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes. Explain that:
- Planning sets the **setpoint** (MPS).
- Routing + Scheduling + Dispatching form the **controller + actuator**.
- Expediting + Inspection form the **sensor**.
- The deviation $(\Delta = \text{Actual} - \text{Plan})$ is the **error signal**.
- Corrective action updates the plan — closing the loop.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Dimension | Job-Shop Production | Mass Production |
|---|---|---|
| **Production Volume** | Low, one-off or small batch | Very high, continuous |
| **Routing** | Unique route per order | Fixed, standardized line |
| **Scheduling** | Complex, FCFS / priority | Simple, takt-time based |
| **Dispatching** | Manual, paper-driven | Automated via PLCs / ERP |
| **Expediting** | Intensive, every job | Exception-based |
| **Inspection** | Often 100% | Statistical (SPC) |
| **Flexibility** | Very high | Very low |
| **Cost per unit** | High | Low |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**An order has three operations with processing times 40, 25, and 50 minutes. Wait times between operations are 15 and 30 minutes. Setup time is 20 minutes. If the throughput rate is 3 units/hour, calculate: (i) Total processing time, (ii) Lead time, (iii) WIP using Little's Law.**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Numerical 7-mark]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Processing times: 40, 25, 50 min
- Wait times: 15, 30 min
- Setup time: 20 min
- Throughput rate: $R = 3$ units/hour

**(i) Total Processing Time (2 marks):**

$$T_p = 40 + 25 + 50 = 115 \text{ minutes}$$

Converting to hours: $T_p = \frac{115}{60} = 1.9167 \text{ hours}$

**(ii) Lead Time (3 marks):**

$$T_{wt} = 15 + 30 = 45 \text{ minutes}$$

$$L = T_{su} + T_p + T_{wt} = 20 + 115 + 45 = 180 \text{ minutes} = 3 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) WIP using Little's Law (2 marks):**

$$W = R \times T_p = 3 \text{ units/hr} \times 1.9167 \text{ hr} = 5.75 \text{ units}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Students forget to convert minutes to hours before applying $W = R \times T_p$. Result: 5.75 becomes wrongly written as 0.1 or 345 — **deduct 2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing lead time with throughput time:** $L$ includes setup + wait + processing; $T_p$ includes *only* processing. Marks are split 50:50 in part (ii).
> 3. **In comparison answers,** never write one-line answers like "Job shop is flexible." Always give a **paired sentence** with the *opposite* extreme: "Job shop is highly flexible, while mass production is rigid." This guarantees the comparison mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Production Planning and Control**

- **Definition:** PPC = the system that plans, routes, schedules, dispatches, expedites, and inspects production to deliver the **5 R's** (Right Product, Quantity, Quality, Time, Cost).
- **6 Functions (Mnemonic: PRSDEI):** Planning → Routing → Scheduling → Dispatching → Expediting → Inspection.
- **5 R's (Memorize verbatim):** Product, Quantity, Quality, Time, Cost.
- **Key Equations (always carry):**
  - Lead time: $L = T_{su} + T_p + T_{wt}$
  - Throughput time: $T_p = \sum t_i$
  - Little's Law: $W = R \times T_p$
  - Utilization: $\eta = \dfrac{T_{productive}}{T_{available}} \times 100\%$
  - Line balance: $P_{balance} = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{n \times t_{max}}$
- **Closed-Loop Insight:** PPC is mathematically equivalent to a **proportional control system** — setpoint (MPS), error signal (deviation), controller (corrective action).
- **Production-Type Linkage:** Job → manual; Batch → Johnson's rule; Mass → takt time; Continuous → 24×7; Project → CPM/PERT.
- **Conflict to Highlight in Essays:** Cost vs. Quality, Utilization vs. Slack, Inventory vs. Shortage.
- **Diagram Power Words (use them in answers):** "closed-loop," "feedback," "setpoint," "actuator," "sensor," "5 R's," "PRSDEI."
- **Common 14-Mark Traps:** Forgetting a labeled diagram (−2 to −3 marks), writing 1-line comparisons (−1 to −2 marks), unit-mismatch in numericals (−2 marks), confusing scheduling with dispatching (−1 mark).
- **One-line Exam Opener (use it for full marks):** "Production Planning and Control is the backbone of any manufacturing enterprise, integrating forecasting, capacity planning, routing, scheduling, dispatching, expediting, and inspection into a single closed-loop system aimed at achieving the five R's of production."

---

*End of Notes — Production Planning and Control: Functions and Objectives | OEIET722 Module 2 | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Batch, Mass and Continuous Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Batch, Mass and Continuous Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Production Systems

In the realm of Industrial Engineering, a **Production System** is defined as the organized set of resources, procedures, and operations that transforms inputs (raw materials, labor, energy) into outputs (finished goods or services) with measurable economic value. The classification of production into **Batch, Mass, and Continuous Production** represents a spectrum of volume, variety, and flow characteristics that fundamentally dictates plant layout, machinery selection, inventory policy, and workforce skill requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must clearly differentiate between the three production types based on **Volume**, **Variety**, **Flow Pattern**, **Lead Time**, and **Flexibility**. Exam questions frequently ask for a comparative tabulation.

## 1.2 Batch Production — The "Baker's Dozen" Analogy

**Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
Batch Production is a manufacturing method in which a limited quantity of identical products (a *batch* or *lot*) is produced as a group, after which the production facility is reconfigured or set up to produce another batch of a different product. Production is **intermittent** and product variety is moderate.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Bakery:**
Imagine a small neighborhood bakery. On Monday, the baker produces **100 loaves of whole wheat bread** using one set of mixing bowls and ovens. On Tuesday, he stops wheat production, cleans the equipment, and produces **150 chocolate pastries** using different molds and a different oven temperature. Each "batch" is finite, the equipment setup changes between runs, and the products are varied. This is **Batch Production** in its purest form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Examples:** Pharmaceutical capsules (limited runs of specific dosages), custom-made furniture, garment manufacturing lots, bakeries, and small machine shops.

## 1.3 Mass Production — The "Assembly Line Symphony" Analogy

**Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
Mass Production is a manufacturing approach where a **high volume of standardized products** is produced using **dedicated, semi-automated machinery** arranged in a line-flow layout. Products pass sequentially through a series of operations, each performed by specialized machines or workstations.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Car Assembly Line:**
Think of the original Ford Model T factory. Thousands of identical cars roll off the line, each station performing one repetitive task (welding the chassis, painting the body, installing the engine). The line is designed to produce *one* standardized product in *enormous* quantities. There is no variety interruption — the symphony of machines plays the same tune, day in and day out. This is **Mass Production**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Examples:** Automobiles (Ford, Toyota), consumer electronics (smartphones, televisions), household appliances (washing machines), and toys.

## 1.4 Continuous Production — The "Oil Pipeline" Analogy

**Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
Continuous Production is the highest-volume, lowest-variety form of manufacturing, where the production process runs **24 × 7 × 365** without interruption. The output is typically a **commodity** (liquid, gas, powder, or sheet) that can be measured in tons, liters, or cubic meters per hour, rather than discrete units.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Refinery Pipeline:**
Imagine a crude oil refinery. Crude oil enters one end of the pipe continuously — 24 hours a day, every single day of the year. Distillation columns crack the hydrocarbons, and refined outputs (petrol, diesel, kerosene) flow out the other end without ever "stopping." Shutting down the plant is a major event costing millions of dollars. This is **Continuous Production**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Examples:** Petroleum refineries, petrochemical plants, cement manufacturing, sugar mills, paper mills, fertilizer plants, and steel rolling mills.

## 1.5 The Production Volume-Variety Spectrum (KTU Conceptual Framework)

The three production systems sit on a continuous spectrum defined by two opposing forces:

| Force | Low End | High End |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Batch (hundreds) | Continuous (millions of units) |
| **Variety** | Continuous (one commodity) | Batch (hundreds of SKUs) |
| **Standardization** | Batch (low) | Continuous (extremely high) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** As Volume ↑, Variety ↓, Setup Cost ↑, and Unit Cost ↓. This inverse relationship is a guaranteed exam question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Analytical Characteristics of Each Production System

### 2.1.1 Batch Production — Detailed Decomposition

- **Volume:** Low to medium (typically **50 – 10,000** units per batch).
- **Variety:** Moderate to high (often **10 – 100** different products per facility).
- **Layout Used:** **Process Layout (Functional Layout)** — machines grouped by type (all lathes together, all milling machines together).
- **Flow Pattern:** **Intermittent** — material moves back and forth based on routing.
- **Lead Time:** **Long and variable** (days to weeks) due to queuing between operations.
- **Setup Time:** Significant downtime between batches for **changeover**.
- **Workforce:** **Skilled operators** capable of handling multiple machines and tasks.
- **Inventory:** High **Work-in-Progress (WIP)** and finished goods inventory.
- **Capital Investment:** Low to moderate (general-purpose machines).
- **Flexibility:** **High** — easy to introduce new products.

### 2.1.2 Mass Production — Detailed Decomposition

- **Volume:** High (**100,000 – millions** of units per year).
- **Variety:** Low (typically **1 – 10** product variants, e.g., sedan/hatchback/SUV).
- **Layout Used:** **Product Layout (Line Layout)** — machines arranged in sequence of operations.
- **Flow Pattern:** **Continuous flow** with no backtracking.
- **Lead Time:** **Short and predictable** (hours to a few days).
- **Setup Time:** Minimal (designed for one product; minor adjustments only).
- **Workforce:** **Semi-skilled** operators performing repetitive tasks.
- **Inventory:** Low WIP, but high raw material buffer at start of line.
- **Capital Investment:** High (special-purpose, automated transfer lines).
- **Flexibility:** **Low** — adding a new product requires major retooling.

### 2.1.3 Continuous Production — Detailed Decomposition

- **Volume:** Extremely high (**millions of tons/liters** per year).
- **Variety:** Extremely low (**1 product** or a few highly related by-products).
- **Layout Used:** **Process Layout (for the chemical process itself)** with rigid piping connections.
- **Flow Pattern:** **Uninterrupted 24/7 flow** measured in **kg/hour** or **m³/min**.
- **Lead Time:** **Long initial ramp-up**, but instantaneous throughput once stabilized.
- **Setup Time:** Initial commissioning only; shutdowns are rare and catastrophic in cost.
- **Workforce:** Highly trained **process technicians and control room engineers**.
- **Inventory:** In-process (in-pipeline) inventory is unavoidable; finished goods stored in tanks/silos.
- **Capital Investment:** **Very high** (specialized process equipment, DCS/PLC control systems).
- **Flexibility:** **Extremely low** — the plant is purpose-built for one chemistry/flow.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Production Rate (Continuous) | $$ R_c = \frac{\text{Total Output Quantity}}{\text{Operating Time}} $$ | units/hour or tons/day | Capacity planning in continuous plants |
| Batch Cycle Time | $$ T_{batch} = T_{setup} + (N \times T_{unit}) + T_{changeover} $$ | minutes or hours | Lead time estimation in batch plants |
| Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ) | $$ Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}} $$ | units | Optimization of lot size to minimize total cost |
| Capacity Utilization | $$ \eta = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Maximum Possible Output}} \times 100\% $$ | percent (%) | OEE calculations in mass/continuous plants |
| Availability (OEE Component) | $$ A = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times 100\% $$ | percent (%) | Continuous plant downtime tracking |
| Line Balancing Efficiency | $$ E_{LB} = \frac{\sum t_i}{n \times T_{cycle}} \times 100\% $$ | percent (%) | Mass production line design |
| Production Cost per Unit | $$ C_{unit} = \frac{C_{setup} + C_{variable} \cdot Q}{Q} $$ | currency/unit | Cost comparison across production types |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:** $D$ = Annual demand, $S$ = Setup cost per batch, $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year, $d$ = Daily demand rate, $p$ = Daily production rate, $N$ = Batch size, $T_{unit}$ = Time per unit, $n$ = Number of workstations, $T_{cycle}$ = Cycle time of the line.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Formula

- The **EBQ formula** comes from setting the derivative of total cost (setup + holding) to zero, giving the *optimal* lot size that minimizes the sum of fixed and variable costs. It is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU Module 2.
- **Line Balancing Efficiency** is critical in mass production because an unbalanced line creates bottlenecks, leading to idle time and WIP buildup. The ideal is $E_{LB} = 100\%$.
- **Capacity Utilization** in continuous plants must be carefully monitored. Running at **< 85%** is often uneconomical due to the high fixed cost structure; conversely, running at **> 95%** risks unscheduled shutdowns.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern **Industry 4.0** smart factories, the boundaries between these production types are blurring through **Flexible Manufacturing Systems (FMS)** and **reconfigurable manufacturing**. A KTU engineer must recognize that selecting the wrong production type for a product results in **30 – 200% higher unit costs**, making this classification one of the most strategic decisions in industrial engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ) — The Most Probable 14-Mark Question

The EBQ model determines the *optimal batch size* that minimizes the **Total Cost (TC)** of inventory for a batch production system.

### Step 1: Define the Cost Components

The total annual cost of a batch production system comprises two parts:

$$
TC = \text{Setup Cost per Year} + \text{Holding Cost per Year}
$$

The annual setup cost is the number of setups per year multiplied by the cost per setup. If annual demand is $D$ and each batch is of size $Q$, then the number of batches per year is $\frac{D}{Q}$, so:

$$
C_{setup} = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S
$$

The average inventory held during the year is approximately $\frac{Q}{2}$ (assuming instantaneous replenishment). With holding cost $H$ per unit per year:

$$
C_{holding} = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H
$$

### Step 2: Combine into the Total Cost Equation

$$
TC(Q) = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot H}{2}
$$

### Step 3: Minimize TC by Differentiating with Respect to Q

We take the first derivative and set it equal to zero:

$$
\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
$$

### Step 4: Solve Algebraically for Q*

Rearranging the terms:

$$
\frac{H}{2} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q^2}
$$

$$
Q^2 = \frac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}
$$

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}
$$

### Step 5: Incorporate the Production-to-Demand Ratio

When production rate $p$ exceeds demand rate $d$, inventory builds up during production and depletes during non-production. The maximum inventory is no longer $Q$ but $Q \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$. Re-deriving with this correction:

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note:** The term $\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$ is a *correction factor*. When $p \gg d$ (production much faster than demand), this term approaches 1 and reduces to the basic EBQ. When $p \approx d$, the batch size grows larger because each batch must sustain sales for a longer period.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Past Year Pattern)

**Problem:** A factory uses a lathe to produce steel shafts. Annual demand $D = 8000$ shafts. Setup cost $S = \text{₹}500$ per setup. Holding cost $H = \text{₹}4$ per shaft per year. Production rate $p = 100$ shafts/day. Demand rate $d = 40$ shafts/day. The factory operates 250 days/year. Find the EBQ and the number of setups per year.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters from the problem statement.

$$
D = 8000, \quad S = 500, \quad H = 4, \quad p = 100, \quad d = 40
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the production-to-demand correction factor.

$$
1 - \frac{d}{p} = 1 - \frac{40}{100} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the EBQ formula.

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000 \times 500}{4 \times 0.6}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator.

$$
2 \times 8000 \times 500 = 8{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the denominator.

$$
4 \times 0.6 = 2.4
$$

**Step 6:** Perform the division.

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{8{,}000{,}000}{2.4}} = \sqrt{3{,}333{,}333.33}
$$

**Step 7:** Take the square root.

$$
Q^* \approx 1825.74 \approx 1826 \text{ shafts per batch}
$$

**Step 8:** Calculate the number of setups per year.

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{8000}{1826} \approx 4.38 \approx 5 \text{ setups per year}
$$

**Step 9:** Verification — compute minimum total cost.

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot S \cdot H \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)} = \sqrt{2 \times 8000 \times 500 \times 4 \times 0.6}
$$

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{19{,}200{,}000} \approx \text{₹}\,4381.78 \text{ per year}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

| Parameter | Batch Production | Mass Production | Continuous Production |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Low – Medium | High | Very High |
| **Variety** | Moderate – High | Low | Nil (single product) |
| **Plant Layout** | Process / Functional | Product / Line | Fixed-position (piping) |
| **Investment (Capital)** | Low | High | Very High |
| **Unit Cost** | High | Low | Very Low |
| **Lead Time** | Long & Variable | Short & Predictable | Continuous output |
| **Inventory (WIP)** | High | Low | In-pipeline only |
| **Workforce Skill** | High (multi-skilled) | Medium (repetitive) | Specialized (process control) |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low | Very Low |
| **Production Flow** | Intermittent | Continuous (line) | Continuous (24/7) |
| **Typical Industries** | Bakeries, Garments | Automobiles, Electronics | Refineries, Cement |
| **Control System** | Manual / ERP | PLC / SCADA | DCS / Distributed Control |
| **Setup Cost Frequency** | Every batch | Rare (model change) | Once at commissioning |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Production System Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Product Characteristics] --> B{Product Variety}
    B -->|High Variety| C{Skill Level of Workforce}
    B -->|Moderate Variety| D[Batch Production]
    B -->|Low Variety| E[Mass Production]
    C -->|Highly Skilled| F[Job Shop Production]
    D --> G[Process Layout Selected]
    D --> H[Functional Grouping of Machines]
    D --> I[General Purpose Equipment]
    D --> J[Inventory Buildup Between Batches]
    E --> K[Product Layout Selected]
    E --> L[Line Flow with Transfer Lines]
    E --> M[Special Purpose Machines]
    E --> N[High Capital Investment]
    F --> O[Continuous Production]
    O --> P[24/7 Operation Scheduled]
    O --> Q[Very High Capital]
    O --> R[Process Control by DCS]
    O --> S[Tight Quality Monitoring]
    G --> T[End: Production System Identified]
    H --> T
    I --> T
    J --> T
    K --> T
    L --> T
    M --> T
    N --> T
    P --> T
    Q --> T
    R --> T
    S --> T
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Volume-Variety Spectrum (Visual Positioning)

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Job Production] -->|Increasing Volume| Y[Batch Production]
    Y -->|Further Increase| Z[Mass Production]
    Z -->|Highest Volume| W[Continuous Production]
    X -.->|Very High Variety| VAR1[Variety: Very High]
    Y -.->|Moderate Variety| VAR2[Variety: Moderate]
    Z -.->|Low Variety| VAR3[Variety: Low]
    W -.->|Almost Zero Variety| VAR4[Variety: Zero]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Continuous Production Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RAW[Raw Material Storage] --> FEED[Feeding and Conveying System]
    FEED --> PROC[Main Process Reactor or Distillation Column]
    PROC --> QQC[Quality Check and Sampling Station]
    QQC -->|Conforms| PACK[Packaging and Storage Silo]
    QQC -->|Rejection| REJ[Recycle or Reject Stream]
    PACK --> OUT[Finished Goods Dispatch]
    CTRL[Central Control Room DCS] -.Controls.-> FEED
    CTRL -.Monitors.-> PROC
    CTRL -.Receives Data.-> QQC
    CTRL -.Schedules.-> PACK
    UTIL[Utilities: Steam Power Water] -.Supports.-> PROC
    MAINT[Maintenance Crew] -.On-call.-> PROC
    MAINT -.On-call.-> FEED
    MAINT -.On-call.-> PACK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The solid arrows represent the **physical material flow** from raw input to finished output. The dotted lines represent **information and control signals** typical of a modern DCS-based continuous plant. This dual-flow architecture (material + information) is the hallmark of Industry 4.0 continuous manufacturing.

## 4.4 Layout Diagram: Comparison of Process vs. Product Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph procL[Process Layout for Batch Production]
        L1[All Lathes Together] 
        M1[All Milling Machines Together]
        G1[All Grinders Together]
        L1 --> M1
        M1 --> G1
    end
    subgraph prodL[Product Layout for Mass Production]
        O1[Station 1: Cutting]
        O2[Station 2: Welding]
        O3[Station 3: Painting]
        O4[Station 4: Assembly]
        O5[Station 5: Testing]
        O1 --> O2
        O2 --> O3
        O3 --> O4
        O4 --> O5
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Mass Production and Continuous Production. Give two examples of products made by each method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mass Production | Continuous Production |
|---|---|---|
| Output Form | Discrete units (cars, TVs) | Bulk commodity (oil, cement) |
| Operating Hours | Usually one or two shifts (8 – 16 hrs) | 24 hours / 7 days a week |
| Measurement | Units per day | Tons / liters per hour |
| Examples | Smartphones, washing machines | Petroleum products, sugar |

**Examples (1 mark):** Mass Production – Maruti Suzuki cars, Samsung TVs. Continuous Production – Indian Oil refinery, UltraTech cement plant.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any four characteristics of Batch Production.**

**Model Answer (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks, 1 mark for coherent presentation):**

1. Production is done in **lots or batches** of limited quantity.
2. The same facility produces **different products** in different time periods.
3. **Process layout** (functional grouping of machines) is used.
4. **Setup/changeover** time is significant between batches.
5. **Work-in-Progress (WIP) inventory** is high.
6. **Lead time** is long and variable.

*(Any four well-explained points fetch full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Compare Batch Production and Mass Production under the following heads: (i) Volume, (ii) Variety, (iii) Plant Layout, (iv) Capital Investment, (v) Lead Time, (vi) Workforce Skill. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical company produces a drug in batches. Annual demand $D = 12{,}000$ units. Setup cost $S = \text{₹}1200$ per batch. Holding cost $H = \text{₹}6$ per unit per year. Production rate $p = 200$ units/day. Demand rate $d = 80$ units/day. Calculate: (i) Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ), (ii) Number of batches per year, (iii) Minimum total annual cost. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

| Head | Batch Production | Mass Production |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Volume | Low – Medium | High |
| (ii) Variety | Moderate – High | Low |
| (iii) Plant Layout | Process / Functional | Product / Line |
| (iv) Capital Investment | Low – Moderate | High |
| (v) Lead Time | Long and variable | Short and predictable |
| (vi) Workforce Skill | High (multi-skilled) | Medium (repetitive tasks) |

*(1 mark for each correctly addressed head = 6 marks; 1 mark for tabulation/presentation.)*

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $D = 12{,}000$, $S = 1200$, $H = 6$, $p = 200$, $d = 80$.

**(i) EBQ Calculation:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}}
$$

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 1200}{6 \times \left(1 - \frac{80}{200}\right)}}
$$

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{28{,}800{,}000}{6 \times 0.6}} = \sqrt{\frac{28{,}800{,}000}{3.6}} = \sqrt{8{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
Q^* = 2828.43 \approx 2828 \text{ units per batch}
$$

**(2 marks for correct EBQ with full working)**

**(ii) Number of Batches per Year:**

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{12{,}000}{2828} = 4.24 \approx 5 \text{ batches per year}
$$

**(2 marks for correct N)**

**(iii) Minimum Total Annual Cost:**

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot S \cdot H \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}
$$

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 1200 \times 6 \times 0.6}
$$

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{103{,}680{,}000} = \text{₹}\,10{,}182.34 \text{ per year}
$$

**(2 marks for correct $TC_{min}$ + 1 mark for units/interpretation)**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the characteristics of **Continuous Production** in detail. List **four industries** that use this system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A furniture manufacturer produces chairs in batches. Annual demand $D = 5000$ chairs. Setup cost $S = \text{₹}800$. Holding cost $H = \text{₹}5$ per chair per year. Production rate $p = 50$ chairs/day. Demand rate $d = 25$ chairs/day. The factory works 300 days/year. Determine the Economic Batch Quantity, optimum number of setups, and the time between two successive setups. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Characteristics of Continuous Production (5 marks):**

1. **24/7 Uninterrupted Operation:** The plant runs continuously; shutdowns are costly.
2. **Standardized Single Product:** The output is a homogeneous commodity (e.g., petrol, cement).
3. **High Capital Investment:** Special-purpose process equipment and DCS control systems.
4. **Process Layout:** Equipment connected by pipelines, conveyors, or transfer lines.
5. **Specialized Workforce:** Process technicians, control room engineers, and chemists.
6. **Low Flexibility:** The plant cannot be reconfigured for a different product.
7. **High Capacity Utilization:** Plants run at 85 – 95% utilization to be economical.
8. **Long Commissioning Time:** Months to years for initial setup and stabilization.

**Four Industries (2 marks):**
1. Petroleum Refineries (Indian Oil, BPCL)
2. Cement Plants (UltraTech, ACC)
3. Fertilizer Plants (IFFCO, FACT)
4. Paper Mills or Sugar Mills

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $D = 5000$, $S = 800$, $H = 5$, $p = 50$, $d = 25$, Working days = 300.

**(i) EBQ:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 800}{5 \times \left(1 - \frac{25}{50}\right)}} = \sqrt{\frac{8{,}000{,}000}{2.5}} = \sqrt{3{,}200{,}000}
$$

$$
Q^* \approx 1788.85 \approx 1789 \text{ chairs per batch}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**(ii) Number of Setups per Year:**

$$
N = \frac{5000}{1789} = 2.79 \approx 3 \text{ setups per year}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Time Between Successive Setups (Cycle Time):**

The cycle time is the time between two consecutive setups:

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{\text{Working Days per Year}}{\text{Number of Setups}} = \frac{300}{2.79} \approx 107.5 \text{ days}
$$

Alternatively, the production time per batch plus the consumption time can be calculated:

$$
\text{Production time per batch} = \frac{Q^*}{p} = \frac{1789}{50} = 35.78 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\text{Cycle Time} \approx 107.5 \text{ days between setups}
$$

**(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing up daily rates (p, d) with annual rates (D, S, H) — convert all to the same time frame before applying the EBQ formula. This is the #1 cause of mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the Correction Factor:** In continuous-batch hybrid systems, students often forget the $\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$ term, leading to a 50% error in the EBQ.
> 3. **Rounding Errors:** Do not round intermediate values — keep them in fraction form and only round the final answer to 2 decimal places.
> 4. **Tabulation in Part (a):** Writing long paragraphs instead of a clean comparison table costs 1 – 2 marks. Use tables!
> 5. **Definition Marks:** When asked to "differentiate," explicitly state **at least 6 distinct parameters** for full 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

- **Three Production Types:** Batch (medium vol/variety), Mass (high vol/low variety), Continuous (very high vol/zero variety).
- **Volume-Variety Inverse Law:** As volume ↑, variety ↓ (memorize the spectrum).
- **Layout Mapping:** Batch → Process Layout, Mass → Product Layout, Continuous → Fixed Position with Piping.
- **EBQ Formula (with correction):** 
$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}}
$$
- **TCmin Formula:**
$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot S \cdot H \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)}
$$
- **Capital Investment Order:** Continuous > Mass > Batch (continuous is most capital-intensive).
- **Workforce Skill Order:** Batch (multi-skilled) > Continuous (specialized) > Mass (semi-skilled repetitive).
- **Lead Time Order:** Batch (longest) > Mass (short) > Continuous (instantaneous output flow).
- **Setup Frequency:** Batch (every batch) > Mass (rarely) > Continuous (only at commissioning).
- **Flexibility Order:** Batch (most flexible) > Mass > Continuous (least flexible).
- **Memory Acronym:** **"B-Ma-C"** for Batch, Mass, Continuous in increasing order of volume and decreasing order of variety.
- **Industry Examples to Memorize:** Batch → Bakery, Garments; Mass → Cars, Phones; Continuous → Oil, Cement, Sugar.
- **For 14-mark numericals:** Always show the formula, substitute values, compute numerator and denominator separately, and box the final answer with units.

**End of Module 2 Notes — Batch, Mass and Continuous Production**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Economic Order Quantity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Economic Order Quantity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** is the **fixed order quantity** that minimizes the **Total Inventory Cost (TIC)**, defined as the sum of the **Ordering Cost** and the **Holding (Carrying) Cost**, under the assumption of **constant, independent, and deterministic demand** with **instantaneous replenishment** and **no stock-outs**.

This classical model is also known as the **Wilson-Harris Lot-Size Model** (formulated by Ford W. Harris in 1913 and refined by R.H. Wilson). It establishes the mathematically optimal trade-off between the cost of placing an order and the cost of keeping inventory in stock.

The KTU 2024 syllabus (OEIET722, Module 2) treats EOQ as a foundational deterministic inventory control technique under the umbrella of **Materials Management & Inventory Control**.

### Key Terminology (KTU Standard Nomenclature)

- **Demand (D):** The annual (or periodic) consumption rate of the inventory item, assumed **constant** and **known**.
- **Order Quantity (Q):** The lot size placed per replenishment cycle.
- **Ordering Cost ($C_o$ or S):** The cost incurred **per order** placed, including paperwork, transport, inspection, and administrative overhead. Expressed in **rupees per order (₹/order)**.
- **Holding Cost ($C_c$ or H):** The cost of carrying **one unit in stock for one year**, including warehousing, insurance, depreciation, and opportunity cost of capital. Expressed in **rupees per unit per year (₹/unit/year)**.
- **Lead Time (L):** The time gap between placing an order and receiving it. For the basic EOQ, reorder is initiated when stock equals demand during lead time.
- **Reorder Point (ROP):** The inventory level at which a new order must be triggered: $\text{ROP} = D \times L$.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Stall Balance"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (The Tea Stall Analogy):** Imagine a small tea stall in Kerala that uses **50 kg of sugar per month**. The stall owner has two opposing pressures:
> - **Ordering Too Frequently** → He pays for transport, phone calls, and time for every small purchase (high *Ordering Cost*).
> - **Ordering Too Rarely in Bulk** → He must store a huge pile of sugar, which attracts ants, occupies space, and ties up cash (high *Holding Cost*).
>
> The **EOQ** is the exact "just-right" bag size that strikes a balance — **not too small, not too big** — so that the **total of (transport cost) + (storage cost)** is at its **lowest possible value**.

### KTU 2024 Syllabus Relevance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the OEIET722 syllabus, this topic falls under **Production Planning & Control → Inventory Models → Deterministic Models**. It is a **high-weightage, high-scoring area** because it is **numerical, formula-driven, and predictable** in University exams. Mastering this single derivation guarantees marks in both Part A (definitions) and Part B (numerical problems).

### Underlying Assumptions of the Basic EOQ Model

The KTU Board expects students to **explicitly state** these assumptions in any full-mark numerical question:

1. Demand is **uniform and deterministic** (perfectly known in advance).
2. Lead time is **constant** and replenishment is **instantaneous** (infinite replenishment rate).
3. Ordering cost and holding cost are **fixed and known** (no quantity discounts, no inflation).
4. **No stock-outs are permitted** (i.e., shortages cost is zero / ignored).
5. The **entire order quantity is received in a single lot** at one instant.
6. The planning horizon is **infinite** (the model is repeated identically each cycle).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Competing Cost Components

The total inventory cost in the basic EOQ model is the sum of two opposing cost functions. Understanding the *behaviour* of each is the key to solving any derivation-based question.

#### A. Ordering Cost per Cycle
Each time an order of size $Q$ is placed, a fixed cost $C_o$ is incurred. Over the year, the number of orders is $\frac{D}{Q}$. Therefore, the **annual ordering cost** is:

$$\text{Annual Ordering Cost} = \left( \frac{D}{Q} \right) \times C_o$$

- **Behaviour:** As $Q$ increases, fewer orders are placed, so this cost **decreases hyperbolically** (proportional to $\frac{1}{Q}$).
- **Curve shape:** A downward-sloping hyperbola.

#### B. Holding (Carrying) Cost per Cycle
On average, the inventory level is $\frac{Q}{2}$ (because the stock depletes linearly from $Q$ down to $0$). Therefore, the **annual holding cost** is:

$$\text{Annual Holding Cost} = \left( \frac{Q}{2} \right) \times C_c$$

- **Behaviour:** As $Q$ increases, average inventory rises, so this cost **increases linearly**.
- **Curve shape:** An upward-sloping straight line.

#### C. Total Annual Inventory Cost (TIC)
Adding the two opposing components:

$$\text{TIC}(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q}}_{\text{Ordering}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q \cdot C_c}{2}}_{\text{Holding}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a unique minimum exist?** Mathematically, the sum of a strictly decreasing function ($\propto 1/Q$) and a strictly increasing function ($\propto Q$) must have a **single unique minimum** for some $Q > 0$. This minimum is the EOQ.

### 2.2 The Economic Order Quantity Formula

Setting the first derivative of TIC to zero (FOC for unconstrained minimum):

$$\frac{d(\text{TIC})}{dQ} = -\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^2} + \frac{C_c}{2} = 0$$

Solving yields the famous **EOQ formula**:

$$\boxed{ \; Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{ \frac{2 \, D \, C_o}{C_c} } \;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most tested formula** in OEIET722. It is also sometimes written as $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ in textbooks, where $S$ denotes $C_o$ and $H$ denotes $C_c$.

### 2.3 Associated Formulas (Essential for Full-Mark Solutions)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Economic Order Quantity** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_c}}$ | units | Optimal lot size per order |
| 2 | **Minimum Total Cost** | $\text{TIC}_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \, D \, C_o \, C_c}$ | ₹/year | Lowest possible annual inventory cost |
| 3 | **Optimal Number of Orders / Year** | $N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \sqrt{\frac{D \, C_c}{2 \, C_o}}$ | orders/yr | How many times stock is replenished |
| 4 | **Optimal Cycle Time** | $T^{*} = \frac{1}{N^{*}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, C_o}{D \, C_c}}$ | years | Time gap between consecutive orders |
| 5 | **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $\text{ROP} = D \times L$ | units | Trigger level for placing next order |
| 6 | **Average Inventory** | $\bar{I} = \frac{Q^{*}}{2}$ | units | Mean stock held across the cycle |
| 7 | **Maximum Inventory** | $I_{\max} = Q^{*}$ | units | Stock peak (just after replenishment) |
| 8 | **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost Property** | $\frac{D C_o}{Q^{*}} = \frac{Q^{*} C_c}{2}$ | ₹/yr | At EOQ, the two costs are **numerically equal** |
| 9 | **EOQ with Purchase Cost** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_c \cdot i}}$ | units | $i$ = carrying cost as a **fraction** of unit price |

> [!NOTE]
> **Property #8 is a KTU favourite trick question:** "Show that at the EOQ, the ordering cost equals the holding cost." Students often skip proving this — it carries **2 to 3 easy marks** every exam cycle.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Setting optimal production batch sizes on shop floors to minimize setup + storage costs.
- **Supply Chain & Logistics:** Determining truck-load sizes and warehouse replenishment cycles.
- **Retail & E-Commerce:** Deciding how many units of a fast-moving SKU to order from suppliers.
- **Hospital & Pharma:** Optimal reorder quantity of critical drugs and consumables.
- **Construction:** Optimal stock of cement, steel, and aggregates on site.

### 2.5 Extensions Beyond the Basic Model (Briefly)

KTU Module 2 sometimes extends EOQ to the following cases — be aware of their existence:

- **EOQ with Finite Replenishment Rate (Production Lot Size Model):** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_o}{C_c \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{P}\right)}}$, where $P$ is the production rate.
- **EOQ with Planned Shortages (Backorders):** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_o}{C_c}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_c + C_s}{C_s}}$, where $C_s$ is the shortage cost per unit per year.
- **EOQ with Quantity Discounts:** EOQ is first computed at the lowest price; if it is infeasible at that price, the optimum shifts to the **break-even quantity**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EOQ Formula

We begin with the Total Annual Inventory Cost function:

$$\text{TIC}(Q) \;=\; \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q} \;+\; \frac{Q \cdot C_c}{2}$$

**Step 1 — First-Order Condition (FOC) for a Minimum**

Differentiate $\text{TIC}(Q)$ with respect to $Q$ and set the derivative equal to zero:

$$\frac{d(\text{TIC})}{dQ} \;=\; -\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^{2}} \;+\; \frac{C_c}{2} \;=\; 0$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the Two Terms**

Rearranging the equation:

$$\frac{C_c}{2} \;=\; \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Q^{2}$**

Multiply both sides by $Q^{2}$ and then by $\dfrac{2}{C_c}$:

$$Q^{2} \;=\; \frac{2 \, D \, C_o}{C_c}$$

**Step 4 — Take the Positive Square Root**

Since order quantity $Q$ must be **strictly positive**, we take the positive root only:

$$Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \, D \, C_o}{C_c}}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the Minimum Using the Second-Order Condition (SOC)**

Differentiate the first derivative once more:

$$\frac{d^{2}(\text{TIC})}{dQ^{2}} \;=\; \frac{2 \, D \, C_o}{Q^{3}}$$

Since $D$, $C_o$, and $Q$ are all **positive real numbers** in a real inventory system, $\dfrac{d^{2}(\text{TIC})}{dQ^{2}} > 0$. This confirms that the critical point is a **local minimum**, hence the solution is a true economic optimum.

**Step 6 — Derive the Minimum Total Cost**

Substitute $Q^{*}$ back into the TIC equation. First, compute the two cost components at $Q = Q^{*}$:

$$\text{Ordering Cost at EOQ} \;=\; \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^{*}} \;=\; \frac{D \cdot C_o}{\sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_c}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot C_o \cdot C_c}{2}}$$

$$\text{Holding Cost at EOQ} \;=\; \frac{Q^{*} \cdot C_c}{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_c}} \cdot C_c \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot C_o \cdot C_c}{2}}$$

Both are **numerically identical** (this proves the "equal cost" property). Therefore:

$$\text{TIC}_{\min} \;=\; 2 \times \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot C_o \cdot C_c}{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{2 \, D \, C_o \, C_c}$$

**Step 7 — Derive the Optimal Number of Orders per Year**

$$N^{*} \;=\; \frac{D}{Q^{*}} \;=\; \frac{D}{\sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_c}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot C_c}{2 \, C_o}}$$

**Step 8 — Derive the Optimal Cycle Time (in years)**

$$T^{*} \;=\; \frac{1}{N^{*}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{D \cdot C_c}{2 \, C_o}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \, C_o}{D \, C_c}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Every transition above is algebraically explicit. The KTU evaluator awards **full marks only when all eight steps are written**, even if the student reaches the same final answer by shortcut. Skipping the SOC verification is the single most common reason students lose 1–2 marks.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**
>
> A manufacturing unit uses **10,000 units/year** of a raw material. The ordering cost is **₹200 per order** and the holding cost is **₹10 per unit per year**. Determine:
> (a) Economic Order Quantity
> (b) Number of orders per year
> (c) Minimum total inventory cost
> (d) Optimal time between two consecutive orders

**Given Data:**
$D = 10{,}000$ units/year, $\quad C_o = ₹200$/order, $\quad C_c = ₹10$/unit/year

**Solution:**

**Part (a): EOQ**

$$Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{10}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{10}} \;=\; \sqrt{400{,}000} \;=\; 632.46 \approx 633 \text{ units}$$

**Part (b): Number of Orders per Year**

$$N^{*} \;=\; \frac{D}{Q^{*}} \;=\; \frac{10{,}000}{632.46} \;=\; 15.81 \approx 16 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Part (c): Minimum Total Cost**

$$\text{TIC}_{\min} \;=\; \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200 \times 10} \;=\; \sqrt{40{,}000{,}000} \;=\; ₹6{,}324.56/\text{year}$$

**Part (d): Optimal Cycle Time**

$$T^{*} \;=\; \frac{1}{N^{*}} \;=\; \frac{1}{15.81} \text{ years} \;=\; 0.0632 \text{ years} \;\approx\; 23.1 \text{ days}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write $Q = 632$ instead of $632.46$ (rounding prematurely). Carry at least **two decimal places** until the final answer. The expected final EOQ is **either 632 or 633 units**; both are accepted, but truncation to 600 is **not**.

### 3.3 Analytical Sensitivity Matrix

The table below shows the directional impact of changing each parameter on the EOQ and TIC. This is frequently asked in **Part A (3-mark) questions** in KTU exams.

| Parameter | Parameter Increases → EOQ ($Q^*$) | Parameter Increases → TIC$_{\min}$ | Engineering Reasoning |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------------|------------------------|
| Demand ($D$) | **Increases** ($\propto \sqrt{D}$) | **Increases** ($\propto \sqrt{D}$) | More usage → bigger optimal batches and higher total cost. |
| Ordering Cost ($C_o$) | **Increases** ($\propto \sqrt{C_o}$) | **Increases** ($\propto \sqrt{C_o}$) | Each order is expensive → order bigger lots less often. |
| Holding Cost ($C_c$) | **Decreases** ($\propto 1/\sqrt{C_c}$) | **Increases** ($\propto \sqrt{C_c}$) | Storage is expensive → order smaller lots more often. |
| Lead Time ($L$) | **No effect on $Q^*$** | **No effect on TIC$_{\min}$** | Lead time only changes ROP, not the optimal lot size. |
| Unit Price (P) | No direct effect unless $C_c$ = fraction $\times P$ | Indirect (via $C_c$) | Discounted items may have lower carrying cost. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Inventory Level vs. Time Profile (Saw-Tooth Diagram)

The following Mermaid block renders a conceptual **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the EOQ cycle, mapping the cost interactions, the saw-tooth inventory profile, and the trigger points. This is the visual standard for KTU 14-mark problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Cycle: Inventory = Q*] --> B[Inventory depletes at constant rate D]
    B --> C{Monitor Stock Level}
    C -- Stock > ROP --> D[Continue Consumption]
    C -- Stock = ROP --> E[Place New Order of Size Q*]
    D --> C
    E --> F[Wait for Lead Time L]
    F --> G[Instantaneous Replenishment]
    G --> H[Inventory jumps back to Q*]
    H --> C

    subgraph "Cost Flow (parallel to cycle)"
        I[Annual Ordering Cost: D*Co / Q] -.decreases with Q.-> J[Total Cost Curve]
        K[Annual Holding Cost: Q*Cc / 2] -.increases with Q.-> J
        J --> L[Minimum at Q*]
    end

    subgraph "Key Outputs"
        M[Q* = sqrt 2 D Co / Cc]
        N[TIC min = sqrt 2 D Co Cc]
        O[N* = D / Q* orders per year]
        P[T* = 1 / N* years]
    end

    L --> M
    L --> N
    L --> O
    L --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **central loop** (A → B → C → D → C) represents the **saw-tooth inventory profile** falling from $Q^*$ to zero.
> - When stock hits **ROP = $D \times L$**, an order is triggered, and after lead time $L$, stock instantly jumps back to $Q^*$.
> - The **parallel "Cost Flow" subgraph** shows the trade-off — the two opposing cost curves intersect precisely at the minimum of the TIC envelope.

### 4.2 Cost-vs-Quantity Graph (Conceptual Sketch)

Although Mermaid cannot render 2D Cartesian plots natively, the **schematic below** mirrors the standard textbook diagram the KTU examiner expects students to redraw in their answer scripts.

| Region | Order Quantity ($Q$) | Dominant Cost | Behaviour of TIC |
|--------|----------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Far Left** ($Q \to 0$) | Tiny orders, many cycles | Ordering Cost dominates | TIC is **very high** (hyperbolic blow-up) |
| **EOQ Point** ($Q = Q^*$) | Optimal balance | **Ordering = Holding** | **TIC is minimum** |
| **Far Right** ($Q \to \infty$) | Massive orders, one cycle | Holding Cost dominates | TIC is **very high** (linear blow-up) |

```
       Cost
        |
        | \
        |  \         Holding Cost (Q*Cc/2)
   TIC  |   \         /
        |    \       /
        |     \     /
        |      \   /
        |       \_/
        |        /\  ___
        |       /  \/   \  Total Cost (TIC)
        |      /    V     \  (U-shaped)
        |     /     |      \
        |____/______|_______ \ ______ Q
            0      Q*         \
                  EOQ          \
        |    /                  \
        |   /  Ordering Cost    \
        |  /   (D*Co/Q)          \
        | /
```

> [!NOTE]
> **U-shaped Total Cost Curve:** The intersection of the *holding cost* (rising line) and the *ordering cost* (falling hyperbola) is exactly at $Q^*$. At this point, the two individual cost curves have the **same height**, which equals half of $\text{TIC}_{\min}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Economic Order Quantity" (EOQ). State any four assumptions of the basic EOQ model.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ):** It is the fixed order quantity that minimizes the total annual inventory cost, defined as the sum of ordering cost and holding (carrying) cost, under conditions of known and constant demand with instantaneous replenishment.

**Assumptions of the basic EOQ model (any four):**

1. Demand is deterministic, constant, and uniformly distributed over time.
2. Lead time is known and constant; replenishment is instantaneous.
3. Ordering cost and holding cost are fixed, known, and independent of order quantity.
4. There are no quantity discounts and no stock-outs (no shortages are allowed).
5. The entire ordered quantity is received in a single lot at one instant.
6. The planning horizon is infinite, and the model repeats identically each cycle.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Show that at the Economic Order Quantity, the annual ordering cost equals the annual holding cost.**

**Model Answer:**

The total annual inventory cost is:

$$\text{TIC} \;=\; \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q} \;+\; \frac{Q \cdot C_c}{2}$$

The EOQ is obtained by setting $\dfrac{d(\text{TIC})}{dQ} = 0$:

$$-\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^{*2}} \;+\; \frac{C_c}{2} \;=\; 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^{*}} \;=\; \frac{Q^{*} \cdot C_c}{2}$$

The **left-hand side** is the **annual ordering cost** at EOQ, and the **right-hand side** is the **annual holding cost** at EOQ. Hence both are equal at $Q = Q^*$.

$$\boxed{\text{Ordering Cost at EOQ} \;=\; \text{Holding Cost at EOQ} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot C_o \cdot C_c}{2}}}$$

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Mark Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Read Before Attempting):**
> 1. **Do not skip the assumption list** — KTU deducts 2 marks if assumptions are not stated at the start of any EOQ numerical.
> 2. **Do not write $Q^*$ directly** — show the full derivation or substitution into the formula to claim method marks.
> 3. **Rounding errors** — always carry two decimals; never round $Q^*$ to a "nice" number like 600 or 700.
> 4. **Unit mismatches** — if $D$ is monthly and $C_c$ is annual, convert consistently; mixing time units is a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Forgetting to compute $N^*$, $T^*$, and ROP** — these "side results" each carry 1–2 marks. Always compute them if the question hints at them.
> 6. **The "equal-cost property" is a free 2 marks** — write it as a verification line at the end.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]**

A company uses **24,000 units/year** of a component. The cost of placing one order is **₹300** and the holding cost is **₹15 per unit per year**. The lead time is **10 days**. Assume 300 working days in a year.

Calculate:
**(a)** The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ). (7 marks)
**(b)** The optimal number of orders, the minimum total cost, the time between orders, and the reorder point. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Given Data:**
$D = 24{,}000$ units/year, $\quad C_o = ₹300$/order, $\quad C_c = ₹15$/unit/year, $\quad L = 10$ days.

**Part (a) — EOQ Calculation (7 marks)**

Substitute into the EOQ formula:

$$Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \, D \, C_o}{C_c}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 300}{15}}$$

$$Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{14{,}400{,}000}{15}} \;=\; \sqrt{960{,}000} \;=\; 979.80 \approx 980 \text{ units}$$

Verification using the equal-cost property:

$$\text{Ordering Cost} \;=\; \frac{24{,}000 \times 300}{979.80} \;=\; ₹7{,}348.47$$

$$\text{Holding Cost} \;=\; \frac{979.80 \times 15}{2} \;=\; ₹7{,}348.47 \quad \checkmark$$

Both are equal, confirming EOQ is correct. The minimum total cost is:

$$\text{TIC}_{\min} \;=\; 2 \times 7{,}348.47 \;=\; ₹14{,}696.94/\text{year}$$

**Part (b) — Other Parameters (7 marks)**

**Optimal number of orders per year:**

$$N^{*} \;=\; \frac{D}{Q^{*}} \;=\; \frac{24{,}000}{979.80} \;=\; 24.49 \approx 25 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Optimal time between orders (cycle time):**

$$T^{*} \;=\; \frac{1}{N^{*}} \;=\; \frac{1}{24.49} \text{ years} \;=\; 0.0408 \text{ years} \;\times\; 300 \text{ days} \;\approx\; 12.25 \text{ days}$$

**Daily demand rate:**

$$d \;=\; \frac{D}{\text{working days}} \;=\; \frac{24{,}000}{300} \;=\; 80 \text{ units/day}$$

**Reorder Point:**

$$\text{ROP} \;=\; d \times L \;=\; 80 \times 10 \;=\; 800 \text{ units}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Summary for Q-A:**
> $Q^* \approx 980$ units, $N^* \approx 25$ orders/yr, $\text{TIC}_{\min} \approx ₹14{,}697$/yr, $T^* \approx 12.25$ days, $\text{ROP} = 800$ units.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

A retail outlet sells **18,000 packets/year** of a product. Each order costs **₹450** to process, and the holding cost is **20% of the unit price**. The unit price is **₹50 per packet**. Lead time is **15 days**, and the store operates **360 days/year**.

Calculate:
**(a)** The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) and the annual total cost of inventory. (7 marks)
**(b)** The reorder point, the time between two consecutive orders, and the number of orders per year. Also, briefly explain the *equal-cost property* at the EOQ. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Given Data:**
