$D = 18{,}000$ packets/year, $\quad C_o = ₹450$/order, $\quad P = ₹50$/packet, $\quad C_c = 0.20 \times 50 = ₹10$/packet/year, $\quad L = 15$ days.

**Part (a) — EOQ and Annual Total Cost (7 marks)**

$$Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 450}{10}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{16{,}200{,}000}{10}} \;=\; \sqrt{1{,}620{,}000} \;=\; 1{,}272.79 \approx 1{,}273 \text{ packets}$$

Annual ordering cost:

$$\text{OC} \;=\; \frac{18{,}000 \times 450}{1{,}272.79} \;=\; ₹6{,}363.96$$

Annual holding cost:

$$\text{HC} \;=\; \frac{1{,}272.79 \times 10}{2} \;=\; ₹6{,}363.96 \quad \checkmark$$

Annual purchase cost (often asked as part of total cost):

$$\text{PC} \;=\; D \times P \;=\; 18{,}000 \times 50 \;=\; ₹9{,}00{,}000$$

Total annual cost (inventory + purchase):

$$\text{TC} \;=\; \text{OC} + \text{HC} + \text{PC} \;=\; 6{,}363.96 + 6{,}363.96 + 9{,}00{,}000 \;=\; ₹9{,}12{,}727.92$$

**Part (b) — ROP, Cycle Time, and Equal-Cost Property (7 marks)**

Daily demand:

$$d \;=\; \frac{18{,}000}{360} \;=\; 50 \text{ packets/day}$$

**Reorder Point:**

$$\text{ROP} \;=\; 50 \times 15 \;=\; 750 \text{ packets}$$

**Time between two consecutive orders:**

$$T^{*} \;=\; \frac{Q^{*}}{D} \;=\; \frac{1{,}272.79}{18{,}000} \text{ years} \;=\; 0.0707 \text{ years} \;\times\; 360 \;\approx\; 25.46 \text{ days}$$

**Number of orders per year:**

$$N^{*} \;=\; \frac{D}{Q^{*}} \;=\; \frac{18{,}000}{1{,}272.79} \;=\; 14.14 \approx 15 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Equal-Cost Property Explanation:**

At the EOQ, the first derivative of the total cost with respect to $Q$ is zero. This algebraically implies:

$$\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^{*}} \;=\; \frac{Q^{*} \cdot C_c}{2}$$

That is, the **annual ordering cost exactly equals the annual holding cost**. Each equals $\sqrt{\dfrac{D C_o C_c}{2}}$. Beyond EOQ, holding dominates (overstocking); below EOQ, ordering dominates (too many small orders). The equality therefore marks the unique cost-minimising lot size.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Summary for Q-B:**
> $Q^* \approx 1{,}273$ packets, $\text{TC} \approx ₹9{,}12{,}728$/yr, $\text{ROP} = 750$ packets, $T^* \approx 25.46$ days, $N^* \approx 15$ orders/yr.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for the KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- **The Master Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o / C_c}$. Memorize it verbatim — units are *units*, not ₹ or years.
- **Six Assumptions:** Constant demand, instantaneous replenishment, no stock-outs, no discounts, known and fixed $C_o$ & $C_c$, single-lot receipt. **Always list them in 14-mark answers.**
- **Equal-Cost Property:** At EOQ, annual ordering cost = annual holding cost. Verify by substitution — earns **2 free marks**.
- **Companion Formulae:** $\text{TIC}_{\min} = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_c}$, $\;N^* = D/Q^*$, $\;T^* = 1/N^*$, $\;\text{ROP} = d \times L$.
- **Sensitivity Direction:** ↑ $D$ or ↑ $C_o$ ⇒ ↑ $Q^*$. ↑ $C_c$ ⇒ ↓ $Q^*$. Lead time affects only ROP, **never** EOQ.
- **Unit Consistency Rule:** If $D$ is in *units/month* and $C_c$ is in *₹/unit/year*, convert $D$ to yearly (×12) **first**, then plug into the formula.
- **Common Distractor:** EOQ with a **purchase cost as a fraction of price**: replace $C_c$ with $i \times P$, where $i$ is the holding-cost percentage and $P$ is the unit price.
- **Time Conversion:** $1 \text{ year} = 12 \text{ months} = 52 \text{ weeks} = 300 \text{ to } 365 \text{ days}$ (use the value given in the problem; do not assume).
- **Rounding Rule:** Keep at least **two decimal places** through all intermediate steps; round only the final answer.
- **Avoid These Phrases:** "Q = 600 approximately" (over-rounded), "TIC is minimum" (must specify at what $Q$), "EOQ is the order quantity" (must add *"that minimises the total inventory cost"*).
- **Visual:** Draw the **U-shaped total cost curve** with ordering cost (hyperbola) and holding cost (line) intersecting at $Q^*$. KTU rewards 1–2 marks for correct schematic even if numbers are wrong.
- **Real-World Tie-Up:** Mention an example from manufacturing/retail/pharma in long answers — it signals conceptual clarity to the examiner.

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Minute KTU Trap:** A common KTU modification is to ask *"What happens to EOQ if the ordering cost is reduced by 20%?"* — students often apply 20% to $Q^*$ instead of to $C_o$ **inside the square root**. The correct method is to recompute $Q^*$ from scratch using the new $C_o$ value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Control Techniques
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inventory Control Techniques

# 1. Inventory Control Techniques — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Inventory Control** is the systematic process of planning, organizing, and regulating the procurement, storage, and consumption of materials to ensure an optimal balance between **service level** (avoiding stockouts) and **carrying cost** (avoiding overstocking). According to KTU 2024 scheme pedagogy, inventory control encompasses techniques that minimize the sum of **ordering cost, carrying (holding) cost, and shortage (stockout) cost**, while satisfying demand variability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *"Inventory Control Techniques refer to the mathematical and statistical methods employed to determine when to order (reorder point) and how much to order (order quantity) so that the total inventory-related costs are minimized and customer service levels are maximized."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank" Model

Imagine your factory's raw material store is a **water tank**. Water flows OUT (demand/consumption) every day, and water flows IN (orders/replenishment) periodically.

- If you order **too little, too late** → the tank runs dry → **stockout** → production halts (lost sales, idle labour).
- If you order **too much, too early** → the tank overflows → **excess holding** → capital gets locked up, spoilage increases.
- **Inventory control =** maintaining the tank at a "Goldilocks level" — neither empty nor overflowing.

The water level at which you call the supplier = **Reorder Level (ROL)**. The quantity you ask for = **Order Quantity (Q)**. The extra water you keep for cloudy days = **Safety Stock (SS)**.

## 1.3 Classification of Inventory (KTU 2024 — Mandatory Coverage)

Inventory in any manufacturing or service organization is broadly classified into four categories:

| Category | Description | Typical Examples |
|----------|-------------|------------------|
| **Raw Materials** | Inputs awaiting processing | Steel coils, copper wire, chemicals |
| **Work-in-Progress (WIP)** | Partially completed goods | Sub-assemblies on shop floor |
| **Finished Goods** | Completed stock awaiting sale | Packed laptops, bottled beverages |
| **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operating)** | Indirect consumables | Lubricants, spare bearings, stationery |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why control is needed:** Per KTU 2024 module weightage, the **5 D's of Inventory Problems** are: Defective items, Damaged goods, Deterioration, Depreciation, and Demand fluctuation. Each imposes a tangible cost.

## 1.4 Inventory Costs — The Four Pillars

Every inventory model in KTU is built around optimizing these four cost components:

1. **Ordering Cost ($C_o$)** — Fixed cost incurred per order (paperwork, transport setup, inspection).
2. **Carrying/Holding Cost ($C_c$ or $C_h$)** — Cost of keeping inventory (rent, insurance, capital tied up, obsolescence) — typically expressed as **a percentage of unit cost per year**, standard value **15%–30%**.
3. **Shortage/Stockout Cost ($C_s$)**** — Penalty for running out (lost sales, backorders, production stoppage).
4. **Purchase Cost ($P \times D$)** — Variable cost proportional to quantity; minimized by EOQ when $C_o > 0$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state units — *Ordering cost is in ₹/order*, *Carrying cost is in ₹/unit/year*, *Demand is in units/year*.

## 1.5 Why Inventory Exists Despite Its Cost (Decoupling Function)

- **Decoupling** operations so that each workstation is independent.
- **Buffering** against demand and supply uncertainty.
- **Economies of scale** in purchasing and transportation.
- **Pipeline stock** in transit between stages.
- **Hedge stock** against anticipated price increases or shortages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Independent vs Dependent Demand:** KTU emphasizes this distinction — *Independent demand* (finished goods, spares) is forecasted using statistical methods; *Dependent demand* (components of a finished product) is calculated using **MRP (Material Requirements Planning)** / Bill of Materials.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deterministic Inventory Models — The KTU Core

### 2.1.1 Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — Wilson's Model (1913)

**Assumptions (KTU assumes these are stated explicitly for full marks):**

1. Demand rate is **known and constant** (uniform).
2. Lead time is **constant and known**.
3. Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate).
4. No **quantity discounts**; unit price is independent of order size.
5. **Only ordering and holding costs** are considered; shortage cost = 0.
6. Order quantity is **replenished instantaneously** when stock reaches zero.

**Logic of the Model:**

- If $Q$ is the order quantity, average inventory = $Q/2$.
- Number of orders per year = $D/Q$.
- Total Annual Cost = Ordering Cost + Holding Cost

$$TAC = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} C_o}_{\text{Ordering}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} C_c}_{\text{Holding}}$$

The **optimal $Q$** is found by differentiating TAC with respect to $Q$ and setting to zero:

$$EOQ = Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_c}}$$

**The Famous 80/20 (Square Root Rule):** If $Q$ is doubled, $TC$ increases by only ~10%. If demand doubles, $EOQ$ increases by only ~41% ($\sqrt{2}$).

### 2.1.2 Production Order Quantity (POQ / EPQ)

When replenishment is **gradual** (production rate $p > $ demand rate $d$), inventory builds up at rate $(p - d)$ and depletes at rate $d$ during consumption.

$$EPQ = Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_c}} \times \sqrt{\frac{p}{p - d}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** Notice $EPQ = EOQ \times \sqrt{p/(p-d)}$. When $p \to \infty$ (instantaneous replenishment), EPQ reduces to EOQ.

### 2.1.3 EOQ with Quantity Discounts

When unit price $P$ varies with order size (all-units discount), the procedure is:

1. Compute EOQ for **each price bracket**.
2. If computed EOQ falls in its own price bracket → **optimal**.
3. Else, compute TAC at the **minimum order quantity** of that bracket and compare.

### 2.1.4 EOQ with Shortages Allowed (Backorder Model)

When stockout is permitted and backordered:

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_c}} \times \sqrt{\frac{C_c + C_s}{C_s}}$$

Maximum inventory: $I_{max} = Q^* \cdot \frac{C_s}{C_c + C_s}$

Maximum backorder: $B_{max} = Q^* \cdot \frac{C_c}{C_c + C_s}$

## 2.2 Probabilistic Inventory Models

### 2.2.1 Reorder Level (ROL)

$$ROL = d \times L + SS$$

Where:
- $d$ = average daily demand
- $L$ = lead time (days)
- $SS$ = safety stock

### 2.2.2 Safety Stock Formulas

| Risk Type | Safety Stock Formula |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Increased demand only | $SS = Z \sigma_d \sqrt{L}$ |
| Increased lead time only | $SS = Z d \sigma_L$ |
| Both demand & lead time variable | $SS = Z \sqrt{L \sigma_d^2 + d^2 \sigma_L^2}$ |

Where $Z$ is the **service-level z-score** (e.g., 95% → $Z = 1.645$, 97.5% → $Z = 1.96$, 99% → $Z = 2.33$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Table

| Formula | Expression | Application |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| **EOQ (Wilson)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{2DC_o/C_c}$ | Instantaneous replenishment |
| **TAC at EOQ** | $TAC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_c}$ | Minimum total cost |
| **Number of orders/yr** | $N = D/Q^*$ | Order frequency |
| **Cycle time (days)** | $T = Q^*/d$ | Time between orders |
| **EPQ (Production)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{2DC_o/C_c \cdot p/(p-d)}$ | Finite production rate |
| **EOQ with shortage** | $Q^* = \sqrt{2DC_o/C_c \cdot (C_c+C_s)/C_s}$ | Backorders permitted |
| **Reorder Level** | $ROL = dL + SS$ | When to reorder |
| **Safety Stock** | $SS = Z\sigma$ variants | Variability buffer |
| **ABC Classification** | By annual ₹ consumption | Selective control |
| **Carrying cost as %** | $C_c = i \cdot P$ | $i$ = holding rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When $C_c$ is given as a percentage, always compute $C_c = i \times P$ before substituting into the EOQ formula. Marks are routinely lost here.

## 2.4 Inventory Classification Techniques (KTU Frequently Tested)

### 2.4.1 ABC Analysis (Always Better Control)

Based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)**:

- **A items** — ~10–20% of SKUs but **70–80%** of annual consumption value → **tight control**.
- **B items** — ~20–30% of SKUs, ~15–25% of value → **moderate control**.
- **C items** — ~50–70% of SKUs, ~5–10% of value → **loose control**.

**Annual Consumption Value** = Annual Demand × Unit Price.

### 2.4.2 Other Classification Systems (Mentioned in KTU)

- **VED** — Vital, Essential, Desirable (used for **spare parts**).
- **FSN** — Fast, Slow, Non-moving (based on issue rate).
- **HML** — High, Medium, Low (unit price classification).
- **SDE** — Scarce, Difficult, Easy (procurement difficulty).
- **GOLF** — Government, Ordinary, Local, Foreign (source).
- **SOS** — Seasonal, Off-Seasonal (demand pattern).

## 2.5 Modern Techniques — JIT, MRP, ERP

### 2.5.1 Just-In-Time (JIT)

A **pull-based** system where materials arrive *exactly* when needed. Originated at Toyota (Taiichi Ohno). Inventory is treated as a **waste (muda)**.

- **JIT Goal:** *Zero inventory, zero defects, zero lead time.*
- **Kanban** — the visual signal card that triggers movement.

### 2.5.2 MRP (Material Requirements Planning)

A computer-based system that explodes the **BOM (Bill of Materials)** against the **Master Production Schedule** to compute *dependent* requirements.

### 2.5.3 P-System vs Q-System

| Feature | P-System (Periodic Review) | Q-System (Continuous Review) |
|---------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| Review | Fixed interval | Every withdrawal |
| Order quantity | Variable (up to $M$) | Fixed ($Q = EOQ$) |
| Trigger | Time-based | Reorder level |
| Suitability | Grouped orders | High-value A items |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Wilson's EOQ Formula

**Given:**
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $Q$ = Order quantity (units/order)
- $C_o$ = Ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $C_c$ = Carrying cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- $P$ = Unit purchase price (₹/unit)

**Step 1: Number of orders placed per year**

$$N = \frac{D}{Q}$$

**Step 2: Annual Ordering Cost**

$$AOC = N \cdot C_o = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot C_o = \frac{DC_o}{Q}$$

**Step 3: Average Inventory Level**

Since each order arrives instantaneously and demand is uniform, inventory drops linearly from $Q$ to $0$. The average over one cycle is:

$$\bar{I} = \frac{Q + 0}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}$$

**Step 4: Annual Carrying (Holding) Cost**

$$AHC = \bar{I} \cdot C_c = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot C_c = \frac{QC_c}{2}$$

**Step 5: Total Annual Cost (excluding purchase cost, which is constant)**

$$TAC = AOC + AHC = \frac{DC_o}{Q} + \frac{QC_c}{2}$$

**Step 6: Minimize TAC by differentiating w.r.t. $Q$**

$$\frac{d(TAC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DC_o}{Q^2} + \frac{C_c}{2} = 0$$

**Step 7: Solve for $Q^*$**

$$\frac{C_c}{2} = \frac{DC_o}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DC_o}{C_c}$$

$$\boxed{Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DC_o}{C_c}}}$$

**Step 8: Substitute back to obtain Minimum Total Cost**

$$TAC^* = \frac{D C_o}{\sqrt{2DC_o/C_c}} + \frac{C_c}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DC_o}{C_c}}$$

$$TAC^* = \sqrt{\frac{D C_o C_c}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{D C_o C_c}{2}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{DC_oC_c}{2}}$$

$$\boxed{TAC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_c}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using second derivative:** $\frac{d^2(TAC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DC_o}{Q^3} > 0$ — confirming minimum.

## 3.2 Detailed Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board-Style, 14-Mark Quality)

**Problem:** A manufacturing firm uses **12,000 units/year** of a component. The ordering cost is **₹150/order** and the carrying cost is **₹6/unit/year**. Lead time is **10 days**. Working days = **300/year**.

**Find:** (a) EOQ (b) Optimal number of orders (c) Minimum total cost (d) Reorder Level (e) Cycle time.

**Solution:**

Given: $D = 12{,}000$ units/yr, $C_o = ₹150$/order, $C_c = ₹6$/unit/yr, $L = 10$ days, working days = 300.

**Part (a) — EOQ:**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 150}{6}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{3{,}600{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{600{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{Q^* \approx 774.6 \text{ units} \approx 775 \text{ units}}$$

**Part (b) — Optimal number of orders per year:**

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{12{,}000}{774.6}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 15.49 \text{ orders/year} \approx 16 \text{ orders}}$$

**Part (c) — Minimum Total Annual Cost (excluding purchase cost):**

$$TAC^* = \sqrt{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 150 \times 6}$$

$$TAC^* = \sqrt{21{,}600{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{TAC^* \approx ₹4{,}647.58/\text{year}}$$

**Part (d) — Average daily demand:**

$$d = \frac{D}{\text{working days}} = \frac{12{,}000}{300} = 40 \text{ units/day}$$

**Reorder Level:**

$$ROL = d \times L + SS$$

Assuming $SS = 0$ (deterministic model):

$$ROL = 40 \times 10 + 0$$

$$\boxed{ROL = 400 \text{ units}}$$

**Part (e) — Cycle Time (time between consecutive orders):**

$$T = \frac{Q^*}{d} = \frac{774.6}{40}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 19.37 \text{ days}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification check:** $TAC$ at $Q = 775$ should equal $TAC$ at $Q = 774.6$.
> At $Q = 775$: $AOC = 12{,}000 \times 150/775 = ₹2{,}322.58$; $AHC = 775 \times 6/2 = ₹2{,}325$; Total = ₹4{,}647.58 ✓

## 3.3 ABC Classification — Step-by-Step Analytical Method

**Procedure:**
1. Compute **Annual Consumption Value (ACV)** = Annual Demand × Unit Price.
2. Sort items in **descending order** of ACV.
3. Compute **cumulative % of ACV** and **cumulative % of items**.
4. Apply classification thresholds:
   - **A items** → first ~70% of cumulative ACV (typically 10–20% of items).
   - **B items** → next ~20% (typically 20–30% of items).
   - **C items** → remaining ~10% (typically 50–70% of items).

**Worked Micro-Example:**

| Item | Annual Demand (units) | Unit Price (₹) | ACV (₹) | % of ACV | Cumulative % | Class |
|------|----------------------|----------------|---------|----------|--------------|-------|
| 01 | 1000 | 500 | 5,00,000 | 50.0% | 50.0% | A |
| 02 | 800 | 250 | 2,00,000 | 20.0% | 70.0% | A |
| 03 | 2000 | 50 | 1,00,000 | 10.0% | 80.0% | B |
| 04 | 5000 | 10 | 50,000 | 5.0% | 85.0% | B |
| 05 | 10000 | 5 | 50,000 | 5.0% | 90.0% | B |
| 06 | 15000 | 2 | 30,000 | 3.0% | 93.0% | C |
| 07 | 25000 | 1 | 25,000 | 2.5% | 95.5% | C |
| 08 | 50000 | 1 | 50,000 | 4.5% | 100.0% | C |

Total ACV = ₹10,05,000. Items 01 & 02 → Class A; Items 03, 04, 05 → Class B; Items 06, 07, 08 → Class C.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EOQ Inventory Cycle — Sawtooth Behaviour

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the classic **sawtooth inventory profile** under Wilson's EOQ model, showing how inventory rises instantaneously to $Q^*$ upon receipt of an order and depletes linearly to zero just before the next order arrives.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Time zero: Order placed]:::start
    A1[Stock rises instantly to Q*]:::peak
    A2[Steady consumption at rate d]:::deplete
    A3[Inventory falls linearly toward zero]:::deplete
    A4[Reorder Point reached at ROL = dL]:::trigger
    A5[New order placed; cycle repeats]:::trigger
    A6[Cycle Time T = Q/d]:::start

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A1

    classDef start fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef peak fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef deplete fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef trigger fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The horizontal axis is time, vertical axis is inventory level. Vertical jumps represent order arrivals; downward slopes represent demand consumption. The **cycle time $T = Q^*/d$** is the time between consecutive order arrivals.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cost Trade-off in EOQ Model

This schematic shows how **ordering cost** (decreasing in $Q$) and **carrying cost** (increasing in $Q$) interact to produce a U-shaped **total cost curve**, minimized at $Q^* = EOQ$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Costs[Cost Behaviour vs Order Quantity Q]
        OC[Ordering Cost per Year]:::oc
        HC[Holding Cost per Year]:::hc
        TC[Total Cost Curve U-shaped]:::tc
    end

    subgraph Optimum[Optimal Zone]
        QStar[EOQ = sqrt 2DCo/Cc]:::opt
        MinPt[Minimum Total Cost = sqrt 2DCoCc]:::opt
    end

    OC -- decreases as Q rises --> TC
    HC -- increases as Q rises --> TC
    TC -- minimum attained at --> QStar
    QStar --> MinPt

    classDef oc fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hc fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tc fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Inventory Model

KTU 2024 requires students to identify the *correct* model based on problem features. Use this diagnostic flowchart:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Problem Statement Given]:::start
    Q1{Is demand known?}:::decision
    Q2{Is replenishment instantaneous?}:::decision
    Q3{Are quantity discounts offered?}:::decision
    Q4{Are stockouts permitted?}:::decision

    EOQ[Use Wilson EOQ Model]:::result
    EPQ[Use Production Order Quantity EPQ]:::result
    Disc[Use Quantity Discount Model]:::result
    Back[Use Backorder EOQ Model]:::result

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -- Yes Constant --> Q2
    Q1 -- No Variable --> Prob[Use Probabilistic Model SS and ROL]:::result
    Q2 -- Yes Instant --> Q3
    Q2 -- No Gradual production --> EPQ
    Q3 -- No --> Q4
    Q3 -- Yes --> Disc
    Q4 -- No --> EOQ
    Q4 -- Yes --> Back

    classDef start fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — ABC Control System Integration

A high-level block diagram showing how ABC classification feeds into differential control policies:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Inp[Raw SKU Data: Demand and Unit Price]:::inp
    Calc[Compute Annual Consumption Value ACV]:::proc
    Sort[Sort Descending by ACV]:::proc
    Cum[Cumulative Percentage Analysis]:::proc
    Split{Threshold 70 percent 90 percent}:::decision

    AClass[Class A 10 to 20 percent of items]:::aclass
    BClass[Class B 20 to 30 percent of items]:::bclass
    CClass[Class C 50 to 70 percent of items]:::cclass

    AP[Tight Control: Frequent review, accurate records, low safety stock]:::apol
    BP[Moderate Control: Periodic review, medium safety stock]:::bpol
    CP[Loose Control: Bulk ordering, high safety stock, two-bin system]:::cpol

    Inp --> Calc
    Calc --> Sort
    Sort --> Cum
    Cum --> Split
    Split -- 0 to 70 percent --> AClass
    Split -- 70 to 90 percent --> BClass
    Split -- 90 to 100 percent --> CClass
    AClass --> AP
    BClass --> BP
    CClass --> CP

    classDef inp fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef aclass fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bclass fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cclass fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef apol fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef bpol fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef cpol fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Economic Order Quantity (EOQ). State any two assumptions of Wilson's EOQ model.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

EOQ is the order quantity that minimizes the sum of ordering cost and carrying cost. It is given by:

$$EOQ = Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DC_o}{C_c}}$$

where $D$ = annual demand, $C_o$ = ordering cost/order, $C_c$ = carrying cost/unit/year.

**Two assumptions (1.5 marks each):**
1. Demand is known, constant, and uniform throughout the year.
2. Lead time is constant and replenishment is instantaneous.
3. (Any other valid assumption — no quantity discounts, no stockouts, single product, infinite planning horizon.)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the concept of Safety Stock. Why is it maintained?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Safety Stock** is the extra inventory carried to protect against uncertainties in **demand** and **lead time**. It acts as a buffer to avoid stockouts when actual demand exceeds the forecast or when supply is delayed.

**Why maintained:** (1) To absorb demand variability, (2) To absorb supply/lead-time variability, (3) To maintain a pre-decided customer service level, (4) To prevent production stoppages in dependent operations.

**Formula:** $SS = Z \sigma_d \sqrt{L}$ (for variable demand only).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** from first principles. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company requires **5,000 units/year** of a raw material. The cost per order is **₹400** and the carrying cost is **20% per annum** of the unit cost. The unit price is **₹50**. Determine: (i) EOQ, (ii) Total annual cost of inventory, (iii) Reorder level if lead time is **12 days** (assume 360 working days/year). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Demand rate is known and constant ($D$).
2. Lead time is constant and known.
3. Replenishment is instantaneous (entire order arrives at once).
4. Ordering cost $C_o$ and carrying cost $C_c$ are known and constant.
5. No quantity discounts; unit price is independent of order size.
6. Shortage cost is zero; stockouts are not permitted.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Let $Q$ be the order quantity.
Number of orders per year = $D/Q$.
Annual Ordering Cost = $\frac{DC_o}{Q}$.
Average inventory = $Q/2$ (since inventory linearly depletes from $Q$ to $0$).
Annual Holding Cost = $\frac{QC_c}{2}$.

Total Annual Cost:

$$TAC = \frac{DC_o}{Q} + \frac{QC_c}{2}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $Q$:

$$\frac{d(TAC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DC_o}{Q^2} + \frac{C_c}{2} = 0$$

Solving:

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DC_o}{C_c} \implies Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DC_o}{C_c}}$$

Minimum total cost:

$$TAC^* = \sqrt{2DC_oC_c}$$

Second derivative $\frac{d^2(TAC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DC_o}{Q^3} > 0$, confirming minimum.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $D = 5{,}000$ units/yr; $C_o = ₹400$/order; $i = 20\% = 0.20$; $P = ₹50$; $L = 12$ days; working days = 360.

**(i) Carrying cost per unit per year:**
$$C_c = i \times P = 0.20 \times 50 = ₹10/\text{unit/yr}$$

**EOQ:**
$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 400}{10}} = \sqrt{400{,}000} = 632.46 \approx 632 \text{ units}$$

**(ii) Total Annual Inventory Cost (excluding purchase cost):**
$$TAC^* = \sqrt{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 400 \times 10} = \sqrt{40{,}000{,}000} = ₹6{,}324.56$$

Including purchase cost: $TAC_{total} = 5{,}000 \times 50 + 6{,}324.56 = ₹2{,}56{,}324.56$.

**(iii) Reorder Level:**
$$d = \frac{D}{\text{working days}} = \frac{5{,}000}{360} = 13.89 \text{ units/day}$$

$$ROL = d \times L + SS = 13.89 \times 12 + 0 = 166.67 \approx 167 \text{ units}$$

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **ABC analysis** of inventory with a suitable example. How is it useful in inventory management? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The annual demand of a component is **10,000 units**. Ordering cost is **₹100 per order** and carrying cost is **₹5 per unit per year**. The production rate is **20,000 units per year**. Calculate the **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)** and the **maximum inventory level**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Concept (2 marks):** ABC analysis classifies inventory items based on their **Annual Consumption Value (ACV = Annual Demand × Unit Price)** into three categories following the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)**.

**Categories (3 marks):**

| Class | % of Items | % of ACV | Control Strategy |
|-------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **A** | 10–20% | 70–80% | Tight, frequent review, accurate records, low safety stock |
| **B** | 20–30% | 15–25% | Moderate, periodic review, medium safety stock |
| **C** | 50–70% | 5–10% | Loose, bulk ordering, two-bin system, high safety stock |

**Example (1 mark):** In a firm with 1000 SKUs, ~150 items are A-class, ~250 are B-class, ~600 are C-class.

**Usefulness (1 mark):** Prioritizes managerial attention, optimizes working capital, reduces obsolescence, balances service level and cost.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $D = 10{,}000$ units/yr; $C_o = ₹100$/order; $C_c = ₹5$/unit/yr; $p = 20{,}000$ units/yr; $d = D = 10{,}000$ units/yr (constant).

**Step 1: EOQ (instantaneous case):**
$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 100}{5}} = \sqrt{400{,}000} = 632.46 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2: EPQ (production case):**
$$EPQ = EOQ \times \sqrt{\frac{p}{p-d}} = 632.46 \times \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000}{20{,}000-10{,}000}}$$

$$EPQ = 632.46 \times \sqrt{2} = 632.46 \times 1.4142$$

$$\boxed{EPQ \approx 894.43 \approx 894 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Inventory Level ($I_{max}$):**

During production phase, inventory builds up at rate $(p-d) = 10{,}000$ units/yr.
Production time per cycle = $Q/p = 894/20{,}000 = 0.0447$ yr.

$$I_{max} = (p - d) \times \frac{Q}{p} = (20{,}000 - 10{,}000) \times \frac{894.43}{20{,}000}$$

$$I_{max} = 10{,}000 \times 0.04472 = 447.21 \text{ units}$$

**Alternative formula:** $I_{max} = Q \times (1 - d/p) = 894.43 \times (1 - 0.5) = 447.21$ units ✓

**Average inventory** = $I_{max}/2 = 223.6$ units.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students frequently forget to compute $C_c = i \times P$ when given a percentage. Always show this step.
> 2. **Annual vs Daily Demand Confusion:** ROL requires **daily demand × lead time in days**, not annual demand × lead time. Marks deducted heavily here.
> 3. **EPQ distinction:** Many students compute EOQ instead of EPQ. If the problem says *"production rate is X"*, you **must** use the EPQ formula.
> 4. **ABC ranking order:** Items must be sorted in *descending* ACV before cumulative %. Wrong sorting = full marks lost.
> 5. **Square root simplification errors:** Always re-check the radical simplification. $632.46$ is NOT $\approx 600$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **EOQ = $\sqrt{2DC_o/C_c}$** — Wilson's formula; minimizes ordering + holding cost.
- ✅ **Assumptions to remember:** constant demand, instantaneous replenishment, no stockouts, no discounts.
- ✅ **Minimum TAC = $\sqrt{2DC_oC_c}$** — direct substitution; useful in valuation questions.
- ✅ **Carrying cost = holding rate × unit price** — when given as %, convert first.
- ✅ **Reorder Level (ROL) = $d \times L + SS$** — demand is **daily** (units/day).
- ✅ **EPQ = EOQ × $\sqrt{p/(p-d)}$** — required when production is gradual.
- ✅ **EOQ with shortages = EOQ × $\sqrt{(C_c+C_s)/C_s}$** — for backorder models.
- ✅ **ABC Analysis** — Pareto Principle; A-items get tight control despite low SKU count.
- ✅ **VED** for spares, **FSN** for movement, **HML** for unit price.
- ✅ **JIT** = pull system, Kanban signal, Toyota origin, *zero inventory* goal.
- ✅ **MRP** = dependent demand, uses BOM, computer-based.
- ✅ **P-System** = periodic review, variable quantity; **Q-System** = continuous review, fixed quantity.
- ✅ **Safety Stock** = $Z \sigma_d \sqrt{L}$ for demand variability; $Z d \sigma_L$ for lead-time variability.
- ✅ **Service Level z-scores:** 90%→1.28, 95%→1.645, 97.5%→1.96, 99%→2.33.
- ✅ **Cycle Time $T = Q/d$** (days) — time between consecutive orders.
- ✅ **Sawtooth diagram** — vertical jumps = order arrivals, downward slopes = consumption.
- ✅ **Kardex / Two-bin system** — simple visual control for C-items.
- ✅ **Inventory Ratios:** Inventory Turnover = $D/\text{Average Inventory}$; days of supply = $\text{Average Inventory}/d$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Month KTU Rapid Revision Mantra:** "Order Less Often ↑ AOC, ↓ AHC. Order More Often ↑ AHC, ↓ AOC. EOQ is where they cross. Add Lead-time buffer for ROL. ABC for selective love. JIT for zero waste."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Quality — The Foundational Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Quality):** Quality is the totality of features and characteristics of a product or service that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs (adapted from ISO 8402 / ISO 9000).

In the KTU 2024 Industrial Engineering context, **Quality** is not a single attribute but a **multi-dimensional construct** that combines:

- **Fitness for Use** (Juran) — the product must serve its intended purpose
- **Conformance to Requirements** (Crosby) — zero deviation from specifications
- **Customer Satisfaction** (Deming) — meeting and exceeding expectations
- **Value for Price** (Feigenbaum) — cost-to-utility ratio

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis (2024 Scheme):** Examiners expect students to distinguish between **Quality of Design** (specifications planned at the design stage) and **Quality of Conformance** (the actual product meeting those specifications). Confusing these two is one of the most frequent mark-deducting errors in 14-mark questions.

## 1.2 Quality Control (QC) — The Operational Tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Quality Control):** Quality Control is the operational technique and the set of activities used to fulfil the requirements of quality. It involves **monitoring processes**, **identifying causes of variation**, and **eliminating them** to achieve consistent product conformance.

**Intuitive Analogy (The "Biryani Pot" Model):**
Imagine a large hotel kitchen preparing 1,000 plates of chicken biryani for a banquet. The head chef tastes a small spoonful from each pot and checks:
- Salt level (a *variable* characteristic)
- Presence of unwanted foreign objects like a fallen hair (an *attribute* characteristic — pass/fail)

If a pot is too salty, he adjusts the next batch. This **sampling-and-correcting** activity during cooking is exactly what **Quality Control** does in manufacturing. It is the *eyes and tongue* of the production process.

## 1.3 Quality Assurance (QA) — The Preventive System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Assurance (QA):** A planned and systematic set of activities implemented within the **quality system** that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements. QA focuses on the **process** rather than the product.

**The Same Analogy Extended:**
Before the banquet, the head chef:
- Trains the cooks (process capability)
- Standardizes the recipe card (procedure documentation)
- Calibrates the weighing scales (measurement system)
- Verifies that the kitchen floor is clean (preventive controls)

This is **Quality Assurance** — building quality *into* the system so that bad output is prevented *before* it occurs.

## 1.4 Distinguishing QA vs QC

| Aspect | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Process & system | Product & output |
| **Nature** | Preventive (proactive) | Detective (reactive) |
| **Approach** | "Build it right the first time" | "Catch defects after they occur" |
| **Responsibility** | Everyone in the organization | Primarily the QC department |
| **Cost Impact** | Cost of prevention (low) | Cost of appraisal + failure (high) |
| **Timing** | Before production | During/after production |
| **KTU Tag** | Management-oriented | Statistical/engineering-oriented |

## 1.5 Total Quality Management (TQM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TQM Definition (KTU Standard):** A management philosophy that seeks to **integrate all organizational functions** (marketing, finance, design, production, service) to focus on meeting customer needs and organizational objectives by improving quality as an ongoing process.

**The Four Pillars of TQM:**
1. **Customer Focus** — internal and external customers
2. **Total Involvement** — every employee, from CEO to shop floor
3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — never-ending incremental upgrades
4. **Fact-Based Decision Making** — statistical evidence, not intuition

## 1.6 Cost of Quality (COQ) — A Mandatory KTU Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Quality (COQ):** The total cost incurred to ensure quality, plus the cost incurred because quality is not achieved. It is the sum of Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs.

| COQ Component | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Prevention Costs** | Costs to prevent defects | Quality training, supplier capability audits |
| **Appraisal Costs** | Costs to detect defects | Inspection, testing, QC equipment calibration |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found before delivery | Rework, scrap, re-inspection |
| **External Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found after delivery | Warranty claims, returns, product recalls, lawsuits |

**Golden Rule (KTU Board Point):** *Every ₹1 spent on prevention saves ₹10 in failure costs* — hence modern TQM pushes organizations to shift investment from **appraisal + failure** toward **prevention**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Statistical Quality Control (SQC) — The Mathematical Backbone

Quality varies because of **variation**. SQC uses statistical tools to distinguish between:

- **Common Cause Variation (Chance Variation):** Inherent to the process, due to countless minor influences (e.g., minor tool wear, ambient humidity). *Manageable by system changes.*
- **Special Cause Variation (Assignable Variation):** Due to specific identifiable factors (e.g., broken tool, wrong material batch, operator error). *Detectable and removable.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Walter Shewhart's Principle:** A process operating with only common-cause variation is said to be in a state of **STATISTICAL CONTROL (in-control)**. A process exhibiting special causes is **OUT-OF-CONTROL**. SQC's primary job is to detect special causes quickly.

## 2.2 Control Charts — The Heart of SQC

A **Control Chart** is a graphical plot of a quality characteristic measured from samples versus the sample number or time, with three horizontal lines:
- **Center Line (CL)** = process mean
- **Upper Control Limit (UCL)**
- **Lower Control Limit (LCL)**

> [!NOTE]
> The famous **3-Sigma Control Limits** are placed at the process mean ± 3 standard deviations. Under the assumption of normality, this captures **99.73%** of the variation, leaving only **0.27%** as the probability of a point falling outside by chance alone (a false alarm). This is the basis of all Shewhart charts.

## 2.3 Classification of Control Charts

### A. Control Charts for Variables (Measurable Data)

| Chart | Statistic Plotted | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **$\bar{X}$ chart** | Process mean of samples | Monitors **central tendency** |
| **R chart** | Range within samples | Monitors **variability** (small samples $n \le 10$) |
| **$\sigma$ (s) chart** | Standard deviation within samples | Monitors **variability** (large samples) |
| **$\bar{X}$ and R together** | Mean + Range | **Most widely used pair** in KTU problems |

### B. Control Charts for Attributes (Go/No-Go Data)

| Chart | Statistic Plotted | Sample Size |
|---|---|---|
| **p-chart** | Fraction defective | Variable ($n$ may change) |
| **np-chart** | Number of defectives | Constant |
| **c-chart** | Number of defects per unit | Constant inspection unit |
| **u-chart** | Defects per unit | Variable inspection unit |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Control Chart Limits

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$, $d_2$, $E_2$** depend on sample size $n$. They are tabulated in standard KTU data tables. Below, $\bar{\bar{X}}$ is the grand mean, $\bar{R}$ is the average range, $\bar{p}$ is the average fraction defective, $\bar{c}$ is the average count, and $n$ is the sample size.

### $\bar{X}$-Chart Limits

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \, \bar{R} \\
CL_{\bar{X}}  &= \bar{\bar{X}} \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \, \bar{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

### R-Chart Limits

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_R &= D_4 \, \bar{R} \\
CL_R  &= \bar{R} \\
LCL_R &= D_3 \, \bar{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

### p-Chart Limits

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_p &= \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} \\
CL_p  &= \bar{p} \\
LCL_p &= \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $LCL_p < 0$, it is set to **0** (a defect count cannot be negative).

### c-Chart Limits

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_c &= \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} \\
CL_c  &= \bar{c} \\
LCL_c &= \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### u-Chart Limits

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_u &= \bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}} \\
CL_u  &= \bar{u} \\
LCL_u &= \bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Process Capability Indices

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

| $C_p$ Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $C_p \ge 1.33$ | Process capable |
| $C_p \ge 1.67$ | Process highly capable (Six-Sigma) |
| $C_p < 1.00$ | Process **not capable** — defective output expected |

## 2.5 Acceptance Sampling — The Lot-Inspection Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Sampling:** The process of inspecting a **random sample** from a lot and deciding whether to **accept** or **reject** the entire lot based on the sample results. It does *not* improve quality — it only **sorts** good lots from bad lots.

### Types of Sampling Plans

1. **Single Sampling Plan:** One sample of size $n$; accept if $d \le c$, reject if $d > c$ (where $d$ = number of defectives, $c$ = acceptance number).
2. **Double Sampling Plan:** Two-stage inspection with possible decision after first sample.
3. **Multiple Sampling Plan:** Three or more stages, often approximated as a sequential plan.
4. **Sequential Sampling:** Each item inspected one-by-one; decision made as soon as enough evidence accumulates.

## 2.6 Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OC Curve:** A plot of the probability of **accepting** a lot ($P_a$) versus the lot's incoming fraction defective ($p$). It is the "fingerprint" of a sampling plan.

- **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$):** Probability of rejecting a **good** lot. Typically fixed at **0.05** (i.e., 5%) corresponding to **AQL** (Acceptable Quality Level).
- **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$):** Probability of accepting a **bad** lot. Typically fixed at **0.10** (i.e., 10%) corresponding to **LTPD** (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective).
- **Ideal OC Curve:** A vertical step-function (accept all good, reject all bad) — impossible to achieve in practice.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| SQC Tool | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| $\bar{X}$, R Charts | CNC machining of piston rings, tolerance control |
| p-Chart | PCB assembly line — % solder defects |
| c-Chart | Fabric mill — number of weave defects per 100 m² |
| u-Chart | Aircraft fuselage inspection — defects per square meter |
| $C_p$, $C_{pk}$ | Bearing manufacturing — capability before shipment |
| OC Curve | Defence procurement — MIL-STD sampling plans |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 3-Sigma Control Limits for the $\bar{X}$-Chart

**Step 1 — Central Limit Theorem Setup.**
Take samples of size $n$ from a process where individual values follow $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. The sample mean $\bar{X}$ is itself a random variable with:

$$
\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)
$$

This is the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** result, which justifies using the $\bar{X}$-chart even if the underlying data are not perfectly normal when $n \ge 4$ or $5$.

**Step 2 — Standardize $\bar{X}$.**
The standardized z-score is:

$$
Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 3 — Set the 3-Sigma Boundary.**
By Shewhart's choice, action limits are placed at $Z = \pm 3$ because:

$$
P(-3 \le Z \le 3) = 0.9973
$$

This means only a **0.27%** chance exists that a sample mean will fall outside the limits purely by chance when the process is in control.

**Step 4 — Solve for $\bar{X}$ Limits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL &= \mu + 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\
CL &= \mu \\
LCL &= \mu - 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Replace $\mu$ and $\sigma$ with Sample Estimates.**
Since $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are unknown, we use the **grand mean** $\bar{\bar{X}}$ in place of $\mu$, and we replace the unknown $\sigma$ using the **average range** $\bar{R}$ via the relationship:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}
$$

where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant that depends on $n$ (e.g., for $n=5$, $d_2 = 2.326$).

**Step 6 — Final KTU-Exam Form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}/d_2}{\sqrt{n}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \, \bar{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the identity:

$$
A_2 = \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}
$$

which is exactly the constant tabulated for each $n$ in the KTU data book.

## 3.2 Derivation: Process Capability Index $C_p$

**Step 1 — Define the Tolerance Window.**
For a two-sided specification, the allowable spread is $USL - LSL$.

**Step 2 — Define the Natural Process Spread.**
Under statistical control, $\pm 3\sigma$ on either side of the mean captures 99.73% of all output. The total natural spread is $6\sigma$.

**Step 3 — Form the Ratio.**
The capability index measures how many "natural spread widths" fit into the specification window:

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{Allowable Spread}}{\text{Natural Spread}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Step 4 — Geometric Interpretation.**
A $C_p = 1$ means the process *just* fits the tolerance. A $C_p = 1.5$ means there is 50% excess room — the process is more than capable.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — $\bar{X}$ and R Chart Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Module 3 Type):** Twenty samples of size $n = 5$ are taken from a manufacturing process. The grand mean is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 102.5$ mm and the average range is $\bar{R} = 4.2$ mm. For $n = 5$, $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$. Construct the control charts.

**Step 1 — Compute $\bar{X}$-Chart Limits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \\
&= 102.5 + (0.577)(4.2) \\
&= 102.5 + 2.4234 \\
&= 104.92 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
CL_{\bar{X}} &= 102.5 \text{ mm} \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= 102.5 - 2.4234 = 100.08 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute R-Chart Limits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_R &= D_4 \bar{R} = 2.114 \times 4.2 = 8.88 \text{ mm} \\
CL_R  &= 4.2 \text{ mm} \\
LCL_R &= D_3 \bar{R} = 0 \times 4.2 = 0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Decision.**
The R-chart is examined **first** (variability must be stable before mean). Here $0 \le R \le 8.88$ — we must check actual sample ranges against these. If a sample's $R$ exceeds 8.88 mm, the process is out-of-control on variability. Otherwise, the $\bar{X}$-chart at 100.08–104.92 mm is the valid reference for the mean.

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — p-Chart Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In a cable-manufacturing unit, 15 samples of size $n = 100$ are inspected. The total number of defectives observed is 90. Construct a p-chart.

**Step 1 — Compute the Average Fraction Defective.**

$$
\bar{p} = \frac{\text{Total defectives}}{\text{Total inspected}} = \frac{90}{15 \times 100} = \frac{90}{1500} = 0.06
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Standard Deviation of $p$.**

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.06 \times 0.94}{100}} = \sqrt{0.000564} = 0.02375
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Control Limits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_p &= 0.06 + 3(0.02375) = 0.06 + 0.07125 = 0.13125 \\
CL_p  &= 0.06 \\
LCL_p &= 0.06 - 0.07125 = -0.01125 \approx 0 \quad \text{(negative, set to 0)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule.**
Any sample with fraction defective between 0 and 0.13125 is acceptable. If $p_{sample} > 0.13125$, the process signals a special cause and must be investigated.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Chart

| Data Type | Sample Size | Distribution | Recommended Chart |
|---|---|---|---|
| Variable (length, weight) | Small ($n \le 10$) | Continuous, Normal | $\bar{X}$-R pair |
| Variable | Large ($n > 10$) | Continuous, Normal | $\bar{X}$-$\sigma$ pair |
| Attribute (pass/fail) | Constant | Binomial | np-chart |
| Attribute (pass/fail) | Variable | Binomial | p-chart |
| Attribute (defect count) | Constant area/unit | Poisson | c-chart |
| Attribute (defect count) | Variable area/unit | Poisson | u-chart |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Quality Control Functional Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Input] --> B[Process Monitoring SPC]
    B --> C[In-Process Quality Control]
    C --> D[Finished Product Inspection]
    D --> E[Acceptance Sampling]
    E --> F{Decision Accept or Reject}
    F -->|Accept| G[Ship to Customer]
    F -->|Reject| H[Rework or Scrap]
    H --> I[Root Cause Analysis]
    I --> J[Corrective Action CAPA]
    J --> B
    G --> K[Customer Feedback Loop]
    K --> J
    B --> L[Control Charts Xbar R p c]
    L --> M[Out of Control Signals]
    M --> J
```

## 4.2 Deming PDCA Cycle — The Improvement Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plan Identify Problem and Analyze]
    P --> DO[Do Implement on Pilot Basis]
    DO --> C[Check Measure Results vs Expected]
    C --> A[Act Standardize or Revise]
    A --> P
```

## 4.3 Cost of Quality Distribution Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Total Cost of Quality] --> PR[Prevention Costs]
    Q --> AP[Appraisal Costs]
    Q --> IF[Internal Failure Costs]
    Q --> EF[External Failure Costs]
    PR --> P1[Training and Education]
    PR --> P2[Process Capability Studies]
    AP --> A1[Inspection and Testing]
    AP --> A2[Equipment Calibration]
    IF --> I1[Scrap]
    IF --> I2[Rework]
    IF --> I3[Re-inspection]
    EF --> E1[Warranty Claims]
    EF --> E2[Returns and Recalls]
    EF --> E3[Loss of Brand Reputation]
```

## 4.4 Control Chart Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Sample Point Plotted] --> Q{Within UCL and LCL}
    Q -->|Yes| R[No Action Process In Control]
    Q -->|No| T[Out of Control Signal]
    T --> U[Investigate Assignable Cause]
    U --> V[Apply Corrective Action]
    V --> W[Recalculate Control Limits]
    W --> S
```

## 4.5 Quality Management System Hierarchy (TQM Layers)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT] --> L1[Customer Focus]
    T --> L2[Leadership Commitment]
    T --> L3[People Involvement]
    T --> L4[Process Approach]
    T --> L5[Continual Improvement]
    T --> L6[Fact Based Decision Making]
    T --> L7[Supplier Partnership]
    L1 --> S1[Voice of Customer VoC]
    L5 --> S2[Kaizen PDCA Six Sigma DMAIC]
    L4 --> S3[Process Mapping and SOP]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between Quality Control and Quality Assurance.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Quality Control (QC)** is the operational technique of monitoring, measuring, and inspecting the product or process to ensure conformance to specifications. It is a **product-oriented, detective** activity (e.g., using control charts, inspection).
- **Quality Assurance (QA)** is the planned and systematic set of activities within the quality system that ensures the process is capable of producing conforming products. It is a **process-oriented, preventive** activity (e.g., training, documentation, audits).
- QC focuses on *outputs*; QA focuses on the *system* that produces those outputs. *(3 marks: 1 mark per distinction point + 1 for example.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term "Cost of Quality" and list its four components.**

**Model Answer:**
**Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure quality, plus the cost incurred because quality is not achieved. The four components are:
1. **Prevention Costs** — cost of activities to prevent defects (e.g., training, process design)
2. **Appraisal Costs** — cost of detecting defects (e.g., inspection, testing)
3. **Internal Failure Costs** — cost of defects found before delivery (e.g., scrap, rework)
4. **External Failure Costs** — cost of defects found after delivery (e.g., warranty, returns, loss of reputation)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (A) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)**. Discuss the difference between common cause and special cause variation with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Twenty-five samples of size 5 are taken from a manufacturing process. The grand average of sample means is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50.0$ cm and the average range is $\bar{R} = 1.2$ cm. Given $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$ for $n = 5$, construct the $\bar{X}$ and R control charts and state the action limits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** is the application of statistical methods to monitor, control, and improve the quality of products and processes. It provides an objective basis for accepting or rejecting lots and for detecting process shifts.

**Common Cause Variation:**
- Inherent to the process; arises from many small, unidentifiable influences.
- Examples: minor fluctuations in raw material hardness, slight variations in ambient temperature, normal machine vibrations.
- To reduce, the **system itself must be changed** (e.g., better machine, redesigned process).

**Special Cause Variation:**
- Due to specific, identifiable, often sporadic factors.
- Examples: a broken cutting tool, a wrong batch of material, an untrained operator, power surge.
- To eliminate, **find and remove the specific cause**.

| Aspect | Common Cause | Special Cause |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | Continuous | Sporadic |
| Source | System itself | External event |
| Action | System redesign | Cause removal |
| Detection | Statistical process control | Out-of-control signals |

*(Award 2 marks for SQC definition, 2 marks for common cause, 2 marks for special cause, 1 mark for comparison table.)*

**(b) Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — $\bar{X}$-Chart Limits (Mean chart):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 50.0 + 0.577 \times 1.2 = 50.0 + 0.6924 = 50.69 \text{ cm} \\
CL_{\bar{X}}  &= 50.0 \text{ cm} \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= 50.0 - 0.6924 = 49.31 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — R-Chart Limits (Range chart):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_R &= D_4 \bar{R} = 2.114 \times 1.2 = 2.537 \text{ cm} \\
CL_R  &= 1.2 \text{ cm} \\
LCL_R &= D_3 \bar{R} = 0 \times 1.2 = 0 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Tabulated Answer:**

| Statistic | LCL | CL | UCL |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\bar{X}$ (cm) | 49.31 | 50.00 | 50.69 |
| R (cm) | 0.00 | 1.20 | 2.537 |

**Step 4 — Engineering Action Rule:** Examine the R-chart first. If all sample ranges are $\le 2.537$ cm, the process is in control on variability. Then examine $\bar{X}$-chart: any sample mean outside 49.31–50.69 cm indicates the process mean has shifted, requiring investigation.

*(Award 2 marks for $\bar{X}$ limits, 2 marks for R limits, 2 marks for tabulation, 1 mark for action rule.)*

---

### Question B — Option (B) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Process Capability Index $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$**. How are they interpreted in terms of process acceptability? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process produces piston rings with a mean diameter $\mu = 74.95$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.02$ mm. The specifications are $LSL = 74.90$ mm and $USL = 75.00$ mm. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Process Capability** is the inherent ability of a process to produce output that meets specifications under stable conditions. It is quantified using two indices:

**1. $C_p$ — Process Capability Index (Two-sided):**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

- It measures the ratio of the specification width to the natural process spread ($6\sigma$).
- It assumes the process is **centered** on the specification midpoint.
- Interpretation: $C_p \ge 1.00$ indicates that the process spread fits inside the tolerance; $C_p \ge 1.33$ is the industry-accepted threshold for capability; $C_p \ge 1.67$ is preferred for safety-critical items (e.g., aerospace).

**2. $C_{pk}$ — Process Capability Index (Actual, accounting for off-centering):**

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

- It accounts for the **location** of the process mean relative to the specification limits.
- $C_{pk} = C_p$ when the process is **perfectly centered**.
- $C_{pk} < C_p$ indicates the process is **off-center**, drifting toward one of the specification limits.

**Interpretation Table:**

| Condition | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $C_p = C_{pk}$ | Process centered and capable |
| $C_p > C_{pk}$ | Process capable in spread but off-center |
| $C_p < 1$ | Process **not capable** — defectives will be produced |
| $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ | Acceptable capability for KTU 2024 standards |

*(Award 2 marks for $C_p$, 2 marks for $C_{pk}$, 2 marks for the centered-vs-off-center logic, 1 mark for interpretation table.)*

**(b) Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**

$$
\mu = 74.95 \text{ mm}, \quad \sigma = 0.02 \text{ mm}, \quad LSL = 74.90 \text{ mm}, \quad USL = 75.00 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{75.00 - 74.90}{6 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.10}{0.12} = 0.833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$:**

The specification midpoint is $\frac{75.00 + 74.90}{2} = 74.95$ mm = $\mu$. The process is therefore **perfectly centered**, so we expect $C_{pk} = C_p$. Confirming by calculation:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) = \min\left(\frac{75.00 - 74.95}{3 \times 0.02}, \frac{74.95 - 74.90}{3 \times 0.02}\right)
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{0.05}{0.06}, \frac{0.05}{0.06}\right) = \min(0.833, 0.833) = 0.833
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Comment:**

- $C_p = C_{pk} = 0.833 < 1.00$
- Since both indices are **less than 1**, the process is **NOT CAPABLE**.
- The natural process spread ($6\sigma = 0.12$ mm) **exceeds** the specification width ($0.10$ mm).
- Approximately **0.27%** of output (≈ 2,700 ppm) will fall outside specifications — about 1 in every 370 rings.
- **Recommendation to management:** Either (i) reduce $\sigma$ to $0.0167$ mm or less (so $6\sigma \le 0.10$ mm), or (ii) widen the specification, or (iii) re-center the process if off-target.

*(Award 2 marks for $C_p$, 2 marks for $C_{pk}$ calculation, 1 mark for centeredness comment, 2 marks for engineering interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 3 Quality Control):**
> 1. **Confusing $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ values:** Forgetting to look up the right $n$ in the data table is a 1-mark deduction per wrong limit. Always state the value of $n$ and the constants used.
> 2. **Forgetting to set $LCL = 0$ when negative:** For p- and c-charts, a negative LCL is *physically meaningless* and must be set to zero. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this oversight.
> 3. **Examining the R-chart first:** In a written answer, students often write the $\bar{X}$-chart first. Convention requires the R-chart to be validated first — otherwise the $\bar{X}$ limits are unreliable. State this explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ formulas:** $C_p$ uses $USL-LSL$ in the numerator; $C_{pk}$ uses $USL-\mu$ or $\mu-LSL$ (whichever is smaller). Writing the same formula twice will cost 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer:** $C_p$, $C_{pk}$ are *dimensionless*; chart limits must carry their proper units (mm, cm, kg, etc.). Examiners expect units in every numerical answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 — Quality Control: High-Yield Recall List**

- **Quality vs Grade:** Quality = conformance to specification; Grade = class/feature variation. KTU often frames a 3-mark question around this distinction.
- **Quality of Design vs Quality of Conformance:** Design sets the target; conformance is the achievement.
- **QA vs QC:** QA is *preventive* and *process-focused*; QC is *detective* and *product-focused*.
- **TQM Pillars:** Customer focus, total involvement, Kaizen, fact-based decisions.
- **Cost of Quality:** Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure. Modern TQM prioritises **prevention**.
- **Common vs Special Cause Variation:** Common → system change; Special → cause identification and removal.
- **Shewhart's 3-Sigma Principle:** $0.27\%$ false-alarm probability; action limits at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.
- **$\bar{X}$-R Chart Pair:** The most frequently asked chart combination in KTU exams. Always give constants $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ for the given $n$.
- **p-Chart for Variable n; np-Chart for Constant n; c-Chart for Defect Counts; u-Chart for Variable Inspection Unit.**
- **Process Capability:** $C_p \ge 1.33$ is capable; $C_{pk} < C_p$ means off-centering; both $<1$ means not capable.
- **OC Curve:** Plots $P_a$ vs incoming $p$. Producer's risk $\alpha$ at AQL; Consumer's risk $\beta$ at LTPD.
- **Acceptance Sampling Plans:** Single, Double, Multiple, Sequential. Sampling does *not* improve quality.
- **Gurus to Remember for 2-Mark Definitions:** Deming (PDCA, 14 points), Juran (Quality Trilogy — Planning, Control, Improvement), Crosby (Zero Defects, Conformance), Ishikawa (Fishbone / Cause-and-Effect Diagram), Feigenbaum (Total Quality Control, COQ).
- **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram Categories:** 4M + E + S = Man, Machine, Material, Method, Environment, Measurement.
- **Seven Tools of Quality (Ishikawa's 7-QC Tools):** Cause-and-effect diagram, Check sheet, Control chart, Histogram, Pareto chart, Scatter diagram, Flowchart.
- **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule):** ~80% of problems are caused by ~20% of causes. Always rank defects by frequency.
- **Six Sigma:** $3.4$ defects per million opportunities (DPMO); process spread $= 12\sigma$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Statistical Quality Control (SQC) — The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** is the application of statistical techniques to measure, monitor, and control the quality of a manufacturing or service process. It was pioneered by **Walter A. Shewhart** at Bell Laboratories in **1924**, and forms the backbone of modern Total Quality Management (TQM), Six Sigma, and ISO 9001 quality systems.

SQC is broadly divided into two branches:

| Branch | Purpose | Tools |
|--------|---------|-------|
| **Acceptance Sampling** | Inspect a *sample* of finished lots to ACCEPT or REJECT them | Single, Double, Multiple Sampling Plans; OC Curves |
| **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** | Monitor an *ongoing process* using control charts to detect variations | Control Charts (this module's focus) |

## 1.2 The Control Chart — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Control Chart**: A control chart is a graphical tool used to study how a process changes over time. It plots a quality characteristic (mean, range, proportion defective, etc.) against time, with a **Center Line (CL)** representing the process average and two control limits — **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** and **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** — drawn at **±3 standard deviations** from the center line (the famous **3-sigma limits**).

### Conceptual Analogy 🏥 — The "Patient's Vitals Monitor"

Imagine a patient lying in a hospital ICU. The nurse hooks up a **vital signs monitor** that continuously displays the heart rate. The screen shows:
- A **green band** (normal range: 60–100 bpm) — the patient is stable.
- A **red alert** if the heart rate spikes above 100 or falls below 60.

A control chart works **exactly the same way**:
- The **process** = the patient
- The **quality characteristic** (e.g., shaft diameter) = the heart rate
- The **center line** = the patient's normal heart rate
- The **UCL / LCL (±3σ)** = the safe green band
- **Data points outside the limits** = the red alert — the process is "sick" and needs investigation!

## 1.3 Two Fundamental Types of Quality Characteristics

In KTU's framework, quality characteristics are classified into two broad categories — and *this classification directly determines the type of control chart you use*:

### A) Variables (Measurable / Continuous Data) 📏
- Data that can be **measured on a continuous scale**.
- Examples: Length (mm), weight (g), voltage (V), tensile strength (MPa), temperature (°C), viscosity.
- **Charts used**: **X̄ chart** (mean), **R chart** (range), **σ chart** (standard deviation).

### B) Attributes (Countable / Discrete Data) 🔢
- Data that can only be **counted** (whole numbers) — they are either *conforming* or *non-conforming*.
- Examples: Number of defectives, number of defects, go/no-go judgements, pass/fail tests.
- **Charts used**: **p-chart** (proportion defective), **np-chart** (number of defectives), **c-chart** (number of defects), **u-chart** (defects per unit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defective vs. Defect — DO NOT CONFUSE THIS!**
> - **Defective** = a *unit* that has one or more defects (it is rejected).
> - **Defect** = a *single flaw* on a unit (the unit may still be usable).
> - Example: A shirt with 3 loose buttons has **3 defects** but is **1 defective unit**.

## 1.4 The 3-Sigma Logic — Why ±3σ?

> [!NOTE]
> Shewhart chose **±3 standard deviations** as the control limits because, under the **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution**, **99.73%** of all data falls within this range. This means there is only a **0.27% chance** (about **1 in 370** points) of a point falling outside purely by random chance. Any such "out-of-control" signal therefore has a very high probability of indicating a **real assignable cause** (a "special cause" variation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Process Variation

Every manufacturing process exhibits two types of variation:

1. **Common Cause (Chance) Variation** — Inherent to the process; due to countless minor, random factors (raw material, machine wear, operator skill, ambient temperature). It produces a **stable, predictable distribution**.
2. **Special Cause (Assignable) Variation** — Due to identifiable, non-random factors (broken tool, new operator, power surge, faulty material batch). It produces an **unstable, unpredictable** pattern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fundamental purpose of a control chart is to distinguish Common Cause variation (background noise) from Special Cause variation (actionable signal).**

## 2.2 Control Charts for Variables (Measurable Data)

When the quality characteristic is **continuous**, we use **X̄ and R charts** (most common in industry) or **X̄ and σ charts** (more precise but computationally heavier).

### 2.2.1 The X̄ Chart (Mean Chart) — Tracks process AVERAGE

**Logic:** Take samples of size *n* from the process at regular intervals. For each sample, compute the mean $\bar{X_i}$. Plot $\bar{X_i}$ versus sample number. The grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}}$ is the center line.

**Control Limits:**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$$
$$CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}$$
$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

where $A_2$ is a constant that depends on sample size *n*. It is derived as $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$.

### 2.2.2 The R Chart (Range Chart) — Tracks process SPREAD

**Logic:** Plot the range $R_i = X_{max} - X_{min}$ for each sample. The average range $\bar{R}$ is the center line. The R chart is always constructed **first** (or simultaneously), because its stability must be verified before interpreting the X̄ chart.

**Control Limits:**

$$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$$
$$CL_R = \bar{R}$$
$$LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$$

where $D_3$ and $D_4$ are constants depending on *n*.

### 2.2.3 The σ Chart (Standard Deviation Chart)

**Logic:** Used when sample size is large (*n* > 10) or when σ is computed directly. More statistically rigorous than the R chart.

**Control Limits:**

$$UCL_\sigma = B_4 \bar{\sigma}$$
$$CL_\sigma = \bar{\sigma}$$
$$LCL_\sigma = B_3 \bar{\sigma}$$

### 2.2.4 Table of Control Chart Constants (Kₜᵤ HIGH-YIELD)

| Sample size $n$ | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $B_3$ | $B_4$ | $d_2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 0 | 2.568 | 1.693 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 0 | 2.266 | 2.059 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 0 | 2.089 | 2.326 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 0 | 1.970 | 2.534 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 0.030 | 1.882 | 2.704 |
| 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 0.118 | 1.815 | 2.847 |
| 9 | 0.337 | 0.184 | 1.816 | 0.185 | 1.761 | 2.970 |
| 10 | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 0.239 | 1.716 | 3.078 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For $n \leq 6$, $D_3 = 0$, meaning the **LCL for the R chart is zero** — you cannot have a negative range! This is a frequently-tested concept.

## 2.3 Control Charts for Attributes (Countable Data)

### 2.3.1 The p-Chart (Proportion Defective)

**Use when:** Sample size *n* **varies** (or constant too). Tracks the **fraction** of defective items in each sample.

**Estimating the average proportion defective:**

$$\bar{p} = \frac{\text{Total number of defectives in all samples}}{\text{Total number of items inspected}} = \frac{\sum n_i p_i}{\sum n_i}$$

**Control Limits:**

$$UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$$
$$CL_p = \bar{p}$$
$$LCL_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$$

If LCL < 0, set it to **0** (a proportion cannot be negative).

### 2.3.2 The np-Chart (Number of Defectives)

**Use when:** Sample size *n* is **constant**. Tracks the actual *count* of defectives per sample.

**Control Limits:**

$$UCL_{np} = n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$$
$$CL_{np} = n\bar{p}$$
$$LCL_{np} = n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$$

where $n\bar{p}$ is the **average number of defectives per sample**.

### 2.3.3 The c-Chart (Number of Defects per Unit)

**Use when:** The sample is a **single unit** (e.g., one roll of fabric, one car body, one page of text). Counts the *number of defects* on each unit.

**Control Limits:**

$$UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$$
$$CL_c = \bar{c}$$
$$LCL_c = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$$

where $\bar{c}$ = average number of defects per unit = $\dfrac{\sum c_i}{k}$ (*k* = number of samples).

### 2.3.4 The u-Chart (Defects per Unit)

**Use when:** Sample size (number of units inspected) **varies** and we want defects per unit. Most general attribute chart.

**Control Limits:**

$$UCL_u = \bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$$
$$CL_u = \bar{u}$$
$$LCL_u = \bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$$

where $\bar{u} = \dfrac{\sum c_i}{\sum n_i}$ = average defects per unit across all samples.

## 2.4 The Master KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **one-stop exam reference** for the topic.

| Chart Type | Center Line | UCL | LCL | Sample Size |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **X̄** | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\bar{R}$ | Fixed *n* |
| **R** | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4\bar{R}$ | $D_3\bar{R}$ | Fixed *n* |
| **σ** | $\bar{\sigma}$ | $B_4\bar{\sigma}$ | $B_3\bar{\sigma}$ | Fixed *n* |
| **p** | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | Variable *n* |
| **np** | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | Fixed *n* |
| **c** | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Unit = 1 |
| **u** | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | Variable *n* |

## 2.5 Decision Rules — When is the Process "Out of Control"?

A process is declared **out of statistical control** if any of these patterns appear:

1. **One point** falls outside the 3σ control limits.
2. **Two out of three** consecutive points fall on the same side of the center line, beyond the 2σ warning line.
3. **Four out of five** consecutive points fall on the same side of the center line, beyond the 1σ line.
4. **Seven or more** consecutive points fall on the **same side** of the center line (a "shift").
5. **Six points in a row** are steadily **increasing** or **decreasing** (a "trend").
6. **Cyclical** or other **non-random patterns** are observed.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world application:** The **Automotive Industry Action Group (AIAG)** and the **IATF 16949** standard require suppliers to react to Western Electric Rules (Rules 1–4) for SPC. A single breach can trigger 100% inspection of a production batch.
