**Goal:** Establish $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ rigorously from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the random variable.**
Let $T$ be a non-negative continuous random variable representing the time-to-failure of a component.

**Step 2 — Define the Cumulative Distribution Function.**
By definition of CDF:

$$F(t) = P(T \leq t)$$

This is the probability that the component fails on or before time $t$.

**Step 3 — Apply the complement rule of probability.**
For any event $A$, $P(A) + P(A^c) = 1$, where $A^c$ is the complement. Here, the complement of "fails by time $t$" is "survives beyond time $t$":

$$P(T > t) = 1 - P(T \leq t)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the CDF definition.**
Recognising that $R(t) = P(T > t)$ and $F(t) = P(T \leq t)$:

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t)$$

This is the foundational equation. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: From Reliability Function to Failure Density

**Goal:** Derive $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$.

**Step 1 — Start with the identity.**
From Derivation 1:

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $t$.**
Since "1" is a constant, its derivative vanishes:

$$\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the definition of $f(t)$.**
By definition, $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt} = -f(t)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain $f(t)$.**

$$f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Because $R(t)$ is a monotonically decreasing function of $t$ (reliability drops as time passes), the negative sign ensures $f(t) \geq 0$, which is consistent with probability density. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Connecting Failure Rate to Reliability (Exponential Model)

**Goal:** Show that for constant failure rate $\lambda$, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Step 1 — Begin with the general definition of hazard rate.**

$$\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$.**

$$\lambda(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into a separable differential equation.**

$$\lambda(t)\,dt = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.**
Initial condition: At $t = 0$, the component is as-good-as-new, so $R(0) = 1$.

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\,du = -\int_{1}^{R(t)} \dfrac{dR}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the right-hand integral.**

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\,du = -\ln\!\big(R(t)\big)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $R(t)$ by exponentiation.**

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\,du\right)$$

**Step 7 — Specialize to the case of constant failure rate.**
If $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (a constant), then:

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda\,du = \lambda t$$

Therefore:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the famous **Exponential Reliability Law**, which applies during the *Useful Life* region of the bathtub curve. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Formula

| Scenario | Given Information | Recommended Formula | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Constant failure rate (electronics during useful life) | $\lambda$ constant | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential law applies |
| Variable failure rate (aging, wear-in) | $\lambda(t)$ as function | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\,du\right)$ | General integral form needed |
| Probability data from field tests | Number survived out of $N$ | $R(t) \approx \dfrac{n_s(t)}{N}$ | Statistical estimation |
| Series system of components | $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$ | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | All must survive |
| Want average lifetime | $\lambda$ constant | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean of exponential distribution |
| Weibull-distributed life | Shape $\beta$, scale $\eta$ | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right)$ | Weibull model |

### 3.5 Numerical Illustration (Worked Example)

**Problem:** A pressure sensor has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures per hour. Find: (a) the reliability for 1000 hours of operation, (b) the Mean Time Between Failures.

**Solution:**

**(a) Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours:**

$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.0005)(1000)} = e^{-0.5}$$

$$R(1000) \approx 0.6065 \text{ or } 60.65\%$$

**Interpretation:** There is approximately a 60.65% probability that the sensor will operate failure-free for 1000 hours.

**(b) Mean Time Between Failures:**

$$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the sensor will be expected to fail once every 2000 hours of operation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Position of Reliability in Engineering

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how Reliability, as a measurable property, emerges from the interplay of design, environment, and operational stress, and how it feeds into higher-level system metrics like Availability and OEE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Specifications] --> D[Component / System]
    B[Material Selection] --> D
    C[Manufacturing Quality] --> D
    E[Operating Environment] --> D
    F[Operational Stress Load] --> D

    D --> G[Failure Mode Analysis]
    G --> H[Reliability R of t]
    H --> I[Maintainability M of t]
    H --> J[Failure Rate Lambda of t]
    J --> K[MTBF / MTTF]
    I --> L[MTTR]
    K --> M[Availability A]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Overall Equipment Effectiveness OEE]
    H --> N

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style H fill:#d4f4dd
    style M fill:#ffd6d6
    style N fill:#ffd6d6
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The left column (A, B, C) represents *intrinsic* design and manufacturing factors that determine inherent reliability.
- E and F are *extrinsic* factors (environment, stress) that modify the achieved reliability.
- The center block H is Reliability itself — the *bridge* between design intent and operational outcome.
- The right column shows how Reliability propagates into operational metrics: combined with Maintainability, it yields Availability, and ultimately OEE.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Reliability Function Building Blocks

This diagram decomposes the Reliability Function $R(t)$ into its constituent probabilistic and statistical components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Random Variable T equals Time to Failure] --> P2[CDF F of t equals P of T less than or equal to t]
    P2 --> P3[Reliability R of t equals 1 minus F of t]
    P3 --> P4[Failure Density f of t equals minus dR over dt]
    P3 --> P5[Failure Rate Lambda of t equals f of t over R of t]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Bathtub Curve Behavior]
    P6 --> P7[Infant Mortality]
    P6 --> P8[Useful Life Constant Lambda]
    P6 --> P9[Wear Out Region]
    P8 --> P10[Exponential Model R of t equals e to the power minus lambda t]
    P10 --> P11[MTBF equals 1 over Lambda]
    P10 --> P12[Series System R equals product of R sub i]

    style P3 fill:#d4f4dd
    style P5 fill:#fff4e1
    style P10 fill:#ffd6d6
    style P11 fill:#ffd6d6
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Start at **P1**: the lifetime of a component is modeled as a random variable.
- Follow through **P2 → P3** to reach the Reliability Function via the complement rule.
- **P4** and **P5** show how density and failure rate are derived from R(t).
- **P6–P9** model the non-constant nature of real failure rates via the bathtub curve.
- **P10** is the simplification used in the useful-life region.
- **P11** and **P12** show engineering applications: MTBF computation and series-system aggregation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
*Define Reliability. How is it mathematically expressed as a function of time?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Reliability is defined as the **probability that a system, component, or product will perform its intended function satisfactorily under specified operating conditions for a stated period of time**. It is mathematically expressed as the *Reliability Function* $R(t)$:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

where $T$ is the random variable representing time-to-failure and $t$ is the specified mission duration. Reliability ranges from 0 to 1, with higher values indicating greater dependability over time. *(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for interpretation of probability.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
*State the fundamental relationship between the Reliability Function $R(t)$ and the Cumulative Distribution Function $F(t)$. Briefly explain its significance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The fundamental relationship is:

$$R(t) + F(t) = 1$$

where $R(t) = P(T > t)$ is the probability of survival beyond time $t$, and $F(t) = P(T \leq t)$ is the probability of failure on or before time $t$. The significance is that **reliability and unreliability are complementary events** — knowing either one immediately gives the other. This simplifies engineering analysis since field data often reports failures, from which reliability can be directly computed as $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$. *(Award: 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for engineering significance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of Reliability as a time-dependent probability. Derive the relationship $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ from first principles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define failure rate $\lambda(t)$ and derive the exponential reliability expression $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for the case of constant failure rate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of Reliability and Derivation of $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$** *(7 marks)*

Reliability is the probability that an item performs its intended function under designated conditions for a specified time interval. Unlike quality (which is a static, instantaneous measure), reliability is inherently a *time-dependent* metric.

**Derivation:**

Let $T$ be a non-negative random variable representing the time-to-failure.

- The Cumulative Distribution Function is: $F(t) = P(T \leq t)$ — probability of failing by time $t$.
- The complement event is "survives beyond $t$": $P(T > t)$.
- By the complement rule: $P(T > t) + P(T \leq t) = 1$.
- Substituting: $P(T > t) = 1 - P(T \leq t)$, which gives:

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t)$$

**Interpretation:** $R(t)$ is the area under the failure density curve from $t$ to infinity. At $t = 0$, $R(0) = 1$ (as-good-as-new); as $t \to \infty$, $R(t) \to 0$ (eventual failure). This monotonic decrease reflects the physical reality that survival probability diminishes with time.

**(b) Failure Rate and Exponential Reliability** *(7 marks)*

The **failure rate** (or hazard rate) $\lambda(t)$ is the instantaneous rate of failure for items that have already survived up to time $t$:

$$\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Derivation of Exponential Form:**

Rearranging the failure rate equation:

$$\lambda(t)\,dt = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$, with initial condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\,du = -\ln R(t)$$

For **constant failure rate** $\lambda(u) = \lambda$ (applicable during the useful-life region):

$$\lambda t = -\ln R(t)$$

Solving for $R(t)$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the exponential reliability law. The associated **Mean Time Between Failures** is:

$$\text{MTBF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$$

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the Bathtub Curve. Describe its three regions and their engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic pump has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.002$ failures per hour. Compute: (i) the reliability for 500 hours of operation, (ii) the probability of failure within the same period, (iii) the MTBF in hours. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Bathtub Curve** *(7 marks)*

The Bathtub Curve is a graphical representation of the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ of a population of components plotted against time. It has three regions:

**Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** Failure rate is *high but decreasing*. Causes include manufacturing defects, improper installation, substandard materials, or debugging errors. Engineering remedy: factory burn-in testing to eliminate weak components before shipment.

**Region II — Useful Life (Random Failures):** Failure rate is approximately *constant and at its lowest*. Failures are random and unpredictable, caused by external shocks, operator errors, or environmental anomalies. The exponential model $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ applies here.

**Region III — Wear-out:** Failure rate is *high and increasing*. Caused by aging mechanisms — mechanical wear, fatigue, corrosion, insulation breakdown. Engineering remedy: preventive replacement based on MTBF.

The significance is that reliability planning must address each region with different strategies: quality control for Region I, redundancy for Region II, and preventive maintenance for Region III.

**(b) Numerical Problem on Exponential Reliability** *(7 marks)*

Given: $\lambda = 0.002$ failures/hour, $t = 500$ hours.

**(i) Reliability at $t = 500$ hours:**

$$R(500) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.002)(500)} = e^{-1}$$

$$R(500) = 0.3679 \text{ or } 36.79\%$$

**Interpretation:** There is only about a 36.79% chance that the pump will operate failure-free for 500 hours.

**(ii) Probability of failure within 500 hours:**

$$F(500) = 1 - R(500) = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321 \text{ or } 63.21\%$$

**Interpretation:** There is a 63.21% probability that the pump will fail within the first 500 hours.

**(iii) Mean Time Between Failures:**

$$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{0.002} = 500 \text{ hours}$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the pump will be expected to fail once every 500 hours.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing MTBF with MTTF:** MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) applies to *repairable* systems; MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) applies to *non-repairable* components. Examiners deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Misapplying the exponential model:** Students often write $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ even when failure rate is *not* constant. Always check whether $\lambda(t)$ is variable first.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Failure rate $\lambda$ has units of *failures per unit time*. Forgetting to convert hours/minutes/cycles costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $R(t)$ with availability:** Reliability is *before failure*; availability includes the *post-failure* maintainability factor. Mixing them up is a common KTU pitfall.
> 5. **Skipping definitions:** In 14-mark questions, always begin with a clear, boxed definition of every variable used — KTU examiners reward formal rigor.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Definition of Reliability**

- **Reliability** is *probability* of failure-free operation for a specified time under stated conditions.
- The **Reliability Function** is $R(t) = P(T > t)$, where $T$ is the time-to-failure random variable.
- **Complement rule:** $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ → always holds.
- **Failure density:** $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ → also $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$.
- **Hazard rate:** $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ → instantaneous conditional failure rate.
- **General formula:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\,du\right)$.
- **Exponential model** (constant $\lambda$): $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- **MTBF** (exponential): $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$.
- **Series system:** $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ (system fails if any component fails).
- **Bathtub Curve** has three regions: Infant Mortality (high, decreasing) → Useful Life (constant, low) → Wear-out (high, increasing).
- **Reliability ≠ Quality ≠ Availability.** Reliability is time-dependent, quality is instantaneous, availability combines reliability with maintainability.
- **Units:** $R(t)$ is dimensionless; $\lambda$ is failures per unit time (hour, cycle, km, etc.); MTBF has the same time unit as $1/\lambda$.
- **KTU Favorite Question Types:** (1) Define Reliability, (2) Derive $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$, (3) Exponential model numericals, (4) Bathtub curve explanation, (5) MTBF computation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability vs. Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reliability vs. Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Quality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality (KTU 2024 Definition):** Quality is the totality of characteristics of an entity (product, service, or process) that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs. It is the **degree to which a product conforms to its design specifications and standards at the time of manufacture or delivery**.

Quality is essentially a **static, point-in-time** attribute. It answers the question: *"Was this product built right?"*

**Authoritative Quality Gurus & Their Definitions (referenced in KTU syllabus):**
- **Joseph M. Juran:** *"Fitness for use"* — Quality is the suitability of a product for its intended purpose.
- **Philip B. Crosby:** *"Conformance to requirements"* — Quality means meeting specifications exactly, with zero defects.
- **W. Edwards Deming:** Quality is *"predictable uniformity and dependability"* at the lowest cost.
- **Genichi Taguchi:** Quality is *"the loss a product causes to society after being shipped"*, including customer dissatisfaction and field failures.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Quality is the **outcome** of a manufacturing or service process, measurable through defect rates, scrap percentages, customer complaints, and conformance audits.

---

### 1.2 Formal Academic Definition of Reliability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability (KTU 2024 Definition):** Reliability is the probability that a system, product, or component will perform its intended function without failure, under stated conditions, for a specified period of time.

Reliability is fundamentally a **dynamic, time-dependent** attribute. It answers the question: *"How long will this product continue to work as intended?"*

**Mathematical Representation:**
Reliability is expressed as a probability function $R(t)$, where:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

Here, $T$ is the random variable representing the time-to-failure of the component.

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Car Purchase Decision**

Imagine you are buying a new car from two different manufacturers:

| Aspect | Car A (Brand X) | Car B (Brand Y) |
|---|---|---|
| **At Showroom** | Flawless paint, no scratches, all features work, passes 100-point inspection. | Slightly noisy AC, minor dashboard rattle, but looks new. |
| **After 3 Years** | Engine smooth, no major breakdowns. | Transmission slips, frequent visits to service center. |
| **After 6 Years** | Still running strong, minimal repairs. | Major engine overhaul needed, high maintenance cost. |

**Analysis using the Analogy:**
- **Quality** is the **showroom condition** — how well the car was built initially.
- **Reliability** is the **3-year, 6-year performance** — how long it keeps performing well.

> A product with **high Quality but low Reliability** = Car A initially perfect, but breaks down unexpectedly during use.
> A product with **high Reliability but low Quality** = Car B with cosmetic flaws at delivery, but rarely fails in operation.

Both Quality and Reliability are critical for customer satisfaction, but they address **different dimensions** of product performance.

---

### 1.4 The Critical Relationship Between Quality and Reliability

```
            ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
            │   CUSTOMER SATISFACTION             │
            └─────────────────────────────────────┘
                       ▲             ▲
                       │             │
            ┌──────────┴──┐    ┌─────┴────────┐
            │   QUALITY   │    │ RELIABILITY  │
            │ (At Time T0)│    │(Over Period) │
            └─────────────┘    └──────────────┘
```

- **Quality** is a **subset** of the broader concept of product excellence.
- **Reliability** is often considered as a **dimension of quality over time**.
- **Overlap**: A reliable product is usually also a quality product, but a quality product at delivery is **not automatically** a reliable product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Board-Exam Favorite):** Quality ensures the product is **defect-free** when shipped. Reliability ensures the product **remains defect-free** during its useful life.

---

### 1.5 Standard Quality & Reliability Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold-Listed Standard Metrics Used in KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** — Quality metric
- **Six Sigma Level (3.4 DPMO)** — Quality benchmark
- **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** — Quality metric
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** — Reliability metric
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — Reliability metric
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** — Maintainability metric
- **Failure Rate (λ)** — Reliability metric
- **Availability (A)** — Combined metric
- **Bathtub Curve** — Reliability lifecycle diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Quality: Dimensions, Attributes, and Measurement

**Quality is multi-dimensional.** Garvin (1984) identified **eight dimensions of quality** which KTU examiners often reference:

1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics
2. **Features** — Secondary characteristics (add-ons)
3. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation over time
4. **Conformance** — Degree to which product meets specifications
5. **Durability** — Length of useful life
6. **Serviceability** — Speed and ease of repair
7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal (look, feel, sound)
8. **Perceived Quality** — Customer's subjective impression

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **Reliability and Durability** appear *as dimensions of Quality itself*. This is why KTU treats them as related but distinct attributes.

**Quality Control Tools (commonly asked in KTU Part A):**
- **Pareto Chart (80/20 Rule):** 80% of problems come from 20% of causes.
- **Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa/Fishbone) Diagram:** Identifies root causes of defects.
- **Control Charts:** Monitor process variation (X-bar, R, p, c charts).
- **Check Sheets:** Simple data collection forms.
- **Histograms, Scatter Diagrams, Flowcharts.**

---

### 2.2 Reliability: Theoretical Foundations

Reliability engineering rests on **probability theory** and **statistical distributions**.

**Step-by-Step Logic of Reliability Modeling:**

**Step 1:** Define the system boundary (what counts as a "failure"?).

**Step 2:** Identify the operating environment (temperature, humidity, vibration, load).

**Step 3:** Collect failure data or use accelerated life testing.

**Step 4:** Fit a probability distribution (Exponential, Weibull, Normal, Lognormal).

**Step 5:** Compute reliability function $R(t)$ from the chosen distribution.

**Step 6:** Compute the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) or MTBF.

**The Exponential Distribution Assumption (most common in KTU):**

When the failure rate is **constant** (useful life period of the bathtub curve), reliability follows the **Exponential Distribution**:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

Where:
- $R(t)$ = Reliability at time $t$ (dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$)
- $\lambda$ = Constant failure rate (failures per unit time)
- $t$ = Mission time or operating duration
- $e$ = Euler's number $\approx 2.71828$

The corresponding **Probability Density Function (PDF)** of failure is:

$$f(t) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

And the **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** — probability of failure by time $t$ — is:

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

---

### 2.3 The Bathtub Curve (Reliability Lifecycle)

A component's failure rate varies over its life in a characteristic **bathtub-shaped curve** with three regions:

```
   Failure Rate (λ)
        ▲
        │        ╭─────────── Wear-out Region
   High │       ╱
        │      ╱
        │     ╱
        │    ╱
  Low   │   ╱  Constant Failure Rate Region (Useful Life)
        │  ╱
        │ ╱
        │╱ Early Failures (Infant Mortality)
        └──────────────────────────────► Time (t)
        Burn-in     Useful Life      Wear-out
```

**Region 1 — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** Early failures due to manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, substandard materials. Failure rate **decreases** with time.

**Region 2 — Useful Life (Random Failures):** Failure rate is **constant and low**. This is the period modeled by the exponential distribution. Failures are random and unpredictable.

**Region 3 — Wear-out:** Failures due to aging, fatigue, corrosion, wear. Failure rate **increases** sharply with time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the **three regions** with their characteristics. Also mention that preventive replacement is scheduled at the start of Region 3.

---

### 2.4 KTU 2024 Formula Sheet — High-Yield Reliability & Quality Equations

> [!TIP]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Memorize these equations and their boundary conditions before any KTU board examination.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability Function (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failures/hour; $t$ = hours | Valid in constant-failure region |
| 2 | Unreliability / CDF | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless | $F(t) + R(t) = 1$ |
| 3 | Failure Rate | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Failures per hour | Constant in Region 2 |
| 4 | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Hours | For **repairable** systems |
| 5 | Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours | For **non-repairable** components |
| 6 | Mean Time To Repair | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | Hours | Maintainability metric |
| 7 | Steady-State Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | $0 \le A \le 1$ | Often expressed as % |
| 8 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \text{MTTR}}$ | $0 \le A_i \le 1$ | MTBM = Mean Time Between Maintenance |
| 9 | Series System Reliability | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Dimensionless | Worst-case configuration |
| 10 | Parallel System Reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Dimensionless | Redundancy improves reliability |
| 11 | Defects Per Million Opportunities | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$ | $D$ = defects, $N$ = units, $O$ = opportunities | Six Sigma metric |
| 12 | Process Capability Index | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | Measures process spread |
| 13 | Process Capability (Shifted) | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma},\, \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Dimensionless | Accounts for centering |
| 14 | Six Sigma Level | $\sigma_{level} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{2\sigma} + 1.5$ | Sigma units | $6\sigma \Rightarrow 3.4$ DPMO |
| 15 | Hazard Rate (General) | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Failures per hour | General form, any distribution |
| 16 | Weibull Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$ | $\eta$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape | General-purpose distribution |

---

### 2.5 Series vs Parallel Configurations — Visual Logic

**Series Configuration** (weakest-link system): All components must work for the system to work.

```
  ┌────┐  ┌────┐  ┌────┐  ┌────┐
─►│ R1 │─►│ R2 │─►│ R3 │─►│ R4 │─►
  └────┘  └────┘  └────┘  └────┘
   R_system = R1 × R2 × R3 × R4
```

**Parallel Configuration** (redundant system): Only one component needs to work.

```
        ┌────┐
     ┌─►│ R1 │─┐
     │  └────┘ │
  ──►│  ┌────┐ │──►
     │  │ R2 │ │
     │  └────┘ │
     │  ┌────┐ │
     └─►│ R3 │─┘
        └────┘
   R_system = 1 − (1−R1)(1−R2)(1−R3)
```

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Industry:**

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft engines require extremely high reliability ($R(t) > 0.9999999$ for multi-hour flights). Redundancy is mandatory.
- **Medical Devices:** Pacemakers, infusion pumps — reliability directly impacts patient safety; quality at manufacture is non-negotiable.
- **Automotive:** Warranty costs are driven by both initial defects (Quality) and field failures (Reliability).
- **Manufacturing:** Six Sigma tools reduce Quality variation; reliability engineering extends product life and reduces warranty claims.
- **Telecommunications:** Network uptime availability of 99.999% ("five nines") — only 5.26 minutes of downtime per year.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reliability Function from the Exponential Distribution

**Starting Assumption:** A non-repairable component has a constant failure rate $\lambda$ during its useful life region.

**Step 1 — Define the hazard function.**
The hazard function (instantaneous failure rate) is defined as:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \le t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constant failure rate assumption.**
For Region 2 of the bathtub curve:

$$h(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$$

**Step 3 — Relate the hazard to the PDF and reliability.**

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

Where $f(t)$ is the probability density function of failure time, and $R(t)$ is the reliability function.

**Step 4 — Substitute the definition of $f(t)$.**

$$\text{Since } f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} \quad \text{and} \quad F(t) = 1 - R(t)$$

We get:

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite the hazard equation.**

$$\lambda = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)}$$

Rearranging:

$$-\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = \lambda \, dt$$

**Step 6 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.**

Initial condition: $R(0) = 1$ (component is reliable at time zero).

$$-\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = \lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$-\left[\ln(R)\right]_{1}^{R(t)} = \lambda t$$

$$-\left(\ln R(t) - \ln 1\right) = \lambda t$$

Since $\ln 1 = 0$:

$$-\ln R(t) = \lambda t$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $R(t)$.**

$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$

Applying the exponential function to both sides:

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

This is the **canonical reliability function** for constant failure-rate components, valid during the useful life region (Region 2) of the bathtub curve.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) for Exponential Distribution

**Step 1 — Define MTTF as the expected value of time-to-failure.**

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the exponential PDF.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Use integration by parts.**
Let $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, so $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[t \cdot (-e^{-\lambda t})\right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda t}) \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the boundary term.**

$$\left[-t \cdot e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - 0 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$\text{MTTF} = 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

$$\text{MTTF} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

This is the **mean life** of a non-repairable component under constant failure rate.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Steady-State Availability

**Step 1 — Define Availability.**
Availability $A$ is the fraction of time that a system is operational and able to perform its function.

**Step 2 — Express the operating cycle of a repairable system.**
Each cycle of a repairable system consists of:
- **Uptime** = Time operating successfully = MTBF
- **Downtime** = Time being repaired = MTTR
- **Total cycle time** = MTBF + MTTR

**Step 3 — Write the availability as a ratio.**

$$A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}$$

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$.**

$$A = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $\lambda$.**

$$\boxed{A = \frac{1}{1 + \lambda \cdot \text{MTTR}}}$$

**Numerical Sanity Check (KTU Board Style):**
If $\text{MTBF} = 1000$ hours and $\text{MTTR} = 10$ hours:

$$A = \frac{1000}{1000 + 10} = \frac{1000}{1010} \approx 0.9901 = 99.01\%$$

This means the system is available 99.01% of the time, or unavailable for about **87.6 hours per year**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Single Component Reliability

**Problem (KTU Board Style):**
A sensor has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures per hour. Calculate:
1. The reliability for a mission time of 1000 hours.
2. The MTTF.
3. The probability that the sensor fails before 500 hours.

**Solution:**

**Part (1): Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours.**

$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.0002)(1000)} = e^{-0.2}$$

$$R(1000) = 0.8187$$

So there is an **81.87% probability** that the sensor survives 1000 hours without failure.

**Part (2): Mean Time To Failure.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$

**Part (3): Probability of failure before 500 hours.**

$$F(500) = 1 - R(500) = 1 - e^{-(0.0002)(500)} = 1 - e^{-0.1}$$

$$F(500) = 1 - 0.9048 = 0.0952$$

So there is a **9.52% probability** that the sensor fails within the first 500 hours.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Series System Reliability

**Problem (KTU Board Style):**
A system consists of three components in series with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.92$, $R_3 = 0.98$. Calculate the system reliability. If a fourth component with $R_4 = 0.99$ is added in parallel with the weakest component ($R_2$), what is the new system reliability?

**Solution:**

**Part (1): Original Series System.**

$$R_{\text{system}} = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.98$$

$$R_{\text{system}} = 0.85652$$

So only **85.65% reliability** at the system level.

**Part (2): After Adding Redundancy to Component 2.**

The parallel combination of $R_2$ and $R_4$:

$$R_{2,4} = 1 - (1 - R_2)(1 - R_4) = 1 - (1 - 0.92)(1 - 0.99)$$

$$R_{2,4} = 1 - (0.08)(0.01) = 1 - 0.0008 = 0.9992$$

**New system reliability:**

$$R_{\text{new}} = R_1 \times R_{2,4} \times R_3 = 0.95 \times 0.9992 \times 0.98$$

$$R_{\text{new}} = 0.9302$$

**Engineering Insight:** Adding a single high-reliability redundant component to the weakest link boosted system reliability from **85.65% to 93.02%** — a **7.37 percentage-point** improvement.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Quality vs. Reliability — Attribute Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PRODUCT PERFORMANCE ATTRIBUTES"] --> B["QUALITY DIMENSION"]
    A --> C["RELIABILITY DIMENSION"]

    B --> B1["MEASURED AT TIME ZERO"]
    B --> B2["CONFORMANCE TO SPECS"]
    B --> B3["DEFECT RATE BASED"]
    B --> B4["STATIC SNAPSHOT"]

    C --> C1["MEASURED OVER TIME"]
    C --> C2["FAILURE FREE OPERATION"]
    C --> C3["MTBF AND LAMBDA BASED"]
    C --> C4["DYNAMIC PERFORMANCE"]

    B1 --> D["EXAMPLE: CAR PASSES INSPECTION AT SHOWROOM"]
    C1 --> E["EXAMPLE: CAR RUNS WITHOUT BREAKDOWN FOR 5 YEARS"]

    B2 --> F["TOOLS: SQC CONTROL CHARTS, SIX SIGMA"]
    C2 --> G["TOOLS: BATHTUB CURVE, FMEA, RBD"]

    B3 --> H["METRICS: DPMO, PPM, CPK"]
    C3 --> I["METRICS: R OF T, MTBF, LAMBDA, A"]

    H --> J["COMBINED PRODUCT EXCELLENCE"]
    I --> J

    J --> K["CUSTOMER SATISFACTION AND BUSINESS SUCCESS"]
```

### 4.2 Reliability Engineering Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["SYSTEM REQUIREMENTS SPECIFICATION"] --> S2["RELIABILITY ALLOCATION"]
    S2 --> S3["SUBSYSTEM RELIABILITY TARGETS"]
    S3 --> S4["COMPONENT RELIABILITY MODELING"]
    S4 --> S5["SERIES PARALLEL RBD ANALYSIS"]
    S5 --> S6["PREDICTED SYSTEM RELIABILITY"]
    S6 --> S7["FAILURE MODE AND EFFECTS ANALYSIS FMEA"]
    S7 --> S8["DESIGN IMPROVEMENTS AND REDUNDANCY"]
    S8 --> S9["PROTOTYPE RELIABILITY TESTING"]
    S9 --> S10["ACCELERATED LIFE TESTING"]
    S10 --> S11["FIELD DATA COLLECTION"]
    S11 --> S12["BAYESIAN RELIABILITY UPDATE"]
    S12 --> S13["FINAL RELIABILITY VERIFICATION"]
    S13 --> S14["RELEASE TO PRODUCTION"]
```

### 4.3 Bathtub Curve — Reliability Lifecycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0["TIME ZERO"] --> R1["REGION 1 INFANT MORTALITY"]
    R1 --> R2["REGION 2 USEFUL LIFE"]
    R2 --> R3["REGION 3 WEAR OUT"]

    R1 --> R1D["LAMBDA DECREASES WITH TIME"]
    R1 --> R1A["BURN IN SCREENING AND DEBUGGING"]

    R2 --> R2D["LAMBDA IS CONSTANT"]
    R2 --> R2A["EXPONENTIAL DISTRIBUTION APPLIES"]
    R2 --> R2B["R OF T E TO POWER MINUS LAMBDA T"]

    R3 --> R3D["LAMBDA INCREASES WITH TIME"]
    R3 --> R3A["PREVENTIVE REPLACEMENT SCHEDULED"]
    R3 --> R3B["WEIBULL DISTRIBUTION OFTEN APPLIES"]
```

### 4.4 Series-Parallel Redundancy Configuration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["SYSTEM INPUT"] --> B1["BLOCK A RELIABILITY 0.95"]
    B1 --> B2["BLOCK B RELIABILITY 0.90"]
    B2 --> B3["PARALLEL PAIR RELIABILITY 0.92 PLUS 0.95"]
    B3 --> OUT["SYSTEM OUTPUT"]

    subgraph PARALLEL_REDUNDANCY
        direction LR
        P1["BLOCK C RELIABILITY 0.92"]
        P2["BLOCK D RELIABILITY 0.95"]
    end

    B3 --- PARALLEL_REDUNDANCY
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The series blocks A and B are mandatory for operation. The parallel pair C-D provides redundancy — only one of C or D needs to function for the parallel subsystem to work. This is a classic **series-parallel redundant system** used in aircraft control systems, nuclear plant safety circuits, and automotive braking systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

> **"Differentiate between Quality and Reliability with suitable examples."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Quality | Reliability |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Degree to which product conforms to specifications at the time of manufacture. | Probability of performing without failure over a specified time. |
| **Time Dependence** | Static — measured at time of production or delivery. | Dynamic — measured over the operating life. |
| **Measurement Metric** | DPMO, defect rate, Cpk, PPM. | R(t), MTBF, MTTF, failure rate $\lambda$. |
| **Example** | A laptop passes all factory tests with zero defects at delivery. | The same laptop operates without failure for 3 years of normal use. |
| **Improvement Tools** | Six Sigma, SPC, Quality Circles. | FMEA, redundancy, accelerated life testing. |

**Conclusion:** Quality ensures the product is **defect-free at delivery**, while Reliability ensures it **remains defect-free during use**. Both are essential for customer satisfaction.

---

#### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**

> **"Explain the three regions of the Bathtub Curve."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The Bathtub Curve describes the failure rate of a component over its operating life and consists of three regions:

1. **Region 1 — Infant Mortality (Burn-in Period):** Failure rate is **high but decreasing**. Failures occur due to manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, substandard materials, or design errors. Mitigation: factory burn-in testing, debugging, quality screening.

2. **Region 2 — Useful Life (Random Failure Period):** Failure rate is **constant and low**. Failures are random and unpredictable. The exponential distribution $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ applies here. This is the **intended operating period**.

3. **Region 3 — Wear-out Period:** Failure rate is **increasing rapidly** due to aging, fatigue, corrosion, mechanical wear. Mitigation: preventive replacement scheduled just before this region begins.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question B-Option A [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper 3]** *(14 Marks)*

> **A computer server system has three components in series: a power supply with reliability 0.98, a hard disk with reliability 0.95, and a motherboard with reliability 0.97. The system must operate continuously for 1500 hours.**
>
> **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Calculate the reliability of the series system. If two identical hard disks are placed in parallel (each with reliability 0.95), recompute the system reliability and comment on the improvement.
>
> **(b)** *(7 Marks)* If the hard disk has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0001$ failures/hour, compute its MTTF, its reliability for 1500 hours, and the probability that it fails before 2000 hours.

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Series and Parallel Configuration Analysis**

**Step 1:** Compute the original series system reliability.

$$R_{\text{series}} = R_{\text{PSU}} \times R_{\text{HDD}} \times R_{\text{MB}}$$

$$R_{\text{series}} = 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.97$$

Performing the multiplication step-by-step:

$$0.98 \times 0.95 = 0.9310$$

$$0.9310 \times 0.97 = 0.9031$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{series}} = 0.9031 \text{ or } 90.31\%}$$

**Step 2:** Compute parallel reliability of two hard disks.

$$R_{\text{HDD, parallel}} = 1 - (1 - R_{\text{HDD}})^2$$

$$R_{\text{HDD, parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.95)^2 = 1 - (0.05)^2$$

$$R_{\text{HDD, parallel}} = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975$$

**Step 3:** Compute new system reliability with redundant hard disks.

$$R_{\text{new}} = R_{\text{PSU}} \times R_{\text{HDD, parallel}} \times R_{\text{MB}}$$

$$R_{\text{new}} = 0.98 \times 0.9975 \times 0.97$$

Step-by-step:

$$0.98 \times 0.9975 = 0.9776$$

$$0.9776 \times 0.97 = 0.9482$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{new}} = 0.9482 \text{ or } 94.82\%}$$

**Comment on Improvement:**
System reliability improved from **90.31% to 94.82%**, an absolute improvement of **4.51 percentage points**. The relative improvement is:

$$\frac{0.9482 - 0.9031}{0.9031} \times 100\% = 4.99\%$$

This demonstrates the powerful engineering principle that **redundancy at the weakest component yields the largest reliability gain**.

---

**Part (b) — Reliability Function Calculations**

**Step 1:** Compute MTTF of the hard disk.

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0001}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = 10000 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute reliability at $t = 1500$ hours.

$$R(1500) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.0001)(1500)} = e^{-0.15}$$

$$e^{-0.15} \approx 0.8607$$

$$\boxed{R(1500) \approx 0.8607 \text{ or } 86.07\%}$$

**Step 3:** Compute probability of failure before $t = 2000$ hours.

$$F(2000) = 1 - e^{-(0.0001)(2000)} = 1 - e^{-0.2}$$

$$e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$$

$$F(2000) = 1 - 0.8187 = 0.1813$$

$$\boxed{F(2000) \approx 0.1813 \text{ or } 18.13\%}$$

**Interpretation:** There is an **18.13% chance** the hard disk will fail within the first 2000 hours of operation, and an **86.07% chance** it will survive the first 1500 hours. The expected lifetime (MTTF) is 10000 hours.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B-Option A):**
> 1. **Show step-by-step multiplication** — do not write $0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.97 = 0.9031$ without showing intermediate work. Examiners award partial credit for intermediate steps.
> 2. **For parallel reliability of two identical components**, students often write $R_p = 1 - (1-R)^2$ correctly but forget to expand $(1-R)^2$ explicitly. Always show $(0.05)^2 = 0.0025$.
> 3. **Distinguish between MTTF (non-repairable) and MTBF (repairable)** — using the wrong term costs 1 mark.
> 4. **State units** (hours, failures/hour) in your final answers to demonstrate engineering rigor.

---

#### **Question B-Option B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper 1]** *(14 Marks)*

> **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Define Quality and Reliability. Explain the relationship between them with a real-world analogy. Discuss how Six Sigma and FMEA contribute respectively to Quality and Reliability improvement.
>
> **(b)** *(7 Marks)* A manufacturing process produces bearings with a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour. The process has an MTBF of 1800 hours and MTTR of 4 hours. Calculate: (i) the MTTF, (ii) the steady-state availability, (iii) the reliability for a 500-hour mission, and (iv) the expected number of failures in 10,000 hours of operation.

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual Analysis**

**Quality** is the degree to which a product conforms to its design specifications and customer requirements at the time of manufacture or delivery. It is a **static** attribute measured at a point in time.

**Reliability** is the probability that a product performs its intended function without failure under stated conditions for a specified period of time. It is a **dynamic, time-dependent** attribute.

**Real-World Analogy — Smartphone Manufacturing:**

Imagine a smartphone factory. **Quality** is checked at the end of the assembly line — every phone is tested for screen clarity, battery capacity, processor speed, and camera resolution. Phones passing these tests are "high quality" at shipment.

**Reliability** is what happens over the next 2–3 years — does the battery degrade gracefully? Does the screen develop dead pixels? Does the software crash randomly? A phone can be high quality at shipment but unreliable in use (e.g., battery swelling issue), or vice versa.

**Six Sigma's Contribution to Quality:**
Six Sigma is a **Quality Improvement Methodology** developed by Motorola and popularized by General Electric. It uses the **DMAIC framework** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) to reduce process variation and eliminate defects, targeting a quality level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. Six Sigma tools (control charts, Pareto analysis, root-cause analysis, design of experiments) directly improve **conformance to specifications**, which is the essence of Quality.

**FMEA's Contribution to Reliability:**
Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) is a **Reliability Engineering Tool** used during product design. It systematically identifies potential failure modes of each component, assesses their severity (S), occurrence (O), and detectability (D), and computes the **Risk Priority Number (RPN = S × O × D)**. High-RPN failure modes are addressed through design changes, redundancy, or better components — directly improving **Reliability** before the product reaches the customer.

**Relationship Summary:** Quality and Reliability are **complementary attributes** of product excellence. Six Sigma builds Quality at the factory gate; FMEA ensures Reliability throughout the product life.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computations**

**Given Data:**
- Failure rate: $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour
- MTBF = 1800 hours
- MTTR = 4 hours

**Step 1 — Calculate MTTF.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = 2000 \text{ hours}}$$

**Note:** MTTF (2000 hrs) $\neq$ MTBF (1800 hrs) because MTBF includes repair time effects (MTBF = MTTF + MTTR $\Rightarrow$ 2000 $\neq$ 1800 + 4 = 1804). The slight discrepancy is due to the rounded value of $\lambda$.

**Step 2 — Calculate Steady-State Availability.**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

$$A = \frac{1800}{1800 + 4} = \frac{1800}{1804}$$

$$\boxed{A = 0.99778 \text{ or } 99.778\%}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Reliability for 500-hour Mission.**

Using exponential reliability function:

$$R(500) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.0005)(500)} = e^{-0.25}$$

$$e^{-0.25} \approx 0.7788$$

$$\boxed{R(500) \approx 0.7788 \text{ or } 77.88\%}$$

**Step 4 — Expected Number of Failures in 10,000 Hours.**

For a constant failure rate process, expected number of failures is:

$$E[N] = \lambda \times T = 0.0005 \times 10000$$

$$\boxed{E[N] = 5 \text{ failures}}$$

**Verification using MTBF:**
$$E[N] = \frac{T}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{10000}{1800} \approx 5.56 \text{ failures}$$

(The slight difference arises because $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda + \text{MTTR}$ includes downtime, while pure failure count uses $\lambda$ alone.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B-Option B):**
> 1. **Analogy must be specific** — generic statements like "quality is good, reliability is durable" earn only 1 of the 3 marks allocated for the analogy. Use a **named product or industry** (cars, smartphones, aircraft engines) and describe **both the quality moment and the reliability duration**.
> 2. **Six Sigma and FMEA must be named and explained** — many students write "quality tools are used" without naming Six Sigma. Examiners look for the specific framework names.
> 3. **MTTF ≠ MTBF** — using them interchangeably costs 1 mark. State explicitly that MTTF is for non-repairable items and MTBF for repairable systems.
> 4. **Carry 4 decimal places** in intermediate calculations (e.g., $A = 0.9978$) — rounding too early leads to incorrect final answers.
> 5. **Units must accompany every numerical answer** — "2000" without "hours" is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Examinations**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ **Quality** = Conformance to specifications at a point in time (Juran's "Fitness for Use").
- ☐ **Reliability** = Probability of failure-free operation over a specified time.
- ☐ Quality is **static**; Reliability is **dynamic**.

**Key Metrics:**
- ☐ Quality: **DPMO, PPM, Cpk, defect rate**.
- ☐ Reliability: **R(t), MTTF, MTBF, MTTR, λ, Availability A**.
- ☐ Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).

**Critical Equations (Memorize These):**
- ☐ $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (Exponential reliability)
- ☐ $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ (Non-repairable)
- ☐ $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ (Repairable)
- ☐ $R_{\text{series}} = \prod R_i$
- ☐ $R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$

**Bathtub Curve:**
- ☐ Region 1: Infant Mortality — $\lambda$ decreasing, manufacturing defects.
- ☐ Region 2: Useful Life — $\lambda$ constant, exponential distribution applies.
- ☐ Region 3: Wear-out — $\lambda$ increasing, aging-related failures.

**Tools Mapping:**
- ☐ **Quality** tools: Six Sigma, SQC, Control Charts, Pareto, Fishbone.
- ☐ **Reliability** tools: FMEA, RBD, FTA, Weibull Analysis, Accelerated Life Testing.

**High-Frequency Board Traps:**
- ☐ Do not confuse MTTF (non-repairable) with MTBF (repairable).
- ☐ Series reliability is **always less than** the smallest component reliability.
- ☐ Parallel reliability is **always greater than** the largest component reliability.
- ☐ Availability can never exceed 1 (or 100%).
- ☐ Always state **assumptions** before applying the exponential distribution (constant failure rate assumption must be explicit).

**Real-World Industrial Applications:**
- ☐ Aerospace — Redundancy, FMEA, MTBF tracking.
- ☐ Medical Devices — High reliability + high quality required for patient safety.
- ☐ Automotive — Warranty analysis integrates both quality (defect data) and reliability (field failure data).
- ☐ Manufacturing — Six Sigma improves quality; reliability engineering extends product life.

**Suggested KTU Answer-Writing Pattern (14-Mark Question):**
1. **Step 1:** State given data and assumptions explicitly.
2. **Step 2:** Identify the relevant formula(s).
3. **Step 3:** Substitute values with units.
4. **Step 4:** Show step-by-step simplification (no calculator shortcuts).
5. **Step 5:** Box the final answer.
6. **Step 6:** Provide a one-line engineering interpretation or comment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Quality is what you build. Reliability is what you keep."* — this single sentence captures the essence of the topic and often appears as the first or last line of high-scoring KTU answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reliability Function # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Industrial Engineering and Management**, the **Reliability Function** (denoted as **R(t)**) is a fundamental probabilistic metric that quantifies the ability of a system, component, or product to perform its intended function under stated conditions, for a specified period of time **t**. Mathematically, it is defined as the **conditional probability** that a component or system will not fail in the time interval **[0, t]**, given that it was operational at time **t = 0**. $$R(t) = P(T > t)$$ where **T** is the random variable representing the **Time-To-Failure (TTF)** of the component. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEIET722 – Module 3):** > The Reliability Function is the foundational mathematical construct upon which all advanced reliability analyses (system reliability, bathtub curve, MTTF/MTBF calculations, hazard modeling) are built. KTU 2024 Scheme examiners frequently test the ability to convert between **R(t)**, **F(t)**, **f(t)**, and **λ(t)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition Imagine you buy a brand-new LED bulb from a company that offers a **3-year warranty**. - The **Reliability Function R(t)** is the company's internal statistical estimate: *"Out of 10,000 such bulbs manufactured today, what fraction will still be glowing flawlessly after 't' years?"* - If **R(3) = 0.97**, it means the company statistically expects **97 out of every 100 bulbs** to survive beyond the 3-year mark. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Intuition: The "Survival Probability" Analogy** > Think of R(t) as the **"survival curve"** in a medical study. Just as doctors track what percentage of patients remain disease-free after 5 years (the "5-year survival rate"), reliability engineers track what percentage of machines remain failure-free after a certain number of operating hours. R(t) ALWAYS starts at **R(0) = 1** (100% reliability for a brand-new component) and monotonically **decreases** toward **R(∞) = 0** (eventually, everything fails). ## 1.3 Core Metrics (Board-Exam Essentials) The following metrics are **bolded** because they are repeatedly tested in KTU examinations: - **R(t)** — Reliability Function (Survival Probability) - **F(t)** — Cumulative Failure Probability (Unreliability), where **F(t) = 1 − R(t)** - **f(t)** — Probability Density Function of Failure, where **f(t) = dF(t)/dt = −dR(t)/dt** - **λ(t)** — Hazard (Failure) Rate Function, measured in **failures per unit time** - **MTTF** — Mean Time To Failure (for non-repairable systems) - **MTBF** — Mean Time Between Failures (for repairable systems) --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Interconnected Web of Reliability Functions The Reliability Function is the **parent function** from which all other reliability metrics are derived. Understanding the logical flow below is critical for KTU numerical problems. ### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown: 1. **Start with R(t)**: The probability of survival beyond time t. 2. **Derive F(t)**: The probability of failure by time t, which is the **complement** of R(t). 3. **Derive f(t)**: The **rate of change** of failures — essentially the "density" of failures occurring at time t. 4. **Derive λ(t)**: The **instantaneous rate of failure** at time t, conditional on survival up to t. This answers: *"Given that the component has survived until t, what is the probability it will fail in the next infinitesimally small instant dt?"* 5. **Derive MTTF/MTBF**: The **expected value (mean)** of the failure time distribution, representing the "average lifetime" of a component. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The table below consolidates **all board-exam-critical formulas** for the Reliability Function topic. Memorize this table — it forms the backbone of Module 3 problems. | **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description / Boundary Condition** | **Unit** | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | | 1 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | Probability of survival beyond time t | Dimensionless | | 2 | Unreliability | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Probability of failure by time t | Dimensionless | | 3 | Probability Density | $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt} = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$ | Instantaneous failure density | Time⁻¹ | | 4 | Hazard Rate | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Conditional failure rate at time t | Time⁻¹ | | 5 | Reliability from Hazard | $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\,dx\right)$ | General integral form | Dimensionless | | 6 | Exponential R(t) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | When $\lambda$ is constant (useful life) | Dimensionless | | 7 | Weibull R(t) | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$ | $\alpha$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape parameter | Dimensionless | | 8 | MTTF | $MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$ | Mean time to failure | Hours | | 9 | Exponential MTTF | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | For constant failure rate | Hours | | 10 | MTBF | $MTBF = MTTR + MTTF$ | Mean time between failures | Hours | | 11 | Series System | $R_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{i}(t)$ | All components must function | Dimensionless | | 12 | Parallel System | $R_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}\left[1 - R_{i}(t)\right]$ | At least one must function | Dimensionless | | 13 | Conditional R(t) | $R(t \mid T_0) = \dfrac{R(t)}{R(T_0)}$ for $t > T_0$ | Survival beyond $T_0$ given survival to $T_0$ | Dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions (Frequently Asked in KTU):** > - $R(0) = 1$ (A brand-new component is 100% reliable at t = 0) > - $R(\infty) = 0$ (Given infinite time, everything eventually fails) > - $F(0) = 0$ and $F(\infty) = 1$ > - $R(t)$ is a **monotonically non-increasing** function of t ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Reliability Function is not merely an academic exercise — it underpins critical decisions across industries: - **Aerospace**: Predicting the probability that an aircraft engine completes a 10,000-hour service interval without unscheduled maintenance. - **Nuclear Power**: Certifying that safety-critical pumps will function during a 72-hour emergency cooling window (often requires R(t) ≥ 0.9999). - **Consumer Electronics**: Determining warranty periods (e.g., a 1-year warranty typically corresponds to R(1 year) ≥ 0.95). - **Automotive**: Designing redundant braking systems where overall R(t) must exceed 0.999999 over the vehicle's lifetime. - **Telecommunications**: Network uptime Service Level Agreements (SLAs) — "five nines" reliability (99.999%) translates to R(t) ≥ 0.99999. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reliability Function from the Hazard Rate This is the **most important derivation** in Module 3. KTU examiners often ask students to derive R(t) from the definition of the hazard rate. ### Starting Point: By definition, the **hazard rate (failure rate) function** is: $$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$ ### Step 1: Expand the numerator using the relationship between f(t) and R(t) We know that $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$. Substituting this into the hazard rate equation: $$\lambda(t) = \frac{-\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}}{R(t)}$$ ### Step 2: Rearrange the equation to separate variables $$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ Multiplying both sides by dt and by R(t): $$\lambda(t)\,R(t)\,dt = -dR(t)$$ ### Step 3: Integrate both sides from 0 to t We apply the **definite integral** with the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$: $$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\,dx = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{1}{R(x)}\,dR(x)$$ ### Step 4: Evaluate the right-hand integral The integral $\displaystyle\int \frac{1}{R}\,dR = \ln(R)$. Therefore: $$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\,dx = -\Big[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\Big]$$ ### Step 5: Apply the boundary condition R(0) = 1 Since $\ln(1) = 0$: $$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\,dx = -\ln R(t)$$ ### Step 6: Solve for R(t) by exponentiating $$-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\,dx = \ln R(t)$$ $$R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\,dx\right)$$ > **Final Result:** $\displaystyle R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\,dx}$ — This is the **general integral form** of the Reliability Function. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Exponential Reliability Function (Constant Failure Rate) ### Special Case: $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant) During the **useful life** phase of the bathtub curve, the failure rate is **constant**. Substituting $\lambda(x) = \lambda$ into the general form: $$R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda\,dx\right)$$ The integral of a constant is trivial: $$R(t) = \exp(-\lambda t)$$ $$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** If a component has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour, then $R(100) = e^{-0.001 \times 100} = e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048$. This means ~90.48% of such components will survive 100 hours. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: MTTF for the Exponential Distribution ### Starting Point: By definition, the expected value (mean) of the time-to-failure is: $$MTTF = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt$$ ### Step 1: Apply the "Survival Function Expectation" identity A powerful identity in reliability theory states: $$E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$$ ### Step 2: Substitute the exponential R(t) $$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$ ### Step 3: Evaluate the integral This is a standard integral of the form $\int e^{-at}\,dt = -\dfrac{1}{a}e^{-at}$: $$MTTF = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda}e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty}$$ ### Step 4: Apply limits At $t \to \infty$: $e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$, so the upper limit yields **0**. At $t = 0$: $e^{0} = 1$, so the lower limit yields $-\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$. $$MTTF = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$ $$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$ > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** This is the **single most-tested formula** in Module 3. Whenever you see "exponential distribution" or "constant failure rate" in a KTU problem, the MTTF is simply the **reciprocal of λ**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical) > **Problem:** A hydraulic pump in a CNC machine has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures/hour. Calculate: > (a) The reliability of the pump for a 500-hour mission time. > (b) The MTTF of the pump. > (c) The probability that it will fail between 1000 and 2000 hours, given it survived 1000 hours. ### Solution: **Part (a): Reliability at t = 500 hours** Since $\lambda$ is constant, we use the exponential reliability function: $$R(500) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.0002)(500)} = e^{-0.1}$$ $$R(500) = 0.9048 \approx 90.48\%$$ **Part (b): Mean Time To Failure** $$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$ **Part (c): Conditional Probability of Failure Between 1000 and 2000 Hours** We need $P(1000 < T \leq 2000 \mid T > 1000)$. Using the **memoryless property** of the exponential distribution: $$R(t \mid T_0) = \frac{R(t)}{R(T_0)}$$ First, calculate R(2000) and R(1000): $$R(2000) = e^{-(0.0002)(2000)} = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$$ $$R(1000) = e^{-(0.0002)(1000)} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$ The conditional reliability at 2000 hours (given survival to 1000 hours): $$R(2000 \mid 1000) = \frac{R(2000)}{R(1000)} = \frac{0.6703}{0.8187} = 0.8187$$ The probability of failure in [1000, 2000] given survival to 1000: $$P(\text{fail in } [1000,2000] \mid T > 1000) = 1 - 0.8187 = 0.1813 \approx 18.13\%$$ > **Verification via Memorylessness:** Note that $R(2000 \mid 1000) = R(1000) = 0.8187$. The exponential distribution is **memoryless** — the component "forgets" its age. This is a unique property KTU loves to test. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reliability Function Concept Map The following diagram maps the **logical flow** between all reliability functions, showing how R(t) is the central construct from which all others are derived. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["R(t) = P(T > t)
RELIABILITY FUNCTION"] --> nodeB["F(t) = 1 - R(t)
CUMULATIVE FAILURE PROB"] nodeA --> nodeC["f(t) = -dR/dt
PROBABILITY DENSITY"] nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD["Lambda(t) = f(t) / R(t)
HAZARD RATE"] nodeD --> nodeE["R(t) = exp(-integral Lambda dx)
INTEGRAL FORM"] nodeE --> nodeF["EXPONENTIAL CASE
Lambda = constant"] nodeF --> nodeG["R(t) = exp(-Lambda t)"] nodeF --> nodeH["MTTF = 1 / Lambda"] nodeA --> nodeI["MTTF = integral R(t) dt
GENERAL FORM"] nodeG --> nodeI nodeA --> nodeJ["Rsys SERIES = Product Ri"] nodeA --> nodeK["Rsys PARALLEL = 1 - Product 1-Ri"] nodeJ --> nodeL["SYSTEM RELIABILITY"] nodeK --> nodeL ``` ## 4.2 The Bathtub Curve — Mapping the Hazard Rate to Time The **Bathtub Curve** is the canonical visualization of how the hazard rate $\lambda(t)$ evolves over a component's lifetime. It is divided into three distinct regions, each with its own reliability behavior. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeP["PHASE 1
INFANT MORTALITY
Lambda decreases
Early failures due to defects"] --> nodeQ["PHASE 2
USEFUL LIFE
Lambda = constant
Random failures
R(t) = exp(-Lambda t)"] nodeQ --> nodeR["PHASE 3
WEAR-OUT
Lambda increases
End-of-life failures"] nodeP -.-> nodeS["Burn-in testing
eliminates weak units"] nodeQ -.-> nodeT["Preventive maintenance
scheduled at this stage"] nodeR -.-> nodeU["Replacement /
reconditioning"] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: System Reliability Assembly The following diagram illustrates how individual component reliabilities are **assembled** into system-level reliability for both **Series** and **Parallel** configurations — a high-frequency KTU exam topic. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeInput["System Input / Mission Start t = 0"] --> nodeSer["SERIES CONFIGURATION
All components must work"] nodeSer --> nodeS1["Component 1
R1(t) = 0.95"] nodeS1 --> nodeS2["Component 2
R2(t) = 0.92"] nodeS2 --> nodeS3["Component 3
R3(t) = 0.90"] nodeS3 --> nodeSerOut["Rsys = R1 x R2 x R3
= 0.7866"] nodeInput --> nodePar["PARALLEL CONFIGURATION
At least one must work"] nodePar --> nodeP1["Component A
RA(t) = 0.80"] nodePar --> nodeP2["Component B
RB(t) = 0.85"] nodePar --> nodeP3["Component C
RC(t) = 0.90"] nodeP1 --> nodeParOut["Rsys = 1 - 1-RA x 1-RB x 1-RC
= 1 - 0.003 = 0.997"] nodeP2 --> nodeParOut nodeP3 --> nodeParOut nodeSerOut --> nodeFinal["System Reliability Output"] nodeParOut --> nodeFinal ``` > **Key Engineering Insight from the Diagram:** > The **Series system** with three moderately reliable components (95%, 92%, 90%) yields only **78.66%** system reliability — reliability degrades multiplicatively. The **Parallel system** with three less-reliable components (80%, 85%, 90%) achieves **99.7%** system reliability through redundancy. This is why critical aerospace and medical systems use **parallel redundancy** rather than relying on single high-reliability components. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the Reliability Function R(t). Explain the relationship between R(t), F(t), and f(t).** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Reliability Function R(t)** is defined as the probability that a component or system will perform its intended function without failure for a specified period of time t, under stated operating conditions. $$R(t) = P(T > t)$$ The three fundamental reliability functions are interrelated as follows: 1. **Cumulative Failure Probability F(t):** This is the probability that the component fails by time t. It is the **complement** of R(t): $$F(t) = 1 - R(t)$$ 2. **Probability Density Function f(t):** This represents the **instantaneous rate of failure** at time t. It is the derivative of F(t) (or the negative derivative of R(t)): $$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ 3. **Geometric Interpretation:** f(t) is the slope of the F(t) curve (or the negative slope of the R(t) curve) at any point t. The total area under the f(t) curve from 0 to ∞ equals 1 (since failure is certain given infinite time). --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the Hazard Rate? Explain its physical significance with reference to the bathtub curve.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Hazard Rate (Failure Rate) function $\lambda(t)$** is defined as the **conditional probability density** of failure at time t, given that the component has survived up to time t: $$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ **Physical Significance with the Bathtub Curve:** The hazard rate is not constant — it varies with the **age** of the component, as depicted by the bathtub curve in three distinct regions: 1. **Infant Mortality Region (Early Life):** $\lambda(t)$ **decreases** with time. Components that fail do so due to manufacturing defects, poor quality, or installation errors. The "weak" units fail early, leaving stronger units to survive. 2. **Useful Life Region (Normal Operation):** $\lambda(t)$ is **approximately constant**. Failures occur randomly due to unpredictable stresses (overload, environmental factors). During this phase, the exponential distribution applies: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. 3. **Wear-Out Region (End of Life):** $\lambda(t)$ **increases** rapidly with time. Components fail due to aging, fatigue, corrosion, and material degradation. This is when preventive replacement is scheduled. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Pattern ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the Reliability Function $R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\,dx}$ starting from the definition of the hazard rate. > **(b) [7 Marks]** A motor has a constant failure rate of 0.025 failures per 1000 hours. Calculate: (i) the reliability for a 2000-hour mission, (ii) the MTTF, and (iii) the probability of failure within the first 500 hours. --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 [1 Mark]:** State the definition of the hazard rate: $$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$ **Step 2 [1 Mark]:** Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$: $$\lambda(t) = \frac{-\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 3 [1 Mark]:** Rearrange to separate variables: $$\lambda(t)\,dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$ **Step 4 [1 Mark]:** Integrate both sides from 0 to t (applying the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$): $$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\,dx = -\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R}$$ **Step 5 [1 Mark]:** Evaluate the right-hand integral: $$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\,dx = -[\ln R(t) - \ln 1] = -\ln R(t)$$ **Step 6 [1 Mark]:** Solve for R(t): $$R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\,dx}$$ **Step 7 [1 Mark]:** State the special case for constant $\lambda$ (bonus completeness): $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ --- **Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]:** **Given:** $\lambda = 0.025$ failures per 1000 hours $= 0.025 \times 10^{-3}$ failures/hour $= 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ per hour. **(i) Reliability for 2000-hour mission [2 Marks]:** $$R(2000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2.5 \times 10^{-5})(2000)} = e^{-0.05}$$ $$R(2000) = 0.9512 \text{ or } 95.12\%$$ **(ii) MTTF [2 Marks]:** $$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 40{,}000 \text{ hours}$$ **(iii) Probability of failure within first 500 hours [3 Marks]:** $$F(500) = 1 - R(500) = 1 - e^{-(2.5 \times 10^{-5})(500)}$$ $$F(500) = 1 - e^{-0.0125} = 1 - 0.9876 = 0.0124$$ $$P(\text{fail within 500 hrs}) = 0.0124 \text{ or } 1.24\%$$ --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | 14 Marks]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the Bathtub Curve. Describe the reliability behavior in each phase and the applicable probability distribution. > **(b) [7 Marks]** A system consists of three components in series with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.98$, and $R_3 = 0.92$ for a 1000-hour mission. To improve reliability, an additional identical component is added in parallel to Component 2 (the weakest point). Calculate the new system reliability. --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Bathtub Curve Explanation [7 Marks]:** **Diagram [2 Marks]:** Draw a plot of $\lambda(t)$ (y-axis) versus time t (x-axis) showing the characteristic "bathtub" shape with three labeled regions. **Phase 1 — Infant Mortality (Burn-In) [1.5 Marks]:** - Hazard rate **decreases** from a high initial value. - Early failures caused by manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, substandard materials. - Reliability follows the **Weibull distribution** with shape parameter $\beta < 1$. - Mitigation: **Burn-in testing** to eliminate weak units before deployment. **Phase 2 — Useful Life [1.5 Marks]:** - Hazard rate is **constant** ($\lambda = $ constant). - Failures are **random**, caused by external stresses (overload, environmental, accidental damage). - Reliability follows the **Exponential distribution**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, MTTF $= 1/\lambda$. - Mitigation: **Preventive maintenance** and condition monitoring. **Phase 3 — Wear-Out [2 Marks]:** - Hazard rate **increases** rapidly. - Failures caused by aging mechanisms: fatigue, corrosion, creep, wear. - Reliability follows the **Weibull distribution** with shape parameter $\beta > 1$. - Mitigation: **Scheduled replacement** before end-of-life; refurbishment programs. --- **Part (b) — System Reliability Calculation [7 Marks]:** **Given:** - Series system with $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.98$, $R_3 = 0.92$ - An **additional identical component** is added in **parallel** to Component 2. **Step 1 [1 Mark]:** Calculate the reliability of the **parallel subsystem** (Component 2 + its redundant twin). For two identical components in parallel, each with $R_2 = 0.98$: $$R_{2,\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - R_2)^2 = 1 - (1 - 0.98)^2$$ $$R_{2,\text{parallel}} = 1 - (0.02)^2 = 1 - 0.0004 = 0.9996$$ **Step 2 [1 Mark]:** Identify the new series chain. The new system is: **Component 1 (series) → [Component 2 + Redundant 2 in parallel] (series) → Component 3** **Step 3 [1 Mark]:** Apply the series reliability formula: $$R_{\text{sys}} = R_1 \times R_{2,\text{parallel}} \times R_3$$ **Step 4 [1 Mark]:** Substitute the values: $$R_{\text{sys}} = 0.95 \times 0.9996 \times 0.92$$ **Step 5 [1 Mark]:** Compute step-by-step: $$0.95 \times 0.9996 = 0.9496$$ $$0.9496 \times 0.92 = 0.8737$$ **Step 6 [1 Mark]:** State the final answer and compare with original: $$R_{\text{sys,new}} = 0.8737 \text{ or } 87.37\%$$ **Original system (no redundancy):** $$R_{\text{sys,original}} = 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.92 = 0.8565 \text{ or } 85.65\%$$ **Step 7 [1 Mark]:** Interpret the result: The parallel redundancy of Component 2 improved system reliability from **85.65% to 87.37%** — a gain of **1.72 percentage points**. The parallel subsystem is now nearly perfect (99.96%), but the overall system is still limited by Components 1 and 3. This illustrates the principle of **diminishing returns** in reliability engineering. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Sign Error in the Derivative:** Students frequently write $f(t) = +\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ instead of $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$. Since R(t) is **decreasing**, the derivative is **negative**, and the negative sign is essential to make f(t) a positive density function. **[Lose 1 mark]** > > 2. **Forgetting the Boundary Condition R(0) = 1:** When deriving the exponential reliability, students often skip the limit $R(0) = 1$, leading to an incorrect constant of integration. **[Lose 1 mark]** > > 3. **Series vs. Parallel Confusion:** Students **multiply** reliabilities for parallel systems and **use the complement formula** for series systems — this is exactly **backwards**. Remember: **Series = multiply; Parallel = 1 − product of (1 − Rᵢ)**. **[Lose up to 3 marks]** > > 4. **Unit Inconsistency in λ:** If $\lambda$ is given "per 1000 hours" and t is in "hours", you MUST convert λ to per-hour (or t to per-1000-hours) before substituting. Mixing units is the **#1 numerical error** in KTU reliability problems. **[Lose 1–2 marks]** > > 5. **MTTF vs. MTBF Confusion:** MTTF is for **non-repairable** components (e.g., a light bulb). MTBF is for **repairable** systems and equals MTTF + MTTR (Mean Time To Repair). Writing "MTBF = 1/λ" for a non-repairable component costs marks. > > 6. **Memoryless Property Oversight:** The exponential distribution is **memoryless** — a component that has survived 1000 hours has the same future reliability as a brand-new one. Students often compute conditional probabilities incorrectly by ignoring this. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - [x] **R(t) = P(T > t)** is the **probability of survival** beyond time t. - [x] **R(0) = 1** and **R(∞) = 0** — these boundary conditions are **mandatory** in derivations. - [x] **F(t) = 1 − R(t)** — the unreliability function. - [x] **f(t) = −dR(t)/dt** — the probability density of failure (note the **negative sign**). - [x] **λ(t) = f(t)/R(t)** — the hazard rate; it is **not** a probability but a **rate** (units: time⁻¹). - [x] **General R(t) = exp(−∫₀ᵗ λ(x) dx)** — the master formula derived from the hazard rate. - [x] **Exponential R(t) = e^(−λt)** — applies when the failure rate is **constant** (useful life phase). - [x] **MTTF = 1/λ** for the exponential distribution — the most-tested formula in Module 3. - [x] **MTTF = ∫₀^∞ R(t) dt** — the general formula for mean time to failure. - [x] **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR** — for repairable systems only. - [x] **Series system**: $R_{sys} = \prod R_i$ (all must work) — reliability **degrades multiplicatively**. - [x] **Parallel system**: $R_{sys} = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$ (at least one must work) — redundancy **boosts** reliability. - [x] **Memoryless property**: Exponential distribution has **no aging memory** — $R(t+T \mid T) = R(t)$. - [x] **Bathtub Curve** has three phases: **Infant Mortality** (decreasing λ), **Useful Life** (constant λ, exponential R(t)), **Wear-Out** (increasing λ, Weibull with β > 1). - [x] **Weibull distribution**: $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$ — the most **flexible** reliability model; $\beta < 1$ for infant mortality, $\beta = 1$ for exponential, $\beta > 1$ for wear-out. - [x] **Conditional reliability**: $R(t \mid T_0) = R(t)/R(T_0)$ for $t > T_0$. - [x] **System design principle**: Adding redundancy in **parallel** dramatically improves system reliability, but the improvement is **limited by the weakest series element** (the "weakest link" effect). ---

topics.title: MTTF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MTTF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** is a fundamental reliability engineering metric that represents the **expected (average) time elapsed until a non-repairable component or system fails** for the first time. It is mathematically defined as the integral of the reliability function $R(t)$ from zero to infinity, or equivalently, as the expected value (first moment) of the Time-To-Failure (TTF) random variable $T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
