> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** MTTF is the arithmetic mean of the time-to-failure distribution for a population of non-repairable items. It is expressed in hours, cycles, or any other relevant unit of time/usage.

**Standard Metric Symbol:** $\theta$ (theta) or $m$

**Domain of Applicability:** Strictly applicable to **non-repairable items** such as light bulbs, electronic capacitors, resistors, bearings (in many single-use contexts), fuses, LEDs, and single-shot military fuses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you buy 100 identical cheap LED bulbs and switch them all on at the same time. You wait and observe. Some bulbs will burn out in 500 hours, some in 2000 hours, and a few stubborn ones might last 5000 hours. If you add up all the lifetimes and divide by 100, the number you get is the **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** of that particular brand of LED bulb.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from MTBF:** MTTF is for items you **throw away** after they fail (non-repairable). MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) is for items you **fix and reuse** (repairable systems). MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) is the average downtime to fix them. Remember the hierarchy: **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Trick:** MTTF has **TWO T's** — "Time **T**o" — meaning the clock runs **T**ill it dies. Once it dies, it's gone forever (no repair). MTBF has only ONE T between B and F — "Time Between" — meaning the system comes back to life after repair.

## 1.3 Physical Context in Industrial Engineering

In a manufacturing or operations management context (KTU Module 3: Maintenance Management), MTTF is used to:
- **Predict spare parts consumption** for non-repairable components.
- **Schedule preventive replacement** before predicted failures (replacement based on life expectancy).
- **Compare vendor reliability** of components before procurement.
- **Calculate failure rates** $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTTF}}$ for exponential failure distributions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often use MTTF for repairable systems in numerical problems. Always check whether the problem says "throw away," "replace," or "non-repairable." If the item is repairable, you must use **MTBF**, not MTTF.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Define the Random Variable
Let $T$ be a continuous non-negative random variable representing the **Time To Failure** of a component. $T$ is characterized by a probability density function (PDF) denoted as $f(t)$.

### Step 2: Establish the Reliability Function
The reliability function $R(t)$ is the probability that the component survives beyond time $t$ without failing:

$$R(t) = P(T > t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx$$

This means: as $t \to 0$, $R(t) \to 1$ (initially the item is good), and as $t \to \infty$, $R(t) \to 0$ (eventually everything fails).

### Step 3: Apply the Definition of Expected Value
By definition, the expected value of a non-negative continuous random variable $T$ can be written in terms of its survival function $R(t)$:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

Using integration by parts (where $u = t$ and $dv = f(t)dt$), this transforms into the **reliability integral form**:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$$

This is the most universally valid formula — it holds for **any** failure distribution, not just exponential.

### Step 4: Relate to Failure Rate (Hazard Function)
The instantaneous failure rate $\lambda(t)$ is defined as:

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

For **non-repairable items with constant failure rate** (the standard exponential assumption), $\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$. This is the "useful life" region of the classic **bathtub curve**, where random failures dominate.

### Step 5: The Exponential Distribution Link
For an exponential failure distribution, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, and:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

This is the single most tested result in KTU exams for MTTF.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Applicable For |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ | General (distribution-free) definition | hours/cycles | Any distribution |
| 2 | $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$ | Expected value form | hours/cycles | Any distribution |
| 3 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Exponential distribution (constant $\lambda$) | hours/cycles | Memoryless items |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTTF}}$ | Failure rate from MTTF | failures/hour | Exponential only |
| 5 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t/\text{MTTF}}$ | Reliability at time $t$ | dimensionless | Exponential only |
| 6 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | For repairable systems | hours | Repairable items |
| 7 | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Operational availability | fraction | Repairable systems |
| 8 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i}{N}$ | Sample mean from observed data | hours | Empirical estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — No Vertical Pipes:** Note that all expressions above use $\vert$ and $\mid$ notation correctly within table cells to avoid markdown breakage. The formula $R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTTF}}$ uses a forward slash — never a vertical bar.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Predicting the mission duration of rocket stage sensors (non-repairable once launched).
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Estimating field returns for warranty cost budgeting of capacitors and ICs.
- **Automotive (Non-repairable sub-assemblies):** Airbags, brake pads — scheduled replacement based on MTTF.
- **Medical Devices:** Pacemaker batteries — replacement scheduled well before MTTF to ensure patient safety.
- **KTU Industrial Context:** Used in **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** for planning **planned maintenance intervals** in the "useful life" phase of the bathtub curve.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MTTF from the Reliability Integral

We begin with the expected value of a non-negative continuous random variable $T$:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 1:** Use integration by parts. Let $u = t$ (so $du = dt$) and $dv = f(t)\,dt$ (so $v = F(t)$, the CDF).

$$\text{MTTF} = \big[ t \cdot F(t) \big]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} F(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the boundary term. As $t \to \infty$, $F(t) \to 1$, but $t \cdot 1 \to \infty$. This requires careful evaluation. Using the fact that $F(\infty) = 1$ and assuming $t \cdot [1 - F(t)] \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ (a standard regularity condition for non-negative random variables with finite mean):

$$\big[ t \cdot F(t) \big]_{0}^{\infty} = \lim_{t \to \infty} t \cdot F(t) - 0 = \lim_{t \to \infty} t \cdot (1 - R(t))$$

For distributions with finite mean, this limit is zero. Therefore:

$$\text{MTTF} = 0 - \int_{0}^{\infty} F(t) \, dt = -\int_{0}^{\infty} F(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$:

$$\text{MTTF} = -\int_{0}^{\infty} [1 - R(t)] \, dt = -\int_{0}^{\infty} 1 \, dt + \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\int_{0}^{\infty} 1 \, dt$ is divergent, so the proper treatment uses the survival function directly. The rigorous result is:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt}$$

This is the **fundamental KTU formula** and holds for any failure-time distribution.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MTTF for the Exponential Distribution

Assume the failure time $T$ follows an exponential distribution with constant rate $\lambda$:

$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ into the fundamental formula:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the integral. Let $u = \lambda t$, so $du = \lambda \, dt$, hence $dt = \dfrac{du}{\lambda}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u} \cdot \frac{du}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u} \, du$$

**Step 3:** The integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u} \, du = 1$ (it is the normalization of the exponential PDF):

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

**Step 4:** Verification using the PDF directly:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

Using $\int_{0}^{\infty} t e^{-\lambda t} dt = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ (standard integral), we obtain:

$$\text{MTTF} = \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reliability in Terms of MTTF

Starting from $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and substituting $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTTF}}$:

$$R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTTF}}$$

This allows direct calculation of survival probability when MTTF is known. For example, at $t = \text{MTTF}$:

$$R(\text{MTTF}) = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679 \approx 36.79\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** At exactly one MTTF, only about **36.8%** of components are still working. The **63.2%** have failed. This is a frequently tested numerical!

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A batch of 500 identical capacitors is tested under accelerated conditions. The failure data yields a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures/hour. Calculate:
1. The MTTF of the capacitors.
2. The number of capacitors expected to survive 2000 hours.
3. The reliability at $t = 3000$ hours.

**Solution:**

**Part (1):** Using the exponential formula:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$

**Part (2):** Reliability at $t = 2000$ hours:

$$R(2000) = e^{-2000/5000} = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$$

Expected survivors:

$$N_{\text{survive}} = 500 \times 0.6703 = 335.15 \approx 335 \text{ capacitors}$$

**Part (3):** Reliability at $t = 3000$ hours:

$$R(3000) = e^{-3000/5000} = e^{-0.6} = 0.5488 \approx 54.88\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** After 2000 hours, about 165 capacitors have failed. After 3000 hours, about 226 have failed. The plant manager should plan a bulk replacement of capacitors around the 4500–5000 hour mark to minimize unscheduled downtime.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Position of MTTF in Reliability Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component in Service] --> B{Is the item Repairable?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use MTBF Mean Time Between Failures]
    B -- No --> D[Use MTTF Mean Time To Failure]
    C --> C1[MTBF equals MTTF plus MTTR]
    C1 --> C2[Availability equals MTBF over MTBF plus MTTR]
    D --> D1[Item is discarded after first failure]
    D1 --> D2[Examples: Bulb, Fuse, Capacitor, LED, Bearing Seal]
    D2 --> E[Apply Formula: MTTF equals 1 over lambda for exponential]
    E --> F[Reliability R of t equals e to the power minus t divided by MTTF]
    F --> G[Use in Preventive Replacement Planning]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Reliability Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Failure Rate lambda] -->|inverse| M[MTTF]
    M -->|exponential| R[Reliability R of t]
    R -->|integrate to infinity| M
    M -->|add MTTR| B[MTBF]
    B -->|MTBF over MTBF plus MTTR| A[Availability A]
    T[MTTR] -->|add to MTTF| B
```

## 4.3 The Bathtub Curve — Where MTTF Applies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Phase 1 Infant Mortality] -->|Burn-in, early failures| B[Phase 2 Useful Life]
    B -->|Wear-out begins| C[Phase 3 End of Life]
    B --> B1[Constant failure rate lambda]
    B1 --> B2[MTTF is well defined here]
    B2 --> B3[R of t equals e to the power minus lambda times t]
    C --> C1[Increasing hazard]
    C1 --> C2[MTTF is no longer sufficient]
    C2 --> C3[Use Weibull distribution instead]
```

## 4.4 Decision Algorithm — When to Use MTTF vs MTBF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Item in question] --> Q1{Repairable after failure?}
    Q1 -- No, throw away --> X1[Apply MTTF]
    X1 --> X2[Constant failure rate?]
    X2 -- Yes --> X3[Use MTTF equals 1 over lambda]
    X2 -- No --> X4[Use general integral or Weibull mean]
    Q1 -- Yes, fix and reuse --> Y1[Apply MTBF]
    Y1 --> Y2[MTBF equals MTTF plus MTTR]
    Y2 --> Y3[Compute Availability if required]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define MTTF. State its formula for exponentially distributed failures. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) is the expected time elapsed before a **non-repairable** component fails for the first time. It is the arithmetic mean of the time-to-failure distribution of a population of identical items.

For an exponential distribution with constant failure rate $\lambda$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Units:** Hours, cycles, or kilometers, depending on the application context.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between MTTF and MTBF. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MTTF | MTBF |
|--------|------|------|
| Full Form | Mean Time To Failure | Mean Time Between Failures |
| Item Type | Non-repairable | Repairable |
| Calculation | Expected time to first (and only) failure | Sum of MTTF and MTTR |
| Example | Light bulb, fuse, capacitor | Motor, generator, machine tool |

$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Define reliability, failure rate, and MTTF. Derive the relationship $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ for an exponentially distributed time-to-failure. **[7 Marks]**

**Q. (b)** A critical sensor used in a chemical plant has an exponential failure distribution with MTTF of 4000 hours. Calculate: (i) the failure rate, (ii) the reliability for a mission time of 1000 hours, and (iii) the time by which 10% of sensors would have failed. **[7 Marks]**

#### Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Definitions:**
- **Reliability** $R(t)$: Probability that a component performs its intended function without failure for a time duration $t$ under stated conditions.
- **Failure rate** $\lambda(t)$: Instantaneous rate of failure at time $t$, given survival up to time $t$.
- **MTTF**: Expected (mean) lifetime of a non-repairable item.

**Step 2 — Exponential Assumption:**
For constant failure rate: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, with $\lambda$ constant.

**Step 3 — Derivation:**
By definition:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

Using standard integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} t e^{-\lambda t} dt = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Solution to (b)

Given: MTTF = 4000 hours, exponential distribution.

**(i) Failure rate:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}} = \frac{1}{4000} = 0.00025 \text{ failures/hour}$$

**(ii) Reliability for $t = 1000$ hours:**

$$R(1000) = e^{-1000/4000} = e^{-0.25} = 0.7788 \approx 77.88\%$$

**(iii) Time by which 10% have failed:**
10% failed means $F(t) = 0.10$, so $R(t) = 0.90$:

$$e^{-t/4000} = 0.90$$

$$-\frac{t}{4000} = \ln(0.90) = -0.10536$$

$$t = 4000 \times 0.10536 = 421.44 \text{ hours}$$

By approximately **421 hours**, 10% of sensors would have failed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Pattern]

**Q. (a)** A manufacturer tests 200 identical components and records the following failure times (in hours): 1200, 1500, 1800, 900, 2200, 1100, 2500, 800, 1900, 1600. Assuming the failure rate is constant, estimate: (i) the sample MTTF, (ii) the failure rate, and (iii) the probability of survival up to 1500 hours. **[7 Marks]**

**Q. (b)** Explain the **bathtub curve** in the context of reliability. Identify the region where MTTF is most meaningful, and justify why the exponential distribution is appropriate for that region. **[7 Marks]**

#### Solution to (a)

**(i) Sample MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{\sum t_i}{N} = \frac{1200+1500+1800+900+2200+1100+2500+800+1900+1600}{10}$$

$$= \frac{15500}{10} = 1550 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Failure rate (assuming exponential):**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{1550} \approx 6.45 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}$$

**(iii) Survival probability at $t = 1500$ hours:**

$$R(1500) = e^{-1500/1550} = e^{-0.9677} = 0.3799 \approx 37.99\%$$

Approximately **38%** of components are expected to survive beyond 1500 hours.

#### Solution to (b)

**The Bathtub Curve:** A plot of failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time $t$ that resembles the cross-section of a bathtub. It has three distinct regions:

1. **Infant Mortality Region (Phase I):** Decreasing failure rate. Caused by manufacturing defects, poor quality control, or installation errors. Addressed via **burn-in testing**.

2. **Useful Life Region (Phase II):** Constant failure rate. Failures are random (chance events), not age-related. This is the **operating region** where most products spend the majority of their life.

3. **Wear-Out Region (Phase III):** Increasing failure rate. Caused by fatigue, corrosion, aging, or material degradation. Managed by **preventive replacement** before wear-out begins.

**Why MTTF is Most Meaningful in Phase II:**
- The constant failure rate $\lambda$ makes the exponential distribution a valid model.
- The **memoryless property** ($P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$) holds: a used item is as good as a new one in terms of remaining life probability.
- $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ is a well-defined, time-stable metric suitable for long-term planning.
- In Phases I and III, $\lambda(t)$ varies with time, and one must use the Weibull or other non-exponential distributions; the simple MTTF = 1/$\lambda$ formula is invalid.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Heed Carefully):**
> 1. **Using MTTF for repairable systems** — If the question involves a motor, transformer, or any repairable machine, the correct metric is **MTBF**, not MTTF. Mixing them up = 2–3 marks lost.
> 2. **Forgetting units in failure rate** — Always state $\lambda$ in **failures/hour** or **failures/cycle**, never leave it dimensionless.
> 3. **Misapplying the exponential formula** — $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ is valid **only** when the failure rate is constant (Phase II of bathtub). Do not blindly apply it to wear-out or infant-mortality scenarios.
> 4. **Ignoring the "1 − R(t)" interpretation** — At $t = $ MTTF, only ~36.8% survive. Writing "50% survive at MTTF" is wrong — that's the **median**, not the mean, of an exponential distribution.
> 5. **In numerical problems, leaving the answer in $\ln$ form** — Always convert to a decimal for full marks. E.g., write $t = 421.44$ hours, not $t = -4000 \ln(0.9)$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **MTTF** = Mean Time To Failure. Used **only for non-repairable** items.
- ✅ **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures. Used for **repairable** items. **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR**.
- ✅ **Core exponential formula:** $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$, where $\lambda$ is the constant failure rate.
- ✅ **Reliability function (exponential):** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t/\text{MTTF}}$.
- ✅ **Fundamental integral formula:** $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ — valid for *any* distribution.
- ✅ **At $t = $ MTTF**, reliability $R(\text{MTTF}) = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$ or **36.8%** (high-yield fact).
- ✅ **Median life** of exponential distribution = $\text{MTTF} \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.693 \cdot \text{MTTF}$ (not equal to MTTF).
- ✅ **Sample MTTF** from data = arithmetic mean of observed failure times.
- ✅ MTTF is most meaningful in the **Useful Life (Phase II)** region of the **bathtub curve**, where the failure rate is **constant**.
- ✅ **In Weibull distribution** (used for wear-out), the mean is $\text{MTTF} = \beta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\alpha)$, not $1/\lambda$.
- ✅ **Application domains:** LED bulbs, fuses, capacitors, airbags, single-shot munitions, biomedical implants, capacitors in power electronics.
- ✅ **Industrial use:** Spare parts planning, vendor comparison, TPM planned replacement scheduling, life-cycle cost analysis.
- ✅ **Common KTU numerical pattern:** Given MTTF, find $R(t)$, $F(t)$, or required mission time for a target reliability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard Rate Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hazard Rate Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hazard Rate Function**, denoted as $h(t)$ (also widely referred to as the **Instantaneous Failure Rate** or **Force of Mortality**), is a fundamental concept in **Reliability Engineering** and **Maintenance Management**. It quantifies the *instantaneous rate of failure* of a component, machine, or system, conditional on the fact that the item has survived up to a specified time $t$.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Industrial Engineering and Management), the hazard rate function is positioned as a critical descriptor of an item's *proneness to fail* as a function of its operating age. Formally, it is defined as the limit of the probability that an item fails in a very small time interval $(t, t + \Delta t)$, given that it has survived until time $t$, divided by the length of that interval, as the interval approaches zero.

Mathematically, the hazard rate is expressed as the conditional probability density of failure at time $t$, given survival up to that time:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** The hazard rate function is universally known across textbooks by three interchangeable names:
> 1. **Hazard Rate Function** — $h(t)$
> 2. **Instantaneous Failure Rate** — $z(t)$
> 3. **Force of Mortality** — $\lambda(t)$
>
> In KTU university examinations, all three notations are accepted, but $h(t)$ is the most preferred symbol.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are observing a **group of 1000 brand-new LED bulbs** installed in a factory workshop on Day 0. Each passing day, some bulbs burn out. The hazard rate function essentially asks the following question at any arbitrary moment $t$:

> *"Of all the bulbs that are still glowing right now, how rapidly are they about to fail in the very next instant?"*

This conditional framing is what distinguishes the hazard rate from a simple failure probability. The hazard rate is **not** asking "How many will fail today?" but rather "Given that a unit has made it this far, what is the *risk* it fails in the next microsecond?"

### Plain-English Explanation

- If $h(t)$ is **high** → the equipment is in a "danger zone" (e.g., during the wear-out phase of its life).
- If $h(t)$ is **low** → the equipment is in a "sweet spot" of reliability (e.g., during its useful life).
- If $h(t)$ is **constant** → failures occur randomly with no memory of age (e.g., during the useful life period for many electronic components).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Misconception:** The hazard rate function $h(t)$ is **NOT** a probability. It is a *rate* — measured in units of **failures per unit time** (e.g., **failures/hour** or **failures/cycle**). It can, in fact, take values greater than **1** for highly unreliable equipment.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

In reliability engineering, the following standard metrics are inherently tied to the hazard rate function:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** $= \frac{1}{\lambda}$ for constant hazard rates.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** The expected time to first failure.
- **Reliability Function $R(t)$:** Probability of survival up to time $t$.
- **Units of $h(t)$:** Failures per unit time. For components with constant hazard, the reciprocal gives the **MTBF**, which is conventionally expressed in **hours** for industrial machinery.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Decomposition of the Hazard Rate Function

The hazard rate function $h(t)$ can be expressed in terms of three fundamental reliability functions. The relationship is built upon the following logical chain:

### Step 1: Failure Probability Density Function (PDF)
The unconditional probability density that a failure occurs at exactly time $t$ is given by:

$$f(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t)}{\Delta t}$$

### Step 2: Conditional Probability of Failure in $(t, t+\Delta t)$
Using the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{f(t)\,\Delta t}{R(t)}$$

where $R(t) = P(T > t)$ is the **Reliability Function** (survival probability).

### Step 3: Construction of the Hazard Rate
Dividing by $\Delta t$ and taking the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{f(t)\,\Delta t / \Delta t}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

This yields the **canonical form**:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

### Step 4: Expressing $h(t)$ via the Cumulative Hazard
Integrating $h(t)$ from $0$ to $t$ gives the **Cumulative Hazard Function** $H(t)$:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$$

From this, the reliability function can be expressed as:

$$R(t) = e^{-H(t)} = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du}$$

This relationship (often called the **Reliability-Hazard Identity**) is one of the most powerful identities in reliability engineering.

## 2.2 Why the Hazard Rate Function Matters: The 'Why' and 'How'

### The "Why" — Engineering Motivation

Industrial systems experience failures at different rates during different phases of their life cycle. A *single number* such as MTBF cannot fully capture this behaviour. The hazard rate function provides a **time-varying signature** of failure propensity, allowing maintenance engineers to:

- **Predict** future failures based on current operating age.
- **Optimize** preventive maintenance schedules (Condition-Based Maintenance).
- **Identify** whether a piece of equipment is in its infant-mortality, useful-life, or wear-out phase.
- **Compute** spare parts requirements over the lifetime of a fleet.

### The "How" — Operational Mechanism

The hazard rate function bridges the gap between **probability** (How likely is failure?) and **rate** (How fast are failures occurring?). By isolating the *instantaneous* risk from the *cumulative* survival probability, it allows engineering decisions to be made on a moment-by-moment basis rather than on average lifetime statistics alone.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **board-exam cheat sheet** — memorize these identities, as nearly every numerical problem in the KTU ESE and internal assessments tests one or more of these relationships.

| **S.No** | **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|:--------:|:-------------|:----------:|:------------|:----------:|
| 1 | Hazard Rate Function | $h(t)$ | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | failures / unit time |
| 2 | Cumulative Hazard | $H(t)$ | $H(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Reliability in terms of $H(t)$ | $R(t)$ | $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Reliability in terms of $h(t)$ | $R(t)$ | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | PDF in terms of $h(t)$ and $R(t)$ | $f(t)$ | $f(t) = h(t)\,R(t)$ | failures / unit time |
| 6 | CDF (Unreliability) | $F(t)$ | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Mean Time To Failure (Exponential $h$) | $\text{MTTF}$ | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours |
| 8 | Weibull Hazard Rate | $h_{\text{WB}}(t)$ | $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | failures / unit time |
| 9 | Weibull Cumulative Hazard | $H_{\text{WB}}(t)$ | $H(t) = \left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Weibull Reliability | $R_{\text{WB}}(t)$ | $R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Exponential Hazard (special case of Weibull, $\beta=1$) | $h(t)$ | $h(t) = \lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | failures / unit time |
| 12 | Bathtub Curve Mid-Region | $h(t)$ | $h(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$ | failures / unit time |
| 13 | Conditional Reliability | $R(t \mid T)$ | $R(t \mid T) = \dfrac{R(T+t)}{R(T)}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Conditional Hazard | $h(t \mid T)$ | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(T+t)}{R(T+t)}$ | failures / unit time |
| 15 | Bathtub Curve Wear-out Phase (Gompertz) | $h_{\text{G}}(t)$ | $h(t) = a\,e^{b\,t}$ | failures / unit time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions Checklist (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - $R(0) = 1$ (an item is always initially reliable).
> - $R(\infty) = 0$ (every item eventually fails).
> - $f(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \geq 0$.
> - $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\,dt = 1$ (PDF normalization).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hazard rate function is the cornerstone of several industrial decision-making frameworks:

1. **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM):** Aircraft, power plants, and heavy industries use hazard rate profiles to schedule intrusive inspections.
2. **Warranty Analysis:** Manufacturers model warranty claims using hazard rates to project future liabilities.
3. **Spare Parts Forecasting:** Logistics teams use $h(t)$ to estimate the expected number of failures per operating hour.
4. **Renewable Energy:** Wind turbine gearbox reliability is tracked using hazard models to schedule blade and bearing replacements.
5. **Medical Devices & Pharma:** Pacemaker battery depletion is described by hazard-rate-driven reliability curves for regulatory submissions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: From First Principles to $h(t) = f(t) / R(t)$

We begin from the foundational definition:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the conditional probability identity:**

By definition of conditional probability, for any two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

Here, $A \equiv \{t < T \leq t + \Delta t\}$ and $B \equiv \{T > t\}$. Since $A \subset B$, we have $A \cap B = A$, so:

$$P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t)}{P(T > t)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify each term with standard reliability functions:**

- The numerator is the probability that failure occurs in the small interval of length $\Delta t$. By the definition of the **Probability Density Function (PDF)**:

$$P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t) = f(t)\,\Delta t$$

- The denominator is the **Reliability Function** $R(t) = P(T > t)$.

Substituting:

$$P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{f(t)\,\Delta t}{R(t)}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio and take the limit:**

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta t} \cdot \frac{f(t)\,\Delta t}{R(t)}$$

The $\Delta t$ in the numerator cancels with the $\Delta t$ in the denominator:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}}$$

This is the **canonical hazard rate formula**, universally cited in KTU textbooks and reference materials.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: $R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du}$

We start with the identity derived above:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 1 — Express $f(t)$ in terms of $R(t)$:**

Since $f(t)$ is the derivative of the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF), and $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$, we have:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the hazard rate identity:**

$$h(t) = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the logarithmic derivative structure:**

$$h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\ln R(t)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:**

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{d}{du}\!\left[\ln R(u)\right]du$$

Applying the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du = -\!\left[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Apply the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$:**

Since $\ln R(0) = \ln 1 = 0$, the expression simplifies to:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du} = e^{\ln R(t)} = R(t)$$

**Step 7 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right]}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Mean Time To Failure for Constant Hazard (Exponential Distribution)

For a constant hazard rate $h(t) = \lambda$ (i.e., the exponential distribution case), the MTTF is defined as the expected value of the time to failure:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,f(t)\,dt$$

**Step 1 — Use the identity $f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$:**

This comes from $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for the exponential distribution, derived in Derivation 2.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the MTTF integral:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda\,e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate using integration by parts or the standard Gamma integral:**

The standard result for the integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} t\,e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ is well known. Therefore:

$$\text{MTTF} = \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour of $h(t)$ Under Common Distributions

| **Distribution** | **Reliability $R(t)$** | **PDF $f(t)$** | **Hazard $h(t)$** | **Trend of $h(t)$** |
|:-----------------|:-----------------------|:---------------|:-------------------|:--------------------|
| Exponential | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ (constant) | Flat (memoryless) |
| Weibull ($\beta < 1$) | $e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\frac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\frac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | **Decreasing** |
| Weibull ($\beta = 1$) | $e^{-t/\eta}$ | $\frac{1}{\eta}e^{-t/\eta}$ | $\frac{1}{\eta}$ | **Constant** |
| Weibull ($\beta > 1$) | $e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\frac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\frac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | **Increasing** |
| Normal | $1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-(t-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $\frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Increases after $\mu$ |
| Gompertz | $e^{-\frac{a}{b}(e^{bt}-1)}$ | $a\,e^{bt}\cdot R(t)$ | $a\,e^{bt}$ | **Exponentially increasing** |
| Lognormal | $1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\ln t - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\frac{1}{t\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-(\ln t-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $\frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | **Bathtub-shape (initially rises, then peaks, then drops)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Parameter Interpretation (Weibull):**
> - **Shape parameter $\beta$** governs the *trend* of the hazard rate.
> - **Scale parameter $\eta$** governs the *time-scale* at which failures occur.
> - When $\beta = 1$, the Weibull reduces to the exponential distribution (constant hazard).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** The time-to-failure of an industrial motor follows a Weibull distribution with shape parameter $\beta = 2$ and scale parameter $\eta = 1000$ hours. Find:
> (a) The reliability at $t = 500$ hours.
> (b) The hazard rate at $t = 500$ hours.
> (c) The mean time to failure.

### Solution

**Part (a): Reliability at $t = 500$ hours**

The Weibull reliability function is:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

Substituting $t = 500$, $\eta = 1000$, $\beta = 2$:

$$R(500) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{500}{1000}\right)^{2}\right] = \exp\!\left[-\left(0.5\right)^{2}\right] = e^{-0.25}$$

$$R(500) = 0.7788 \approx 0.7788$$

So there is approximately a **77.88%** probability the motor survives 500 hours.

**Part (b): Hazard Rate at $t = 500$ hours**

The Weibull hazard function is:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

Substituting:

$$h(500) = \frac{2}{1000}\left(\frac{500}{1000}\right)^{2-1} = 0.002 \times (0.5)^{1} = 0.001 \text{ failures/hour}$$

So the conditional failure rate at 500 hours is **0.001 failures per hour**, equivalent to **1 failure per 1000 operating hours**.

**Part (c): Mean Time To Failure**

For a Weibull distribution, the MTTF is given by:

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

where $\Gamma(\cdot)$ is the Gamma function. For $\beta = 2$:

$$\text{MTTF} = 1000 \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) = 1000 \cdot \Gamma(1.5)$$

Using the known value $\Gamma(1.5) = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862$:

$$\text{MTTF} = 1000 \times 0.8862 = 886.2 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification via Alternative Formula:** For Weibull, MTTF $= \eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$. This is a high-frequency exam formula in KTU Module 3 — memorize it.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Bathtub Curve — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The **Bathtub Curve** is the iconic three-region reliability diagram that visualizes how $h(t)$ evolves across the operational life of a typical component. Below is the Mermaid-rendered block diagram that maps the conceptual flow of the hazard rate through its three distinct phases.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Component Powered ON at t=0] --> B[REGION 1: Infant Mortality Phase]
    B --> B1[Cause: Manufacturing Defects]
    B --> B2[Cause: Poor Installation]
    B --> B3[Cause: Material Inconsistencies]
    B1 --> C[REGION 2: Useful Life Phase]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Cause: Random External Stresses]
    C --> C2[Hazard Rate h(t) = CONSTANT]
    C2 --> D[REGION 3: Wear Out Phase]
    D --> D1[Cause: Mechanical Fatigue]
    D --> D2[Cause: Corrosion and Erosion]
    D --> D3[Cause: Material Aging]
    D1 --> E[END: Component Fails]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Reliability-to-Hazard Mapping

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional dependency chain between $f(t)$, $R(t)$, and $h(t)$, which is essential for solving KTU problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fPDF["Probability Density Function f(t)"] -->|numerator| divNode["Division h(t) = f(t) / R(t)"]
    fRel["Reliability Function R(t) = 1 - F(t)"] -->|denominator| divNode
    divNode --> hOut["Hazard Rate h(t)"]
    fRel -->|integrate| cHazard["Cumulative Hazard H(t)"]
    hOut -->|integrate| cHazard
    cHazard -->|exponentiate negative| fRel
    hOut -->|time domain| bathtub["Bathtub Curve h(t) vs t"]
    bathtub --> R1["Region 1: Decreasing h(t)"]
    bathtub --> R2["Region 2: Constant h(t)"]
    bathtub --> R3["Region 3: Increasing h(t)"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Process Flow — Hazard Rate Computation

The following flowchart captures the **decision-and-calculation flow** that an engineer (or KTU student) follows when computing or interpreting a hazard rate value.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Given a Component]) --> Q1{Is a Failure Distribution Specified?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> DistType["Identify Distribution Type"]
    Q1 -- No --> EmpData["Use Empirical Failure Data to Estimate h(t)"]
    DistType --> DistChoice{Distribution Type?}
    DistChoice -- Exponential --> CalcExp["h(t) = lambda, constant"]
    DistChoice -- Weibull --> CalcWei["h(t) equals beta divided by eta times t over eta raised to beta minus 1"]
    DistChoice -- Normal --> CalcNorm["h(t) equals f(t) divided by R(t)"]
    CalcExp --> Result["Compute h(t) at Desired t"]
    CalcWei --> Result
    CalcNorm --> Result
    EmpData --> EmpMethod["Bin failures in intervals, divide by survivors in each interval, average"]
    EmpMethod --> Result
    Result --> Decision{Maintenance Decision Required?}
    Decision -- Yes --> SchedPM["Schedule Preventive Maintenance"]
    Decision -- No --> Doc["Document h(t) for Reliability Block Diagram"]
    SchedPM --> End1([End])
    Doc --> End1
```

## 4.4 Schematic Mapping of Weibull Shape Parameter Effects on $h(t)$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WStart([Weibull Shape Parameter beta]) --> W1{Value of beta}
    W1 -- beta less than 1 --> Dec["h(t) is DECREASING"]
    W1 -- beta equals 1 --> Con["h(t) is CONSTANT equals 1 over eta"]
    W1 -- beta greater than 1 --> Inc["h(t) is INCREASING"]
    Dec --> AppDec["Application: Infant Mortality Items, Burn-in Periods"]
    Con --> AppCon["Application: Useful Life of Electronic Components"]
    Inc --> AppInc["Application: Mechanical Wear-out Items like Bearings"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for the Diagrams:** All Mermaid diagrams above use **purely alphanumeric node identifiers** (no reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph` are used as node IDs) and **plain text labels** (no bold/italic markdown inside node labels), per KTU rendering safeguards.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Hazard Rate Function. State any two of its important properties.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **Hazard Rate Function** $h(t)$ is defined as the instantaneous rate of failure of a component at time $t$, conditional on its survival up to that time:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Two important properties:**

1. **$h(t) \geq 0$** for all $t \geq 0$ — the hazard rate is non-negative at all times.
2. **$h(t)$ has units of inverse time** (e.g., failures/hour) — it is a *rate*, not a probability.
3. *(Optional third)* $h(t)$ need not be less than **1**; it can take values greater than 1 for highly unreliable systems.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between the Reliability Function $R(t)$ and the Hazard Rate Function $h(t)$.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| **Aspect** | **Reliability $R(t)$** | **Hazard Rate $h(t)$** |
|:-----------|:-----------------------|:-----------------------|
| Definition | Probability of survival up to time $t$ | Instantaneous conditional failure rate at time $t$ |
| Mathematical form | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ |
| Range of values | $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ (dimensionless) | $h(t) \geq 0$ (can exceed 1) |
| Units | Dimensionless | Failures per unit time |
| Physical meaning | Likelihood the component has not failed | Rate at which survivors are failing |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

> **(a)** Derive the relationship between the Hazard Rate Function $h(t)$, the Reliability Function $R(t)$, and the Probability Density Function $f(t)$. Hence, show that:
>
> $$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right]$$
>
> **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The time-to-failure of a machine component follows a Weibull distribution with shape parameter $\beta = 1.5$ and scale parameter $\eta = 2000$ hours. Compute:
> - (i) The reliability at $t = 1000$ hours.
> - (ii) The hazard rate at $t = 1000$ hours.
> - (iii) The mean time to failure.
>
> **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define the Hazard Rate Function from first principles:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Apply the conditional probability identity:

$$P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t)}{P(T > t)}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Recognize that $P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t) = f(t)\,\Delta t$ and $P(T > t) = R(t)$:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{f(t)\,\Delta t}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Express $f(t)$ in terms of $R(t)$ via the identity $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$:

$$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)}\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Rewrite as a logarithmic derivative:

$$h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Apply $R(0) = 1$ and exponentiate:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right]$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** Weibull distribution with $\beta = 1.5$, $\eta = 2000$ hours, $t = 1000$ hours.

**(i) Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours (2 Marks):**

$$R(1000) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{1000}{2000}\right)^{1.5}\right] = \exp\!\left[-(0.5)^{1.5}\right]$$

$$(0.5)^{1.5} = 0.5^{1} \cdot 0.5^{0.5} = 0.5 \cdot 0.7071 = 0.3536$$

$$R(1000) = e^{-0.3536} = 0.7022$$

So $R(1000) \approx 0.7022$ or **70.22%**.

**(ii) Hazard Rate at $t = 1000$ hours (2 Marks):**

$$h(1000) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} = \frac{1.5}{2000}\left(\frac{1000}{2000}\right)^{0.5}$$

$$h(1000) = 0.00075 \times (0.5)^{0.5} = 0.00075 \times 0.7071$$

$$h(1000) \approx 5.303 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}$$

Equivalently, **0.5303 failures per 1000 hours**.

**(iii) Mean Time To Failure (3 Marks):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) = 2000 \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{1.5}\right) = 2000 \cdot \Gamma(1.6667)$$

Using the recursion $\Gamma(1.6667) = (0.6667)\,\Gamma(0.6667)$ and $\Gamma(0.6667) \approx 1.3541$:

$$\Gamma(1.6667) = 0.6667 \times 1.3541 = 0.9027$$

$$\text{MTTF} = 2000 \times 0.9027 \approx 1805.4 \text{ hours}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]`

> **(a)** What is the Hazard Rate Function? Explain the three regions of the **Bathtub Curve** in detail, indicating the dominant failure mechanisms and the trend of $h(t)$ in each region. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a system with constant hazard rate $\lambda = 0.002$ failures/hour:
> - (i) Determine the reliability for a mission time of 500 hours.
> - (ii) Determine the cumulative hazard $H(500)$.
> - (iii) Compute the probability that the system fails between 200 and 500 hours, given it has survived 200 hours.
>
> **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Bathtub Curve Explanation (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):** The Hazard Rate Function $h(t)$ represents the instantaneous rate of failure at time $t$, conditional on survival up to $t$. The Bathtub Curve plots $h(t)$ versus time $t$ and reveals three distinct phases:

**Region 1 — Infant Mortality Phase / Burn-in (2 Marks):**
- **Trend of $h(t)$:** **Decreasing** with time.
- **Dominant failures:** Manufacturing defects, latent material flaws, installation errors, poor workmanship.
- **Engineering action:** Burn-in testing to weed out early failures before deployment.

**Region 2 — Useful Life Phase / Random Failures (2 Marks):**
- **Trend of $h(t)$:** **Constant** (flat).
- **Dominant failures:** Random external stresses such as voltage surges, operator errors, overloads, environmental shocks.
- **Engineering action:** Condition-based monitoring; failures are essentially *memoryless* (exponential distribution).

**Region 3 — Wear-out Phase (1 Mark):**
- **Trend of $h(t)$:** **Increasing** (often exponentially — Gompertz-like).
- **Dominant failures:** Mechanical fatigue, corrosion, abrasion, material aging.
- **Engineering action:** Preventive replacement schedules; reliability decreases rapidly with age.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.002$ failures/hour (constant hazard).

**(i) Reliability at $t = 500$ hours (2 Marks):**

For a constant hazard, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$R(500) = e^{-0.002 \times 500} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$

**(ii) Cumulative Hazard at $t = 500$ hours (2 Marks):**

$$H(500) = \int_{0}^{500} \lambda\,du = \lambda \cdot 500 = 0.002 \times 500 = 1.0$$

Equivalently, $H(500) = -\ln R(500) = -\ln(0.3679) = 1.0$. ✓

**(iii) Probability of failure in (200, 500] given survival at 200 (3 Marks):**

Using the conditional reliability identity:

$$R(300 \mid 200) = \frac{R(500)}{R(200)}$$

Compute $R(200)$:

$$R(200) = e^{-0.002 \times 200} = e^{-0.4} \approx 0.6703$$

Therefore:

$$R(300 \mid 200) = \frac{0.3679}{0.6703} \approx 0.5489$$

The probability of failure in (200, 500] given survival at 200 is:

$$P(\text{fail in } (200, 500] \mid \text{survived 200}) = 1 - R(300 \mid 200) = 1 - 0.5489 = 0.4511$$

So there is approximately a **45.11%** probability of failure in that interval, conditional on surviving the first 200 hours.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Hazard Rate Problems:**
>
> 1. **Units Mismatch (–1 Mark):** Students frequently write $h(t)$ as a *probability* (dimensionless) instead of as a *rate* (failures/hour). Always state units explicitly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Boundary Condition $R(0) = 1$ (–1 Mark):** When integrating $h(t)$ to recover $R(t)$, students often forget the constant of integration $\ln R(0) = 0$. Without this, the exponential form $e^{-\int h\,du}$ will be wrong by a constant factor.
>
> 3. **Confusing $h(t)$ with $f(t)$ (–2 Marks):** $f(t)$ is the unconditional probability density. $h(t)$ is the **conditional** rate. In an exam, mixing them up will lose substantial marks.
>
> 4. **Weibull Parameter Confusion (–1 Mark):** Many students swap $\beta$ and $\eta$. Remember: $\beta$ = *shape* (controls trend), $\eta$ = *scale* (controls time magnitude, has units of time).
>
> 5. **MTBF vs MTTF (–1 Mark):** For **non-repairable** systems, the correct term is **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure). For **repairable** systems, use **MTBF**. KTU examiners check this distinction.
>
> 6. **Bathtub Curve Region Identification (–1 Mark):** When asked about wear-out, students sometimes confuse it with the infant mortality region. Wear-out is the **right** (third) region, not the left.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hazard Rate Function**
>
> - **Definition:** $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ — instantaneous conditional failure rate at time $t$.
> - **Canonical identities:**
>   - $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$
>   - $f(t) = h(t)\,R(t)$
>   - $R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du}$
>   - $H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$
> - **Units:** $h(t)$ is in *failures per unit time* — NOT a probability.
> - **Range:** $h(t) \geq 0$, may exceed **1**; $R(t) \in [0, 1]$.
> - **Exponential distribution** = constant hazard rate (memoryless property).
> - **Weibull distribution:** $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ — three cases based on $\beta$:
>   - $\beta < 1$: hazard decreases (infant mortality).
>   - $\beta = 1$: hazard constant (exponential).
>   - $\beta > 1$: hazard increases (wear-out).
> - **MTTF formulas:**
>   - Exponential: $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$
>   - Weibull: $\text{MTTF} = \eta\,\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$
> - **Conditional reliability:** $R(t \mid T) = R(T + t) / R(T)$.
> - **Bathtub Curve has 3 regions:** Burn-in (decreasing $h$), Useful life (constant $h$), Wear-out (increasing $h$).
> - **Reliability-Hazard Identity** $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ is the single most-tested equation in this topic.
> - **Always state assumptions** (repairable vs non-repairable, distribution used, parameter values) before starting numerical problems.
> - **Common distributions tested:** Exponential, Weibull, Normal, Lognormal, Gompertz.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bathtub Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bathtub Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bathtub Curve** is a reliability engineering graphical model that depicts the **instantaneous failure rate** $\lambda(t)$ of a population of components, machines, or systems as a function of time $t$ over their operational lifecycle. The curve derives its name from its characteristic cross-sectional shape resembling a traditional domestic bathtub — high on the left, flat across the middle, and high on the right.

It is formally constructed as the composite superposition of **three distinct hazard regimes**:

$$ \lambda(t) = \lambda_{early}(t) + \lambda_{random}(t) + \lambda_{wear}(t) $$

where each term corresponds to a specific phase of equipment life governed by different underlying probabilistic and physical failure mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The bathtub curve is a foundational concept in **Reliability Engineering**, a sub-domain of Industrial Engineering that deals with the probability of failure-free operation of systems. It is mandatory for understanding maintenance strategies like Preventive Maintenance (PM) and Total Productive Maintenance (TPM).

## 1.2 The Three Operational Phases

| Phase | Domain | Failure Behaviour | Dominant Cause |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Phase I** | Infant Mortality | Decreasing failure rate | Manufacturing defects, poor quality control, installation errors |
| **Phase II** | Useful Life | Constant failure rate | Random / Chance failures, external shocks |
| **Phase III** | Wear-out | Increasing failure rate | Aging, fatigue, corrosion, material degradation |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Life Cycle

Imagine a brand-new car fresh from the dealership showroom:

- **Infant Mortality Analogy:** Think of a human infant in its first year of life — statistically fragile due to congenital issues and an underdeveloped immune system. Likewise, new components fail often initially due to **latent manufacturing defects** (e.g., poor soldering, micro-cracks, substandard materials). These "weak" units are quickly weeded out, so the surviving population becomes progressively more reliable. This is why manufacturers run **burn-in tests** — they deliberately operate the product to expose and eliminate the weak units *before* shipping to customers.

- **Useful Life Analogy:** A healthy adult in their 30s — failures occur randomly and unpredictably (a sudden accident, a viral infection). The failure rate becomes **constant and low** because the obvious flaws have been eliminated, and wear-out has not yet set in. For machines, this translates to random failures from operator error, power surges, or unexpected load spikes.

- **Wear-out Analogy:** A senior citizen in their 70s — the body begins to break down due to accumulated wear. Similarly, components like bearings, cutting tools, conveyor belts, and insulation experience **age-related degradation** (metal fatigue, oxidation, wear). Failure rate climbs steeply as the system approaches the end of its physical service life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** The bathtub curve is the **decision-making foundation** for selecting an appropriate maintenance strategy. During the useful life (Phase II), corrective maintenance is optimal. During wear-out (Phase III), preventive replacement becomes economically justified.

## 1.4 Key Reliability Metrics (Standardized Terminology)

- **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) — for repairable systems
- **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure) — for non-repairable components
- **$\lambda$** (lambda) — Failure rate, expressed in failures per unit time (e.g., **failures per million hours**)
- **R(t)** — Reliability function, the probability of survival to time $t$
- **F(t)** — Cumulative failure distribution function

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam answers, always express failure rate in standard units: $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{Number of failures}}{\text{Total operating time}}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Anatomy of the Curve

### 2.1.1 Phase I — Infant Mortality Region (Burn-in Region)

**Characteristic Shape:** Convex, decreasing failure rate.

The failure rate is mathematically modeled by a **Weibull distribution with shape parameter $\beta < 1$**:

$$ \lambda_{I}(t) = \lambda_{0} \cdot \alpha \cdot (\alpha t)^{\beta - 1} \quad \text{where } \beta < 1 $$

**Underlying Physics:**
- Defective components possess below-average mechanical strength.
- The probability of early failure is proportional to the **residual defect severity**.
- As time progresses, the weakest units fail first and are removed from the population.
- The remaining population is progressively "stronger" on average.

**Engineering Action:** Burn-in testing under accelerated stress conditions (heat, voltage, vibration) to pre-eliminate weak units. This is standard practice in the semiconductor, automotive, and aerospace industries.

### 2.1.2 Phase II — Useful Life Region (Random Failure Region)

**Characteristic Shape:** Approximately constant, flat failure rate.

Mathematically modeled by the **Exponential Distribution**:

$$ \lambda_{II}(t) = \lambda = \text{constant} $$

The reliability function becomes:

$$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} $$

**Key Property — The Memoryless Property:**

$$ P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t} $$

This means the probability of surviving an additional $t$ hours is **independent of how long the component has already operated**. A bearing that has run for 5 years has the same conditional failure probability in the next hour as one that was installed yesterday.

**Underlying Physics:**
- Random external shocks (voltage spikes, mechanical impacts, operator errors).
- No dominant wear mechanism yet.
- Failures follow a **Poisson process** with constant intensity.

**Engineering Action:** Corrective (run-to-failure) maintenance is economically optimal. Spare parts inventory is sized using the constant $\lambda$.

### 2.1.3 Phase III — Wear-out Region

**Characteristic Shape:** Concave, increasing failure rate.

Modeled by the **Weibull distribution with shape parameter $\beta > 1$** or the **Normal / Lognormal distribution**:

$$ \lambda_{III}(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left( \frac{t}{\eta} \right)^{\beta - 1} \quad \text{where } \beta > 1 $$

where $\eta$ is the **scale parameter** (characteristic life) and $\beta$ is the **shape parameter** (wear-out severity).

**Underlying Physics:**
- Cumulative damage: metal fatigue follows the **Basquin's law** $\sigma_{a} = \sigma_{f}'(2N_{f})^{b}$.
- Corrosion kinetics (Arrhenius-type).
- Lubricant depletion, surface pitting, crack propagation.

**Engineering Action:** Scheduled preventive replacement before the inflection point. Condition-Based Monitoring (CBM) using vibration analysis, thermography, oil analysis, and ultrasonic thickness gauging.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Applicable Phase | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability at time $t$ | Phase II (Useful life) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Cumulative failure probability | Phase II | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between/To Failure | Phase II | Hours / cycles |
| 4 | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability Density Function | Phase II | Per hour |
| 5 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Instantaneous failure rate (Hazard) | All phases | Failures / hour |
| 6 | $\lambda_{s}(t) = \dfrac{R(t+s) - R(t)}{R(t) \cdot s}$ | Conditional failure probability | All phases | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du}$ | General reliability function | All phases | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $M(t) = \dfrac{\int_{0}^{t} R(u) \, du}{R(t)}$ | Mean residual life | All phases | Hours / cycles |
| 9 | $\eta = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$ | Mean life (general) | All phases | Hours / cycles |
| 10 | $f(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Weibull PDF | Phases I & III | Per hour |
| 11 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Weibull hazard function | Phases I & III | Failures / hour |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** MTBF applies only to **repairable systems** (machines, vehicles, production lines). MTTF applies to **non-repairable components** (light bulbs, bearings, capacitors). Mixing these terms in a KTU answer will cost marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Aerospace Industry:** Aircraft engine components are routinely retired well before their wear-out phase based on bathtub-curve-derived service life limits. The "hard time" component replacement program is a direct application.

2. **Electronics Manufacturing:** Every smartphone chipset undergoes 48–168 hours of burn-in under elevated temperature and voltage to screen out infant-mortality defects. This is Phase I in action.

3. **Power Generation:** Nuclear power plants use **Preventive Maintenance Optimization (PMO)** models derived from bathtub-curve analysis to schedule turbine overhauls, reducing both unplanned downtime and wasteful over-maintenance.

4. **Automotive Industry:** Engine oil change intervals (e.g., every 10,000 km) are calibrated to keep the vehicle operating in the flat portion of the bathtub curve, avoiding both early failure and wear-out regimes.

5. **Medical Devices:** Pacemakers and defibrillators are designed with **redundancy and condition monitoring** specifically because failure in Phase II (random failure) can be catastrophic and unpredictable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Constant Failure Rate in Phase II

**Starting Premise:** In the useful life region, components fail purely due to random external shocks. The probability of a failure in a small interval $\Delta t$ is proportional to $\Delta t$ and independent of prior history.

**Step 1: Set up the differential reliability equation**

The probability of survival through an infinitesimal interval $\Delta t$ is:

$$ P(\text{survive } \Delta t) = 1 - \lambda \Delta t + o(\Delta t) $$

where $o(\Delta t)$ represents vanishingly small higher-order terms.

**Step 2: Apply the memoryless property to split time interval $[0, t]$ into $n$ sub-intervals of width $\Delta t = t/n$**

Since survival through the full interval $[0, t]$ requires survival through all $n$ sub-intervals, and shocks are independent:

$$ R(t) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left( 1 - \lambda \cdot \frac{t}{n} \right)^{n} $$

**Step 3: Recognize the fundamental limit definition of the exponential function**

By the standard limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} \left( 1 + \dfrac{x}{n} \right)^{n} = e^{x}$:

$$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} $$

**Step 4: Derive the probability density function**

Differentiating the reliability function with respect to $t$:

$$ f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\left( e^{-\lambda t} \right) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} $$

**Step 5: Compute the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)**

The expected life is the first moment of the probability density:

$$ \text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt $$

Integrating by parts with $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$:

$$ \text{MTTF} = \left[ -t e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt $$

The boundary term vanishes (since $t e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$):

$$ \text{MTTF} = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{\lambda} \right) = \frac{1}{\lambda} $$

**Conclusion:** For a constant failure rate $\lambda$ in Phase II, the reliability, PDF, and mean life follow a clean exponential relationship. This is why MTBF $= 1/\lambda$ is the single most important equation in reliability engineering.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Failure Rate and Reliability

**Starting Premise:** The instantaneous failure rate (hazard function) is defined as the conditional probability density of failure at time $t$, given survival up to time $t$:

$$ \lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T \le t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} $$

**Step 1: Expand the conditional probability**

By the definition of conditional probability, $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$:

$$ \lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T \le t + \Delta t)}{P(T > t) \cdot \Delta t} $$

**Step 2: Replace the probabilities with their density and reliability functions**

The numerator approaches $f(t) \cdot \Delta t$ as $\Delta t \to 0$, and the denominator equals $R(t)$:

$$ \lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} $$

**Step 3: Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$**

$$ \lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{d \ln R(t)}{dt} $$

**Step 4: Integrate both sides from 0 to $t$**

$$ \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{d \ln R(u)}{du} \, du = -[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)] $$

Since at $t = 0$, the reliability is $R(0) = 1$, so $\ln R(0) = 0$:

$$ \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du = -\ln R(t) $$

**Step 5: Solve for $R(t)$**

Exponentiating both sides yields the general reliability function:

$$ R(t) = \exp\left( -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du \right) $$

**Step 6: Specialize to constant failure rate**

Substituting $\lambda(u) = \lambda$ (constant):

$$ R(t) = \exp\left( -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda \, du \right) = \exp(-\lambda t) = e^{-\lambda t} $$

This confirms the result of Derivation 1 from a different starting point, validating internal consistency.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Phase Comparison Table

| Parameter | Phase I (Infant Mortality) | Phase II (Useful Life) | Phase III (Wear-out) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Statistical Distribution** | Weibull ($\beta < 1$) | Exponential | Weibull ($\beta > 1$) or Normal |
| **Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$** | Decreasing sharply | Constant, minimum | Increasing sharply |
| **Reliability $R(t)$** | Decays faster than exponential | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | Decays slower initially, then collapses |
| **Mean Life** | Reduced by early failures | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Reduced by wear-out tail |
| **Maintenance Strategy** | Burn-in, screening | Corrective (run-to-failure) | Preventive replacement, overhaul |
| **Failure Cause** | Defects, quality issues | Random shocks, accidents | Fatigue, corrosion, aging |
| **Cost Implication** | High initial warranty cost | Stable, predictable | Rising maintenance, downtime cost |
| **Suitable Industrial Example** | Newly manufactured ICs | Mature production line CNC machine | 20-year-old boiler in a thermal plant |
| **Probability of Failure** | Highest at $t = 0$, decreases | Uniform over time | Highest near end of life |
| **Reliability Curve Shape** | Concave upward | Exponential decay | Concave downward, then cliff drop |

## 3.4 Numerical Illustration for KTU Problem-Solving

**Problem:** A pump has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.002$ failures per hour during its useful life. Find:
- (a) The reliability for 100 hours of operation.
- (b) The probability of failure in the interval from 100 to 200 hours, given it has survived 100 hours.
- (c) The MTBF.

**Solution:**

**(a) Reliability at $t = 100$ hours:**

$$ R(100) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.002)(100)} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187 $$

This means there is an **81.87% probability** that the pump operates failure-free for 100 hours.

**(b) Conditional failure probability between 100 and 200 hours:**

Using the conditional failure formula:

$$ P(100 \le T \le 200 \mid T > 100) = 1 - \frac{R(200)}{R(100)} $$

Computing $R(200)$:

$$ R(200) = e^{-(0.002)(200)} = e^{-0.4} \approx 0.6703 $$

Therefore:

$$ P(100 \le T \le 200 \mid T > 100) = 1 - \frac{0.6703}{0.8187} \approx 1 - 0.8187 \approx 0.1813 $$

Note that this equals $1 - R(100) = 0.1813$, which is the **memoryless property** in action: the conditional probability of failing between hour 100 and 200 is identical to the probability of failing in the first 100 hours.

**(c) Mean Time Between Failures:**

$$ \text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.002} = 500 \text{ hours} $$

This is the expected average operating time between successive failures of the pump.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Bathtub Curve — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the structural logic of the bathtub curve, including its three phases, dominant failure mechanisms, characteristic failure rate behaviour, and the corresponding maintenance strategies recommended in each regime.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Equipment Life at t equals 0] --> B{Phase I: Infant Mortality}
    B --> C[Failure Cause: Manufacturing Defects]
    C --> D[Failure Rate Behaviour: Decreasing Convex Curve]
    D --> E[Maintenance Strategy: Burn-in Testing and Quality Screening]
    E --> F{Phase II: Useful Life}
    F --> G[Failure Cause: Random External Shocks]
    G --> H[Failure Rate Behaviour: Constant Flat Line]
    H --> I[Maintenance Strategy: Corrective Run to Failure]
    I --> J{Phase III: Wear Out}
    J --> K[Failure Cause: Fatigue Corrosion Aging]
    K --> L[Failure Rate Behaviour: Increasing Concave Curve]
    L --> M[Maintenance Strategy: Preventive Replacement and Overhaul]
    M --> N[End of Useful Service Life]
    E --> O[Weibull Distribution with beta less than 1]
    I --> P[Exponential Distribution with constant lambda]
    M --> Q[Weibull Distribution with beta greater than 1]
```

## 4.2 Phase Transition Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Component Population] --> B[Quality Screening at Factory]
    B --> C{Defect Density Exceeds Threshold}
    C -- Yes --> D[Reject Unit in Burn in]
    C -- No --> E[Ship to Customer in Phase II]
    E --> F{Operating Time Reaches Design Life}
    F -- No --> G[Continue Corrective Maintenance]
    F -- Yes --> H[Schedule Preventive Replacement]
    H --> I[Move into Phase III Managed Operation]
    I --> J{End of Service Life Reached}
    J -- No --> K[Continue Condition Monitoring]
    J -- Yes --> L[Retire and Replace Component]
```

## 4.3 Failure Rate vs. Time — Conceptual Plot

```
Failure Rate λ(t)
    │  \                              /
    │   \                            /
    │    \                          /
    │     \                        /
    │      \______________________/
    │       
    │       
    └──────────────────────────────────────► Time (t)
         Phase I    Phase II    Phase III
        (Infant)   (Useful)    (Wear-out)
```

**Interpretation of the plot:**

- The **left descending arm** represents Phase I: failure rate falls rapidly as defective units are eliminated.
- The **flat middle trough** represents Phase II: failure rate stabilizes at its minimum value $\lambda_{min}$ due to purely random shocks.
- The **right ascending arm** represents Phase III: failure rate rises steeply as wear-out mechanisms dominate.
- The **minimum point** of the curve defines the **useful life operating window** where the equipment is most reliable.

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU 2024 exam, when asked to "sketch and explain the bathtub curve," a student must label all three regions, indicate the failure rate axis ($\lambda(t)$), the time axis ($t$), and identify the dominant failure mechanism in each region. Drawings without labels typically receive **partial credit only**.

---
