# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define the term "Bathtub Curve" in reliability engineering. Name its three distinct regions.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bathtub Curve** is a graphical representation in reliability engineering that plots the **instantaneous failure rate** $\lambda(t)$ of a component or system as a function of operating time $t$. The curve resembles the cross-sectional shape of a domestic bathtub and consists of three distinct regions:

1. **Infant Mortality Region (Phase I):** Exhibits a high but rapidly **decreasing** failure rate. Failures are caused by manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, or substandard materials. Eliminated by **burn-in testing**.

2. **Useful Life Region (Phase II):** Exhibits a **constant** and low failure rate. Failures are random in nature and independent of operating time. Corrective maintenance is the optimal strategy.

3. **Wear-out Region (Phase III):** Exhibits an **increasing** failure rate due to physical aging, fatigue, corrosion, and material degradation. Managed through scheduled preventive replacement.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between MTBF and MTTF. Under which phase of the bathtub curve is the exponential distribution applicable?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Average time interval between two consecutive failures of a repairable system | Average expected lifetime of a non-repairable component before it fails |
| **Applicability** | Repairable systems (machines, vehicles, aircraft) | Non-repairable items (light bulbs, capacitors, fuses, bearings) |
| **Numerical Value** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ |
| **Usage Context** | Operational reliability of production equipment | Component design reliability in product development |

**Exponential Distribution Applicability:** The exponential distribution is strictly applicable only to **Phase II (Useful Life)** of the bathtub curve, where the failure rate is **constant** over time. The probability density function is $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ and the reliability function is $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. This constant-rate behaviour arises from the **memoryless property** of the exponential distribution, which models purely random failure events independent of prior operating history.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the three regions of the bathtub curve. For each region, identify the dominant failure mechanism, the typical shape of the failure rate function, and the recommended maintenance strategy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A component has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures per hour during its useful life phase. Determine:
- (i) The reliability of the component for 500 hours of continuous operation.
- (ii) The probability that the component will fail between 500 and 1000 hours, given that it has already survived 500 hours.
- (iii) The Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF). **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Three Regions of the Bathtub Curve [7 Marks]

The bathtub curve is a fundamental reliability engineering tool that characterizes the failure behaviour of repairable systems over their operational life. It comprises three regions, each with distinct physical, statistical, and managerial characteristics.

**Region 1: Infant Mortality Region (Burn-in Phase)**

This is the initial period of equipment life, typically lasting from a few hours to several months depending on the complexity of the system. The failure rate is initially **high** and **decreases rapidly** with operating time, producing a **convex downward** shape. The dominant failure mechanisms are **manufacturing and assembly defects** such as poor solder joints, contaminated materials, incorrect tolerances, misaligned components, and inadequate quality control. Statistically, this region is best modeled by a **Weibull distribution with shape parameter $\beta < 1$**. The engineering response is to conduct **burn-in testing** under accelerated stress conditions (elevated temperature, voltage, vibration) to screen out defective units before they are delivered to customers. This region is sometimes called the "debugging period."

**Region 2: Useful Life Region (Normal Operating Phase)**

This is the long, stable middle portion of the bathtub curve where the failure rate is approximately **constant** at its minimum value. The curve is essentially a **flat horizontal line**. Failures during this phase are **random in nature** and result from unpredictable external events such as voltage surges, operator errors, accidental impacts, sudden load changes, or environmental disturbances. There is no systematic wear mechanism yet, and surviving the infant mortality phase implies the population is now homogeneous in quality. The statistical model is the **exponential distribution** with $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, which has the **memoryless property**: the conditional probability of failure in the next interval is independent of past operation. The recommended maintenance strategy is **corrective maintenance** (run-to-failure) combined with a well-stocked inventory of critical spares sized using the constant $\lambda$.

**Region 3: Wear-out Region (End-of-Life Phase)**

In this final region, the failure rate **increases progressively** with operating time, producing a **concave upward** shape that may rise sharply. The dominant mechanisms are **physical degradation** processes: metal fatigue (Basquin's law of cyclic loading), corrosion (Arrhenius kinetics), surface wear and pitting, lubricant depletion, creep in high-temperature components, and electrical insulation breakdown. The statistical model is the **Weibull distribution with shape parameter $\beta > 1$** or the **lognormal distribution**. The recommended strategy is **preventive maintenance** with scheduled component replacement, overhaul, or refurbishment. Condition-based monitoring techniques (vibration analysis, thermography, oil debris analysis, ultrasonic testing) are deployed to predict remaining useful life (RUL) and trigger replacement before catastrophic failure occurs.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour (Phase II)
- Operating time $t_1 = 500$ hours and $t_2 = 1000$ hours

**(i) Reliability at $t = 500$ hours [2 Marks]**

$$ R(500) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.0005)(500)} = e^{-0.25} $$

$$ R(500) \approx 0.7788 $$

This means there is a **77.88% probability** that the component will operate failure-free for the first 500 hours.

**(ii) Conditional failure probability between 500 and 1000 hours [3 Marks]**

Step 1: Compute reliability at 1000 hours.

$$ R(1000) = e^{-(0.0005)(1000)} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065 $$

Step 2: Apply the conditional probability formula.

$$ P(500 \le T \le 1000 \mid T > 500) = 1 - \frac{R(1000)}{R(500)} $$

$$ P(500 \le T \le 1000 \mid T > 500) = 1 - \frac{0.6065}{0.7788} $$

$$ P(500 \le T \le 1000 \mid T > 500) = 1 - 0.7788 = 0.2212 $$

**Verification using memoryless property:** This should equal $1 - R(500) = 1 - 0.7788 = 0.2212$. ✓ The result confirms the memoryless property of the exponential distribution.

**(iii) Mean Time Between Failures [2 Marks]**

$$ \text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours} $$

The expected average operating time between successive failures is **2000 hours**, or equivalently, **83.33 days** of continuous operation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**Q: (a)** Define the **hazard rate function** $\lambda(t)$ and derive the general relationship $\lambda(t) = -\dfrac{d \ln R(t)}{dt}$. Using this relationship, show that for a constant failure rate $\lambda$, the reliability function reduces to $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A production line operates with 50 identical machines. During a 6-month observation period (4380 hours), the following data was collected:

- **Phase II (Useful Life):** Total operating hours across all machines = 200,000 hours; Total failures observed = 100.
- **Phase III (Wear-out):** Total operating hours across all machines = 50,000 hours; Total failures observed = 80.

Calculate:
- (i) The average failure rate in each phase.
- (ii) The MTBF for Phase II.
- (iii) Comment on which phase requires more aggressive maintenance intervention and justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Hazard Rate Function [7 Marks]

**Definition [2 Marks]:** The **hazard rate function** (also called the **instantaneous failure rate** or **force of mortality**) $\lambda(t)$ is defined as the conditional probability density of failure at time $t$, given that the component has survived up to that time. Formally:

$$ \lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T \le t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} $$

The unit of $\lambda(t)$ is **failures per unit time** (e.g., failures per hour, failures per million hours, or **FPMH** = Failures Per Million Hours in industrial practice).

**Derivation [5 Marks]:**

Step 1: Expand the conditional probability using the definition $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$:

$$ \lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T \le t + \Delta t)}{P(T > t) \cdot \Delta t} $$

Step 2: Recognize that the numerator is the probability of failure in the interval $[t, t + \Delta t]$, which in the limit becomes $f(t) \cdot \Delta t$, and the denominator is the reliability $R(t)$:

$$ \lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{f(t) \cdot \Delta t}{R(t) \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} $$

Step 3: Use the identity $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ (since the area under the PDF equals 1 and $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$):

$$ \lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$

Step 4: Apply the chain rule. The derivative of $\ln R(t)$ is $\dfrac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$ \lambda(t) = -\frac{d}{dt} \left[ \ln R(t) \right] $$

**Specialization to constant failure rate [Included in 7 marks]:**

If $\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$, the differential equation becomes:

$$ -\frac{d \ln R(t)}{dt} = \lambda $$

Separating variables and integrating from 0 to $t$:

$$ -\int_{0}^{t} d \ln R(u) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda \, du $$

$$ -[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)] = \lambda t $$

Since $R(0) = 1$, we have $\ln R(0) = 0$, so:

$$ -\ln R(t) = \lambda t \implies \ln R(t) = -\lambda t $$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} $$

This is the desired result.

#### Part (b) — Industrial Application [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**

| Phase | Total Operating Hours (T) | Number of Failures (N) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Phase II (Useful Life) | 200,000 hours | 100 |
| Phase III (Wear-out) | 50,000 hours | 80 |

**(i) Average failure rate in each phase [2 Marks]**

Phase II:

$$ \lambda_{II} = \frac{\text{Number of failures}}{\text{Total operating hours}} = \frac{100}{200{,}000} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour} $$

Phase III:

$$ \lambda_{III} = \frac{80}{50{,}000} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ failures/hour} $$

**(ii) MTBF for Phase II [2 Marks]**

$$ \text{MTBF}_{II} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{II}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2000 \text{ hours} $$

This means on average, a machine operating in Phase II will function for 2000 hours between consecutive failures.

**(iii) Maintenance strategy recommendation [3 Marks]**

Phase III requires **more aggressive maintenance intervention**. The justification rests on three quantitative observations:

1. **Failure rate comparison:** $\lambda_{III} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ is **3.2 times higher** than $\lambda_{II} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$. This is consistent with the wear-out region exhibiting a sharply increasing failure rate.

2. **Failure density:** In Phase II, failures occur at a density of 0.5 failures per 1000 hours. In Phase III, this rises to 1.6 failures per 1000 hours — over three times denser.

3. **Economic rationale:** Phase III failures are predictable and progressive (fatigue, wear, corrosion), so **scheduled preventive replacement** is far more economical than allowing unpredictable failures to cause production stoppages. In contrast, Phase II failures are random and cannot be cost-effectively prevented by scheduled action, so **corrective maintenance with spare-parts readiness** is the optimal strategy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing MTBF and MTTF** — Examiners deduct up to 1 full mark if these are used interchangeably. Always state explicitly which applies: repairable (MTBF) or non-repairable (MTTF).
> 2. **Forgetting the memoryless property** — When asked about conditional probabilities in Phase II, many students incorrectly compute $R(1000) - R(500)$ instead of $1 - R(1000)/R(500)$. This loses 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Not labeling diagrams** — A bathtub curve sketch without axis labels ($\lambda(t)$ and $t$) and region names is considered incomplete. Always label all three regions and the axes.
> 4. **Applying exponential distribution to all three phases** — The exponential distribution is valid **only** in Phase II. Using it for Phases I and III shows lack of conceptual understanding.
> 5. **Confusing "failure rate" with "failure probability"** — These are different. Failure rate $\lambda(t)$ has units of $\text{time}^{-1}$, while $F(t)$ is dimensionless. Examiners check units carefully.
> 6. **Not mentioning the Weibull distribution** — When discussing Phases I and III, always mention that the Weibull distribution (with appropriate $\beta$) is the appropriate model. This demonstrates depth of understanding.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bathtub Curve** plots failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time $t$ and has three regions: Infant Mortality, Useful Life, and Wear-out.
- **Phase I (Infant Mortality):** Decreasing failure rate; failures due to **manufacturing defects**; managed by **burn-in testing**; modeled by **Weibull with $\beta < 1$**.
- **Phase II (Useful Life):** **Constant** failure rate; failures are **random**; managed by **corrective maintenance**; modeled by **exponential distribution** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- **Phase III (Wear-out):** **Increasing** failure rate; failures due to **fatigue, corrosion, aging**; managed by **preventive replacement**; modeled by **Weibull with $\beta > 1$**.
- **Key Equations to Memorize:**
  - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (reliability in Phase II)
  - $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ (cumulative failure probability)
  - $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ (mean life in Phase II)
  - $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ (general hazard rate)
  - $R(t) = \exp\left( -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du \right)$ (general reliability)
  - $P(t_1 \le T \le t_2 \mid T > t_1) = 1 - \dfrac{R(t_2)}{R(t_1)}$ (conditional failure probability)
- **MTBF is for repairable systems; MTTF is for non-repairable components** — never interchange these.
- The **memoryless property** of the exponential distribution means the future failure probability depends only on the remaining interval length, not on past operating time.
- The **minimum point of the bathtub curve** defines the most reliable operating window and is the target of industrial maintenance planning.
- **Burn-in tests** screen out Phase I failures before customer delivery; **preventive replacement** pre-empts Phase III catastrophic failures.
- Standard unit for failure rate in industry: **FPMH (Failures Per Million Hours)** or its modern equivalent **FIT (Failures In Time, per $10^9$ device-hours)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy for KTU 2024:** For 3-mark questions, define the bathtub curve and list its three phases with one-line descriptions. For 7-mark questions, add the dominant failure mechanism, mathematical model, and recommended maintenance strategy for each phase. For 14-mark numerical questions, always show the substitution of values into the reliability formula step-by-step and clearly state units in the final answer. Examiners reward clarity, units, and labels heavily.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Derivation of the Reliability Function

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability (R(t))** is defined as the *probability that a system, component, or item will perform its intended function satisfactorily under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time, denoted `t`*.

Mathematically, if `T` is a continuous non-negative random variable representing the *time-to-failure* of a component, then the **Reliability Function** is:

$$R(t) = P(T > t), \quad t \geq 0$$

The complementary function is the **Unreliability Function** (or Cumulative Distribution Function of failure), denoted `F(t)`:

$$F(t) = P(T \leq t) = 1 - R(t)$$

Boundary conditions that every reliability function must satisfy:

- $R(0) = 1$ (the item is initially functional with probability 1)
- $R(\infty) = 0$ (eventually, every item fails with certainty)
- $R(t)$ is a **monotonically non-increasing** function of time
- $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Bulb Intuition"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you purchase **100 identical LED bulbs** rated for 50,000 hours. You switch them all on simultaneously. Out of these 100 bulbs, suppose **82 bulbs are still glowing after 10,000 hours**. Then, empirically, the reliability of a bulb at `t = 10,000` hours is `R(10000) = 82/100 = 0.82`. As time progresses, the count of surviving bulbs will keep dropping, and the curve of survivors versus time — *that curve is the Reliability Function R(t)*.

This everyday intuition captures the essence of reliability engineering:
- It is a **probability statement** (not a guarantee).
- It is **time-dependent** (reliability at `t = 1` hour ≠ reliability at `t = 10000` hours).
- It is **statistical in nature** — derived from a population of identical items.

### 1.3 Standard Reliability Metrics (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Symbol | Metric | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **R(t)** | Reliability Function | Probability of survival up to time `t` |
| **F(t)** | Cumulative Failure Probability (CDF) | Probability of failure by time `t` |
| **f(t)** | Failure Density Function (PDF) | Instantaneous failure probability density |
| **λ(t)** | Hazard / Failure Rate Function | Conditional rate of failure at time `t` |
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure | Expected life for non-repairable items |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | Expected uptime for repairable items |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, always remember: **MTTF** is used for *non-repairable* items (e.g., a light bulb, a resistor), whereas **MTBF** is used for *repairable* systems (e.g., a CNC machine, a server).

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Probability Framework

The derivation rests on three foundational functions that fully describe the random variable `T` (time-to-failure):

### 2.1.1 Failure Density Function `f(t)`

This is the probability density of failure occurring in the infinitesimally small interval `[t, t+dt]`:

$$f(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t)}{\Delta t}$$

It has units of **(1/time)**, typically *per hour* or *per million hours*.

### 2.1.2 Cumulative Failure Function `F(t)`

The probability that failure has occurred *on or before* time `t`:

$$F(t) = P(T \leq t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(x) \, dx$$

### 2.1.3 Reliability Function `R(t)`

The probability that the item is *still functioning* at time `t`:

$$R(t) = P(T > t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx$$

Since the item must either survive past `t` or fail on or before `t`, and these are mutually exclusive exhaustive events:

$$R(t) + F(t) = 1$$

Differentiating `F(t)` with respect to `t` recovers the density:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

This is the **fundamental differential relationship** between `R(t)` and `f(t)`.

---

## 2.2 The Concept of Hazard Rate (Failure Rate) `λ(t)`

The **hazard rate** answers a vital engineering question: *"Given that the item has already survived up to time `t`, what is the *instantaneous* rate of failure now?"*

Formally, it is the conditional probability density of failure at time `t`, given survival up to `t`:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The hazard rate is the **ratio of the failure density to the surviving fraction**. This single ratio is the gateway from `R(t)` to all standard reliability distributions (Exponential, Weibull, etc.).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | Definition of reliability | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ | Complement rule | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(x)\, dx$ | Cumulative failure via density | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $R(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx$ | Reliability via density tail | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Density as negative slope of `R(t)` | 1/time |
| 6 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)}\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Hazard rate definition | 1/time (FITs, %/1000h) |
| 7 | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\, dx\right)$ | **General Reliability Function** | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential (constant failure rate) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$ | Weibull (2-parameter) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ | Mean Time To Failure | time |
| 11 | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | Exponential MTTF | time |
| 12 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | For repairable systems | time |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize rows 5, 6, and 7. Nearly every 14-mark KTU question on this topic reduces to these three identities.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Reliability Function is the *backbone* of:

- **Aerospace & Defence:** Predicting mission success of satellites (where `R(t)` must be > 0.999 over 15-year orbits).
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Calculating warranty reserves and field-failure budgets using Weibull-fitted data.
- **Power Plants:** Justifying preventive maintenance schedules for turbines and transformers.
- **Medical Devices:** Certifying pacemakers and infusion pumps where failure is life-critical.
- **Automotive Industry:** Setting recall thresholds (typically when `R(t)` drops below 0.95 over warranty period).

The derived general form $R(t) = e^{-\int \lambda(t)dt}$ is **distribution-free** — it lets engineers plug in *any* empirical or theoretical hazard curve (constant, linearly increasing, bathtub-shaped) and obtain `R(t)` analytically.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation of the Reliability Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation is the **core KTU board examination content**. Master every step and you can solve any numerical problem on this topic.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Part A: From Failure Density to Reliability Function

**Given:** Let `T` be a continuous random variable representing the time-to-failure of a component. Let `f(t)` be its probability density function.

**To Prove:** $R(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx$

**Proof:**

**Step 1.** By definition of a probability density function, the probability that `T` lies in the interval `[a, b]` is:

$$P(a \leq T \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx$$

**Step 2.** The reliability `R(t)` is the probability that the component survives *beyond* time `t`. Survival beyond `t` means `T > t`. Therefore:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

**Step 3.** Since the component must fail at *some* finite time (assume unbounded support for full derivation), `T` lies in `[0, \infty)`. The probability of survival beyond `t` equals the area under `f(x)` from `t` to infinity:

$$R(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx$$

**Step 4.** Since the total area under any PDF is 1:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$$

**Step 5.** Splitting the integral:

$$\int_{0}^{t} f(x) \, dx + \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$$

**Step 6.** Recognizing that $\int_{0}^{t} f(x) \, dx = F(t)$ (cumulative failure probability) and $\int_{t}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = R(t)$:

$$\boxed{F(t) + R(t) = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R(t) = 1 - F(t) = 1 - \int_{0}^{t} f(x) \, dx}$$

This completes the first half of the derivation. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Part B: From Hazard Rate to Reliability Function (The Master Derivation)

**Given:** Hazard rate function $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$.

**To Prove:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx\right)$

**Proof:**

**Step 1.** Start from the definition of hazard rate:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ (from the relation derived in Part A):

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \left[-\frac{dR(t)}{dt}\right]$$

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3.** Cross-multiply by separating the variables `R(t)` and `t`:

$$\lambda(t) \, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4.** Integrate both sides from `0` to `t`. Note that $R(0) = 1$ (initial condition — item starts working with probability 1):

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx = -\int_{R(0)=1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R}$$

**Step 5.** Evaluate the right-hand integral:

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx = -\Big[\ln R\Big]_{1}^{R(t)} = -\left[\ln R(t) - \ln 1\right]$$

**Step 6.** Since $\ln 1 = 0$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 7.** Multiply both sides by `-1`:

$$-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx = \ln R(t)$$

**Step 8.** Exponentiate both sides:

$$e^{\ln R(t)} = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx}$$

**Step 9.** Since $e^{\ln R(t)} = R(t)$:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx\right)}$$

This is the **most general closed-form expression of the Reliability Function** in terms of the hazard rate. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Special Case Derivation — The Exponential Reliability Function

The exponential distribution arises when $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ is **constant** (i.e., the component has no memory — past usage does not affect future failure probability; this is the "useful life" region of the bathtub curve).

**Starting from the master result:**

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx\right)$$

**Substitute** $\lambda(x) = \lambda$ (constant):

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda \, dx\right) = \exp\!\left(-\lambda \big[x\big]_{0}^{t}\right) = \exp(-\lambda t)$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

**Derivation of MTTF for exponential:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

---

## 3.4 Summary Matrix of Identities

| Starting Point | Mathematical Manipulation | Resulting Identity |
|---|---|---|
| $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ | Differentiate | $f(t) = -R'(t)$ |
| $f(t) = -R'(t)$ | Divide by `R(t)` | $\lambda(t) = -\dfrac{R'(t)}{R(t)}$ |
| $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Separate variables, integrate | $R(t) = e^{-\int \lambda dt}$ |
| $\lambda = \text{const}$ | Substitute | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ |
| $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Integrate from 0 to ∞ | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoryless Property of the Exponential Distribution:** $P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$. This means a 5-year-old bulb that has not yet failed has the *same* future reliability as a brand-new bulb. This is unique to the exponential and has deep KTU examination implications.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Relationship Block Diagram — The Reliability Function Family

The following block diagram maps the complete mathematical dependency between the four fundamental reliability functions. This is the conceptual "engine room" of Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Random Variable T time to failure"] --> B["Failure Density f of t"]
    A --> C["Cumulative Failure F of t"]
    A --> D["Reliability R of t"]
    A --> E["Hazard Rate lambda of t"]

    B -- "integral from 0 to t" --> C
    C -- "1 minus F of t" --> D
    B -- "integral from t to infinity" --> D
    B -- "derivative of F" --> C
    D -- "negative derivative" --> B
    E -- "f of t divided by R of t" --> B
    D -- "exponent of minus integral lambda dt" --> E

    classDef def fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d47a1
    class A,B,C,D,E def
```

**Interpretation of the diagram (read top → bottom):**

1. The random variable `T` is the *source*. All other functions are derived from it.
2. `f(t)` and `R(t)` are the two **primary** functions (one is the negative derivative of the other).
3. `F(t)` and `λ(t)` are **secondary** (ratios/integrals of the primary pair).
4. The arrow `D → E` (with the `e^{-∫λdt}` label) is the **master derivation result**.

---

## 4.2 The Bathtub Curve — Hazard Rate vs Time

The hazard rate `λ(t)` is rarely constant in real life. The classical "bathtub curve" describes how it varies across an item's life:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STAGE1["STAGE I: Infant Mortality Region"]
        S1["Burn-in failures due to manufacturing defects, weak components, poor quality control. Hazard rate is HIGH but DECREASING with time. Reliability drops quickly."]
    end

    subgraph STAGE2["STAGE II: Useful Life Region"]
        S2["Random failures due to chance overloads or environmental stress. Hazard rate is approximately CONSTANT and LOW. Failure times follow EXPONENTIAL distribution. This is the operating region for most engineered components."]
    end

    subgraph STAGE3["STAGE III: Wear-out Region"]
        S3["Wear-out failures due to fatigue, corrosion, erosion, aging. Hazard rate is INCREASING rapidly. Reliability drops sharply toward zero. Weibull distribution is most appropriate here."]
    end

    STAGE1 --> STAGE2
    STAGE2 --> STAGE3

    classDef burnin fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#b71c1c
    classDef useful fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20
    classDef wearout fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#e65100
    class S1 burnin
    class S2 useful
    class S3 wearout
```

**Engineering implications:**

| Stage | Engineering Action | Distribution Used |
|---|---|---|
| I — Infant Mortality | **Burn-in screening** at factory to eliminate weak units | Decreasing hazard |
| II — Useful Life | **Preventive replacement** before wear-out begins; allow run-to-failure | **Exponential** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ |
| III — Wear-out | **Scheduled overhaul/replacement** based on Weibull parameters | **Weibull** $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ |

---

## 4.3 Reliability Allocation Flow — Series vs Parallel Systems

Although Module 3 focuses on the single-component derivation, the function `R(t)` extends naturally to multi-component systems:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SERIES["Series System weakest link"]
        direction LR
        SA["Component A R sub A of t"] -- "must both work" --> SR["System Reliability R sub sys"]
        SB["Component B R sub B of t"] -- "must both work" --> SR
    end

    subgraph PARALLEL["Parallel System redundant"]
        direction LR
        PA["Component A R sub A of t"] -- "at least one works" --> PR["System Reliability R sub sys"]
        PB["Component B R sub B of t"] -- "at least one works" --> PR
    end

    SERIES -.- F1["R sub sys of t equals R sub A times R sub B"]
    PARALLEL -.- F2["R sub sys of t equals 1 minus quantity 1 minus R sub A times 1 minus R sub B"]
```

**Key formulas (KTU syllabus extension):**

$$R_{\text{series}}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

$$R_{\text{parallel}}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[1 - R_i(t)\right]$$

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU numericals:** In a 2-component parallel system with $R_A(t) = 0.9$ and $R_B(t) = 0.8$:
> $R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.9)(1 - 0.8) = 1 - 0.1 \times 0.2 = 0.98$. Parallel redundancy always improves reliability.
