# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Reliability Function R(t). State any two of its mathematical properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Reliability Function** $R(t)$ is the probability that a component or system performs its intended function satisfactorily under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time `t`. If `T` is the random variable representing time-to-failure:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

**Two mathematical properties (any two):**

1. **Boundary conditions:** $R(0) = 1$ and $R(\infty) = 0$.
2. **Monotonicity:** $R(t)$ is a non-increasing function of time, i.e., $\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt} \leq 0$ for all $t \geq 0$.
3. **Range:** $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$.
4. **Complementarity:** $R(t) + F(t) = 1$, where $F(t)$ is the cumulative failure probability.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 0.5 marks each for the two properties, 1 mark for the equation.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between MTTF and MTBF. Under what condition are they numerically equal?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MTTF | MTBF |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | Mean Time To Failure | Mean Time Between Failures |
| Applicable to | **Non-repairable** items (resistor, bulb, capacitor) | **Repairable** systems (CNC machine, server) |
| Includes repair time? | No | Yes (MTBF = MTTF + MTTR) |

**Condition for equality:** When the **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** is negligible compared to MTTF, i.e., $MTTR \ll MTTF$. In practice, when MTTR → 0, MTBF ≈ MTTF. This is often assumed for electronic components with quick swap-repair policies.

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**
> - ❌ Forgetting the **initial condition** $R(0) = 1$ when integrating (loses 2 marks).
> - ❌ Confusing `λ(t)` (hazard rate, units 1/time) with `f(t)` (density, units 1/time) — they are *different* functions.
> - ❌ Writing $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ but failing to *show* how $F(t) = \int f(t)dt$ is used — derivations need explicit steps.
> - ❌ Skipping the step $f(t) = -dR/dt$ in the master derivation (board examiners specifically look for this).
> - ❌ Confusing MTTF (integral of `R(t)` from 0 to ∞) with MTBF (sum of uptimes + repair times).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the definition of the hazard rate function, derive the general expression for the Reliability Function:

$$R(t) = \exp\left[-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx\right]$$

State and justify the initial condition used in the integration.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a complex electronic system, the hazard rate is observed to follow:

$$\lambda(t) = 0.002 \, t \quad \text{(per hour, for } t \geq 0\text{)}$$

Compute:
- (i) The reliability at `t = 100 hours`.
- (ii) The MTTF of the system.
- (iii) The probability that the system survives its first 200 hours given it has already survived 100 hours.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the hazard rate. By definition:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Cross-multiply and separate variables:

$$\lambda(t) \, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Integrate both sides from `0` to `t`. The lower limit of integration for `R` is $R(0) = 1$ (initial condition: a brand-new, non-defective component is functional with probability 1):

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx = -\int_{R(0)=1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -[\ln R]_{1}^{R(t)}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Evaluate the right side:

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx = -\ln R(t) + \ln 1 = -\ln R(t) \quad (\text{since } \ln 1 = 0)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Solve for `R(t)`:

$$\ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx$$

$$R(t) = \exp\left[-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx\right]$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Justification of initial condition: $R(0) = 1$ because at time `t = 0`, the item has just been put into service and is assumed to be working (no failures have occurred yet), so the probability of survival at `t = 0` is unity.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $\lambda(t) = 0.002 \, t$ per hour.

**(i) Reliability at `t = 100` hours [3 marks]:**

**Step 1:** Compute the cumulative hazard:

$$\int_{0}^{100} \lambda(x) \, dx = \int_{0}^{100} 0.002 \, x \, dx = 0.002 \cdot \left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{100} = 0.002 \cdot 5000 = 10$$

**Step 2:** Apply the master formula:

$$R(100) = e^{-10} = 4.54 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Interpretation:** The system is extremely unreliable after 100 hours because the linearly increasing hazard has accumulated a very high cumulative hazard.

---

**(ii) MTTF [2 marks]:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\int_{0}^{t} 0.002 x \, dx} \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-0.001 t^2} \, dt$$

Using the standard Gaussian integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-a t^2} \, dt = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2\sqrt{a}}$ with $a = 0.001$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2\sqrt{0.001}} = \frac{1.7725}{2 \times 0.03162} \approx \frac{1.7725}{0.06325} \approx 28.0 \text{ hours}$$

---

**(iii) Conditional reliability [2 marks]:**

$$P(T > 200 \mid T > 100) = \frac{R(200)}{R(100)}$$

**Compute $R(200)$:**

$$\int_{0}^{200} 0.002 x \, dx = 0.002 \cdot \frac{200^2}{2} = 0.002 \cdot 20000 = 40$$

$$R(200) = e^{-40} \approx 4.25 \times 10^{-18}$$

**Compute the ratio:**

$$P(T > 200 \mid T > 100) = \frac{e^{-40}}{e^{-10}} = e^{-30} \approx 9.36 \times 10^{-14}$$

**Interpretation:** Effectively zero — the system is guaranteed to fail within the next 100 hours.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following terms with mathematical expressions:
- (i) Failure density function `f(t)`
- (ii) Cumulative failure function `F(t)`
- (iii) Hazard rate `λ(t)`

Show that $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ and that $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A machine has a constant failure rate of `λ = 0.0005` per hour.
- (i) Find the reliability for a 1000-hour mission.
- (ii) Calculate the MTTF.
- (iii) If a parallel redundant identical machine is added, what is the new system reliability at 1000 hours?
- (iv) Comment briefly on the engineering trade-off involved.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**(i) Failure density function [1.5 marks]:**

$$f(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t)}{\Delta t}$$

Units: failures per unit time.

**(ii) Cumulative failure function [1.5 marks]:**

$$F(t) = P(T \leq t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(x) \, dx$$

**(iii) Hazard rate [1.5 marks]:**

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Proof of $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ [1 mark]:**

Since "survival beyond `t`" and "failure on or before `t`" are complementary events:

$$P(T > t) + P(T \leq t) = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R(t) + F(t) = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R(t) = 1 - F(t)$$

**Proof of $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$ [1.5 marks]:**

Differentiating $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ with respect to `t`:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -f(t) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.0005$ per hour (constant → exponential distribution).

**(i) Reliability for 1000-hour mission [2 marks]:**

$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0005 \times 1000} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**(ii) MTTF [1.5 marks]:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Parallel redundant system reliability [2 marks]:**

For 2 identical components in parallel:

$$R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 1 - (1 - 0.6065)^2 = 1 - (0.3935)^2 = 1 - 0.1548 = 0.8452$$

**(iv) Engineering trade-off [1.5 marks]:**

- **Benefit:** Reliability rises from `0.6065` to `0.8452`, a ~39% improvement.
- **Cost:** Doubling the number of machines means doubled capital cost, doubled maintenance cost, doubled floor space, and possibly doubled operating energy.
- **Engineering decision:** The redundancy is justified only if the cost of failure (downtime, lost production, safety hazard) exceeds the cost of the redundant machine. For mission-critical applications (avionics, medical, nuclear), redundancy is mandatory; for consumer goods, single-component design is preferred.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Reliability Function Derivation**

- ✅ **Master Definition:** $R(t) = P(T > t)$, where `T` is the time-to-failure random variable.
- ✅ **Boundary Conditions:** $R(0) = 1$, $R(\infty) = 0$, $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$.
- ✅ **Complement Rule:** $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ — the most used identity in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Density-Reliability Link:** $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ — the *single most important* differential relationship.
- ✅ **Hazard Rate Definition:** $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ — a *conditional* rate, not an unconditional probability.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x) \, dx\right)$ — memorize this verbatim.
- ✅ **Initial Condition:** Always use $R(0) = 1$ when solving the differential equation.
- ✅ **Exponential Special Case:** When $\lambda$ is constant, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$.
- ✅ **MTTF Formula:** $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ — works for *any* distribution, not just exponential.
- ✅ **Memoryless Property:** Exponential is the *only* continuous distribution with memorylessness — favorite 2-mark question.
- ✅ **System Formulas:** Series → multiply; Parallel → $1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$.
- ✅ **Units Vigilance:** `R(t)` is dimensionless, `f(t)` is 1/time, `λ(t)` is 1/time (often in %/1000h or FITs = failures per $10^9$ hours).
- ✅ **Bathtub Curve Awareness:** Be ready to explain Infant Mortality (burn-in), Useful Life (exponential regime), and Wear-out (Weibull regime) in one sentence each.
- ✅ **Conditional Reliability:** $P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = \dfrac{R(s + t)}{R(s)}$ — frequently tested in 7-mark problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure and Failure Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Failure and Failure Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Failure

In the context of Industrial Engineering and Reliability Engineering, **Failure** is formally defined as the **inability of a system, component, or process to perform its intended function or to meet the desired performance standards** under stated conditions for a specified period of time.

A **Failure Mode**, on the other hand, is the **specific manner or mechanism by which a failure is observed** — essentially, *how* the failure manifests itself physically, functionally, or chemically. While "failure" tells us *that* something has stopped working, the "failure mode" tells us *why* and *how* it stopped working.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes distinguishing between **system failure** (complete breakdown) and **partial failure** (degraded performance), and identifying the various **physical, electrical, and human-induced modes** through which failures occur in industrial systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Definition (per ISO 9000 / IEC 60050):** Failure is the *termination of the ability of an item to perform a required function*. The "item" may be a machine, a printed circuit board, a software module, or an entire production line.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a brand new ceiling fan installed in your home. On day 1, it works perfectly. After about 6 months, the bearing oil dries up and the motor starts making a grinding noise — this is **early-life failure (infant mortality)**. By year 3, the fan runs smoothly every day, with only rare breakdowns — this is the **useful life** phase. By year 8, the winding insulation cracks and the fan finally burns out — this is **wear-out failure**.

Now, the *manner* in which it failed in year 8 (insulation cracking due to heat) is the **failure mode**. The fact that it has *stopped functioning* is the **failure** itself. The bathtub curve below beautifully captures this lifecycle.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Memory Aid:** 
> - **Failure** = The "WHAT happened" (system stopped)
> - **Failure Mode** = The "HOW it happened" (bearing seized, wire broke, capacitor leaked)

## 1.3 Categories of Failure in Industrial Systems

| S.No. | Category | Description | Common Industrial Example |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Catastrophic Failure** | Sudden, total cessation of function | Shaft fracture, transformer burn-out |
| 2 | **Degradation Failure** | Gradual decline in performance over time | Tool wear, sensor drift, pump efficiency loss |
| 3 | **System Failure** | Failure of the entire integrated system | Plant-wide power outage |
| 4 | **Partial Failure** | Component fails but system continues at reduced capacity | One of three pumps in parallel fails |
| 5 | **Human-Induced Failure** | Caused by operator error, poor maintenance | Improper lubrication, wrong part installation |

## 1.4 Key Reliability Metrics (Standard Terms)

The following standard metrics will be used throughout the KTU 2024 examination:

- **Reliability R(t)**: The probability that a component performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified time interval $[0, t]$.
- **Failure Rate (Hazard Rate) $\lambda(t)$**: The number of failures per unit time at time $t$, given survival up to time $t$. Expressed in **failures per hour** or **FIT (Failures In Time = 10⁻⁹ failures/hour)**.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**: Average time between two consecutive failures for a repairable system.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)**: Average time to first failure for a non-repairable component.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**: Average time taken to restore a failed system to working condition.
- **Availability A**: Fraction of time a system is operationally ready. $A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bathtub Curve — Conceptual Breakdown

The **Bathtub Curve** is the foundational graphical model that describes how the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ of a typical industrial component/system varies over its operational lifetime. It consists of three distinct phases:

### Phase I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in / Early Life)
- **Failure behavior:** Failure rate is **high but decreasing**.
- **Causes:** Manufacturing defects, poor quality control, installation errors, substandard materials, workmanship flaws.
- **Why it happens:** Defective units fail early; those that survive this phase are typically "good" units.
- **Industrial practice:** Companies often run new equipment through a **burn-in test** (running it for 48–168 hours under load) to weed out weak units before shipping.

### Phase II — Useful Life (Normal Operating Period)
- **Failure behavior:** Failure rate is **constant** and **low** — this is the most desirable phase.
- **Causes:** Random failures caused by unpredictable external stresses (overload, power surge, accidental damage, environmental factors).
- **Mathematical model:** Failure rate $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant). This is the most mathematically tractable region and forms the basis of most reliability calculations in KTU problems.

### Phase III — Wear-out Phase
- **Failure behavior:** Failure rate is **increasing** (often exponentially).
- **Causes:** Mechanical wear, fatigue, corrosion, creep, aging of materials, accumulated stress damage.
- **Industrial practice:** **Preventive maintenance** is scheduled before this phase to extend useful life. Components are replaced based on **P-F interval** (Potential Failure to Functional Failure interval) analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The bathtub curve is *not* a law of nature — it is a heuristic model. Modern electronics with no moving parts often show **no wear-out phase** (only constant or even decreasing failure rate). However, mechanical systems in factories (motors, bearings, pumps) follow it very closely.

## 2.2 Probability Foundations of Reliability

The reliability function is defined as:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

where $T$ is the random variable representing the time-to-failure, and $t$ is the specified mission time.

The **cumulative distribution function (CDF)** of failure, also called the **unreliability function** $F(t)$, is:

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = P(T \le t)$$

The **probability density function (PDF)** of failure is:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key relationship:** $\int_0^{\infty} f(t)\, dt = 1$ — the area under the PDF is unity, since a component must fail with probability 1 over infinite time.

## 2.3 The Hazard Function (Failure Rate Function)

The **conditional failure rate** (also called the **hazard rate** or **instantaneous failure rate**) is defined as:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \le t + \Delta t \,\vert\, T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

The relationship between reliability and the hazard function is:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\, du\right)$$

For the **useful life phase** (Phase II), $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant), and this simplifies to the famous **exponential reliability formula**:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the most heavily tested formula in KTU examinations on this topic.

## 2.4 Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) and MTBF

For the exponential distribution (constant failure rate), the **expected value** of time-to-failure is:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_0^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

Substituting $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

For **repairable systems**, the same formula gives the **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**:

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

## 2.5 Availability of a Repairable System

A repairable system alternates between **operational (uptime)** and **repair (downtime)** states. **Inherent Availability** $A_i$ is given by:

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Achieved Availability** $A_a$ includes preventive maintenance downtime (MTTPM):

$$A_a = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} + \text{MTTPM}}$$

**Operational Availability** $A_o$ additionally accounts for administrative and logistics delays:

$$A_o = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}}$$

where **MDT = Mean Down Time** (includes all delays).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** "MTBF is the *good* time, MTTR is the *bad* time. Availability = Good / (Good + Bad)."

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability under constant failure rate | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Cumulative failure probability | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | PDF of exponential failure | $\text{time}^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Hazard function (general) | $\text{time}^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\, du\right)$ | Reliability (general hazard) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure (exponential) | time |
| 7 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures (exponential) | time |
| 8 | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Inherent Availability | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | System reliability (series) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i(t))$ | System reliability (parallel / redundant) | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{FIT} = \lambda \times 10^{9}$ | Failures In Time (standard metric for ICs) | $\text{failures} / 10^{9}\,\text{hrs}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** The series reliability formula $R_s = \prod R_i$ is the most tested *after* the exponential formula. A common trick in the question paper: give $\lambda$ for a system of 3 components in series, ask to find reliability for 1000 hours.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic abstractions — they are the backbone of:

- **Predictive maintenance scheduling** in manufacturing plants
- **Warranty period calculation** for consumer electronics (e.g., why a 5-year warranty is statistically safe)
- **Spare parts inventory planning** (how many spares to keep in the store)
- **Safety-critical systems** in aerospace, nuclear, and medical devices
- **Six-Sigma / TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** programs
- **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** — a mandatory deliverable in automotive and aerospace quality systems (IATF 16949, AS9100)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Exponential Reliability from the Constant Failure Rate Assumption

**Given:** During the useful life phase of a component, the failure rate is constant, i.e., $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ for all $t \ge 0$.

**To Prove:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of hazard function.**

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2 — Express $f(t)$ in terms of $R(t)$.**

Since $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$, substituting:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{-R'(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into a first-order separable differential equation.**

$$-R'(t) = \lambda(t) \cdot R(t)$$

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t)\, dt$$

**Step 4 — Apply the constant failure rate condition.**

Since $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant):

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda\, dt$$

**Step 5 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.**

$$\int_0^t \frac{dR(u)}{R(u)} = \int_0^t -\lambda\, du$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integrals.**

The left side is $\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R(t)}{R(0)}\right)$, and the right side is $-\lambda t$.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{R(t)}{R(0)}\right) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 7 — Apply the initial condition $R(0) = 1$** (a brand new component has 100% reliability at $t = 0$).

$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 8 — Exponentiate both sides to obtain the result.**

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: MTTF for the Exponential Distribution

**Given:** PDF of failure time is $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ for $t \ge 0$.

**To Prove:** $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$

**Step 1 — Definition of expected value.**

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply integration by parts** with $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$. Then $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ t \cdot (-e^{-\lambda t}) \right]_0^{\infty} - \int_0^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda t})\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the boundary term.**

As $t \to \infty$, the term $t e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ (exponential dominates). At $t = 0$, the term is $0 \cdot (-1) = 0$. So the boundary term is $0 - 0 = 0$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the remaining integral.**

$$\text{MTTF} = 0 + \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \left[ \frac{-e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} \right]_0^{\infty}$$

$$= 0 - \left(\frac{-1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Pattern Problem

**Problem:** A CNC machine in a manufacturing cell has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour. Calculate:

**(a)** The reliability of the machine for a continuous 100-hour production run.

**(b)** The MTTF of the machine.

**(c)** The probability that the machine will fail between 200 and 400 hours of operation, given it has already survived 200 hours.

**(d)** If the average repair time (MTTR) is 8 hours, calculate the inherent availability.

### Solution (a) — Reliability for 100 hours

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.** $t = 100$ hrs, $\lambda = 0.0005$ /hr.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the exponential reliability formula.**

$$R(100) = e^{-0.0005 \times 100} = e^{-0.05}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerically.**

$$R(100) = 0.9512 \quad \text{or} \quad 95.12\%$$

**Interpretation:** There is a **95.12% chance** the machine will run continuously for 100 hours without failure.

### Solution (b) — MTTF

**Step 1 — Apply the formula $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the machine will run for **2000 hours** before its first failure.

### Solution (c) — Conditional Probability of Failure

**Step 1 — Compute reliability at 200 hours and 400 hours.**

$$R(200) = e^{-0.0005 \times 200} = e^{-0.1} = 0.9048$$

$$R(400) = e^{-0.0005 \times 400} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$

**Step 2 — Compute the conditional failure probability** $P(200 < T \le 400 \mid T > 200)$.

By definition of conditional probability:

$$P(200 < T \le 400 \,\vert\, T > 200) = \frac{P(200 < T \le 400)}{P(T > 200)}$$

**Step 3 — Express using $R(t)$.**

$$= \frac{R(200) - R(400)}{R(200)} = \frac{0.9048 - 0.8187}{0.9048}$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation.**

$$= \frac{0.0861}{0.9048} = 0.0952 \quad \text{or} \quad 9.52\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Notice that $0.0952 = 1 - R(200)$. This is a fundamental property of the exponential distribution — **it is memoryless**. The conditional probability of failure in the next 200 hours *does not depend* on how long the machine has already been running, as long as it has survived so far. This is *only* true in Phase II (constant failure rate).

### Solution (d) — Inherent Availability

**Step 1 — Identify the values.** $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda = 2000$ hrs, $\text{MTTR} = 8$ hrs.

**Step 2 — Apply the availability formula.**

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 8} = \frac{2000}{2008}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerically.**

$$A_i = 0.9960 \quad \text{or} \quad 99.60\%$$

**Interpretation:** The CNC machine is operationally ready **99.60% of the time** — a benchmark often required for **World-Class Manufacturing**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes Across Industrial Components

| Component | Primary Failure Mode | Physical Cause | Detection Method | Preventive Action |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Ball Bearing** | Spalling, brinelling, fatigue flaking | Cyclic Hertzian contact stress, lubrication failure | Vibration analysis (AE, FFT), acoustic emission | Periodic re-lubrication, alignment check |
| **Electric Motor Winding** | Insulation breakdown, short circuit | Thermal aging, voltage surge, moisture | Insulation resistance test (Megger), thermography | Cleaning, drying, surge protection |
| **Gear Tooth** | Pitting, scuffing, tooth fracture | Bending fatigue, surface fatigue, overload | Oil analysis (ferrography), vibration | Lube oil change, load monitoring |
| **Pump Impeller** | Cavitation erosion, wear ring seizure | NPSH drop, abrasive slurry, dry running | Pressure & flow monitoring, vibration | Maintain NPSH, filter fluid |
| **PCB / Integrated Circuit** | Solder joint fatigue, ESD damage | Thermal cycling, electrostatic discharge | Functional test, X-ray inspection | ESD-safe handling, conformal coating |
| **Hydraulic Hose** | Burst, blistering, abrasion | Pressure spike, chemical incompatibility, flex fatigue | Pressure test, visual inspection | Scheduled replacement, hose routing |
| **Cutting Tool** | Flank wear, crater wear, chipping | Heat, abrasion, built-up edge, chatter | Tool wear measurement, surface finish | Correct speed/feed, tool-grade selection |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Answers:** When asked "list the failure modes of a given component," use the format: **Mechanism → Cause → Effect → Detection → Prevention**. This structured approach fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Bathtub Curve and Failure Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["PHASE I - INFANT MORTALITY"]:::phase1
    nodeB["High decreasing failure rate"]:::detail
    nodeC["Causes: Defects, workmanship errors"]:::detail
    nodeD["Solution: Burn-in testing"]:::detail
    nodeE["PHASE II - USEFUL LIFE"]:::phase2
    nodeF["Constant low failure rate"]:::detail
    nodeG["Causes: Random external stresses"]:::detail
    nodeH["Solution: Redundancy, monitoring"]:::detail
    nodeI["PHASE III - WEAR OUT"]:::phase3
    nodeJ["Increasing failure rate"]:::detail
    nodeK["Causes: Fatigue, corrosion, wear"]:::detail
    nodeL["Solution: Preventive replacement"]:::detail
    nodeM["Failure rate axis lambda"]:::axis
    nodeN["Time axis t"]:::axis

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL

    classDef phase1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef phase2 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef phase3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef detail fill:#F7F7F7,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef axis fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Repairable System's Availability Cycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["System Powered ON"]:::state
    nodeB["OPERATIONAL STATE - MTBF accumulates"]:::good
    nodeC["Failure Event Occurs"]:::event
    nodeD["DIAGNOSE - Log fault, isolate cause"]:::step
    nodeE["REPAIR - Replace / refurbish part"]:::step
    nodeF["TEST - Verify system performance"]:::step
    nodeG["System Restored - Return to service"]:::good
    nodeH["PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE - Scheduled inspection"]:::step
    nodeI["MTTR Window Active"]:::bad
    nodeJ["MTTPM Window Active"]:::neutral

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeB
    nodeB -.Scheduled.-> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeG

    classDef state fill:#E8E8E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef good fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef bad fill:#F38181,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef neutral fill:#FCE38A,stroke:#8B7500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef step fill:#EAFFD0,stroke:#556B2F,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef event fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Reliability Block Configurations (Series and Parallel)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph S1["SERIES CONFIGURATION - R_sys = R1 x R2 x R3"]
        nodeS1A["Component 1 R1"]:::comp
        nodeS1B["Component 2 R2"]:::comp
        nodeS1C["Component 3 R3"]:::comp
        nodeS1A --> nodeS1B --> nodeS1C
    end

    subgraph S2["PARALLEL CONFIGURATION - R_sys = 1 - product of 1-Ri"]
        nodeS2A["Component 1 R1"]:::comp
        nodeS2B["Component 2 R2"]:::comp
        nodeS2C["Component 3 R3"]:::comp
        nodeS2A -.-> nodeS2B
        nodeS2A -.-> nodeS2C
        nodeS2B -.-> nodeS2C
    end

    classDef comp fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** In the **Series** configuration, ALL components must function for the system to function — a single failure brings down the whole system. In the **Parallel (Redundant)** configuration, the system survives even if one or more components fail, as long as at least one path remains operational. This is why **redundant pumps, parallel CPUs, and dual-power-supplies** are used in safety-critical industrial systems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define "Failure" and "Failure Mode" in the context of industrial engineering. Distinguish between them with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Failure:** The termination of an item's ability to perform a required function under stated conditions for a specified period of time (per ISO/IEC 60050 standards).

**Failure Mode:** The specific physical mechanism or manner in which a failure is observed — i.e., *how* the item has failed.

**Distinction:** "Failure" describes the *event/state* (system has stopped working), while "Failure Mode" describes the *mechanism* (the cause of stoppage).

**Example:** A centrifugal pump in a cooling water system fails (pump stops delivering water). The failure modes could be: (a) impeller cavitation erosion, (b) bearing seizure, (c) mechanical seal leakage, (d) motor winding burn-out. The *failure* is the same (no water delivery), but the *modes* are different.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "Failure Mode = Failure" — this is a 0-mark answer. The examiner looks for the *distinction*: failure = WHAT happened, failure mode = HOW it happened.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the "Bathtub Curve" of failure rate. Name the three phases.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bathtub Curve** is a reliability engineering graph showing the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ of a typical industrial component as a function of time $t$. Its shape resembles a bathtub in cross-section.

**The three phases are:**

1. **Phase I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** Failure rate is high but decreasing. Caused by manufacturing defects and poor workmanship. Mitigated by *burn-in testing*.

2. **Phase II — Useful Life:** Failure rate is low and constant. Caused by random, unpredictable external stresses. Mitigated by *redundancy and condition monitoring*.

3. **Phase III — Wear-out:** Failure rate increases sharply. Caused by aging, fatigue, corrosion, and wear. Mitigated by *preventive replacement* before the wear-out phase begins.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently label Phase I as "early failure" but forget to mention that the failure rate is *decreasing* in Phase I and *increasing* in Phase III. The trend (decreasing/constant/increasing) is the most-scored part of the answer.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Define Reliability $R(t)$, Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$, MTTF, MTBF, and MTTR. State the relationship between them for a system with constant failure rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic press in a production line has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.002$ failures per hour. Calculate:
  - (i) The reliability of the press for a 50-hour continuous operation. **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) The MTTF of the press. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) The probability that the press will fail between 100 and 200 hours of operation. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Reliability $R(t)$:** The probability that a component performs its intended function under stated conditions for a time period $[0, t]$.
$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

**Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$:** The conditional instantaneous rate of failure at time $t$, given survival up to time $t$.
$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** The expected (average) time to first failure for a non-repairable component. For exponential distribution: $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$.

**MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The expected time between two consecutive failures of a *repairable* system. For exponential distribution: $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$.

**MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The expected time required to restore a failed system to operational state, including diagnosis, repair, and re-test.

**Relationship under constant failure rate:**

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \text{MTTF} = \text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.002$ failures/hour.

**(i) Reliability for 50 hours:**

$$R(50) = e^{-0.002 \times 50} = e^{-0.1} = 0.9048$$

The press has a **90.48% probability** of completing 50 hours of operation without failure.

**(ii) MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.002} = 500 \text{ hours}$$

On average, the press will run for **500 hours** before its first failure.

**(iii) Failure probability between 100 and 200 hours:**

$$P(100 < T \le 200) = F(200) - F(100) = R(100) - R(200)$$

Compute each:

$$R(100) = e^{-0.002 \times 100} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$

$$R(200) = e^{-0.002 \times 200} = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$$

$$P(100 < T \le 200) = 0.8187 - 0.6703 = 0.1484$$

There is a **14.84% probability** the press will fail in the interval (100, 200] hours.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** What is the "Bathtub Curve"? Explain its three phases with reference to industrial examples. What is the engineering significance of the constant failure rate phase? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC turning center has MTBF = 1500 hours and MTTR = 10 hours. Calculate:
  - (i) The failure rate of the machine. **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) The inherent availability. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) The reliability for a 200-hour production shift, assuming constant failure rate. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The **Bathtub Curve** plots the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus operating time $t$. It is divided into three phases:

**Phase I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in / Debugging Phase):**
- Failure rate: high but **decreasing**.
- Causes: manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, improper installation, substandard materials.
- Industrial example: A new batch of laptop batteries; a small percentage fails within the first week due to defective cells. Companies run **burn-in tests** (continuous charging/discharging for 24–72 hours) to weed out defective units before sale.

**Phase II — Useful Life (Normal Operation):**
- Failure rate: low and **constant** at $\lambda$.
- Causes: random, unpredictable, external events — power surge, operator error, accidental damage.
- Industrial example: A well-designed electric motor running for 3–8 years with proper maintenance. Failures during this period are essentially **memoryless** (exponential distribution applies), and reliability is given by $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Phase III — Wear-out:**
- Failure rate: **increasing**, often rapidly.
- Causes: mechanical wear, metal fatigue, corrosion, creep, insulation aging, lubrication breakdown.
- Industrial example: Rolling-element bearings in industrial gearboxes — after rated L10 life, pitting and spalling occur at increasing rates.

**Engineering significance of the constant failure rate phase:**

This is the *most economically productive* phase of the equipment's life. During this phase, the exponential distribution applies, and we can apply powerful mathematical tools (MTBF, MTTF, series/parallel reliability formulas) for:
- Warranty period design
- Spare parts inventory planning
- Production scheduling and OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) calculations
- Cost optimization of preventive vs. corrective maintenance

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $\text{MTBF} = 1500$ hrs, $\text{MTTR} = 10$ hrs.

**(i) Failure rate:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{1500} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hr}$$

**(ii) Inherent availability:**

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{1500}{1500 + 10} = \frac{1500}{1510} = 0.9934$$

The CNC turning center is operational **99.34%** of the time.

**(iii) Reliability for 200-hour shift:**

$$R(200) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(1/1500) \times 200} = e^{-0.1333} = 0.8752$$

There is an **87.52% probability** the CNC turning center will operate without failure for the full 200-hour production shift.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** When failure rate is given in *failures per million hours* (FITS = 0.001%/1000 hr) vs. *failures per hour*, students often forget to convert. Always check units first.
> 2. **Series vs. parallel confusion:** For a system of 3 components with $R_1, R_2, R_3$, students often multiply for parallel — WRONG. Series uses multiplication; parallel uses $1 - \prod(1-R_i)$.
> 3. **The "memoryless" property trap:** A common exam question gives $\lambda$ and asks for $P(T > 200 \mid T > 100)$. Many students compute $1 - R(200)$ — but the correct answer is $R(100) = e^{-100\lambda}$ due to memoryless property. Failing to recognize this is a 3-mark loss.
> 4. **MTBF vs. MTTF confusion:** MTTF is for *non-repairable* components (light bulbs, capacitors, ICs); MTBF is for *repairable* systems (machines, vehicles). The distinction is worth 1–2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Failure** = termination of function. **Failure Mode** = the mechanism (how) of failure.
- [x] The **Bathtub Curve** has three phases: Infant Mortality (decreasing $\lambda$) → Useful Life (constant $\lambda$) → Wear-out (increasing $\lambda$).
- [x] **Reliability $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$** is valid ONLY in the constant failure rate (Phase II) region.
- [x] **MTTF = MTBF = 1/$\lambda$** for exponential distribution.
- [x] **Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)** — measures operational readiness of repairable systems.
- [x] The exponential distribution is **memoryless** — past reliability does not affect future reliability.
- [x] **Series system** reliability: $R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times \dots \times R_n$ (all must work).
- [x] **Parallel (redundant) system** reliability: $R_p = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)\dots(1 - R_n)$ (at least one must work).
- [x] **FIT** (Failures In Time) = $\lambda \times 10^9$ — standard unit for IC/component reliability.
- [x] **High reliability demands long MTBF** (e.g., $R(1000) = 0.99$ requires $\lambda \approx 0.00001$/hr, i.e., MTBF $\approx$ 100,000 hrs).
- [x] **Failure modes in mechanical systems** include wear, fatigue, corrosion, fracture, creep, buckling, and deformation.
- [x] **Failure modes in electronic systems** include ESD damage, electromigration, dielectric breakdown, solder fatigue, and thermal runaway.
- [x] **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** is a structured methodology to identify, prioritize, and mitigate potential failures — RPN = Severity $\times$ Occurrence $\times$ Detection.
- [x] **Burn-in testing** is used to eliminate Phase I failures before customer delivery.
- [x] **Preventive maintenance** is scheduled to prevent entry into Phase III.
- [x] **Condition monitoring** (vibration, thermography, oil analysis, acoustic emission) is used to detect incipient Phase III failures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** Always write the *standard formula* first, then the *given values* with units, then the *substitution step*, and finally the *numerical answer with unit and interpretation* (e.g., "reliability = 0.9048, meaning 90.48% chance of success"). Examiners reward methodical working — partial marks are given for correct formula setup even if the final numerical answer is wrong.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causes of Failures and Unreliability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Causes of Failures and Unreliability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure (KTU Standard Definition):** Failure is defined as the *inability of a system, component, or equipment to perform its intended function satisfactorily* or to meet the performance criteria for which it was designed. Failure is not limited to complete breakdown; it includes any deviation from the specified operational envelope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Unreliability (Engineering Metric):** Unreliability is the *probability that a system, component, or item will fail to perform its intended function under stated conditions, for a specified period of time*. It is the mathematical complement of Reliability.

Mathematically, if $R(t)$ represents reliability, then unreliability $Q(t)$ is expressed as:

$$Q(t) = 1 - R(t)$$

Where $R(t)$ is the probability that the system survives the interval $[0, t]$ without failure.

## 1.2 Key Reliability Metrics (KTU Standard)

The following metrics form the **core vocabulary** of failure analysis in KTU's Industrial Engineering syllabus:

| Metric | Full Form | Symbol | Unit |
|--------|-----------|--------|------|
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | $\overline{T}$ | Hours / Cycles |
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure | $\overline{T}_f$ | Hours / Cycles |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | $\overline{T}_r$ | Hours |
| **λ** | Failure Rate (Hazard Rate) | $h(t)$ | Failures / Hour |
| **R(t)** | Reliability Function | $R(t)$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Human Body**
> Think of a machine the same way you think of the **human body**:
> - A new-born infant (and a brand-new machine) is delicate → **Infant Mortality phase** (early failures)
> - A healthy adult (and a "broken-in" machine) is robust and reliable → **Useful Life phase** (random failures)
> - An elderly person (and a worn-out machine) becomes increasingly fragile → **Wear-out phase** (end-of-life failures)
>
> **Unreliability** is essentially the "probability of falling sick" at any given age. Just like a doctor studies the causes of illness (genetic, environmental, lifestyle), an Industrial Engineer studies causes of failures (design, operational, environmental).

## 1.4 The Three Phases of System Life

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Every engineered product passes through **three life phases**, collectively represented by the famous **Bathtub Curve** (discussed in Section 2).

1. **Burn-in / Infant Mortality Phase:** High but decreasing failure rate.
2. **Useful Life Phase:** Low, constant failure rate.
3. **Wear-out Phase:** Increasing failure rate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Failure Causes

Failures in industrial systems are caused by a **multitude of factors** that can be classified into four fundamental categories. KTU examiners frequently ask students to list and explain these classifications.

### 2.1.1 Design-Related Failures (Inherent Unreliability)

These failures originate from the **conception and engineering** of the product, even before it is built.

- **Poor design margins:** Insufficient safety factor against expected loads.
- **Inadequate material selection:** Wrong alloy, brittleness, low fatigue strength.
- **Design oversights:** Stress concentration points, sharp corners, miscalculated tolerances.
- **Lack of standardization:** Use of non-interchangeable parts increasing complexity.
- **Software/Hardware mismatch:** Electronics designed beyond thermal limits.

### 2.1.2 Manufacturing / Production Failures

Errors introduced **during fabrication and assembly**.

- **Defective materials:** Castings with porosity, inclusions, voids.
- **Poor workmanship:** Bad welds, loose fasteners, improper alignment.
- **Quality control lapses:** Out-of-tolerance dimensions, surface roughness issues.
- **Improper heat treatment:** Wrong hardness, residual stresses.

### 2.1.3 Operational / Usage Failures (Most Common in KTU Case Studies)

Failures caused by the **manner in which the equipment is operated**.

- **Misuse / Overloading:** Operating beyond rated capacity.
- **Improper maintenance:** Skipped lubrication, ignored warnings.
- **Human error:** Operator mistakes, poor training.
- **Lack of operating procedures:** Ad-hoc operating practices.

### 2.1.4 Environmental / External Failures

Failures induced by the **surrounding conditions**.

- **Temperature extremes:** Thermal cycling leading to fatigue.
- **Corrosion / Oxidation:** Chemical degradation of surfaces.
- **Dust, moisture, vibration:** Contamination and resonance.
- **Power fluctuations:** Surges, sags, harmonic distortion.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** A 14-mark question often asks: *"Discuss the various causes of failure in industrial equipment with suitable examples."* Always structure your answer using the above **four-bucket classification** — this is the examiner's preferred taxonomy.

## 2.2 Specific Failure Mechanisms (Engineering Physics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following failure mechanisms are **mechanism-level causes** (physical/chemical) and carry high KTU weightage in numerical and theoretical questions.

| # | Failure Mechanism | Description | Typical Example |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Fatigue Failure** | Failure under cyclic loading below ultimate strength | Rotating shafts, springs |
| 2 | **Creep Failure** | Time-dependent deformation at high temperature | Turbine blades, boiler tubes |
| 3 | **Wear Failure** | Progressive material loss due to relative motion | Bearings, gears, brake pads |
| 4 | **Corrosion Failure** | Chemical/electrochemical surface degradation | Pipes, marine structures |
| 5 | **Fracture Failure** | Brittle or ductile crack propagation | Pressure vessels |
| 6 | **Thermal Failure** | Damage from temperature extremes | Electronic components, engines |
| 7 | **Electrical Failure** | Insulation breakdown, short circuits | Transformers, motors |

### 2.2.1 The Bathtub Curve (Detailed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MOST IMPORTANT DIAGRAM IN MODULE 3** — appears in nearly every KTU board paper in some form.

The Bathtub Curve plots **failure rate $h(t)$** on the Y-axis against **time $t$** on the X-axis, revealing three distinct regions:

1. **Region I — Infant Mortality (Decreasing Failure Rate / DFR):**
   - Failures occur due to substandard components, manufacturing defects, or installation errors.
   - Failure rate **decreases** as defective items are weeded out.
   - **Counter-measure:** "Burn-in" testing, debugging, run-in periods.

2. **Region II — Useful Life (Constant Failure Rate / CFR):**
   - Failures are **random** and independent of time.
   - Follows **Exponential Distribution**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
   - **Counter-measure:** Preventive maintenance, redundancy.

3. **Region III — Wear-out (Increasing Failure Rate / IFR):**
   - Failures due to aging, wear, fatigue, corrosion.
   - Failure rate **increases** with time.
   - **Counter-measure:** Replacement scheduling, life-extension programs.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this entire table** — it covers roughly **70% of numerical problems** in this module. The symbols use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential distribution, $0 \le R(t) \le 1$ |
| 2 | Unreliability | $Q(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Cumulative failure probability |
| 3 | Failure Rate (Hazard) | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Failures per unit time |
| 4 | Exponential PDF | $f(t) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability density of failure |
| 5 | MTBF (Exponential) | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours or cycles |
| 6 | MTTF (Non-repairable) | $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ | Mean life |
| 7 | System Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| 8 | Series Reliability | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All must survive |
| 9 | Parallel Reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$ | At least one must survive |
| 10 | Weibull Distribution | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\eta$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape |

### 2.3.1 Relationship Between Reliability and Time

> [!NOTE]
> For an exponential distribution (constant failure rate region of the bathtub curve), reliability is **highest at $t = 0$** and **decays exponentially**. The probability of surviving a period equal to the MTBF is always $R(\text{MTBF}) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ or **36.8%**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The study of failure causes and unreliability is **not academic** — it directly impacts:

- **Aviation:** FAA mandates reliability analysis for every flight-critical system.
- **Nuclear Power:** Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA) requires unreliability data.
- **Manufacturing:** Six Sigma and Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) depend on failure metrics.
- **Automotive:** Warranty cost prediction uses MTBF/MTBR analysis.
- **Electronics:** Semiconductor reliability testing (HTOL, HTSL) governs chip qualification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industry Connect:** The transition from **Reactive Maintenance → Preventive Maintenance → Predictive Maintenance → Prescriptive Maintenance** is driven entirely by understanding failure causes. Modern Industry 4.0 systems use **IoT sensors + AI** to predict failures *before* they occur — the ultimate goal of failure analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reliability Function from the Exponential Distribution

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting assumption:** The failure rate $\lambda$ is **constant** in the useful-life region. This assumption is the foundation of all reliability mathematics in KTU Module 3.

### Step 1: Define the Hazard Function

The hazard function (failure rate) $h(t)$ is defined as the conditional probability of failure in the next instant, given survival up to time $t$:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t + \Delta t \mid T \ge t)}{\Delta t}$$

For the **useful life region**, we assume this rate is constant:

$$h(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$$

### Step 2: Relate Hazard Function to Reliability

The relationship between the probability density function $f(t)$, reliability $R(t)$, and hazard $h(t)$ is:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

By definition, $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ (since the PDF is the negative derivative of the survival function).

### Step 3: Substitute and Form the Differential Equation

Substituting into the hazard equation:

$$\lambda = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda \, dt$$

### Step 4: Integrate Both Sides

Integrating from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

### Step 5: Apply the Initial Condition

At $t = 0$, the system is operational with probability 1, so $R(0) = 1$.

$$\ln(R(t)) - \ln(1) = -\lambda t$$

$$\ln(R(t)) = -\lambda t$$

### Step 6: Exponentiate to Get the Final Form

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most derived equation in KTU Module 3** — understanding the constant hazard assumption is the key to unlocking every other formula.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

### Step 1: Definition of Mean

The expected (mean) time to failure is the first moment of the probability density function $f(t)$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Exponential PDF

For the exponential distribution, $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

### Step 3: Integration by Parts

Let $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, so $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ -t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \right]_0^{\infty} + \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

### Step 4: Evaluate Each Term

The first term: $\lim_{t \to \infty} (-t e^{-\lambda t}) - 0 = 0$ (by L'Hôpital's rule, exponential dominates).

The second term:

$$\int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \left[ \frac{-e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} \right]_0^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

### Step 5: Combine

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: System Availability

### Step 1: Define the Operating Cycle

In the long run, a repairable system alternates between **operational** and **failed** states.

- Average time operational = $\text{MTBF}$
- Average time failed (down) = $\text{MTTR}$

### Step 2: Define Availability

Availability $A$ is the **fraction of time** the system is operational:

$$A = \frac{\text{Time Operational}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$\boxed{A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If a CNC machine has $\text{MTBF} = 200$ hours and $\text{MTTR} = 4$ hours, then $A = 200/204 = 0.9804$ or **98.04%**. This means out of 8760 hours in a year, the machine is operational for $\approx 8586$ hours.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the standard 7-mark numerical problem format** for Module 3.

**Problem:** A machine has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.002$ failures/hour. Find:
(a) The reliability for a 100-hour mission.
(b) The MTBF.
(c) The probability of failure in 200 hours.
(d) Availability if MTTR = 5 hours.

### Solution:

**(a) Reliability for 100 hours:**

$$R(100) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.002 \times 100} = e^{-0.2}$$

$$R(100) = 0.8187 \quad \text{(or 81.87%)}$$

**(b) MTBF:**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.002} = 500 \text{ hours}$$

**(c) Probability of failure in 200 hours:**

$$Q(200) = 1 - R(200) = 1 - e^{-0.002 \times 200} = 1 - e^{-0.4}$$

$$Q(200) = 1 - 0.6703 = 0.3297 \quad \text{(or 32.97%)}$$

**(d) Availability:**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{500}{500 + 5} = \frac{500}{505}$$

$$A = 0.9901 \quad \text{(or 99.01%)}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Series System | Parallel System | Standby (k-out-of-n) |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Reliability Formula** | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | $R_p = 1 - \prod (1-R_i)$ | $R = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} p^i (1-p)^{n-i}$ |
| **Failure Behavior** | Any single failure → system failure | All must fail → system failure | At least k must work |
| **Reliability Magnitude** | **Lowest** (less than weakest link) | **Highest** (more than strongest) | **Intermediate** |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Highest | Intermediate |
| **Application** | Simple assemblies, chain links | Critical safety systems | Aircraft engines, power grids |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Bathtub Curve (KTU Most-Asked Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Start: t equals 0] --> B[Region I: Infant Mortality]
    B --> C[Region II: Useful Life]
    C --> D[Region III: Wear-out]
    D --> E[End of Useful Life]
    style A fill:#90EE90
    style E fill:#FF6B6B
```

> [!NOTE]
> The textual nodes above map to the **three regions of the bathtub curve**. The shading is conceptual: green = safe start, red = end-of-life risk.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Classification of Failure Causes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Causes of Equipment Failure] --> B[Design Related]
    A --> C[Manufacturing Related]
    A --> D[Operational Related]
```
