```
  SLM  WDV  SYD  DDB   Machine  Unit        Sinking
                    Hour Rate Production     Fund
```

## 2.2 Method 1 — Straight Line Method (SLM)

Also called **Fixed Installment Method** or **Original Cost Method**.

### 2.2.1 Concept
An equal amount of depreciation is charged every year over the **entire useful life** of the asset. The asset value reduces *linearly* with time, hence the name.

### 2.2.2 Assumptions
- Depreciation is a function of **time only**, not usage.
- Repair and maintenance expenses remain roughly constant every year.
- Asset provides equal utility each year.

### 2.2.3 Formula Derivation (Logic Step-by-Step)

- Let $C$ = Original cost of the asset
- Let $S$ = Salvage value at end of life
- Let $N$ = Estimated useful life in years
- Total depreciation over the life = $C - S$
- Annual depreciation (equal in every year) = $\dfrac{C - S}{N}$

$$\boxed{D_{\text{annual}} = \frac{C - S}{N}}$$

The **rate of depreciation** (as a percentage) is:

$$r = \frac{D_{\text{annual}}}{C} \times 100 = \frac{C - S}{N \cdot C} \times 100 \; (\%) $$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** When salvage value is negligible (S ≈ 0), the formula simplifies to $D = C/N$. This is a frequent shortcut in numerical problems.

## 2.3 Method 2 — Written Down Value Method (WDV)

Also called **Diminishing Balance Method** or **Reducing Installment Method**.

### 2.3.1 Concept
A **fixed percentage** of the *diminishing book value* is charged as depreciation every year. As the book value reduces each year, the depreciation amount also reduces — but the **rate remains constant**.

### 2.3.2 Formula Derivation

- Let $r$ = fixed rate of depreciation (as a decimal)
- Book value at the start of year = $B_{n-1}$
- Depreciation in year $n$ = $r \times B_{n-1}$

$$\boxed{D_n = r \cdot B_{n-1}}$$

The book value after $n$ years is:

$$B_n = C \cdot (1 - r)^n$$

When salvage value $S$ is given, the rate $r$ is found by solving:

$$S = C \cdot (1 - r)^N \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; r = 1 - \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/N}$$

## 2.4 Method 3 — Sum of Years' Digits Method (SYD)

### 2.4.1 Concept
An **accelerated depreciation** method where higher depreciation is charged in earlier years (when the asset is more productive) and lower depreciation in later years.

### 2.4.2 Formula Derivation

Sum of years' digits (SYD) for an asset with life $N$:

$$\text{SYD} = 1 + 2 + 3 + \dots + N = \frac{N(N+1)}{2}$$

Depreciation in year $k$ (counted from year 1):

$$\boxed{D_k = \frac{(N - k + 1)}{\text{SYD}} \cdot (C - S)}$$

So in Year 1: fraction = $N / \text{SYD}$ (largest); Year 2: $(N-1)/\text{SYD}$; …; Year $N$: $1/\text{SYD}$.

## 2.5 Method 4 — Double Declining Balance Method (DDB)

A specific case of WDV where the rate is **twice the straight-line rate** (ignoring salvage value):

$$r_{\text{DDB}} = \frac{2}{N}$$

$$\boxed{D_n = \frac{2}{N} \cdot B_{n-1}}$$

The asset is never depreciated below its **salvage value**.

## 2.6 Method 5 — Sinking Fund Method

### 2.6.1 Concept
Depreciation is invested annually in a **sinking fund** earning interest at a known rate. At the end of the asset's life, the accumulated fund equals the **depreciable amount** $(C - S)$, which is then used to replace the asset.

### 2.6.2 Formula Derivation

- Let $i$ = rate of interest per annum (as decimal)
- Let $A$ = annual sinking fund instalment

By the **sinking fund annuity formula**:

$$\boxed{A = \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1 + i)^N - 1}}$$

Total annual charge to Profit & Loss A/c in year $k$:

$$\text{Charge}_k = A + \text{Interest on accumulated fund up to year } (k-1)$$

## 2.7 Method 6 — Machine Hour Rate Method (Usage-Based)

$$\boxed{D_{\text{year}} = \frac{C - S}{\text{Total estimated working hours}} \times \text{Hours worked in the year}}$$

Suitable for plant and machinery where depreciation is more closely related to **operating hours** than to calendar years.

## 2.8 Method 7 — Unit Production / Service Output Method

$$\boxed{D_{\text{year}} = \frac{C - S}{\text{Total estimated production units}} \times \text{Units produced in the year}}$$

Used for assets producing a measurable output (e.g., a vehicle per km, a press per tonne).

## 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table** — Memorize before every KTU exam. Avoid the `|` pipe symbol inside table cells by writing any modulus as `\vert`.

| Method | Depreciable Base | Annual Charge Formula | Book Value after $n$ years |
|---|---|---|---|
| Straight Line (SLM) | $C - S$ | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{N}$ | $B_n = C - nD$ |
| Written Down Value (WDV) | Diminishing value | $D_n = r \cdot B_{n-1}$ | $B_n = C(1 - r)^n$ |
| Sum of Years' Digits (SYD) | $C - S$ | $D_k = \dfrac{(N - k + 1)}{N(N+1)/2} \cdot (C-S)$ | $B_n = C - \sum D_k$ |
| Double Declining (DDB) | Diminishing value | $D_n = \dfrac{2}{N} \cdot B_{n-1}$ | $B_n = C(1 - \frac{2}{N})^n$ |
| Sinking Fund | $C - S$ | $A = \dfrac{(C-S)\cdot i}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ (plus interest) | $B_n = C - nA$ |
| Machine Hour Rate | $C - S$ | $D = \dfrac{C-S}{\text{Hours}} \times \text{Hours}_{\text{yr}}$ | Varies with usage |
| Unit Production | $C - S$ | $D = \dfrac{C-S}{\text{Units}} \times \text{Units}_{\text{yr}}$ | Varies with output |

**Standard symbols used throughout KTU 2024 scheme:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $C$ | Original cost of asset | ₹ (Rupees) |
| $S$ | Salvage / Scrap value | ₹ |
| $N$ | Useful life | Years / Hours / Units |
| $r$ | Depreciation rate | Dimensionless (decimal) |
| $i$ | Interest rate (sinking fund) | Dimensionless (decimal) |
| $B_n$ | Book value at end of year $n$ | ₹ |
| $D_n$ | Depreciation in year $n$ | ₹ |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing industries** use SLM for buildings and WDV for machinery (as per Income Tax Rules).
- **Mines and quarries** use depletion/unit-production methods since value depends on extraction.
- **Power plants and airlines** prefer **machine-hour** methods since usage varies drastically.
- **Tech companies** use accelerated methods (DDB, SYD) for electronics due to rapid obsolescence.
- **Public utilities** use sinking fund to ensure replacement funds are available at the end of life.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Working Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No step is skipped. Each numerical is carried out in full. Multi-line equations use `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` inside a single `$$ ... $$` block, separated by exactly one blank line from surrounding text.

---

## 3.1 WORKED EXAMPLE 1 — Straight Line Method (SLM)

**Problem:**
A machine is purchased for **₹2,00,000**. Freight and installation cost an additional **₹20,000**. Estimated useful life is **10 years** with a salvage value of **₹20,000**. Calculate annual depreciation using SLM and the book value at the end of year 6.

### Step 1 — Compute total cost (book value base)

$$C = \text{Purchase price} + \text{Freight} + \text{Installation}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000 \\
  &= 2{,}20{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2{,}20{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\
S &= 20{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\
N &= 10 \; \text{years} \\
\text{Depreciable amount} &= C - S = 2{,}20{,}000 - 20{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply SLM formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{annual}} &= \frac{C - S}{N} \\
                  &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{10} \\
                  &= 20{,}000 \; \text{₹ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Depreciation rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{D_{\text{annual}}}{C} \times 100 \\
  &= \frac{20{,}000}{2{,}20{,}000} \times 100 \\
  &= 9.09 \; \% \text{ per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Book value at end of year 6

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_6 &= C - n \cdot D_{\text{annual}} \\
    &= 2{,}20{,}000 - 6 \times 20{,}000 \\
    &= 2{,}20{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 \\
    &= 1{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Annual depreciation = **₹20,000**; Rate = **9.09% p.a.**; Book value after 6 years = **₹1,00,000**.

---

## 3.2 WORKED EXAMPLE 2 — Written Down Value Method (WDV)

**Problem:**
A machine costs **₹5,00,000**. The depreciation rate is **15% p.a.** on WDV basis. Life is 8 years. Compute depreciation for each of the first 3 years and the book value at the end of year 3.

### Step 1 — Identify parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 5{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\
r &= 0.15 \\
B_0 &= C = 5{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹ (book value at start of Year 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Year 1 calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= r \cdot B_0 = 0.15 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 75{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\
B_1 &= B_0 - D_1 = 5{,}00{,}000 - 75{,}000 = 4{,}25{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Year 2 calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2 &= r \cdot B_1 = 0.15 \times 4{,}25{,}000 = 63{,}750 \; \text{₹} \\
B_2 &= B_1 - D_2 = 4{,}25{,}000 - 63{,}750 = 3{,}61{,}250 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Year 3 calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_3 &= r \cdot B_2 = 0.15 \times 3{,}61{,}250 = 54{,}187.50 \; \text{₹} \\
B_3 &= B_2 - D_3 = 3{,}61{,}250 - 54{,}187.50 = 3{,}07{,}062.50 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Cross-check using compact formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_n &= C \cdot (1 - r)^n \\
B_3 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times (0.85)^3 \\
    &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.614125 \\
    &= 3{,}07{,}062.50 \; \text{₹} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer Summary Table:**

| Year | Opening BV (₹) | Depreciation (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | 5,00,000 | 75,000.00 | 4,25,000.00 |
| 2 | 4,25,000 | 63,750.00 | 3,61,250.00 |
| 3 | 3,61,250 | 54,187.50 | 3,07,062.50 |

---

## 3.3 WORKED EXAMPLE 3 — Sum of Years' Digits (SYD)

**Problem:**
Cost of equipment = **₹6,00,000**; Salvage = **₹1,00,000**; Life = **5 years**. Compute depreciation schedule using SYD.

### Step 1 — Compute SYD

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SYD} &= \frac{N(N+1)}{2} = \frac{5 \times 6}{2} = 15
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute depreciable base

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depreciable amount} = C - S = 6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Year 1 (highest fraction)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= \frac{5}{15} \times 5{,}00{,}000 = \frac{1}{3} \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}66{,}666.67 \; \text{₹} \\
B_1 &= 6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}66{,}666.67 = 4{,}33{,}333.33 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Year 2

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2 &= \frac{4}{15} \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}33{,}333.33 \; \text{₹} \\
B_2 &= 4{,}33{,}333.33 - 1{,}33{,}333.33 = 3{,}00{,}000.00 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Year 3

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_3 &= \frac{3}{15} \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000.00 \; \text{₹} \\
B_3 &= 3{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000.00 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Year 4

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_4 &= \frac{2}{15} \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 66{,}666.67 \; \text{₹} \\
B_4 &= 2{,}00{,}000 - 66{,}666.67 = 1{,}33{,}333.33 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Year 5

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_5 &= \frac{1}{15} \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 33{,}333.33 \; \text{₹} \\
B_5 &= 1{,}33{,}333.33 - 33{,}333.33 = 1{,}00{,}000.00 \; \text{₹} \; (= S) \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **SYD Depreciation Schedule:**

| Year | Fraction | Depreciation (₹) | Book Value End (₹) |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | 5/15 | 1,66,666.67 | 4,33,333.33 |
| 2 | 4/15 | 1,33,333.33 | 3,00,000.00 |
| 3 | 3/15 | 1,00,000.00 | 2,00,000.00 |
| 4 | 2/15 | 66,666.67 | 1,33,333.33 |
| 5 | 1/15 | 33,333.33 | 1,00,000.00 |
| **Total** | **15/15** | **5,00,000.00** | — |

---

## 3.4 WORKED EXAMPLE 4 — Sinking Fund Method

**Problem:**
A machine costs **₹4,00,000** with a salvage value of **₹40,000** and a useful life of **5 years**. Interest on the sinking fund investment is **6% p.a.** Compute:
1. Annual sinking fund instalment.
2. Total depreciation charge in year 3 (instalment + interest on accumulated fund).

### Step 1 — Identify parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 4{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\
S &= 40{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\
N &= 5 \; \text{years} \\
i &= 0.06 \\
C - S &= 3{,}60{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Annual sinking fund instalment

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1 + i)^N - 1} \\
  &= \frac{3{,}60{,}000 \times 0.06}{(1.06)^5 - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.06)^5 &= 1.06 \times 1.06 \times 1.06 \times 1.06 \times 1.06 \\
         &= 1.338226 \\
(1.06)^5 - 1 &= 0.338226
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{3{,}60{,}000 \times 0.06}{0.338226} = \frac{21{,}600}{0.338226} = 63{,}864.50 \; \text{₹ (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Accumulated sinking fund at end of Year 2

The fund accumulates as: Year 1 deposit earns interest for 4 years, Year 2 deposit earns for 3 years, etc.

For year 2, accumulated fund:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= A \cdot \left[ (1+i)^2 - 1 \right] / i \\
    &= 63{,}864.50 \times \left[ (1.06)^2 - 1 \right] / 0.06 \\
    &= 63{,}864.50 \times \frac{0.1236}{0.06} \\
    &= 63{,}864.50 \times 2.06 \\
    &= 1{,}31{,}560.87 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interest on accumulated fund in Year 3

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Interest}_{Y3} &= F_2 \times i = 1{,}31{,}560.87 \times 0.06 = 7{,}893.65 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Total depreciation charge in Year 3

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Charge}_{Y3} &= A + \text{Interest}_{Y3} \\
                        &= 63{,}864.50 + 7{,}893.65 \\
                        &= 71{,}758.15 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Annual sinking fund instalment ≈ **₹63,864.50**; Total depreciation in Year 3 ≈ **₹71,758.15**.

---

## 3.5 WORKED EXAMPLE 5 — Comparison of SLM and WDV

**Problem:**
Asset cost = **₹1,00,000**; Life = **5 years**; Salvage = **₹10,000**.
Compute and compare the depreciation schedule under SLM and WDV (assume WDV rate gives same salvage after 5 years).

### Step 1 — SLM depreciation (constant)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{C - S}{N} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}000}{5} = 18{,}000 \; \text{₹ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Find WDV rate $r$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= C(1 - r)^N \\
10{,}000 &= 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1 - r)^5 \\
(1 - r)^5 &= 0.1 \\
1 - r &= (0.1)^{1/5} = 0.63096 \\
r &= 0.36904 \approx 36.90 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Side-by-side comparison table

| Year | SLM Dep. (₹) | SLM Closing BV (₹) | WDV Dep. (₹) | WDV Closing BV (₹) |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | 18,000 | 82,000 | 36,904 | 63,096 |
| 2 | 18,000 | 64,000 | 23,283 | 39,813 |
| 3 | 18,000 | 46,000 | 14,693 | 25,120 |
| 4 | 18,000 | 28,000 | 9,270 | 15,850 |
| 5 | 18,000 | 10,000 | 5,850 | 10,000 |
| **Total** | **90,000** | — | **90,000** | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Key observation:** Under SLM, depreciation is constant. Under WDV, depreciation is **higher in early years** and lower later. Both methods charge the **same total** depreciation of ₹90,000 — only the *timing* differs.

---

## 3.6 WORKED EXAMPLE 6 — Machine Hour Rate Method

**Problem:**
Cost of machine = **₹3,00,000**; Salvage = **₹30,000**; Estimated total working hours over life = **54,000 hours**. The machine worked for **8,000 hours** in Year 1 and **9,500 hours** in Year 2. Compute depreciation for both years.

### Step 1 — Depreciation per hour

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{per hour}} &= \frac{C - S}{\text{Total hours}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}000}{54{,}000} \\
                    &= \frac{2{,}70{,}000}{54{,}000} = 5 \; \text{₹ per hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Year 1 depreciation

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 5 \times 8{,}000 = 40{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Year 2 depreciation

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2 &= 5 \times 9{,}500 = 47{,}500 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** ₹40,000 (Year 1); ₹47,500 (Year 2). Notice depreciation tracks **usage** rather than time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree — Depreciation Methods

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["DEPRECIATION METHODS"] --> B["TIME BASED"]
    A --> C["USAGE BASED"]
    A --> D["FUND BASED"]

    B --> B1["STRAIGHT LINE METHOD SLM"]
    B --> B2["WRITTEN DOWN VALUE WDV"]
    B --> B3["SUM OF YEARS DIGITS SYD"]
    B --> B4["DOUBLE DECLINING BALANCE DDB"]

    C --> C1["MACHINE HOUR RATE"]
    C --> C2["UNIT PRODUCTION METHOD"]

    D --> D1["SINKING FUND METHOD"]

    B1 --- F1["Constant charge every year"]
    B2 --- F2["Fixed percent of reducing book value"]
    B3 --- F3["Higher charge in early years"]
    B4 --- F4["Rate is twice the SLM rate"]
    C1 --- F5["Charge per operating hour"]
    C2 --- F6["Charge per unit produced"]
    D1 --- F7["Fund earns compound interest"]
```

## 4.2 Book Value Trajectory — SLM vs WDV vs SYD

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "BOOK VALUE OVER TIME"
        direction LR
        P0["Year 0 Cost 100000"]:::start
        P1["Year 5 SLM 10000 WDV 10000 SYD 10000"]
        P0 --> P1
    end

    P2["SLM Linear decrease uniform slope"]:::linear
    P3["WDV Steep early then flat curved"]:::curve
    P4["SYD Steep early then sharp drop at end"]:::accel

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef linear fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef curve fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef accel fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Sinking Fund Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph "SINKING FUND FLOW"
        A["Asset in Use Year 1 to N"] --> B["Charge A per year to P and L"]
        B --> C["Invest A in Sinking Fund"]
        C --> D["Fund earns interest at rate i"]
        D --> E["At end of year N accumulated fund equals C minus S"]
        E --> F["Use fund to replace old asset"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Method

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q["Need to depreciate an asset"] --> Q1{"Is usage uniform across years?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> SLM["Use STRAIGHT LINE METHOD"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Does the asset produce measurable output?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> USAGE["Use MACHINE HOUR or UNIT PRODUCTION"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Is asset subject to rapid obsolescence?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ACC["Use WDV or DDB or SYD"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Does firm want to build replacement fund?"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> SF["Use SINKING FUND METHOD"]
    Q4 -- No --> SLM2["Default STRAIGHT LINE METHOD"]
```

## 4.5 Cash Flow Impact Timeline (KTU Analytical Tool)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "ANNUAL PROFIT IMPACT"
        Y1["Year 1 High expense Lower profit Lower tax"]:::high
        Y2["Year 2 High expense Lower profit Lower tax"]:::high
        Y3["Year 3 Moderate expense Moderate profit"]:::mid
        Y4["Year 4 Lower expense Higher profit Higher tax"]:::low
        Y5["Year 5 Lower expense Higher profit Higher tax"]:::low
        Y1 --> Y2 --> Y3 --> Y4 --> Y5
    end

    classDef high fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef mid fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef low fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Accelerated methods (WDV, DDB, SYD) shift depreciation to **earlier years**, reducing taxable profit early and providing **higher cash flow** via tax savings — a concept called the **time value of money advantage**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**"Distinguish between Straight Line Method and Written Down Value Method of depreciation. Which method is preferred for income tax purposes in India?"**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

| Basis | SLM | WDV |
|---|---|---|
| **Depreciation charge** | Equal every year | Decreases every year |
| **Base for calculation** | Original cost | Written-down (diminishing) value |
| **Book value at end** | Reduces linearly | Reduces geometrically |
| **Repair expenses** | Assumed constant | Assumed increasing — WDV balances this |
| **Effect on profit** | Steady P&L impact | Higher expenses early → lower profit early |
| **Suitability** | Buildings, leases | Machinery, equipment |

**Income Tax preference:** WDV method is generally preferred (and required) under the **Income Tax Act, 1961** for most plant & machinery because it matches the *actual wear pattern* and provides tax relief in early years.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**"What is the Sinking Fund Method of depreciation? State its suitability."**

**Model Answer:**

Under the Sinking Fund Method, a fixed amount (called the *sinking fund instalment*) is charged to Profit & Loss A/c every year and invested in a separate fund (e.g., government securities) that earns compound interest. At the end of the asset's useful life, the accumulated fund equals the depreciable amount $(C - S)$, which is used to replace the asset.

**Suitability:**
- Long-life, high-value assets (bridges, dams, power plants, large machinery).
- Companies that want a ready replacement fund at the end of life.
- Public utilities and infrastructure projects.

**Formula (recall):**
$$A = \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1 + i)^N - 1}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ QUESTION 1 / CHOICE A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

A factory purchased a machine for **₹10,00,000** on 1st January 2021. Additional expenditure incurred: freight ₹50,000; installation ₹30,000; trial-run material ₹20,000. Salvage value is **₹1,00,000** and useful life is **10 years**.

**(a)** Compute the annual depreciation and the book value at the end of the 4th year using the **Straight Line Method**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** If the same machine were depreciated under the **WDV method at 18% p.a.**, prepare the depreciation schedule for the first 4 years and compute the book value at the end of year 4. (7 Marks)

#### 📝 Model Solution

**(a) SLM Solution**

Total cost = Purchase + Freight + Installation + Trial-run

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 10{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 20{,}000 \\
  &= 11{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

Depreciable amount:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C - S &= 11{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

Annual depreciation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{C - S}{N} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{10} = 1{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Book value at end of year 4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_4 &= C - 4D = 11{,}00{,}000 - 4 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) WDV Solution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 11{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}, \quad r = 0.18, \quad B_0 = 11{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Year 1:**
$$
D_1 = 0.18 \times 11{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}98{,}000; \quad B_1 = 9{,}02{,}000
$$

**Year 2:**
$$
D_2 = 0.18 \times 9{,}02{,}000 = 1{,}62{,}360; \quad B_2 = 7{,}39{,}640
$$

**Year 3:**
$$
D_3 = 0.18 \times 7{,}39{,}640 = 1{,}33{,}135.20; \quad B_3 = 6{,}06{,}504.80
$$

**Year 4:**
$$
D_4 = 0.18 \times 6{,}06{,}504.80 = 1{,}09{,}170.86; \quad B_4 = 4{,}97{,}333.94
$$

**WDV Schedule:**

| Year | Opening BV (₹) | Depreciation (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | 11,00,000 | 1,98,000.00 | 9,02,000.00 |
| 2 | 9,02,000 | 1,62,360.00 | 7,39,640.00 |
| 3 | 7,39,640 | 1,33,135.20 | 6,06,504.80 |
| 4 | 6,06,504.80 | 1,09,170.86 | 4,97,333.94 |

> **Final Answer:** SLM → ₹1,00,000/year, BV at year 4 = ₹7,00,000. WDV → BV at year 4 ≈ ₹4,97,333.94.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION 2 / CHOICE B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

A construction company purchases an excavator for **₹25,00,000**. Estimated life = **8 years**; Salvage value = **₹2,50,000**.

**(a)** Calculate the depreciation for each year using the **Sum of Years' Digits Method**. Show the complete schedule. (7 Marks)

**(b)** If the company instead uses the **Sinking Fund Method** with interest at **8% p.a.**, calculate the annual sinking fund instalment and the total charge to P&L in the **3rd year**. (7 Marks)

#### 📝 Model Solution

**(a) SYD Solution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SYD} &= \frac{8 \times 9}{2} = 36 \\
C - S &= 25{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000 = 22{,}50{,}000 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

For year $k$, fraction = $(9 - k)/36$.

**Year 1:** $D_1 = (8/36) \times 22{,}50{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000$; $B_1 = 20{,}00{,}000$
**Year 2:** $D_2 = (7/36) \times 22{,}50{,}000 = 4{,}37{,}500$; $B_2 = 15{,}62{,}500$
**Year 3:** $D_3 = (6/36) \times 22{,}50{,}000 = 3{,}75{,}000$; $B_3 = 11{,}87{,}500$
**Year 4:** $D_4 = (5/36) \times 22{,}50{,}000 = 3{,}12{,}500$; $B_4 = 8{,}75{,}000$
**Year 5:** $D_5 = (4/36) \times 22{,}50{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000$; $B_5 = 6{,}25{,}000$
**Year 6:** $D_6 = (3/36) \times 22{,}50{,}000 = 1{,}87{,}500$; $B_6 = 4{,}37{,}500$
**Year 7:** $D_7 = (2/36) \times 22{,}50{,}000 = 1{,}25{,}000$; $B_7 = 3{,}12{,}500$
**Year 8:** $D_8 = (1/36) \times 22{,}50{,}000 = 62{,}500$; $B_8 = 2{,}50{,}000 = S$ ✓

**Total depreciation** = ₹22,50,000 ✓

**(b) Sinking Fund Solution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{(C - S) \cdot i}{(1 + i)^N - 1} \\
  &= \frac{22{,}50{,}000 \times 0.08}{(1.08)^8 - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.08)^8 &= 1.85093 \\
(1.08)^8 - 1 &= 0.85093
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{22{,}50{,}000 \times 0.08}{0.85093} = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{0.85093} = 2{,}11{,}528.74 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

Fund at end of year 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= A \times \frac{(1.08)^2 - 1}{0.08} = 2{,}11{,}528.74 \times \frac{0.1664}{0.08} \\
    &= 2{,}11{,}528.74 \times 2.08 = 4{,}39{,}979.78 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

Interest in year 3:

$$
\text{Interest}_{Y3} = 4{,}39{,}979.78 \times 0.08 = 35{,}198.38 \; \text{₹}
$$

Total P&L charge in Year 3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total}_{Y3} &= A + \text{Interest}_{Y3} = 2{,}11{,}528.74 + 35{,}198.38 = 2{,}46{,}727.12 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Annual sinking fund instalment ≈ ₹2,11,528.74; Total charge in Year 3 ≈ ₹2,46,727.12.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on depreciation questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add freight/installation to the cost** — Examiners frequently plant these in the problem to test whether students include them in $C$. Maximum cost = 1 mark lost.
> 2. **Using the wrong base in WDV** — Always apply the rate to the **opening book value of that year**, not the original cost. Easy 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Sinking fund instalment formula** — Students confuse the formula with simple interest or forget to subtract 1 in the denominator. Always verify: $A = \dfrac{(C-S)i}{(1+i)^N - 1}$.
> 4. **SYD fraction error** — Year 1 fraction is $N/\text{SYD}$, NOT $1/\text{SYD}$. Common slip-up costing 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Machine hour method** — Convert total estimated hours correctly and do not mix up "hours worked" with "total life hours".
> 6. **Unit mismatch** — Keep life and rate in the same units. If life is in years and rate is annual, do not mix monthly.
> 7. **Not showing the salvage value check** — In SYD, verify $B_N = S$. Examiners reward this verification step with bonus marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the KTU exam.**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- **Depreciation** = permanent, continuous decrease in **book value** of a tangible fixed asset.
- **Causes**: wear & tear, obsolescence, effluxion of time, accidents, inadequacy.
- **Three categories**: Time-based, Usage-based, Fund-based.

### 🔑 Formula Memory Anchors
- **SLM**: $D = (C - S)/N$ → **constant**.
- **WDV**: $D_n = r \cdot B_{n-1}$; $B_n = C(1 - r)^n$ → **decreasing**.
- **SYD**: $D_k = \dfrac{(N - k + 1)}{N(N+1)/2}(C - S)$ → **decreasing, accelerated**.
- **DDB**: $r = 2/N$ on diminishing value → **most aggressive acceleration**.
- **Sinking Fund**: $A = \dfrac{(C-S)i}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ + interest component.
- **Machine Hour Rate**: per-hour depreciation × actual hours.
- **Unit Production**: per-unit depreciation × actual units.

### 🔑 Method Selection Heuristics
| Situation | Best Method |
|---|---|
| Buildings, leases, equal utility | SLM |
| Plant & machinery, Income Tax | WDV |
| High obsolescence (electronics, IT) | DDB / SYD |
| Replacement fund needed | Sinking Fund |
| Usage-driven (cranes, vehicles) | Machine Hour / Unit Production |

### 🔑 Exam Day Tips
1. **Always identify** whether salvage value is given — affects the entire calculation.
2. **Show all substitutions** in the formula — partial marks for correct setup.
3. **Cross-verify** $B_N = S$ at the end of any schedule.
4. **Use a year-by-year table** for SLM, WDV, SYD, and DDB — never skip a column.
5. For sinking fund, **show the compound interest calculation** step explicitly.
6. **State the formula first**, then substitute values — earns full method marks even if arithmetic fails.

### 🔑 One-Line Definitions (For 2-Mark Questions)
- **SLM**: Equal annual depreciation throughout the life of the asset.
- **WDV**: Fixed percentage on diminishing book value every year.
- **SYD**: Depreciable amount distributed in proportion to remaining life each year.
- **DDB**: Double the SLM rate applied on diminishing book value.
- **Sinking Fund**: Depreciation invested in a fund earning compound interest.
- **Depletion**: Reduction in value of natural resources.
- **Obsolescence**: Loss of value due to technological change.

> **All the best for your KTU 2024 Scheme examination!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Personnel Management: Objectives and Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Personnel Management: Objectives and Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Personnel Management (PM)** is the administrative discipline concerned with planning, organizing, directing, and controlling the procurement, development, compensation, integration, and maintenance of human resources within an organization, with the objective of attaining individual, organizational, and societal goals simultaneously.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Personnel Management is treated as a sub-system of **Industrial Engineering and Management** that focuses exclusively on the **"people dimension"** of an enterprise. It is often used interchangeably with **Human Resource Management (HRM)**, although HRM is considered its modern, strategic extension.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Personnel Management is the planning, organizing, directing and controlling of the procurement, development, compensation, integration and maintenance of human resources to achieve individual, organizational and social objectives."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **orchestra**. A musical orchestra has many specialized musicians (violinists, pianists, percussionists) — each with different skills, temperaments, and pay expectations. The **Conductor** is the Personnel Manager.

- The Conductor doesn't *play* every instrument, but ensures the **right musician** is in the **right seat** (Procurement/Placement).
- The Conductor **trains** weak performers through rehearsals (Development/Training).
- The Conductor ensures each musician is **fairly compensated** based on their skill and contribution (Compensation/Wages).
- The Conductor builds **harmony** among the musicians so they play as one unit (Integration/Motivation).
- The Conductor maintains the **instruments and the well-being** of the musicians (Maintenance/Welfare).

Without a Conductor, even the world's best musicians would produce **noise, not music**. Similarly, without effective Personnel Management, even the best engineers, workers, and managers will not deliver organizational performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of PM Success:**
> 1. **Right Person** – for the right job (Selection)
> 2. **Right Training** – to grow the person (Development)
> 3. **Right Environment** – to retain the person (Welfare & Motivation)

### 1.3 Core Terminology Box

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Procurement** | Hiring the right people (Manpower Planning → Recruitment → Selection → Placement) |
| **Development** | Training, education, and growth of employees |
| **Compensation** | Wages, salaries, incentives, and benefits paid in return for work |
| **Integration** | Aligning individual goals with organizational goals (motivation, morale) |
| **Maintenance** | Ensuring health, safety, and welfare of employees |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Nature and Scope of Personnel Management

Personnel Management is **pervasive** (applies to all departments), **continuous** (never-ending process), **development-oriented** (focuses on employee growth), and **people-centered** (concerned with human element).

It operates at **three distinct levels**:

1. **Operational Level** – Day-to-day tasks like attendance, payroll, leave records.
2. **Tactical Level** – Mid-term plans like training programs, grievance handling.
3. **Strategic Level** – Long-term decisions like succession planning, organizational culture.

### 2.2 Objectives of Personnel Management

The objectives are grouped into **three categories** — this is a very high-yield KTU question.

#### A. Organizational Objectives
- To secure the right number and type of people at the right time.
- To maximize the **effective utilization of human resources**.
- To improve productivity and reduce waste of human effort.
- To build and maintain a stable, efficient workforce.

#### B. Functional Objectives
- To maintain the contribution of the personnel department at an optimum level.
- To establish and maintain productive working relationships among all members.
- To integrate HR activities with the overall corporate strategy.

#### C. Personal Objectives
- To assist employees in achieving their personal goals (career, skills, income).
- To enhance job satisfaction and morale of each employee.
- To provide opportunities for personal growth and self-fulfillment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams — "O-F-P":** *Organizational → Functional → Personal*. Always state objectives in this order for full marks.

### 2.3 Functions of Personnel Management

Functions are broadly classified into **two types**.

#### 1. Managerial Functions (POLC Framework)

These are the universal management functions applied to the personnel domain:

| Function | Application in Personnel Context |
|---|---|
| **Planning** | Forecasting manpower needs, succession planning, training calendar |
| **Organizing** | Designing HR structure, defining roles, departmentalization |
| **Directing** | Leading, motivating, communicating orders, supervising |
| **Controlling** | Performance appraisal, discipline, audit of HR policies |

#### 2. Operative Functions (Core HR Functions)

The **six operative functions** form the heart of PM and are extremely important for KTU exams.

```
1. PROCUREMENT
   └─ Manpower Planning → Job Analysis → Recruitment → Selection → Placement → Induction

2. DEVELOPMENT
   └─ Training → Education → Skill Enhancement → Career Progression

3. COMPENSATION
   └─ Wage/Salary Policy → Incentives → Bonus → Fringe Benefits

4. INTEGRATION
   └─ Motivation → Morale Building → Grievance Handling → Collective Bargaining

5. MAINTENANCE
   └─ Health & Safety → Welfare Measures → Social Security → Industrial Relations
```

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since this is a management topic, the "formulas" here are **conceptual equations and frameworks**.

| # | Framework / Equation | Description | Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PM = f(Procurement, Development, Compensation, Integration, Maintenance)** | Master function formula | Defines scope of PM |
| 2 | **Manpower Demand Equation:** $D = (W \times S) + T - R$ | D = Demand, W = Workload, S = Staff per unit, T = Transfers, R = Retirements | Numerical questions |
| 3 | **Optimal Workforce:** $W_{opt} = \dfrac{O}{P}$ | O = Output required, P = Productivity per worker | Numerical questions |
| 4 | **Turnover Rate:** $T_r = \dfrac{L}{\bar{N}} \times 100$ | L = Leavers, $\bar{N}$ = Average workforce | Numerical problems |
| 5 | **Absenteeism Rate:** $A_r = \dfrac{M}{W \times D} \times 100$ | M = Man-days lost, W = Workers, D = Days | Numerical problems |
| 6 | **Training Effectiveness:** $TE = \dfrac{PI_{post} - PI_{pre}}{PI_{pre}} \times 100$ | PI = Performance Index | Theory questions |
| 7 | **Compensation Ratio:** $CR = \dfrac{AC}{MC} \times 100$ | AC = Actual, MC = Market compensation | Theory questions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Questions like *"Explain the objectives of Personnel Management"* (7 marks) **always** expect you to use the **three-tier classification (Organizational, Functional, Personal)**. Skipping a tier = losing 2 marks.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

In manufacturing industries (e.g., Kitex Garments, HMT, BHEL Kerala), Personnel Management ensures:
- Right **operators** on CNC machines.
- Proper **shift scheduling** for continuous production.
- **Safety training** to prevent industrial accidents.
- **Wage incentive schemes** linked to productivity.

In service sectors (IT, hospitals), PM focuses on:
- **Soft-skills training** for customer-facing employees.
- **Retention strategies** to reduce attrition in software firms (Infosys, TCS).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Objectives vs. Functions

This matrix is the most common KTU 14-mark question framework. It must be memorized.

| Function ↓ / Objective → | Organizational | Functional | Personal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Procurement** | Right person, right place | Structured hiring process | Fair selection, no bias |
| **Development** | Skilled workforce | Training policy aligned to strategy | Career growth, learning |
| **Compensation** | Productivity-linked pay | Internal pay equity | Fair wages, incentives |
| **Integration** | Team synergy, low conflict | Smooth coordination | Job satisfaction, morale |
| **Maintenance** | Stability, low turnover | Welfare schemes | Health, safety, work-life |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Manpower Demand Calculation

A KTU favorite numerical problem.

> **Problem:** A factory plans to produce **24,000 units** in a year. Standard hours per unit = 4. Each worker works **8 hours/day**, **300 days/year**. Productivity index = **80%**. Find the number of workers required.

**Step 1 — Total Standard Hours Required**
$$
SH_{total} = Output \times Standard\ Hours\ per\ Unit
$$
$$
SH_{total} = 24{,}000 \times 4 = 96{,}000\ hours
$$

**Step 2 — Effective Hours per Worker per Year**
$$
EH = Hours\ per\ day \times Working\ days \times Productivity\ Index
$$
$$
EH = 8 \times 300 \times 0.80 = 1{,}920\ hours/worker/year
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Workers**
$$
N = \dfrac{SH_{total}}{EH}
$$
$$
N = \dfrac{96{,}000}{1{,}920} = 50\ workers
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The factory needs exactly **50 workers** to meet the production target.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Turnover Rate

> **Problem:** A company had **200 employees on Jan 1** and **240 employees on Dec 31**. During the year, **30 employees left** the organization. Calculate the turnover rate.

**Step 1 — Calculate Average Workforce**
$$
\bar{N} = \dfrac{N_{start} + N_{end}}{2}
$$
$$
\bar{N} = \dfrac{200 + 240}{2} = 220\ employees
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Turnover Formula**
$$
T_r = \dfrac{L}{\bar{N}} \times 100
$$
$$
T_r = \dfrac{30}{220} \times 100 = 13.64\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Turnover rate = **13.64 %**. A rate above 15% indicates serious retention problems in the HR system.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Absenteeism Rate

> **Problem:** In a department of **100 workers**, total man-days available in a month = **3,000**. Man-days lost due to absence = **450**. Calculate the absenteeism rate.

**Step 1 — Apply the Absenteeism Formula**
$$
A_r = \dfrac{M}{W \times D} \times 100
$$
$$
A_r = \dfrac{450}{100 \times 30} \times 100 = 15\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Absenteeism rate = **15 %**. Industry benchmark is < 5%; a 15% rate signals motivational or welfare failures.

### 3.5 Detailed Walkthrough: Classification of Functions

This is the most theory-heavy topic in the module. Let us expand each operative function.

#### (a) Procurement Function — Step-by-Step
$$
Procurement = Manpower\ Planning \rightarrow Job\ Analysis \rightarrow Recruitment \rightarrow Selection \rightarrow Placement \rightarrow Induction
$$

- **Manpower Planning:** Forecasting future human resource needs.
- **Job Analysis:** Documenting duties, skills, and qualifications of each job.
- **Recruitment:** Attracting potential candidates.
- **Selection:** Choosing the best candidate using tests and interviews.
- **Placement:** Assigning the right person to the right job.
- **Induction (Orientation):** Introducing the new employee to the organization.

#### (b) Development Function — Step-by-Step
$$
Development = Training \rightarrow Education \rightarrow Skill\ Enhancement \rightarrow Career\ Progression
$$

#### (c) Compensation Function — Step-by-Step
$$
Compensation = Wages \rightarrow Salary \rightarrow Incentives \rightarrow Bonus \rightarrow Fringe\ Benefits
$$

#### (d) Integration Function — Step-by-Step
$$
Integration = Motivation \rightarrow Morale \rightarrow Grievance\ Handling \rightarrow Collective\ Bargaining
$$

#### (e) Maintenance Function — Step-by-Step
$$
Maintenance = Health \rightarrow Safety \rightarrow Welfare \rightarrow Social\ Security \rightarrow Industrial\ Relations
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Objectives of Personnel Management

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["Personnel Management Objectives"]
    orgObj["Organizational Objectives"]
    funcObj["Functional Objectives"]
    persObj["Personal Objectives"]
    org1["Right people at right time"]
    org2["Effective HR utilization"]
    org3["Improve productivity"]
    func1["Maintain HR dept contribution"]
    func2["Productive working relationships"]
    func3["Integrate HR with strategy"]
    pers1["Achieve personal goals"]
    pers2["Job satisfaction & morale"]
    pers3["Personal growth & development"]
    root --> orgObj
    root --> funcObj
    root --> persObj
    orgObj --> org1
    orgObj --> org2
    orgObj --> org3
    funcObj --> func1
    funcObj --> func2
    funcObj --> func3
    persObj --> pers1
    persObj --> pers2
    persObj --> pers3
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functions of Personnel Management

```mermaid
graph TD
    pmf["Personnel Management Functions"]
    mgr["Managerial Functions"]
    op["Operative Functions"]
    mgr1["Planning"]
    mgr2["Organizing"]
    mgr3["Directing"]
    mgr4["Controlling"]
    op1["Procurement"]
    op2["Development"]
    op3["Compensation"]
    op4["Integration"]
    op5["Maintenance"]
    pmf --> mgr
    pmf --> op
    mgr --> mgr1
    mgr --> mgr2
    mgr --> mgr3
    mgr --> mgr4
    op --> op1
    op --> op2
    op --> op3
    op --> op4
    op --> op5
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Procurement Function (Detailed)

```mermaid
graph LR
    mp["Manpower Planning"] --> ja["Job Analysis"]
    ja --> rec["Recruitment"]
    rec --> sel["Selection"]
    sel --> place["Placement"]
    place --> ind["Induction Orientation"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Maintenance Function (Detailed)

```mermaid
graph TD
    maint["Maintenance Function"]
    h["Health Services"]
    s["Safety Measures"]
    w["Welfare Activities"]
    ss["Social Security"]
    ir["Industrial Relations"]
    maint --> h
    maint --> s
    maint --> w
    maint --> ss
    maint --> ir
```

### 4.5 Functional Architecture: PM as a Closed-Loop System

```mermaid
graph TD
    input["INPUT: Manpower Needs Forecast"]
    proc["PROCESS: HR Activities - Procurement - Development - Compensation - Integration - Maintenance"]
    output["OUTPUT: Effective Workforce & Organizational Goals Achieved"]
    feedback["FEEDBACK: Performance Appraisal & Grievance Data"]
    input --> proc
    proc --> output
    output --> feedback
    feedback --> input
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This is a **closed-loop control system**. The **feedback loop** is *Performance Appraisal* — it tells the system whether the HR process succeeded or needs correction. This mirrors engineering control systems (e.g., a thermostat) and is a favorite KTU concept link between IE and Management.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define Personnel Management. State any two of its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**
Personnel Management is the planning, organizing, directing and controlling of the procurement, development, compensation, integration and maintenance of human resources to achieve individual, organizational and social objectives.

Two objectives:
1. **Organizational:** To secure the right number and type of people at the right time to maximize productivity.
2. **Personal:** To assist employees in achieving their personal goals and enhance their job satisfaction.

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: List any four operative functions of Personnel Management.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Procurement
2. Development
3. Compensation
4. Integration
*(Maintenance may also be given as the fifth.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A [14 MARKS] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the various **objectives** of Personnel Management in detail. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the **managerial functions** of Personnel Management. **(7 marks)**

**OR**

#### QUESTION B [14 MARKS] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **operative functions** of Personnel Management with examples. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A company has a workforce of 500 employees. During the year, 50 employees resigned and 20 were retrenched. Calculate the **turnover rate** if the workforce at year-end is 480. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A — Part (a) — 7 marks

The objectives of Personnel Management are classified into three categories:

**1. Organizational Objectives (2 marks)**
- To recruit and retain the right number and type of personnel.
- To ensure optimum utilization of human resources.
- To improve productivity and reduce wastage of human effort.

**2. Functional Objectives (2 marks)**
- To maintain the contribution of the personnel department at an appropriate level.
- To establish and maintain productive working relationships among all employees.
- To integrate HR activities with the corporate strategy.

**3. Personal Objectives (2 marks)**
- To help employees achieve their personal goals.
- To enhance job satisfaction and morale.
- To provide opportunities for personal growth.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Three-tier objective classification diagram.

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A — Part (b) — 7 marks

The **Managerial Functions** are the four universal management functions applied to personnel:

**1. Planning (2 marks)**
- Forecasting manpower requirements.
- Designing training and development programs.
- Succession and career planning.

**2. Organizing (2 marks)**
- Designing the HR department structure.
- Defining roles, responsibilities, and authority.
- Allocating work to HR staff.

**3. Directing (1.5 marks)**
- Leading and motivating employees.
- Communicating orders, instructions, and policies.
- Supervising work.

**4. Controlling (1.5 marks)**
- Performance appraisal.
- Discipline and grievance handling.
- Auditing HR policies and procedures.

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B — Part (a) — 7 marks

The **Operative Functions** are the core HR functions:

**1. Procurement (1.5 marks)**
Includes manpower planning, job analysis, recruitment, selection, placement, and induction.

**2. Development (1.5 marks)**
Includes training, education, and career progression of employees. *Example: A software company sending engineers to Java certification programs.*

**3. Compensation (1.5 marks)**
Includes wages, salaries, incentives, bonuses, and fringe benefits. *Example: HRA, medical allowance, performance bonus.*

**4. Integration (1.5 marks)**
Includes motivation, morale building, grievance handling, and collective bargaining. *Example: Suggestion schemes in Tata Steel.*

**5. Maintenance (1 mark)**
Includes health services, safety measures, welfare activities, and social security. *Example: ESI, PF, canteen, crèche.*

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- Starting workforce (assumed) = 500
- Ending workforce = 480
- Resigned = 50, Retrenched = 20
- Total leavers $L = 50 + 20 = 70$

**Step 1 — Average Workforce**
$$
\bar{N} = \dfrac{500 + 480}{2} = 490
$$

**Step 2 — Turnover Rate**
$$
T_r = \dfrac{70}{490} \times 100 = 14.29\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Turnover rate = **14.29 %**.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing "Objectives" with "Functions"** — Objectives are *goals*, functions are *activities to achieve goals*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Personal Objectives" tier** — Most students only mention organizational and functional. Always include personal objectives (job satisfaction, personal growth) to get full marks.
> 3. **No diagrams in 14-mark questions** — Always draw at least one figure (objective classification chart or function tree). No diagram = 1–2 marks deducted.
> 4. **In numerical problems**, forgetting to take the *average* of starting and ending workforce for turnover rate is a frequent error. Use $\bar{N}$, not $N_{start}$.
> 5. **Confusing Managerial vs Operative functions** — Managerial = POLC (universal). Operative = Procurement, Development, Compensation, Integration, Maintenance (HR-specific).

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** PM is the planning, organizing, directing and controlling of **procurement, development, compensation, integration, and maintenance** of human resources.
- **Objectives (3 tiers — memorize "O-F-P"):**
  - *Organizational* — right people, productivity.
  - *Functional* — HR strategy, working relationships.
  - *Personal* — job satisfaction, growth.
- **Managerial Functions (POLC):** Planning, Organizing, Directing, Controlling.
- **Operative Functions (5):** Procurement, Development, Compensation, Integration, Maintenance.
- **Key Formula — Turnover Rate:**
$$
T_r = \dfrac{L}{\bar{N}} \times 100
$$
- **Key Formula — Absenteeism Rate:**
$$
A_r = \dfrac{M}{W \times D} \times 100
$$
- **Key Formula — Manpower Demand:**
$$
N = \dfrac{Output \times Standard\ Hours}{Hours\ per\ day \times Days \times Productivity\ Index}
$$
- **Closed-loop analogy:** PM works like a control system — *input (manpower plan) → process (HR functions) → output (workforce performance) → feedback (appraisal)*.
- **Real-world application:** Used in factories for shift management, in IT firms for retention, in PSUs for industrial relations.
- **Always include a diagram** in 14-mark answers — KTU examiners reward visualization.
- **Examiner's hint:** A 14-mark question on this topic is almost always split as *(a) 7 marks theory + (b) 7 marks theory/numerical* with an internal choice between **Managerial vs Operative** functions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recruitment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recruitment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Recruitment** is the process of identifying, attracting, and shortlisting a sufficient pool of **qualified and capable candidates** from which the organization can **select** the right person for a specific job vacancy. It is a *positive* activity aimed at **creating a talent bank** of prospective employees, and it forms the **upstream function** of the broader Human Resource Management (HRM) cycle.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, recruitment is positioned as a **subset of the procurement function** in HRM. It is the bridge between **manpower planning** (identifying *how many* and *what kind* of people are needed) and **selection** (deciding *which* candidate gets hired).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Fishing Net

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine Recruitment as Casting a Fishing Net:**
> Think of an organization as a fisherman who needs a specific type of fish (the right employee). The fisherman first **identifies what kind of fish** he needs (Job Analysis → Manpower Planning). Then, instead of grabbing a single fish from a known pond, he **casts a wide net into multiple bodies of water** — rivers, lakes, oceans, fish markets — to attract as many suitable fish as possible. *Casting the net = Recruitment.* *Picking the best fish from the net = Selection.* The wider and better-placed the net, the higher the chance of catching the *right* fish.

### 1.3 Recruitment vs. Selection — The Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Recruitment | Selection |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Attract a large pool of applicants | Reject unsuitable, pick the best |
| **Approach** | **Positive** (encourages applications) | **Negative** (rejects applications) |
| **Outcome** | Creates a **talent pool** | Produces a **hired employee** |
| **Complexity** | Simpler, often outsourced to agencies | Complex, involves tests/interviews |
| **Sequence** | Comes **first** | Comes **after** recruitment |

> [!WARNING]
> A frequent KTU exam pitfall: Students often use *recruitment* and *selection* interchangeably. Remember — **Recruitment is about quantity and reach; Selection is about quality and precision.**

### 1.4 Standard Recruitment Metrics (KTU Board-Favorite Constants)

- **Yield Ratio** = (Number of candidates who reached a stage / Number of candidates at previous stage) × 100
- **Cost per Hire** = Total Recruitment Cost / Number of Hires
- **Time to Fill** = Average days taken to fill a vacancy
- **Application/Acceptance Ratio** = Applicants Received / Applicants Accepted
- **Selection Ratio** = (Number Selected / Number of Applicants) × 100
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Strategic Purpose of Recruitment

Recruitment is **not** merely a clerical activity of collecting résumés. From an Industrial Engineering and Management (IEM) perspective, recruitment is a **strategic capability** that directly influences:

1. **Organizational Performance** — Right talent → Higher productivity
2. **Employer Brand** — A transparent process builds goodwill
3. **Cost Efficiency** — Bad hires cost ~1.5× to 2× annual salary (industry standard)
4. **Diversity & Inclusion** — Wider net → Diverse talent pool
5. **Succession Planning** — Continuous inflow enables leadership pipelines

### 2.2 Sources of Recruitment — The Two Main Buckets

Recruitment sources are broadly classified into **Internal Sources** and **External Sources**. The choice between them is one of the **most frequently asked KTU 14-mark questions.**

#### A. Internal Sources of Recruitment

These are candidates **already working within** the organization. They are tried, tested, and familiar with the culture.

- **Transfers** — Shifting an employee from one branch/department to another of the **same grade**.
- **Promotions** — Moving an employee to a **higher position** with greater responsibility, authority, and pay. It is the *strongest motivator* and is a direct reward for loyalty.
- **Re-employment of Ex-employees** — Re-hiring former employees who left on good terms (e.g., retirees, women returning after maternity).
- **Employee Referrals** — Existing employees recommend suitable candidates from their network. Often called the *“warm call”* approach.
- **Internal Job Postings (IJP)** — Vacancies are advertised on company notice boards, intranet portals, or HRMS systems.
- **Deputation / Job Rotation** — Temporary movement across departments for cross-functional exposure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Merits:** Cheaper, faster, no induction cost, boosts morale, known performance.
> **Demerits:** Limits innovation, creates "inbreeding," no fresh ideas, may cause internal politics.

#### B. External Sources of Recruitment

When the organization needs **fresh talent, new skills, or diversity**, it taps external sources.

- **Advertisements (Print, Electronic, Digital)** — The most common method. Appears in newspapers, LinkedIn, job portals, radio, TV.
- **Campus Recruitment / Placement Drives** — Tie-ups with engineering colleges, B-schools, and training institutes. **Highly relevant for KTU context.**
- **Employment Exchanges** — Government-run exchanges (e.g., National Career Service portal) that register job seekers and employers.
- **Walk-in Interviews** — Open-door recruitment where candidates appear directly on a given date. Common for **BPOs, retail, hospitality, and IT support roles.**
- **Recruitment Agencies / Consultants** — Third-party vendors (e.g., Adecco, ManpowerGroup, TeamLease) who specialize in headhunting.
- **E-Recruitment / Online Recruitment** — Using AI-driven job portals (Naukri, Monster, Indeed), ATS (Applicant Tracking Systems), and social media.
- **Poaching / Headhunting** — Luring top performers from competitors. Used for **senior and specialized roles**.
- **Outsourcing / Staff Augmentation** — Hiring through third-party payroll partners.
- **Word of Mouth / Publicity** — Informal but highly effective in small towns.
- **Recruitment at Factory Gate** — Hiring workers at the factory gate when needed. Common in manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Merits:** Wider choice, fresh ideas, specialized skills available, no internal politics.
> **Demerits:** Costly, time-consuming, uncertain quality, longer induction, may demotivate existing staff.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Recruitment Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Yield Ratio** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{No. advanced at stage } n}{\text{No. at stage } n-1} \times 100$ | Measures effectiveness of each recruitment filter |
| **Cost per Hire (CPH)** | $CPH = \dfrac{\text{Total Recruitment Cost}}{\text{Number of Hires}}$ | Used to benchmark recruitment spend |
| **Selection Ratio (SR)** | $SR = \dfrac{\text{No. Selected}}{\text{No. of Applicants}} \times 100$ | Lower SR = more selective process |
| **Acceptance Rate** | $AR = \dfrac{\text{Offers Accepted}}{\text{Offers Made}} \times 100$ | Indicates attractiveness of employer brand |
| **Time to Fill (TTF)** | $TTF = \text{Date of Offer Accepted} - \text{Date Requisition Raised}$ | Lower is better; KPIs track this |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry benchmarks (bolded for quick recall):**
> - Average **Time to Fill = 30–45 days**
> - Average **Cost per Hire = ~₹40,000–₹1,00,000** in India
> - Healthy **Yield Ratio at Interview Stage ≈ 50%**

### 2.4 The Strategic Choice — Internal vs. External (When to Use What?)

| Situation | Preferred Source | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Filling entry-level roles in bulk | External — Walk-ins, Campus | Volume, cost-effectiveness |
| Need for fresh ideas / new technology | External — Headhunting, Campus | Innovation, new skills |
| Filling senior / leadership roles | Internal — Promotion, OR External — Poaching | Continuity OR specialized talent |
| Cost-cutting in HR budget | Internal — Referrals, Promotions | Lowest cost, fastest |
| Diversity mandate (women, PWD) | External — Job fairs, NGOs | Wider net, inclusion goals |
| Filling a niche/specialist role | External — Recruitment agency | Access to passive candidates |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

### 3.1 The 7-Stage Recruitment Process (KTU-Standard Flow)

Every KTU answer on "Recruitment Process" should follow this **linear, exhaustive sequence:**

1. **Identification of Vacancy** — The HR department receives a *requisition* from the line manager stating the need, justification, and job specifications.
2. **Job Analysis & Job Description** — The HR team, with the help of the department head, prepares a **Job Description (JD)** and **Job Specification (JS)**.
3. **Choice of Recruitment Source** — Decision: Internal (cheaper, faster) vs. External (fresh skills, wider reach). The choice is guided by **cost, urgency, availability, and organizational policy.**
4. **Execution of Recruitment Plan** — Advertising in newspapers, posting on portals, contacting agencies, scheduling campus drives, etc.
5. **Receipt of Applications / Résumés** — Candidates submit their applications either online (ATS portal) or offline (paper forms).
6. **Screening of Applications** — Initial shortlisting based on eligibility, qualifications, and keywords. The **Applicant Tracking System (ATS)** automates this stage in modern firms.
7. **Creating a Shortlist for Selection** — The filtered list is forwarded to the **Selection Department** for the next phase (tests, interviews, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output of the **recruitment process is a shortlist**, *not* a hired employee. This is the **boundary where recruitment ends and selection begins.**

### 3.2 Workthrough — Computing Yield Ratios (Typical KTU Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A software company in Technopark, Trivandrum, advertised for 100 developer posts. The recruitment data was as follows:
> - Applications received: **10,000**
> - Screened-in for written test: **2,000**
> - Cleared written test: **500**
> - Appeared for interview: **500**
> - Selected after interview: **200**
> - Offered: **200**
> - Joined: **150**

**Step 1 — Calculate Yield Ratio at each filter stage**

$$
Y_{\text{Application} \to \text{Screen}} = \frac{2{,}000}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 20\%
$$

This means **20% of applicants passed the initial screening.** A yield of 20% is *healthy* — it means the screening criteria are reasonable, not too strict.

$$
Y_{\text{Screen} \to \text{Written Test Clear}} = \frac{500}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 25\%
$$

**Logic:** Out of every 4 candidates who sat for the written test, 1 cleared it. This is the typical industry benchmark for technical screening.

$$
Y_{\text{Written} \to \text{Interview}} = \frac{500}{500} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

**Logic:** All who cleared the written test were called for the interview. Yield = 100% indicates *no drop-off* between the written test and interview call.

$$
Y_{\text{Interview} \to \text{Selected}} = \frac{200}{500} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

**Logic:** 2 out of every 5 interviewed candidates were found suitable. This is a *moderate selectivity* — neither too lenient nor too harsh.

$$
Y_{\text{Selected} \to \text{Offer Accepted}} = \frac{150}{200} \times 100 = 75\%
$$

**Logic:** 25% of those offered the job **rejected** the offer. The *offer-dropout rate* is **25%**, which signals either a **weak employer brand**, a **low salary offer**, or **delayed offer letters**.

**Step 2 — Calculate the Overall Selection Ratio**

$$
\text{Selection Ratio (SR)} = \frac{200}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 2\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Only **2 out of every 100 applicants** were selected. A low selection ratio (≤5%) means a **highly competitive process**, which generally improves the quality of the hired pool.

**Step 3 — Calculate the Final Joining-to-Application Ratio (Funnel Efficiency)**

$$
\text{Final Funnel Efficiency} = \frac{150}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 1.5\%
$$

**Interpretation:** For every **10,000 applications**, the company successfully onboarded **150 candidates** — meaning a final conversion efficiency of **1.5%**. This is a *standard funnel* for IT mass recruitment drives in India.

**Step 4 — Diagnose the Bottleneck**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The largest drop occurred between **Offer Made → Offer Accepted** (25% drop). **Management action:** Investigate reasons — Was the offer letter delayed? Was the salary below market? Was the company’s Glassdoor rating low? Address these to improve **joining ratio** in the next cycle.

### 3.3 Factors Affecting Recruitment (High-Frequency KTU 14-Mark Topic)

Recruitment is influenced by **internal** and **external** factors:

**A. Internal Factors (Within the Organization)**
- **Organizational Size** — Larger firms have formal HR departments and dedicated budgets.
- **Recruitment Policy** — Some firms prefer internal promotion; others hire externally (e.g., Google vs. traditional PSUs).
- **Organizational Image / Employer Brand** — Companies like Tata, Infosys, and Google attract talent easily; unknown firms struggle.
- **Cost & Budget Constraints** — Determines whether agencies, ads, or campus drives are used.
- **Nature of Job** — Senior specialized roles need headhunters; entry-level bulk needs portals.

**B. External Factors (Outside the Organization)**
- **Labour Market Conditions** — Tight market (low unemployment) → Tough recruitment. Loose market → Easier.
- **Government Policies & Laws** — Reservation policies, equal opportunity laws, minimum wage rules.
- **Social & Cultural Factors** — Caste, gender, regional, and family preferences influence applicant pool.
- **Educational Infrastructure** — Availability of qualified graduates in the region.
- **Technology & Industry Trends** — AI, remote work, gig economy all reshape recruitment.
- **Unemployment Rate** — Higher unemployment = higher application volume.

### 3.4 Recruitment Policy — The Governing Document

> [!NOTE]
> A **Recruitment Policy** is a written document that defines:
> - The **objectives** of recruitment (e.g., "To attract the top 10% of graduating engineers in Kerala by 2026").
> - The **preferred sources** (Internal-first vs. External-first).
> - The **approval hierarchy** for hiring.
> - The **diversity & inclusion commitments.**
> - The **budget allocation** per role.
> - The **evaluation metrics** (TTF, CPH, Yield Ratios).

A robust recruitment policy ensures **consistency, fairness, and cost discipline** across the organization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The 7-Stage Recruitment Process (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stage 1: Vacancy Identification"]:::start --> B["Stage 2: Job Analysis and JD Preparation"]
    B --> C["Stage 3: Source Selection - Internal or External"]
    C --> D["Stage 4: Recruitment Plan Execution - Ads Portals Campus"]
    D --> E["Stage 5: Receive Applications and Resumes"]
    E --> F["Stage 6: Screening via ATS or Manual"]
    F --> G["Stage 7: Shortlist Handed to Selection Team"]:::end

    classDef start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Recruitment begins at the *green node* (vacancy) and flows downstream through process stages. The **final pink node** represents the *handoff to Selection* — recruitment's job ends here.

### 4.2 Sources of Recruitment — Comprehensive Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Recruitment Sources"]:::root --> B["Internal Sources"]:::int
    A --> C["External Sources"]:::ext

    B --> B1["Promotion"]
    B --> B2["Transfer"]
    B --> B3["Re-employment"]
    B --> B4["Employee Referrals"]
    B --> B5["Internal Job Posting"]
    B --> B6["Deputation and Rotation"]

    C --> C1["Print and Digital Ads"]
    C --> C2["Campus Placement Drives"]
    C --> C3["Employment Exchanges"]
    C --> C4["Walk-in Interviews"]
    C --> C5["Recruitment Agencies"]
    C --> C6["E-Recruitment Portals"]
    C --> C7["Headhunting and Poaching"]
    C --> C8["Outsourcing and Staff Aug"]

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef int fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 The Recruitment Funnel — Volume at Each Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["10,000 Applications Received"]:::top --> B["2,000 Cleared Screening (20%)"]:::mid
    B --> C["500 Cleared Written Test (25%)"]:::mid
    C --> D["200 Selected Post Interview (40%)"]:::mid
    D --> E["150 Joined After Offer (75%)"]:::bottom

    classDef top fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef mid fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000
    classDef bottom fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **width of the funnel** should *narrow* at each stage. If it does not narrow (e.g., 10,000 → 9,500), the **screening filter is too weak**, and selection is being overloaded with low-quality candidates.

### 4.4 Internal vs. External — Decision Matrix Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Need to Fill Vacancy"]:::root --> B{"Is Role Senior or Leadership?"}:::decision
    B -- Yes --> C{"Are Internal Candidates Ready?"}:::decision
    B -- No --> D{"Is Specialized Skill Needed?"}:::decision

    C -- Yes --> E["Use Internal Promotion"]:::int
    C -- No --> F["Use External Headhunting"]:::ext
    D -- Yes --> G["Use Recruitment Agency or Campus"]:::ext
    D -- No --> H["Use Internal Job Posting First"]:::int

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef int fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define recruitment. How is it different from selection?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Recruitment** is the process of **searching for and attracting a pool of qualified candidates** from which the organization can select the right person for a job. It is a *positive* process aimed at generating a large applicant pool. **Selection**, on the other hand, is the *negative* process of **screening, evaluating, and rejecting** unsuitable candidates to choose the best fit. The key difference is that recruitment **ends with a shortlist**, while selection **ends with a hired employee.** *(Approx. 80–100 words — 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any **four internal sources** of recruitment.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The four internal sources of recruitment are:
1. **Promotion** — Moving an employee to a higher position with more responsibility and pay.
2. **Transfer** — Shifting an employee to a different branch or department of the same grade.
3. **Employee Referrals** — Existing employees recommending candidates from their network.
4. **Internal Job Postings** — Vacancies advertised internally via notice boards, intranet, or HRMS portals.
*(1 mark for naming each source correctly; 3 marks for any 4.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **A. (a)** Explain the **internal and external sources of recruitment** in detail with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **A. (b)** Discuss the **factors affecting recruitment** in an industrial organization. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution A (a):**

**Internal Sources of Recruitment:**

1. **Transfers** — Movement of an employee from one job, branch, or department to another at the **same pay grade and designation**. Example: A sales officer in the Kochi branch being transferred to the Calicut branch. *(1 mark)*

2. **Promotions** — Elevation of an employee to a **higher position** with greater responsibilities, authority, and usually a salary hike. Example: A Foreman promoted to Production Supervisor. It boosts morale and rewards loyalty. *(1 mark)*

3. **Re-employment of Ex-employees** — Re-hiring retirees or ex-employees who had a clean record. Useful for capitalizing on past experience, especially in government and PSU sectors. Example: A retired BSNL engineer re-employed as a consultant. *(1 mark)*

4. **Employee Referrals** — Existing employees recommend friends or former colleagues. This is the *warm-call* method. Reduces cost and time-to-fill. Example: Infosys and TCS heavily rely on referrals. *(1 mark)*

5. **Internal Job Postings (IJP)** — Vacancies advertised on the company intranet, giving all eligible internal employees an equal chance to apply. Example: Infosys Lex — internal job portal. *(1 mark)*

6. **Deputation / Job Rotation** — Temporary assignment of employees to different departments for cross-functional skill building. Example: An HR executive on 6-month deputation to the Training Department. *(1 mark)*

7. **Layoff Recall** — Re-hiring laid-off workers when business picks up. Common in cyclical industries like shipping and manufacturing. *(1 mark — bonus point for extra credit)*

**External Sources of Recruitment:**

1. **Advertisement** — Most common external source. Can be in newspapers, trade journals, online portals, LinkedIn, or social media. Example: TCS advertising for 10,000 freshers on LinkedIn. *(1 mark)*

2. **Campus Recruitment** — Companies visit engineering colleges and B-schools to hire fresh graduates. Highly relevant in India, especially in Kerala where KTU colleges produce a large engineering workforce. Example: TCS, Infosys, Wipro visiting NIT Calicut. *(1 mark)*

3. **Employment Exchanges** — Government-run centers that match job seekers with employers. Example: National Career Service (NCS) portal, District Employment Exchange offices. *(1 mark)*

4. **Recruitment Agencies / Consultants** — Specialized firms that handle hiring on behalf of the employer. Example: ManpowerGroup, Adecco, TeamLease. They charge a fee, usually 8–12% of annual CTC. *(1 mark)*

5. **Walk-in Interviews** — Open, on-the-spot interviews where candidates appear directly. Common for BPOs, retail, and IT support. Example: Wipro BPO walk-in drives in Bangalore. *(1 mark)*

6. **E-Recruitment / Online Recruitment** — Use of AI-driven job portals (Naukri, Monster, Indeed), ATS systems, and social media for sourcing. The fastest-growing method. Example: LinkedIn Recruiter, HackerEarth for tech talent. *(1 mark)*

7. **Poaching / Headhunting** — Luring top performers from competitors or other industries. Used for senior roles. Example: Ola hiring a top Flipkart executive for CPO role. *(1 mark)*

8. **Outsourcing / Staff Augmentation** — Hiring through third-party payroll partners. Useful for short-term or project-based needs. Example: IT companies hiring contract developers via MindTree, Mphasis. *(1 mark)*

**Model Solution A (b):**

Recruitment is influenced by a mix of **internal** and **external** factors:

**Internal Factors:**

1. **Organizational Size** — Larger firms like Tata have dedicated recruitment teams; smaller firms rely on informal methods. *(1 mark)*
2. **Recruitment Policy** — Some firms follow a "promote-from-within" policy (e.g., Indian Railways), while others prefer external hiring (e.g., Google). *(1 mark)*
3. **Organizational Image / Employer Brand** — Companies like Infosys, Tata, and Google attract talent easily; lesser-known firms must pay premium salaries. *(1 mark)*
4. **Cost & Budget** — A startup with limited HR budget relies on referrals and LinkedIn; a Fortune 500 firm can afford headhunters and global ads. *(1 mark)*
5. **Nature of the Job** — Specialized roles need niche hiring; routine roles use mass recruitment. *(1 mark)*

**External Factors:**

1. **Labour Market Conditions** — In a tight market (e.g., Kerala IT in 2024), recruitment is tough. In a loose market, it's easier. *(1 mark)*
2. **Government Policies & Laws** — Reservation policies, Equal Remuneration Act, Apprentices Act, etc. shape the recruitment process. *(1 mark)*
3. **Social & Cultural Factors** — Caste, gender, regional, and family preferences affect the applicant pool. Example: Difficulty hiring women in night-shift manufacturing roles in rural Kerala. *(1 mark)*
4. **Educational Infrastructure** — Availability of qualified graduates in the region. Example: Trivandrum and Kochi have richer talent pools than rural districts. *(1 mark)*
5. **Technological Trends** — AI, remote work, gig economy have reshaped recruitment. Example: COVID-19 accelerated 100% remote hiring. *(1 mark)*

*(Total: 7 internal + 7 external points — 7 marks for a balanced answer.)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

> **B. (a)** Explain the **recruitment process** in detail with a neat flowchart. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **B. (b)** What is a **recruitment policy**? Explain its **features and advantages** with examples. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution B (a):**

The recruitment process is a **7-stage linear workflow** that converts a *vacancy* into a *shortlist*:

1. **Identification of Vacancy** — A formal *requisition* is raised by the line manager stating the position, justification, and job specifications. The HR department reviews and approves it. *(1 mark)*

2. **Job Analysis & Description Preparation** — HR, in collaboration with the department head, prepares the **Job Description (JD)** — duties, tasks, responsibilities — and the **Job Specification (JS)** — skills, qualifications, experience required. *(1 mark)*

3. **Choice of Recruitment Source** — HR decides whether to use **internal sources** (cheaper, faster) or **external sources** (fresh talent, wider reach). The decision is based on cost, urgency, and policy. *(1 mark)*

4. **Execution of Recruitment Plan** — The chosen sources are activated. This may include placing ads in newspapers, posting on LinkedIn, scheduling campus drives, or engaging recruitment agencies. *(1 mark)*

5. **Receipt of Applications** — Interested candidates submit their applications — either through online ATS portals or via paper forms. *(1 mark)*

6. **Screening of Applications** — The HR team shortlists applications based on eligibility, qualifications, and keywords. **Modern firms use ATS (Applicant Tracking Systems)** to automate this step. *(1 mark)*

7. **Shortlist Handed to Selection** — The final filtered list is passed on to the **Selection Department**, which conducts tests, interviews, and reference checks. *This is the boundary where recruitment ends.* *(1 mark)*

*(Reference Diagram: See Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid flowchart.)*

**Model Solution B (b):**

**Recruitment Policy Definition:**
A **Recruitment Policy** is a **written, top-management-approved document** that defines the *objectives, sources, approval hierarchy, and evaluation criteria* for hiring across the organization. It serves as a **rulebook** to ensure consistency, fairness, and strategic alignment in every hiring decision. *(2 marks for definition)*

**Features of a Good Recruitment Policy:** *(5 marks — 1 mark each for any five)*

1. **Clear Objectives** — Defines what the policy aims to achieve (e.g., "Hire the top 5% of graduating engineers in Kerala").
2. **Preferred Sources** — Specifies whether internal or external sources take priority.
3. **Approval Hierarchy** — Defines who has the authority to approve which level of hiring (e.g., Manager for junior roles, Director for senior).
4. **Diversity & Inclusion Commitments** — Mandates reservations for women, PWD, SC/ST, OBC as per law.
