Industrial Disputes)) Workers Side Low Wages Long Hours Unsafe Conditions No Welfare Facilities Unfair Dismissal Management Side Authoritarian Style Poor Communication Weak Grievance System Unfair Transfers External Factors Recession Government Policies Political Interference Technology Change Globalization ``` --- ## 4.3 Forms of Industrial Disputes — Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Industrial Disputes] --> B[Workers Tools] A --> C[Employer Tools] A --> D[Employment Status Changes] B --> B1[Strike] B --> B2[Sympathetic Strike] B --> B3[Sit-down Strike] B --> B4[Go-slow Strike] B --> B5[Hunger Strike] C --> C1[Lockout] C --> C2[Closure] D --> D1[Lay-off] D --> D2[Retrenchment] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style B fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B style C fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#DC143C style D fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B ``` --- ## 4.4 Three-Tier Settlement Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph T1[TIER 1 - PREVENTIVE] T1A[Grievance Procedure] T1B[Works Committee Sec 3] T1C[Collective Bargaining] end subgraph T2[TIER 2 - FACILITATIVE] T2A[Conciliation Officer Sec 4] T2B[Board of Conciliation Sec 5] T2C[Mediation] end subgraph T3[TIER 3 - INTERVENTIVE] T3A[Labour Court Sec 7A] T3B[Industrial Tribunal Sec 7B] T3C[National Tribunal Sec 7C] end T1 --> T2 --> T3 style T1 fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#228B22 style T2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520 style T3 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#B22222 ``` --- ## 4.5 Strike vs Lockout — Comparative Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Conflict Triggered] --> B{Who Acts First?} B -- Workmen --> S[STRIKE] B -- Employer --> L[LOCKOUT] S --> S1[Refusal to Work] S --> S2[Picketing] S --> S3[Loss of Wages for Workers] L --> L1[Factory Closed] L --> L2[Workers Barred Entry] L --> L3[Loss of Production for Employer] style S fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style L fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B ``` --- # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Rapid Recap --- ## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Industrial Dispute. Mention its essential features. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** As per **Section 2(k) of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947**, an industrial dispute is *"any dispute or difference between employers and employers, or between employers and workmen, or between workmen and workmen, which is connected with the employment or non-employment, or the terms of employment, or with the conditions of labour, of any person."* **Essential Features (1.5 marks):** 1. It is a **dispute or difference** of opinion. 2. The parties must belong to the **same industry or industrial establishment**. 3. The dispute must relate to **employment, terms of employment, or conditions of labour**. 4. The dispute must be **real and genuine**, not fictitious. --- ### Q2. Distinguish between Strike and Lockout. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | Strike | Lockout | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | Cessation of work by workmen (Sec 2(q)) | Temporary closure of establishment by employer (Sec 2(l)) | | **Initiator** | Workers / Trade Union | Employer / Management | | **Nature** | Offensive (to pressurize) | Defensive / Preventive | | **Effect on wages** | Workers lose wages | Workers remain unpaid; employer loses revenue | --- ## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1) **Q. (a) Define Industrial Disputes. Explain the various causes of industrial disputes. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** An industrial dispute, as per **Section 2(k) of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947**, is a disagreement between employers and workmen (or between two groups of workmen) connected with **employment, terms of employment, or conditions of labour**. The 7th Amendment of 1956 expanded the scope to include disputes raised by even a single workman. **Causes (5 marks):** **(i) Demand-Side Causes (Workers' Grievances):** - Inadequate wages and bonus structures - Poor working conditions — heat, dust, ventilation - Long working hours without overtime pay - Denial of leave and welfare benefits - Unfair dismissal or victimization - Non-implementation of statutory labour laws **(ii) Supply-Side Causes (Management Issues):** - Authoritarian leadership style - Weak communication channels - Ineffective grievance redressal mechanism - Unfair promotion, transfer, or disciplinary policies - Inter-union rivalry - Indiscipline among workers **(iii) External Causes:** - Economic recession and market downturns - Government policy changes (tax, labour reforms) - Political interference in union activities - Technological changes (automation fear) - Globalization and outsourcing pressure --- **(b) Discuss the various forms of industrial disputes with suitable examples. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Model Answer:** **(i) Strike (2 marks):** Cessation of work by workmen acting in combination (Sec 2(q)). - **General Strike** — e.g., nationwide 24-hour general strike. - **Sympathetic Strike** — workers of Plant A strike in support of Plant B. - **Sit-down Strike** — workers remain at workplace but refuse to work. - **Go-slow Strike** — deliberate reduction in work pace. - **Tool-down Strike** — workers refuse to operate tools/machinery. - **Wildcat Strike** — sudden, unauthorized, not union-sanctioned. - **Hunger Strike** — workers fast inside the factory premises. **(ii) Lockout (1.5 marks):** Temporary closure of establishment by the employer to prevent or counter a strike. Example: Factory gates shut during wage negotiation deadlock. **(iii) Lay-off (1 mark):** Temporary inability of employer to provide work due to shortage of power, raw materials, or breakdown of machinery. Worker is entitled to **50% of basic + DA** under Sec 25C. **(iv) Retrenchment (1 mark):** Permanent termination of service for reasons other than discipline. Employer must pay **15 days' wages per year** of service (Sec 25F) and give 1-month notice. **(v) Closure (1.5 marks):** Permanent shutting down of the establishment. Employer must obtain **government permission** (if 100+ workmen), serve **60 days' notice**, and pay retrenchment compensation (Sec 25-O, 25FF). --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2) **Q. (a) Explain the machinery for the settlement of industrial disputes in India. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 provides a **3-tier settlement mechanism**: **Tier 1 — Preventive Machinery (Inside the Organisation) (2 marks):** - **Grievance Procedure** — formal step-by-step channel for complaint handling. - **Works Committee (Sec 3)** — joint forum with equal representation of employers and workmen (for establishments with 100+ workmen) to resolve disputes before they escalate. - **Collective Bargaining** — negotiation between union and management. - **Joint Management Councils** — for worker participation in decision-making. **Tier 2 — Conciliation Machinery (Statutory) (2.5 marks):** - **Conciliation Officer (Sec 4)** — appointed by the Government; investigates the dispute, mediates, and promotes a settlement. Best effort within **14 days** in public utility services. - **Board of Conciliation (Sec 5)** — a **constituted ad-hoc board** for a specific dispute; submits a report within **2 months** with binding or non-binding recommendations. - **Mediation** — informal facilitation; mediator has no statutory power. **Tier 3 — Adjudication Machinery (Compulsory) (2.5 marks):** - **Labour Court (Sec 7A)** — adjudicates **rights disputes** (e.g., wrongful dismissal, interpretation of standing orders). Single presiding officer. - **Industrial Tribunal (Sec 7B)** — adjudicates **interest disputes** (e.g., wage revision). Award is binding. - **National Tribunal (Sec 7C)** — for disputes affecting **industries in more than one state**. - **Voluntary Arbitration** — parties may submit disputes to an arbitrator of their choice; award is binding and enforceable. --- **(b) Calculate the retrenchment compensation payable to a workman with the following data: Last drawn basic + DA = ₹ 800/day; Service period = 12 years 4 months. State the relevant legal provisions. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Legal Provisions (3 marks):** As per **Section 25F of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947**, retrenchment compensation is calculated as **15 days' wages for every completed year of service**. The employer must also: - Give **1-month notice** in writing (Sec 25F(a)) OR pay notice pay in lieu. - Issue notice in **Form O** to the appropriate Government authority (Sec 25F(c)). **Calculation (4 marks):** **Step 1 — Identify completed years of service:** Service period = 12 years 4 months - Completed years = **12** (4 months < 12, hence ignored). **Step 2 — Apply the retrenchment compensation formula:** $$ C_{\text{retrenchment}} = W_{\text{daily}} \times 15 \times Y_{\text{completed}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the values:** $$ C_{\text{retrenchment}} = 800 \times 15 \times 12 $$ **Step 4 — Compute step-by-step:** $$ C_{\text{retrenchment}} = 800 \times 180 = \text{₹ } 1{,}44{,}000 $$ **Result (1 mark):** The workman is entitled to **₹ 1,44,000** as retrenchment compensation. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the "completed year" rule** — Students often use 12 years 4 months as 12.33 years. This is **wrong** under the Act. The fractional part is **discarded** unless it is 6 months or more. > 2. **Confusing basic wages with gross wages** — Retrenchment compensation is based on **basic + DA only**, NOT gross salary. This is the most common error in KTU valuation scripts. > 3. **Ignoring the LIFO (Last-in-First-Out) rule** — Senior workmen cannot be retrenched while juniors in the same category are retained. Forgetting this loses 1-2 marks. > 4. **Not stating the legal Section number** — Always quote **Section 25F** explicitly; without it, the answer is incomplete. > 5. **Confusing lay-off and retrenchment compensation** — Lay-off is **50% of daily wages per day laid off**; Retrenchment is **15 days' wages per year of service**. These are **NOT** the same formula. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-minute revision? Just read this!** - ✅ **Section 2(k)** = Industrial Dispute - ✅ **Section 2(l)** = Lockout - ✅ **Section 2(q)** = Strike - ✅ **Section 2(kkk)** = Lay-off - ✅ **Section 2(oo)** = Retrenchment - ✅ **Section 2(cc)** = Closure - ✅ **Retrenchment Compensation Formula** = `Basic + DA × 15 × Completed Years` - ✅ **Lay-off Compensation Formula** = `½ × Basic + DA × Days of Lay-off` - ✅ **Closure Notice Period** = **60 days** - ✅ **Retrenchment Notice Period** = **1 month** OR notice pay - ✅ **Works Committee Threshold** = **100+ workmen** (Sec 3) - ✅ **Three-Tier Settlement System**: - **Tier 1:** Grievance Procedure + Works Committee + Collective Bargaining - **Tier 2:** Conciliation Officer + Board of Conciliation + Mediation - **Tier 3:** Labour Court (Rights) + Industrial Tribunal (Interest) + National Tribunal (Multi-State) - ✅ **Legal conditions for illegal strike/lockout** (Sec 24/25): During conciliation, arbitration, or adjudication pendency; without 14-day notice in public utilities. - ✅ **LIFO Rule** = Last-in-First-Out during retrenchment (junior workers retrenched first). - ✅ **Form O** is used to notify the Government about retrenchment (Sec 25F(c)). - ✅ **Form Q** is used to apply for closure permission (Sec 25-O). - ✅ **Types of Strikes (commonly tested):** General, Sympathetic, Sit-down, Go-slow, Wildcat, Tool-down, Hunger, Lightning. - ✅ **Prevention strategies:** Standing Orders, Grievance Redressal, WPM, Collective Bargaining, Fair HR Policies, Employee Welfare. - ✅ **Dispute Originators:** - **Workers →** Strike - **Employer →** Lockout / Lay-off / Retrenchment / Closure ---

topics.title: Collective Bargaining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Collective Bargaining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collective Bargaining (CB)** is the formal process of *negotiation* between an **employer (or a group of employers)** and a **group of employees**, typically represented by a **registered Trade Union**, to determine the **conditions of employment** — including wages, working hours, benefits, workplace safety, grievance procedures, and other terms of the employment contract — through the creation of a mutually binding written instrument known as the **Collective Bargaining Agreement (CBA)**.

The term was formally introduced by **Sidney and Beatrice Webb** in their seminal work (1891), who defined it as:

> *"Collective Bargaining is a process of discussion and negotiation between two parties, one representing the workers and the other the employer, in order to arrive at a mutually agreeable settlement of issues relating to the terms and conditions of employment."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bazaarlike Negotiation

Imagine a **weekly vegetable market** where individual buyers and sellers haggling one-on-one often produce uneven outcomes — buyers either overpay or sellers under-recover. Now suppose the sellers form a **Vendors' Cooperative Union** and the buyers form a **Traders' Association**. Each side now negotiates as a bloc, leveraging collective strength and information symmetry. The result is a fair, predictable, and enforceable market price for the entire season.

**This is exactly Collective Bargaining**:
- **Workers = Vendors' Cooperative** (sell their labour collectively)
- **Employer = Traders' Association** (buy labour collectively)
- **Settlement = Market Price** (CBA)

The transaction moves from chaotic, atomised "individual haggling" (where the employer typically holds the leverage) to an orderly, rule-based, and transparent **institutional negotiation**.

### 1.3 Key Participants and Their Roles

| Party | KTU Term | Typical Role in CB |
|---|---|---|
| Employer / Management | **Party of the First Part** | Seeks productivity, cost control, and operational flexibility |
| Trade Union (Agent) | **Bargaining Agent / Union Representative** | Negotiates on behalf of workers for better wages & conditions |
| Workers (Principal) | **Principal Bargaining Unit** | The constituency on whose behalf the union bargains |
| Conciliation Officer | **Mediator** *(Government appointed)* | Intervenes only on failure of negotiations |
| Labour Court / Tribunal | **Adjudicator** | Decides disputes if conciliation fails |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the Indian context, the **Trade Unions Act, 1926** grants registered unions the legal standing to bargain, while the **Industrial Disputes Act, 1947** provides the statutory framework for negotiation, conciliation, and adjudication of disputes arising from failed CB.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Essential Characteristics (Features) of Collective Bargaining

A process must satisfy the following features to qualify as genuine CB under KTU/ILO standards:

1. **It is a Group Process** — Negotiations are between *groups*, not individuals.
2. **It is a Continuous Process** — Not a one-time event; it establishes ongoing institutional dialogue.
3. **It is a Bipartite Process** — Involves *two* parties (employer and union); the government is an external regulator, not a participant.
4. **It is a Voluntary Process** — Driven by mutual consent, not legal compulsion (although statute may compel *recognition*).
5. **It is a Dynamic Process** — Subject to constant change in response to economic, social, and technological shifts.
6. **It is a Flexible Process** — Both parties retain the freedom to apply pressure tactics (strikes/lock-outs) as leverage.
7. **It produces a Binding Agreement** — The outcome is enforceable as a legal contract (the CBA).
8. **It is essentially a Power Equation** — The final settlement reflects the relative economic and political strength of the two parties.

### 2.2 Types of Collective Bargaining (High-Yield for KTU)

| Type | Core Focus | Typical Win-Win? |
|---|---|---|
| **Distributive (Conjunctive)** | Dividing a *fixed* economic pie (e.g., wage pool) | **Zero-sum** — one's gain is the other's loss |
| **Integrative (Cooperative)** | Expanding the pie through joint problem-solving (e.g., productivity-linked pay) | **Positive-sum** — both gain |
| **Productivity Bargaining** | Linking wage increments to measurable productivity gains | **Positive-sum** |
| **Composite Bargaining** | Combines economic issues *with* non-economic issues (e.g., shop-floor rights, job security) | **Mixed** |
| **Concessionary Bargaining** | Workers *concede* benefits to help the firm survive a crisis | **Lose-lose** (short-term) |

### 2.3 The Collective Bargaining Process (Step-by-Step Logic)

The negotiation unfolds in a structured sequence:

1. **Preparation Phase** — Both parties collect data (market wages, inflation, productivity, financial health).
2. **Opening Stage** — Exchange of initial proposals (charter of demands from union; offer letter from management).
3. **Negotiation / Discussion Stage** — Joint sessions, counter-proposals, use of *logrolling* and *trade-offs*.
4. **Bargaining Stage** — Formal give-and-take, possibly involving mediation by a third party.
5. **Settlement / Agreement Stage** — Drafting, signing, and ratification of the CBA.
6. **Administration / Enforcement Stage** — Monitoring compliance; resolving subsequent grievances.
7. **Review / Renewal Stage** — Periodic review (usually every **3 to 5 years**) leading to the next round.

### 2.4 Prerequisites for Successful CB (Pre-Conditions — KTU Favourite)

For CB to function effectively, **all** the following pre-conditions must be satisfied:

- **Strong, Representative Trade Union** — recognised and supported by a majority of workers.
- **Good Faith** from both sides — willingness to negotiate honestly.
- **Willing and Enlightened Management** — accepting workers' right to organise.
- **Right to Strike and Lock-out** — existence of legitimate economic weapons as leverage.
- **Adequate Information** — open sharing of financial and operational data.
- **Sound Industrial Relations Climate** — mutual trust and cooperation.
- **Acceptance of Collective Bargaining as a Way of Life** — institutionalisation of the practice.

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Meaning / Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $B$ | Bargaining Strength Index (qualitative) | $B \propto U \cdot S \cdot L$ |
| $U$ | Union Solidarity (0 to 1) | Membership density and unity |
| $S$ | Strike Fund / Economic Reserve | Workers' capacity to sustain industrial action |
| $L$ | Legal & Political Support | Statutory backing and public sympathy |
| $\Delta W$ | Wage Increment Negotiated | $\Delta W = W_{\text{final}} - W_{\text{initial}}$ |
| $W_{\text{eff}}$ | Effective (Real) Wage | $W_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{nominal}}}{1 + \text{Inflation Rate}}$ |
| $\text{CBA Term}$ | Validity of Agreement | Typically **3 to 5 years** |

> [!NOTE]
> For trade-off analysis, the classic integrative bargaining equation for joint gains is:
>
> $$G_{\text{joint}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (V_i^{\text{employer}} + V_i^{\text{union}})$$
>
> where $V_i^{\text{party}}$ is the valuation of issue $i$ by each party. Successful integration requires that issues be prioritised differently by the two parties — the foundation of *logrolling*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Analytical Matrix — Strategy Walkthroughs and Worked Examples

### 3.1 Distributive vs Integrative Bargaining — A Comparative Derivation

Let us analyse how the two extremes differ mathematically in outcome.

**Scenario:** A firm has a fixed budget surplus of **₹1,00,000** to distribute among its workforce of **100 workers**. The union demands **₹2,000 per worker** (₹2,00,000 total), while the management offers **₹800 per worker** (₹80,000 total).

**Distributive Bargaining Outcome:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta W_{\text{aspiration}} &= W_{\text{union}} - W_{\text{management}} \\
&= ₹2{,}000 - ₹800 \\
&= ₹1{,}200 \text{ per worker}
\end{aligned}$$

The ZOPA (Zone of Possible Agreement) is bounded by the budget surplus:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{min (employer)}} &= ₹800 \\
W_{\text{max (employer)}} &= ₹1{,}000 \quad \left(\text{since } \dfrac{₹1{,}00{,}000}{100} = ₹1{,}000\right) \\
\text{ZOPA} &= [₹800,\; ₹1{,}000]
\end{aligned}$$

Since the union's demand (₹2,000) lies *outside* the ZOPA, a **distributive settlement is impossible** without altering the surplus.

**Integrative Bargaining Resolution:**

The union agrees to a productivity-linked wage proposal. Suppose a **5% productivity gain** generates an additional ₹50,000 in value-added.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{New Surplus} &= ₹1{,}00{,}000 + ₹50{,}000 = ₹1{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{Per-worker headroom} &= \dfrac{₹1{,}50{,}000}{100} = ₹1{,}500
\end{aligned}$$

Now the new ZOPA is $[₹800,\; ₹1{,}500]$, which contains the union's modified target of ₹1,400.

$$\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{joint}} &= (₹1{,}500 - ₹800)_{\text{employer gain}} + (₹1{,}400 - ₹800)_{\text{union gain}} \\
&= ₹700 + ₹600 = ₹1{,}300 \text{ per worker of total new value created}
\end{aligned}$$

This demonstrates the **positive-sum nature** of integrative bargaining.

### 3.2 Bargaining Strength Index — Worked Example

A union has the following parameters:
- Membership Density: **90%** ($U = 0.90$)
- Strike Fund Sufficient for **60 days** (assume normalised $S = 0.75$)
- Strong public sympathy during a price-rise cycle ($L = 0.60$)

$$\begin{aligned}
B &= U \times S \times L \\
&= 0.90 \times 0.75 \times 0.60 \\
&= 0.405
\end{aligned}$$

If the employer side has $B_{\text{management}} = 0.30$ (lockout tolerance, inventory buffer, replacement labour readiness), then the **bargaining power differential** favours the union:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Power Ratio} &= \dfrac{B_{\text{union}}}{B_{\text{management}}} = \dfrac{0.405}{0.30} = 1.35
\end{aligned}$$

A ratio $> 1$ implies the union holds the leverage — it can realistically push for a settlement closer to its aspiration point.

### 3.3 The BATNA Framework (Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement)

The strength of each party is anchored by its **BATNA** — the course of action it will pursue if negotiation collapses.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Settlement Target}_{\text{union}} &= \text{Reservation Price}_{\text{union}} + \alpha \cdot (\text{Aspiration} - \text{Reservation})_{\text{union}} \\
\text{Settlement Target}_{\text{management}} &= \text{Reservation Price}_{\text{management}} - \beta \cdot (\text{Reservation} - \text{Aspiration})_{\text{management}}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\alpha, \beta \in [0,1]$ represent negotiation aggressiveness. A skilled negotiator reveals a *modified BATNA* to influence the counterpart's expectations without disclosing the true BATNA.

### 3.4 Real-Time Wage Inflation Adjustment (Worked Example)

Suppose the CBA was signed at a nominal wage of **₹25,000/month** when CPI was **125**. One year later, CPI rises to **138**.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Inflation Rate} &= \dfrac{138 - 125}{125} = \dfrac{13}{125} = 0.104 \text{ or } 10.4\% \\
W_{\text{required (nominal)}} &= ₹25{,}000 \times (1 + 0.104) = ₹27{,}600 \\
\text{Demand in next round} &= ₹27{,}600 + \text{Productivity share} + \text{Real increment demand}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the analytical basis for the **escalator clause** found in most long-term CBAs.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Collective Bargaining Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[step1Prep] --> B[step2Data]
    B --> C[step3Charter]
    C --> D[step4Offer]
    D --> E[step5Negotiate]
    E --> F{step6Settle}
    F -->|Yes| G[step7CBA]
    F -->|No| H[step8Mediation]
    H --> I{step9Resolve}
    I -->|Yes| G
    I -->|No| J[step10Adjudication]
    J --> G
    G --> K[step11Monitor]
    K --> L[step12Review]
    L --> A
```

### 4.2 Pre-Conditions for Successful Collective Bargaining

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[pillar1Union] --> CB[coreCBSuccess]
    P2[pillar2GoodFaith] --> CB
    P3[pillar3EnlightenedMgmt] --> CB
    P4[pillar4BargPower] --> CB
    P5[pillar5Information] --> CB
    P6[pillar6IRClimate] --> CB
    P7[pillar7WayOfLife] --> CB
```

### 4.3 Types of Collective Bargaining — Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[CBTypes] --> A1[typeDist]
    root --> A2[typeInt]
    root --> A3[typeProd]
    root --> A4[typeComp]
    root --> A5[typeConc]
    A1 --> A1a[Zero Sum Pie Splitting]
    A2 --> A2a[Win Win Joint Problem Solving]
    A3 --> A3a[Pay Linked to Output]
    A4 --> A4a[Economic plus Non Economic Issues]
    A5 --> A5a[Give Backs During Crisis]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Parties and Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UNION_SIDE
        U1[Workers Principal] --> U2[Trade Union Agent]
    end
    subgraph MGMT_SIDE
        M1[HR Dept] --> M2[Top Mgmt Decision Maker]
    end
    subgraph FACILITATOR
        F1[Conciliation Officer]
        F2[Labour Court]
    end
    U2 <-->|Charter of Demands and Offers| M1
    M1 --> M2
    U2 -.->|If Deadlock| F1
    F1 -.->|If Still Deadlocked| F2
    F2 -.->|Award| M2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Collective Bargaining. List any four essential features.**

**Model Answer:**
Collective Bargaining is the process of negotiation between an employer (or group of employers) and a group of workers, usually represented by a registered trade union, aimed at determining the conditions of employment through a mutually binding written agreement called the Collective Bargaining Agreement (CBA).

Four essential features:
1. It is a **group process** involving collective representation.
2. It is a **continuous process**, not a one-time event.
3. It is a **bipartite process** between two parties.
4. It produces a **legally binding** agreement (the CBA).

*(Any four of the eight features listed in §2.1 accepted.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between Distributive and Integrative Bargaining.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Distributive Bargaining | Integrative Bargaining |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Zero-sum / Win-lose | Positive-sum / Win-win |
| Focus | Dividing existing pie | Expanding the pie jointly |
| Information sharing | Limited / Concealed | Open and full disclosure |
| Relationship | Adversarial | Cooperative |
| Typical issues | Wage hikes, bonus quantum | Productivity-linked pay, training, job design |
| Outcome | Often short-term | Long-term sustainable gains |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay-Type Questions (14 Marks)

> **[Internal Choice Provided — Answer ANY ONE of the following]**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **concept and features** of Collective Bargaining. Discuss the **role of the trade union** as the bargaining agent.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **step-by-step process of collective bargaining** and explain any **three pre-conditions** necessary for its success.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Concept, Features, and Role of Trade Union:*

**Concept:** Collective Bargaining is a formal, institutional process of negotiation between employers and employees (represented by a trade union) to arrive at a mutually agreeable settlement on the terms and conditions of employment. The resultant document is the Collective Bargaining Agreement (CBA), which is binding on both parties.

**Features (any 5):**
1. Group process — representatives negotiate on behalf of groups.
2. Continuous — establishes an ongoing relationship.
3. Bipartite — two parties only; government is external.
4. Voluntary — based on mutual consent.
5. Flexible — allows use of legitimate pressure tactics.
6. Dynamic — adapts to changing economic/social conditions.
7. Power equation — outcome reflects relative strength.

**Role of Trade Union as Bargaining Agent:**
- Acts as the **spokesperson** and legal representative of the workers.
- Aggregates the **collective voice** of otherwise atomised individuals.
- Pools financial resources (strike funds) to sustain collective action.
- Leverages **information asymmetry reduction** by employing experts (lawyers, economists).
- Provides **continuity** of representation across shifts, generations, and disputes.
- Ensures enforcement of the CBA through grievance handling.

**(b)** *Process and Pre-conditions:*

**Step-by-step Process** *(refer §2.3):*
1. Preparation (data collection)
2. Opening (exchange of charter of demands and offer)
3. Discussion / Negotiation
4. Bargaining (give-and-take)
5. Settlement (drafting CBA)
6. Administration (compliance monitoring)
7. Review and renewal

**Three Pre-conditions** *(refer §2.4):*
1. **Strong and Representative Trade Union** — Only when a union enjoys majority support can it credibly commit the workforce to a settlement.
2. **Mutual Good Faith** — Both parties must enter negotiations with an honest intention to settle.
3. **Right to Strike and Lock-out** — The existence of legitimate economic weapons ensures that failure to negotiate has real consequences, motivating compromise.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **various types of collective bargaining** with suitable examples.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Collective Bargaining Agreement (CBA)** — its contents, validity period, and the advantages and limitations of collective bargaining to the workers.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Types of Collective Bargaining:*

Refer to §2.2. The five major types are:

1. **Distributive** — Negotiating a wage hike from a fixed pool. Example: Annual bonus negotiation in a PSU.
2. **Integrative** — Joint problem-solving. Example: Toyota's collaboration with its union to introduce kaizen-based productivity gains, leading to wage growth without layoffs.
3. **Productivity** — Linking pay to measurable output. Example: A garment factory paying ₹5 per piece stitched above a base quota.
4. **Composite** — Mixing economic and non-economic issues. Example: A union negotiating wages *and* a no-layoff clause simultaneously.
5. **Concessionary** — Workers accept lower benefits during a downturn. Example: Airline pilots accepting pay cuts during the COVID-19 crisis to prevent layoffs.

**(b)** *CBA and Advantages / Limitations:*

**Contents of a CBA:**
- Scope and parties covered
- Wages, DA, and bonus formula
- Working hours, overtime, and shift policy
- Leave and holiday entitlements
- Grievance redressal procedure
- Discipline and discharge rules
- Health, safety, and welfare provisions
- Duration and termination clauses

**Validity:** Typically **3 to 5 years**, after which it must be renegotiated.

**Advantages to Workers:**
- Collective strength overcomes individual helplessness.
- Better wages and job security.
- Voice in workplace decisions.
- Protection against arbitrary management action.

**Limitations to Workers:**
- Union dues and possible internal union politics.
- Majority may not reflect every individual's preference.
- Strike action can lead to wage loss during the dispute.
- Slow decision-making in fast-changing industries.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. Writing only the definition without the **features** (loses 2–3 marks in 7-mark sub-parts).
> 2. Confusing **collective bargaining** with **collective negotiation** or simply "union meetings" — CB must produce a **binding written agreement**.
> 3. Forgetting to mention the **statutory backing** under the **Industrial Disputes Act, 1947** (Sections 9, 10, 12, 18) and the **Trade Unions Act, 1926**.
> 4. Omitting the **pre-conditions** — these are *compulsory* test points in KTU theory papers.
> 5. Confusing **distributive** with **integrative** bargaining outcomes — remember: distributive = fixed pie, integrative = expanded pie.
> 6. Spelling/terminology errors: "barganing," "agreemnt," "trde union" — these are flagged by strict examiners.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Negotiation between *groups* on terms of employment → leads to a **binding CBA**.
- **Originators of the concept:** Sidney & Beatrice Webb (1891).
- **Statutes:** Trade Unions Act, **1926** (recognition) + Industrial Disputes Act, **1947** (enforcement).
- **Two parties:** Employer and Union. **Government is a regulator, not a participant.**
- **Five types:** Distributive, Integrative, Productivity, Composite, Concessionary.
- **Seven-stage process:** Prep → Opening → Discussion → Bargaining → Settlement → Administration → Review.
- **Eight features:** Group, Continuous, Bipartite, Voluntary, Dynamic, Flexible, Binding, Power-based.
- **Seven pre-conditions** are mandatory — especially *strong union, good faith, right to strike/lockout, mutual information, enlightened management, sound IR climate, acceptance as way of life.*
- **CBA validity:** 3–5 years.
- **BATNA** drives every negotiation — *never reveal your true BATNA; reveal your modified BATNA.*
- **Integrative bargaining** is the modern, KTU-preferred answer for sustainable industrial relations.
- **Real wage erosion** must be factored into long-term CBAs via **escalator (DA) clauses**.
- **Strike and lock-out** are the twin economic weapons that give CB its teeth.
- **Mediation** (conciliation officer) is attempted before **adjudication** (labour court/tribunal).
- **Bargaining Strength Index:** $B \propto U \cdot S \cdot L$ — union strength multiplies the three pillars.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trade Unions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET722 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trade Unions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Trade Union** is a continuous and permanent association of wage-earners, whether manual or intellectual, salaried or self-employed, formed for the purpose of regulating the relations between workers and employers, or between workers and workers, or between employers and employers, with the view to maintaining and improving the conditions of their working lives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Section 2, Indian Trade Union Act 1926):**
> "A Trade Union is any combination, whether temporary or permanent, formed primarily for the purpose of regulating the relations between workmen and employers, or between workmen and workmen, or between employers and employers, or for imposing restrictive conditions on the conduct of any trade or business."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Neighborhood Association:**

Imagine you live in a colony where the municipal water supply is irregular. One single resident complaining to the water department is easily ignored. But if the **entire colony forms an association** and collectively writes a petition, the department is *forced* to listen because:
- The volume of complaints is now massive.
- There is **collective bargaining power** — the association can threaten to stop paying water tax collectively.
- The association negotiates on equal terms with the municipal authority.

A **Trade Union works exactly like this**:
- The **workers** = residents of the colony
- The **employer** = the municipal water department
- The **collective association** = the Trade Union
- The **collective bargaining** = the petition/negotiation

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The fundamental purpose of a Trade Union is to **balance the power equation** between an individual worker (weak) and an employer (strong) by uniting workers into a single, organized body.

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of a Trade Union

A body to qualify as a Trade Union must satisfy the following:

1. **Association of Workers** — It is an organization of employees, not of employers.
2. **Voluntary Membership** — Workers join by choice, although pressure from peers may exist.
3. **Continuous Existence** — It is not a temporary body; it is permanent in nature.
4. **Common Interest** — Members share common goals like better wages, working conditions, and job security.
5. **Regulates Relations** — It regulates the relationship between employees and employers.
6. **Registered Body** — Under the Indian **Trade Union Act, 1926**, a union of 7 or more workers can be registered as a Trade Union.
7. **Democratic Governance** — Generally run on democratic principles (one member, one vote).

## 1.4 Historical Background

> [!NOTE]
> **Evolution of Trade Unionism in India:**
> - **Pre-Independence Era:** Trade unionism began in **1918** with the formation of the **Madras Labour Union** by B.P. Wadia.
> - **1920:** All India Trade Union Congress (AITUC) was established — the first central trade union body.
> - **1926:** The **Indian Trade Union Act** was enacted to provide legal recognition and protection to registered trade unions.
> - **Post-Independence:** Multiple central trade unions emerged representing different political ideologies (INTUC, BMS, CITU, HMS, etc.).

## 1.5 Objectives of Trade Unions

| **S.No** | **Primary Objective** | **Description** |
|----------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Wage & Salary Improvement | Negotiate higher wages, bonuses, and increments |
| 2 | Better Working Conditions | Demand safer workplaces, proper ventilation, sanitation |
| 3 | Job Security | Protect workers from unjust termination and lay-offs |
| 4 | Welfare Measures | Push for medical benefits, housing, education for children |
| 5 | Workers' Participation | Ensure representation in management decisions |
| 6 | Social Security | Demand pension, gratuity, insurance benefits |
| 7 | Grievance Redressal | Provide a collective voice against unfair treatment |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Functions of Trade Unions

The functions of Trade Unions are classified into two broad categories:

### A. Militant Functions (Inside the Workplace)
These are primarily **protective and combative** in nature:

1. **Collective Bargaining** — Negotiating wages, hours, and conditions as a group rather than individually.
2. **Strike/Work Stoppage** — Withholding labour to pressure the employer.
3. **Grievance Handling** — Representing individual members in disputes with management.
4. **Ensuring Discipline** — Sometimes restraining workers from wildcat strikes.
5. **Resistance to Unfair Labour Practices** — Combating victimization, unfair layoffs, etc.

### B. Fraternal Functions (Outside the Workplace)
These are **cooperative and welfare-oriented** in nature:

1. **Educational Activities** — Conducting classes, workshops, and literacy programs.
2. **Recreational Facilities** — Arranging sports, cultural events, and outings.
3. **Welfare Schemes** — Providing loans, insurance, and financial aid in emergencies.
4. **Legal Assistance** — Offering legal help to workers in court cases.
5. **Publishing Journals** — Producing newsletters and magazines to keep members informed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**M**ilitant functions = **M**oney & **M**atters of work; Fraternal functions = **F**amily, **F**un, and **F**uture welfare."

## 2.2 Types / Structure of Trade Unions

Trade Unions can be classified on multiple criteria:

### Classification by Structure

| **Type** | **Description** | **Example** |
|----------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Craft Union** | Members belong to a single craft or skill (e.g., all carpenters) | Indian Typographical Union |
| **Industrial Union** | All workers in a single industry, regardless of skill | Steel Workers Union |
| **General Union** | Workers across industries and skills | General Workers Union |

### Classification by Affiliation

| **Type** | **Description** |
|----------|-----------------|
| **National/All-India Unions** | Operate across the country with multiple branches |
| **Federation of Unions** | A central body of multiple affiliated unions (e.g., INTUC, BMS) |
| **Local/Plant-level Unions** | Confined to a single factory or establishment |
| **Independent Unions** | Not affiliated with any central federation |

## 2.3 Major Central Trade Unions in India

> [!NOTE]
> **List of Recognized Central Trade Unions in India (as per KTU syllabus):**

| **Abbreviation** | **Full Form** | **Founded** | **Ideological Affiliation** |
|------------------|---------------|-------------|----------------------------|
| **INTUC** | Indian National Trade Union Congress | 1947 | Indian National Congress |
| **AITUC** | All India Trade Union Congress | 1920 | Communist Party of India |
| **BMS** | Bharatiya Mazdoor Sangh | 1955 | RSS-affiliated (Bharatiya Janata Party) |
| **CITU** | Centre of Indian Trade Unions | 1970 | CPI(M) |
| **HMS** | Hind Mazdoor Sabha | 1948 | Socialist Party |
| **UTUC** | United Trade Union Congress | 1949 | Revolutionary Socialist Party |
| **TUCC** | Trade Union Coordination Centre | 1970 | Forward Bloc |

## 2.4 The Trade Union Act, 1926 — Key Provisions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Highlights of the Indian Trade Union Act, 1926 (Mandatory for KTU):**

| **Provision** | **Detail** |
|---------------|------------|
| **Minimum Members for Registration** | 7 workers (or where the union has a majority of members, in cases of establishments with fewer than 7 workers) |
| **Registration Authority** | Registrar of Trade Unions appointed by the State Government |
| **Who Can Form a Union** | Any 7 or more workers can apply for registration |
| **Legal Status** | A registered union becomes a body corporate with perpetual succession |
| **Immunity** | Trade union leaders are immune from civil/criminal liability for bona fide trade union activities (Section 21) |
| **Disqualification** | A person below 15 years of age or not engaged in the trade cannot be a member |
| **Funds & Accounts** | Annual audit is mandatory; funds can only be used for legitimate trade union purposes |
| **Recognition** | The Act does **not** provide statutory recognition (this is a major criticism) |

## 2.5 Problems / Limitations of Trade Unions in India

1. **Multiplicity of Unions** — Multiple unions in a single factory dilute bargaining power.
2. **Political Interference** — Unions often serve party interests rather than workers' interests.
3. **Lack of Professional Leadership** — Many leaders lack formal training in industrial relations.
4. **Inadequate Membership** — A large percentage of the Indian workforce remains un-unionized, especially in the unorganized sector.
5. **Internal Politics** — Factionalism leads to frequent splits and mergers.
6. **Outdated Laws** — The Trade Union Act, 1926 has not been substantially revised.
7. **Limited Financial Resources** — Low membership subscription hampers union activities.

## 2.6 The Role of Trade Unions in Modern Industrial Engineering

In the context of **Industrial Engineering and Management**, trade unions are relevant because they:
- Influence **productivity norms** and **work-study** measurements.
- Negotiate **incentive schemes** and **wage structures**.
- Are stakeholders in **Total Quality Management (TQM)** and **Lean Manufacturing** implementation.
- Participate in **workers' participation in management** schemes (e.g., Joint Management Councils, Works Committees, Shop Councils).
- Impact **human resource planning** and **labour relations** strategies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** An Industrial Engineer designing a new assembly line must consider ergonomic factors (a key union demand), time-study allowances, and statutory labour welfare provisions — all areas where trade unions actively intervene.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Structured Reasoning

Since Trade Unions is a **conceptual management topic** rather than a mathematical one, this section provides a **structured analytical walkthrough** of how to approach KTU-style questions on this topic.

## 3.1 Framework for Answering "Explain Trade Unions" (14-Mark Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Recommended Answer Structure (Follow this exact flow for full marks):**

### **Step 1: Opening Definition (2 marks)**
Begin with a formal definition citing the **Trade Union Act, 1926** and the **Webb's definition**.

> **Webb's Definition (Highly Demanded in KTU Exams):**
> "A trade union is a continuous association of wage-earners for the purpose of maintaining or improving the conditions of their working lives."

### **Step 2: State the Characteristics (2 marks)**
List **at least 5 characteristics** such as voluntary membership, continuous existence, common interest, etc.

### **Step 3: Discuss Functions (4 marks)**
Divide into **Militant** and **Fraternal** functions with 2-3 examples each. Use a table for clarity.

### **Step 4: Types / Structure (2 marks)**
Discuss **Craft, Industrial, and General Unions** with industrial examples.

### **Step 5: Major Central Trade Unions (2 marks)**
Briefly list **INTUC, AITUC, BMS, CITU, HMS** with their affiliations and founding years.

### **Step 6: Problems and Conclusion (2 marks)**
Mention **multiplicity, political interference, lack of professional leadership**, and conclude with the **significance of unions in industrial harmony**.

---

## 3.2 Framework for "Trade Union Act, 1926" Questions

### **Step 1: Introduction (2 marks)**
State the year, purpose, and the historical context (post-WWI labour unrest in India).

### **Step 2: Salient Features (8 marks)**
Cover the following points systematically:
- Minimum members (7 workers)
- Registration procedure
- Rights and liabilities of registered unions
- Immunity provisions (Section 21)
- Funds and accounts
- Disqualifications

### **Step 3: Limitations / Criticisms (2 marks)**
- No statutory recognition
- Outdated
- Weak enforcement

### **Step 4: Conclusion (2 marks)**
Suggest reforms and emphasize the Act's foundational importance.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Union Type

When an industrial engineer must recommend the type of trade union appropriate for a given factory, the following decision framework applies:

| **Condition** | **Recommended Union Type** | **Reasoning** |
|---------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| Single industry, multiple skills (e.g., a steel plant) | Industrial Union | Cross-skill solidarity is essential for industry-wide bargaining |
| Single skilled trade across many firms (e.g., typographers) | Craft Union | Specialized skill demands uniform standards |
| Mixed workforce, no dominant industry | General Union | Broad-based representation is required |
| Politically sensitive workforce | Federated Union | Greater political leverage and resource pooling |
| Single plant, small workforce | Plant-level Union | Localized grievances can be addressed quickly |

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (Conceptual)

**Question:** *A multinational automobile company has 5,000 workers across 3 plants in India. The workforce includes welders, painters, assemblers, electricians, and helpers. The HR head wants to form a single trade union. Recommend the type of union and justify.*

**Analytical Walkthrough:**

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Workforce Nature:** Multiple trades within a single industry.
2. **Step 2 — Map to Union Type:** This is a classic case for an **Industrial Union** (all workers of the automobile industry regardless of skill).
3. **Step 3 — Justification:**
   - A craft union would only represent one trade (e.g., welders), leaving others un-represented.
   - A general union would be too broad and would lack industry-specific focus.
   - An industrial union allows the entire workforce to bargain as one, increasing collective power.
4. **Step 4 — Additional Recommendation:** Consider **affiliation with a national federation** like INTUC or BMS for greater leverage.
5. **Step 5 — Conclusion:** The industrial union model is best suited, supplemented by a national federation for political and legal support.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trade Union Classification Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Trade Unions] --> nodeB[By Structure]
    nodeA --> nodeC[By Affiliation]
    nodeA --> nodeD[By Scope]

    nodeB --> nodeE[Craft Union]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Industrial Union]
    nodeB --> nodeG[General Union]

    nodeC --> nodeH[National Level]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Federation of Unions]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Local or Plant Level]
    nodeC --> nodeK[Independent Unions]

    nodeD --> nodeL[Single Skill Workers]
    nodeD --> nodeM[Single Industry Workers]
    nodeD --> nodeN[All Workers Regardless of Skill]
```

## 4.2 Functions of Trade Unions — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeX[Trade Union Functions] --> nodeY[Militant Functions]
    nodeX --> nodeZ[Fraternal Functions]

    nodeY --> nodeY1[Collective Bargaining]
    nodeY --> nodeY2[Strike Action]
    nodeY --> nodeY3[Grievance Redressal]
    nodeY --> nodeY4[Resistance to Unfair Practices]

    nodeZ --> nodeZ1[Education Programs]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ2[Recreational Activities]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ3[Welfare Schemes]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ4[Legal Aid]

    nodeY1 -.->|Leads to| nodeO[Better Wages]
    nodeY2 -.->|Leads to| nodeP[Employer Pressure]
    nodeZ1 -.->|Leads to| nodeQ[Skilled Workforce]
    nodeZ3 -.->|Leads to| nodeR[Member Loyalty]
```

## 4.3 Major Central Trade Unions of India — Network Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRoot[Central Trade Unions of India]

    nodeRoot --> nodeINTUC[INTUC - 1947 - INC Affiliated]
    nodeRoot --> nodeAITUC[AITUC - 1920 - CPI Affiliated]
    nodeRoot --> nodeBMS[BMS - 1955 - RSS Affiliated]
    nodeRoot --> nodeCITU[CITU - 1970 - CPIM Affiliated]
    nodeRoot --> nodeHMS[HMS - 1948 - Socialist Party]
    nodeRoot --> nodeUTUC[UTUC - 1949 - RSP Affiliated]
    nodeRoot --> nodeTUCC[TUCC - 1970 - Forward Bloc]

    nodeINTUC -.->|Recognized| nodeReg[Government of India List]
    nodeAITUC -.->|Recognized| nodeReg
    nodeBMS -.->|Recognized| nodeReg
    nodeCITU -.->|Recognized| nodeReg
    nodeHMS -.->|Recognized| nodeReg
    nodeUTUC -.->|Recognized| nodeReg
    nodeTUCC -.->|Recognized| nodeReg
```

## 4.4 Trade Union Recognition Process — Workflow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[7 or More Workers Decide to Form Union]
    nodeStart --> nodeDraft[Adopt Constitution and Rules]
    nodeDraft --> nodeElect[Elect Office Bearers]
    nodeElect --> nodeApply[Apply to Registrar of Trade Unions]
    nodeApply --> nodeScrutiny[Registrar Scrutinizes Application]
    nodeScrutiny --> nodeQ{All Requirements Met?}
    nodeQ -->|Yes| nodeApprove[Registration Certificate Issued]
    nodeQ -->|No| nodeReject[Application Rejected or Returned for Correction]
    nodeApprove --> nodeFinal[Registered Trade Union Body Corporate]
    nodeReject --> nodeDraft
```

## 4.5 Trade Union Hierarchy — Organizational Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeTop[National Federation e.g. INTUC]
    nodeTop --> nodeState[State Level Union]
    nodeState --> nodeDistrict[District Level Union]
    nodeDistrict --> nodePlant[Plant or Factory Level Union]
    nodePlant --> nodeMember[Individual Worker Members]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define a Trade Union. State any two characteristics of a Trade Union.*

**Model Answer:**

A Trade Union, as defined by the **Indian Trade Union Act, 1926**, is a combination of workmen or employers formed primarily for the purpose of regulating the relations between workmen and employers, or for imposing restrictive conditions on the conduct of any trade or business.

**Two Characteristics:**
1. **Voluntary Association:** Workers join the union by choice, not by compulsion.
2. **Continuous Existence:** A trade union is a permanent body and is not dissolved once its immediate objective is achieved.

*Valuator's Note:* Award full 3 marks if definition + 2 valid characteristics are provided. A definition without reference to the Act is acceptable but gets only 1.5 marks.

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Distinguish between Craft Union and Industrial Union.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Craft Union** | **Industrial Union** |
|---------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Membership | Workers of a single craft or skill | All workers in an industry regardless of skill |
| Strength | Strong in craft identity | Strong in industry-wide solidarity |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a) Define Trade Union. Explain the functions of Trade Unions in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
A Trade Union is a continuous association of wage-earners formed primarily for the purpose of maintaining and improving the conditions of their working lives. According to the **Indian Trade Union Act, 1926**, it is any combination of seven or more workmen established to regulate relations between workers and employers.

**Functions of Trade Unions (5 Marks):**

The functions of Trade Unions are broadly classified into two categories:

**A. Militant Functions (Protective):**
- **Collective Bargaining:** Negotiating wages, working hours, and conditions collectively rather than individually.
- **Strike/Work Stoppage:** Withholding labour as a pressure tactic during disputes.
- **Grievance Redressal:** Representing individual workers in complaints against management.
- **Resistance to Unfair Labour Practices:** Combating victimization, wrongful termination, etc.

**B. Fraternal Functions (Welfare):**
- **Educational Activities:** Conducting literacy classes, vocational training, and awareness programs.
- **Recreational Facilities:** Organizing sports, cultural events, and community gatherings.
- **Welfare Schemes:** Providing loans, insurance, and medical aid to members.
- **Legal Assistance:** Offering legal aid in disputes, accidents, and compensation cases.

**Conclusion:** Trade Unions play a dual role — protecting workers' immediate interests through militant action while also promoting long-term welfare through fraternal activities, contributing to overall industrial harmony.

---

**(b) Explain the salient features of the Indian Trade Union Act, 1926. What are its limitations? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Salient Features (4 Marks):**

1. **Definition of Trade Union:** The Act provides a legal definition of a trade union as a combination of workmen or employers.
2. **Minimum Membership:** A minimum of **7 workers** is required to form and register a trade union.
3. **Registration Procedure:** Application is made to the **Registrar of Trade Unions** appointed by the State Government.
4. **Legal Status:** A registered union becomes a **body corporate** with perpetual succession, a common seal, and the power to hold property.
5. **Immunity Provision (Section 21):** Trade union leaders are protected from civil and criminal liability for bona fide trade union activities.
6. **Funds and Accounts:** Annual audit of accounts is mandatory; funds can only be used for legitimate trade union purposes.
7. **Disqualifications:** Membership is restricted to persons above 15 years of age and engaged in the trade.

**Limitations (3 Marks):**

1. **No Statutory Recognition:** The Act does not provide a legal mechanism for recognition of a trade union as the sole bargaining agent.
2. **Outdated Provisions:** The Act has not been substantially updated since 1926, despite massive changes in Indian industry.
3. **Weak Enforcement:** Penalties for violations are minimal, leading to non-compliance.
4. **Multiplicity of Unions:** The Act's liberal registration rules allow multiple unions in a single establishment, diluting bargaining strength.
5. **No Provision for Recognition Disputes:** Disputes between rival unions are not adequately addressed.

**Conclusion:** While the Act laid the foundation for trade unionism in India, modern reforms are needed to address the complexities of contemporary industrial relations.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) Discuss the structure and types of Trade Unions with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Trade Unions can be classified on the basis of their structure, scope, and affiliation.

**Types of Trade Unions (6 Marks):**

**1. Craft Union:**
A craft union comprises workers belonging to a single craft, trade, or skill. These unions aim to protect the interests of a specific skilled group.
- *Example:* The Indian Typographical Union represents only typographers.
- *Strength:* Strong craft identity; high bargaining power on skill-specific issues.
- *Weakness:* Excludes semi-skilled and unskilled workers.

**2. Industrial Union:**
An industrial union includes all workers in a particular industry, irrespective of their skill or craft.
- *Example:* The Steel Workers Union of India covers all employees in the steel industry.
- *Strength:* Large membership; industry-wide solidarity.
- *Weakness:* May have internal conflicts between different skill groups.

**3. General Union:**
A general union is one where workers from different industries, skills, and trades are members.
- *Example:* The Indian National Trade Union Congress (INTUC) in its early years.
- *Strength:* Broad-based; inclusive of all workers.
- *Weakness:* Lacks focused bargaining on specific industry issues.

**Other Classifications:**
- **National Federations:** INTUC, AITUC, BMS, CITU, HMS
- **Local/Plant Unions:** Confined to a single establishment.

**Conclusion:** The type of union formed depends on the industry, workforce composition, and socio-political environment.

---

**(b) Explain the role of major Central Trade Unions in India. Discuss the problems faced by Trade Unions in India. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Major Central Trade Unions (4 Marks):**

| **Union** | **Year** | **Affiliation** | **Role** |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|----------|
| **INTUC** | 1947 | Indian National Congress | Largest central trade union; focuses on bipartite settlements |
| **AITUC** | 1920 | Communist Party of India | Oldest central trade union; emphasizes workers' rights and class struggle |
| **BMS** | 1955 | RSS (BJP-affiliated) | Strong among unorganized sector workers; emphasizes nationalism |
| **CITU** | 1970 | CPI(M) | Focuses on working-class solidarity and anti-globalization |
| **HMS** | 1948 | Socialist Party | Advocates for socialist labour policies |

**Problems Faced by Trade Unions in India (3 Marks):**

1. **Multiplicity of Unions:** A single factory may have 5-10 unions, leading to fragmented bargaining.
2. **Political Interference:** Unions are often used as vote banks by political parties.
3. **Lack of Professionalism:** Many union leaders lack formal training in industrial relations or labour law.
4. **Inadequate Membership:** A majority of India's workforce (especially in the unorganized sector) remains un-unionized.
5. **Internal Factionalism:** Frequent splits and mergers weaken union influence.
6. **Financial Constraints:** Low membership subscriptions restrict union activities.
7. **Outdated Legal Framework:** The 1926 Act has not been modernized to address current realities.

**Conclusion:** Despite their challenges, trade unions remain essential for safeguarding workers' interests and maintaining industrial democracy in India.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **No Reference to the Trade Union Act, 1926:** Examiners expect students to cite the Act. Missing this loses **1-2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing Trade Union with Employer Association:** A Trade Union is for **workers**; an Employer Association is for **employers**. Mixing these up is a fatal error.
> 3. **Listing Functions Without Classification:** Always classify functions into **Militant** and **Fraternal**. A plain list loses at least **2 marks**.
> 4. **No Examples in Type Questions:** KTU examiners expect at least **one real-world example** for each type of union.
> 5. **Writing Vague Conclusions:** End with a strong, specific conclusion (e.g., "Trade Unions are essential for industrial democracy") — not generic statements.
> 6. **Skipping the Year of Establishment of Central Unions:** Always mention the year (e.g., INTUC — 1947) to demonstrate depth.
> 7. **No Diagram/Flowchart:** Including a simple flowchart or table (as shown above) can earn **1-2 extra marks** for presentation.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Trade Unions:**

### **Core Definition**
- A Trade Union is a **continuous association of wage-earners** formed to improve working conditions.
- Legal definition is provided by the **Indian Trade Union Act, 1926**.

### **Key Numbers to Remember**
- **Minimum 7 workers** required for registration.
- **Year of Act:** 1926.
- **First Trade Union in India:** Madras Labour Union (1918).
- **First Central Trade Union:** AITUC (1920).
- **Largest Central Union:** INTUC (1947).

### **Classification**
- **By Structure:** Craft, Industrial, General.
- **By Affiliation:** National, Federated, Local, Independent.

### **Functions (Mnemonic: "M-F")**
- **M**ilitant: Bargaining, Strikes, Grievances.
- **F**raternal: Education, Welfare, Recreation.

### **Five Central Trade Unions (Mnemonic: "I-A-B-C-H")**
- **I**NTUC, **A**ITUC, **B**MS, **C**ITU, **H**MS.

### **Salient Features of the Act**
- Registration, Body Corporate status, Immunity (Section 21), Annual Audit, Disqualifications.

### **Major Problems**
- Multiplicity, Political interference, Lack of professionalism, Low membership, Outdated laws.

### **Key Industrial Engineering Linkages**
- Productivity norms, Wage structures, Ergonomics, Workers' participation, TQM/Lean implementation.

### **Commonly Asked KTU Questions**
1. "Define Trade Union and state its functions."
2. "Explain the Trade Union Act, 1926."
3. "Discuss the problems of trade unions in India."
4. "Distinguish between Craft and Industrial Unions."

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Study Tip:** Always structure your answer with a **definition → classification → functions → Act → problems → conclusion** flow. This is the KTU-preferred answer pattern and guarantees high marks.
